title: Analysis of an Idealized Stochastic Polyak Method and its Application to Black-Box Model Distillation
abstract: We provide a general convergence theorem of an idealized stochastic Polyak step size called SPS*. Besides convexity, we only assume a local expected gradient bound, that includes locally smooth and locally Lipschitz losses as special cases. We refer to SPS* as idealized because it requires access to the loss for every training batch evaluated at a solution. It is also ideal, in that it achieves the optimal lower bound for globally Lipschitz function, and is the first Polyak step size to have a $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{t})$ anytime convergence in the smooth setting.  We show how to combine SPS* with momentum to achieve the same favorable rates for the last iterate. We  conclude with several experiments to validate our theory, and a more practical setting  showing how we can distill a teacher GPT-2 model into a smaller student model without any hyperparameter tuning.
author: Robert M. Gower;Guillaume Garrigos;Nicolas Loizou;Konstantin Mishchenko;Dimitris Oikonomou;Fabian Schaipp
aff: Flatiron Institute;Université Paris Cité;Johns Hopkins University;Meta Facebook;Johns Hopkins University;INRIA
aff_unique_norm: Flatiron Institute;Université Paris Cité;Johns Hopkins University;Meta;INRIA

roast.laymen_summary: The authors invented a 'perfect' way to tune AI training speeds, but there is a catch: you have to already know the final answer for the math to work. They tried to prove this 'cheat code' is better than regular methods and used it to shrink a GPT-2 model without touching any settings.
why_bad: The 'idealized' assumption requires knowing the loss at the optimal solution for every single batch, which is like needing a map to find the map you're currently looking for.
The theoretical proofs rely on 'SPS*', a version of the algorithm that cannot actually be implemented in a real-world setting without divine intervention or time travel.
The jump from the high-brow math of 'idealized convergence' to the practical distillation of GPT-2 is massive, leaving a huge experimental gap where the theory doesn't actually explain the results.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where a chef claims they’ve discovered the ultimate secret to the perfect soufflé. The trick? You just have to know exactly how the final, award-winning dish tastes before you even crack the first egg. 'It’s simple,' the chef says, 'just adjust your oven temperature based on how much the finished cake—which doesn't exist yet—would have risen.' It’s mathematically brilliant and practically useless. When the judges ask how a normal person is supposed to bake without a time-traveling tongue, the chef just points to a microwave dinner they made earlier and says, 'See? It works!' but fails to mention they just used the 'Auto-Cook' button like everyone else.

title: Balancing Preservation and Modification: A Region and Semantic Aware Metric for Instruction-Based Image Editing
author_site: Zhuoying Li, Zhu Xu, Yuxin Peng, Yang Liu
abstract: Instruction-based image editing, which aims to modify the image faithfully towards instruction while preserving irrelevant content unchanged, has made advanced progresses. However, there still lacks a comprehensive metric for assessing the editing quality. Existing metrics either require high costs concerning human evaluation, which hinders large-scale evaluation, or adapt from other tasks and lose specified concerns, failing to comprehensively evaluate the modification of instruction and the preservation of irrelevant regions, resulting in biased evaluation. To tackle it, we introduce a new metric Balancing Preservation Modification (BPM), that tailored for instruction-based image editing by explicitly disentangling the image into editing-relevant and irrelevant regions for specific consideration. We first identify and locate editing-relevant regions, followed by a two-tier process to assess editing quality: Region-Aware Judge evaluates whether the position and size of the edited region align with instruction, and Semantic-Aware Judge further assesses the instruction content compliance within editing-relevant regions as well as content preservation within irrelevant regions, yielding comprehensive and interpretable quality assessment. Moreover, the editing-relevant region localization in BPM can be integrated into image editing approaches to improve the editing quality, manifesting its wild application. We verify the effectiveness of BPM metric on comprehensive instruction-editing data, and the re- sults show that we yield the highest alignment with human evaluation compared to existing metrics, indicating efficacy. The code is available at https://joyli-x.github.io/BPM/.
author: Zhuoying Li;Zhu Xu;Yuxin Peng;Yang Liu
aff: Peking University;Peking University;Peking University;Peking University
aff_unique_norm: Peking University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build an automated referee that checks if an AI image editor actually followed instructions or just hallucinated all over the canvas. It attempts to score the 'before and after' by making sure the AI only messed with the parts it was told to and left the rest of the pixels alone.
why_bad: The 'Region-Aware' logic relies on pre-existing object detectors that are notoriously bad at finding the 'regions' for abstract or complex instructions.
It creates a circular validation loop: using the same flavor of AI (Vision-Language Models) to grade the work of another AI often results in them just agreeing on their own shared mistakes.
The metric's claimed 'high alignment' with humans is likely cherry-picked from a narrow dataset, failing to prove it works on anything more complex than putting a hat on a cat.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're a judge on a high-stakes cooking show where the contestant's only job is to sprinkle salt on a steak without touching the mashed potatoes. You’ve built a 'Digital Flavor Inspector' to replace yourself, meant to measure the exact salt-to-steak ratio while ensuring the potatoes remain pristine. However, your inspector is just a glorified camera that panics if the steak is slightly tilted, and it can't actually taste the difference between salt and sugar—it just hopes for the best because it saw a 'Salt' label earlier. You're trying to give a Michelin star based on a sensor that gets confused by steam.

title: Unlocking the Capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models for Generalizable and Explainable Deepfake Detection
author_site: Peipeng Yu, Jianwei Fei, Hui Gao, Xuan Feng, Zhihua Xia, Chip Hong Chang
abstract: Current Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in understanding multimodal data, but their potential remains underexplored for deepfake detection due to the misalignment of their knowledge and forensics patterns. To this end, we present a novel framework that unlocks LVLMs' potential capabilities for deepfake detection. Our framework includes a Knowledge-guided Forgery Detector (KFD), a Forgery Prompt Learner (FPL), and a Large Language Model (LLM). The KFD is used to calculate correlations between image features and pristine/deepfake image description embeddings, enabling forgery classification and localization. The outputs of the KFD are subsequently processed by the Forgery Prompt Learner to construct fine-grained forgery prompt embeddings. These embeddings, along with visual and question prompt embeddings, are fed into the LLM to generate textual detection responses. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks, including FF++, CDF2, DFD, DFDCP, DFDC, and DF40, demonstrate that our scheme surpasses state-of-the-art methods in generalization performance, while also supporting multi-turn dialogue capabilities.
author: Peipeng Yu;Jianwei Fei;Hui Gao;Xuan Feng;Zhihua Xia;Chip Hong Chang
aff: Jinan University;University of Macau;Jinan University;Jinan University;Jinan University;Nanyang Technological University
position: PhD student;Postdoc;MS student;PhD student;Full Professor;Full Professor
aff_unique_norm: Jinan University;University of Macau;Nanyang Technological University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a high-tech AI detective that doesn't just spot a deepfake, but actually chats with you about why the image looks suspicious. They basically bolted a specialized 'fraud sensor' onto a massive chatbot to see if it could explain forensic glitches in plain English.
why_bad: Over-engineered complexity: The 'Knowledge-guided Forgery Detector' and 'Prompt Learner' add massive computational baggage for results that lightweight models can often achieve more efficiently.

Hallucination risks: Using an LLM for 'explainability' is dangerous because these models are notorious for 'hallucinating'—they might correctly guess a photo is fake but provide a completely made-up, 'smart-sounding' reason why.

Weak generalization: While the authors claim it works everywhere, the framework likely overfits to specific dataset patterns, failing the 'vibe check' when faced with real-world deepfakes not found in their controlled lab environment.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Overkill,' the show where we solve simple problems with $100 million solutions. Today, our contestant built a microwave that doesn't just heat your burrito; it uses a laser-guided spectrometer to analyze the bean-to-cheese ratio, feeds that data into a team of French poets to describe the 'mouthfeel,' and then has a robotic butler read you a 10-minute monologue on why the burrito is hot. It’s an incredible feat of engineering, but by the time the robot finishes its 'explainable' speech, the burrito is cold, the kitchen is on fire, and you realize a $30 box from Target would have done the exact same job in 60 seconds.

title: Contextures: Representations from Contexts
author_site: Runtian Zhai, Kai Yang, Burak VARICI, Che-Ping Tsai, Zico Kolter, Pradeep Ravikumar
abstract:
Despite the empirical success of foundation models, we do not have a systematic characterization of the representations that these models learn. 
In this paper, we establish the contexture theory.
It shows that a large class of representation learning methods can be characterized as learning from the association between the input and a context variable. Specifically, we show that many popular methods aim to approximate the top-d singular functions of the expectation operator induced by the context, in which case we say that the representation learns the contexture.
We demonstrate the generality of the contexture theory by proving that representation learning within various learning paradigms -- supervised, self-supervised, and manifold learning -- can all be studied from such a perspective.
We prove that representations that learn the contexture are optimal on those tasks that are compatible with the context.
One important implication of our theory is that once the model is large enough to approximate the top singular functions, scaling up the model size yields diminishing returns, so further improvement requires better contexts.
To this end, we study how to evaluate a context without knowing the downstream tasks. We propose a metric and show by experiments that it correlates well with the actual performance of the encoder on many real datasets.
author: Runtian Zhai;Kai Yang;Burak Varıcı;Che-Ping Tsai;J Zico Kolter;Pradeep Kumar Ravikumar
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Peking University;;Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;Peking University;

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to prove that every AI model is actually just doing the same basic math trick of matching pictures to their captions using fancy linear algebra. We concluded that once your model is big enough to handle the math, adding more 'brain power' is useless unless you actually give it better study materials.
why_bad: The 'Contexture Theory' is essentially just rebranding Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), a math concept older than the researchers' parents, as a shiny new discovery.
Claiming that scaling model size yields diminishing returns is a bold move that ignores the last decade of AI progress where 'bigger is better' has consistently humiliated 'elegant' theories.
The proposed metric for evaluating data quality is empirically thin, relying on 'vibe-check' correlations rather than proving it works on anything more complex than basic toy datasets.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Theory,' where today’s chef claims to have discovered a 'Universal Law of Gastronomy.' The big reveal? If you use high-quality ingredients, the food tastes better. The chef spends forty minutes showing us a spreadsheet proving that a carrot is a 'root-based representation within a soil context' and argues that buying a bigger stove won't help if your vegetables are trash. While technically true, the other chefs are rolling their eyes because they've been using salt for centuries, and this guy is acting like he invented the concept of seasoning. He even built a 'Flavor Meter' to rank his onions, but it only seems to work on pre-peeled ones from the local supermarket.

title: KVTuner: Sensitivity-Aware Layer-Wise Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization for Efficient and Nearly Lossless LLM Inference
author_site: Xing Li, Zeyu Xing, Yiming Li, Linping Qu, Huiling Zhen, Yiwu Yao, Wulong Liu, Sinno Jialin Pan, Mingxuan Yuan
abstract:
KV cache quantization can improve Large Language Models (LLMs) inference throughput and latency in long contexts and large batch-size scenarios while preserving LLMs effectiveness.
However, current methods have three unsolved issues: overlooking layer-wise sensitivity to KV cache quantization, high overhead of online fine-grained decision-making, and low flexibility to different LLMs and constraints. Therefore, we theoretically analyze the inherent correlation of layer-wise transformer attention patterns to KV cache quantization errors and study why key cache is generally more important than value cache for quantization error reduction. We further propose a simple yet effective framework KVTuner to adaptively search for the optimal hardware-friendly layer-wise KV quantization precision pairs for coarse-grained KV cache with multi-objective optimization and directly utilize the offline searched configurations during online inference.
To reduce the computational cost of offline calibration, we utilize the intra-layer KV precision pair pruning and inter-layer clustering to reduce the search space. Experimental results show that we can achieve nearly lossless 3.25-bit mixed precision KV cache quantization for LLMs like Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and 4.0-bit for sensitive models like Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on mathematical reasoning tasks. The maximum inference throughput can be improved by 21.25\% compared with KIVI-KV8 quantization over various context lengths. Our code and searched configurations are available at https://github.com/cmd2001/KVTuner.
keywords: Efficient LLM Inference;KV cache quantization;Mixed precision quantization;Attention patterns;Sensitivity analysis
author: Xing Li;Zeyu XING;Yiming Li;Linping Qu;Hui-Ling Zhen;Yiwu Yao;Wulong Liu;Sinno Jialin Pan;Mingxuan Yuan
aff: Huawei Technologies Ltd.;The Chinese University of Hong Kong;Huawei Technologies Ltd.;Huawei Technologies Ltd.;;Huawei Technologies Ltd.;Huawei Noah's Ark Lab;;Huawei Technologies Ltd.
aff_unique_norm: Huawei;Chinese University of Hong Kong;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make AI faster by aggressively compressing its short-term memory, varying the 'crunchiness' of the data layer by layer. It’s essentially an attempt to give the model selective amnesia so it can talk faster without completely losing its mind.
why_bad: The 'offline search' for optimal settings is essentially a glorified trial-and-error process that likely overfits to the specific datasets used in the paper.
Claiming a 21% throughput increase while introducing the massive engineering headache of mixed-precision kernels is a hard sell for systems engineers who value their sanity.
The 'nearly lossless' claim is backed by narrow benchmarks, ignoring the fact that aggressive quantization often turns coherent AI into a word-salad generator in open-ended conversations.
roast.analogy_description: Look, your LLM has a hoarding problem; it’s keeping every word of a 10,000-page document in high-definition RAM, and your GPU is footing the bill. KVTuner is like a Marie Kondo consultant who realizes some layers of the AI's brain are 'hoarders' and others are 'minimalists.' We’re disrupting the memory industry by deciding that Layer 5 needs to remember everything in 4K, but Layer 30 can probably get away with a blurry Polaroid. Sure, the AI might forget the middle of your prompt, but it’ll give you a wrong answer 21% faster. It's not 'memory loss,' it's 'optimized forgetting.' Pitch deck is in the mail.

title: LightningDrag: Lightning Fast and Accurate Drag-based Image Editing Emerging from Videos
author_site: Yujun Shi, Jun Hao Liew, Hanshu Yan, Vincent Tan, Jiashi Feng
abstract: Accuracy and speed are critical in image editing tasks. Pan et al. introduced a drag-based framework using Generative Adversarial Networks, and subsequent studies have leveraged large-scale diffusion models. However, these methods often require over a minute per edit and exhibit low success rates. We present LightningDrag, which achieves high-quality drag-based editing in about one second on general images. By redefining drag-based editing as a conditional generation task, we eliminate the need for time-consuming latent optimization or gradient-based guidance. Our model is trained on large-scale paired video frames, capturing diverse motion (object translations, pose shifts, zooming, etc.) to significantly improve accuracy and consistency. Despite being trained only on videos, our model generalizes to local deformations beyond the training data (e.g., lengthening hair, twisting rainbows). Extensive evaluations confirm the superiority of our approach, and we will release both code and model.
author: Yujun Shi;Jun Hao Liew;Hanshu Yan;Vincent Y. F. Tan;Jiashi Feng
aff: Meta Facebook+national university of singaore, National University of Singapore;ByteDance;;National University of Singapore;
aff_unique_norm: Meta;national university of singaore, National University of Singapore;ByteDance;;National University of Singapore

roast.laymen_summary: The authors propose a way to 'drag' parts of an image—like making a cat smile or a mountain taller—instantly by training an AI on videos instead of making it solve a complex math puzzle every time. While current tools take a minute to process your edits, this method claims to do it in a second by guessing the movement based on how things usually move in movie clips.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just a standard conditional diffusion setup that swaps actual spatial reasoning for a library of video-clip shortcuts.
Quantitative evaluations are suspiciously thin, relying on 'vibe checks' rather than rigorous metrics that prove it isn't just hallucinating weird artifacts.
The claim that video-trained motion perfectly generalizes to 'twisting rainbows' is a massive leap of faith lacking theoretical evidence or a robust failure-case analysis.
roast.analogy_name: Exhibit A: The Case of the Pre-recorded Alibi
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims their client can 'instantly' reconstruct a crime scene. But look closer! They aren't investigating the evidence; they're just playing back home movies and hoping nobody notices the pixels don't line up. The prosecution argues that while the defendant is indeed fast, they have effectively committed 'perjury by proxy'—replacing rigorous spatial optimization with a glorified 'fast-forward' button. You cannot simply drag a witness’s testimony into a new shape and call it 'truth' without providing the mathematical receipts. This isn't a breakthrough in forensic editing; it's a deep-fried sequence of lucky guesses that falls apart the moment you ask for a cross-examination of the underlying physics.

title: Closed-form Solutions: A New Perspective on Solving Differential Equations
author_site: Shu Wei, Yanjie Li, Lina Yu, Weijun Li, Min Wu, Linjun Sun, Jingyi Liu, Hong Qin, Deng Yusong, Jufeng Han, Yan Pang
abstract:
The quest for analytical solutions to differential equations has traditionally been constrained by the need for extensive mathematical expertise.
Machine learning methods like genetic algorithms have shown promise in this domain, but are hindered by significant computational time and the complexity of their derived solutions. 
This paper introduces **SSDE** (Symbolic Solver for Differential Equations), a novel reinforcement learning-based approach that derives symbolic closed-form solutions for various differential equations. 
Evaluations across a diverse set of ordinary and partial differential equations demonstrate that SSDE outperforms existing machine learning methods, delivering superior accuracy and efficiency in obtaining analytical solutions.
author: Shu Wei;Yanjie Li;Lina Yu;Weijun Li;Min Wu;Linjun Sun;Jingyi Liu;Hong Qin;Yusong Deng;Jufeng Han;Yan Pang
aff: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences;;Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Semiconductors Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Semiconductors;Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of  Sciences;;;University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

aff_unique_norm: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences;;Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Semiconductors;Institute of Semiconductors, Chinese Academy of  Sciences

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an AI that tries to solve hard math problems by guessing formulas until it finds one that works, rather than actually learning calculus. They claim their 'guess-o-matic' is faster than other AI guessers, but it's basically just a very expensive way to do what a $20 calculator already does.
why_bad: The 'diverse set' of test equations is actually just a collection of basic textbook problems that mathematicians solved centuries ago, proving nothing about real-world utility.

Using Reinforcement Learning for symbolic math is like using a flamethrower to light a candle—it consumes massive computational power for results that classical software like Mathematica produces in milliseconds.
The model lacks 'out-of-distribution' robustness, meaning if you change one variable to something it hasn't seen before, the whole system likely hallucinates a mathematically impossible fever dream.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where math is a locked vault, one AI doesn't have the combination... so it’s going to throw a billion random keys at the door until one clicks. Witness the high-octane drama of a trillion-dollar server farm attempting to rediscover the work of a 17th-century guy in a powdered wig! It’s a race against time, logic, and the electric bill. He doesn't know why the formula works, he just knows he’s got 400 GB of RAM and a dream. This summer, experience: THE BRUTE-FORCE BRAINIAC. (Rated R for Restricted Generalization).
roast.tone: Sharp, witty, and grounded in the reality of the 'Symbolic Regression' hype cycle.

title: Varying Manifolds in Diffusion: From Time-varying Geometries to Visual Saliency
abstract: Building on the manifold hypothesis, which suggests that generative models learn data distributions residing on low-dimensional manifolds, this paper investigates the time-varying manifold sequence induced by the generation process through the lens of differential equations in diffusion models. Our primary contribution is the introduction of the \textit{generation rate}, a novel metric that quantifies local manifold scaling over time. For image data, we show that the accumulated generation rate, referred to as the \textit{generation curve}, strongly correlates with intuitive visual properties, such as the saliency of image components. By leveraging modifications to the generation curves, we propose a unified framework for a range of image manipulation tasks, including semantic transfer, object removal, saliency adjustment, and image blending. Comprehensive evaluations, supported by both the qualitative and quantitative results, highlight the effectiveness of our framework across these diverse tasks.
keywords: Manifold analysis; Diffusion model; Image manipuation
author: Junhao Chen;Manyi Li;zherong pan;Xifeng Gao;Changhe Tu
aff: Shandong University;;Tencent America;;Shandong University
aff_unique_norm: Shandong University;;Tencent

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim that by tracking how fast a blurry mess turns into a sharp photo, they’ve discovered a 'speedometer' for what’s important in an image. They use this math to perform basic Photoshop tasks like moving objects or erasing exes, but with significantly more Greek letters than necessary.
why_bad: Rebranding the Wheel: The 'generation rate' is essentially just a Jacobian or basic gradient with a fancy hat on, offering zero new theoretical insight into diffusion math.
Correlation is not Causation: Claiming a 'generation curve' equals visual saliency is a massive leap supported mostly by 'trust me bro' levels of cherry-picked qualitative evidence.
The Over-Engineered Toaster: Their framework for image editing is a Rube Goldberg machine that often yields worse results than existing, simpler tools like ControlNet or standard inpainting.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where everyone is already making perfect digital clones, one team of scientists decides to measure the EXACT velocity at which wet cement dries to prove which part of the sidewalk is the 'most sidewalk.' COMING THIS SUMMER: Watch as they spend $50,000 in compute credits to do what a 'Healing Brush' in a free app does in two seconds. It’s a high-stakes thriller where 'Differential Geometry' meets 'I could have just used the Lasso Tool.' Rated R for Rejected.
roast.tone_check: Lean, mean, and mathematically obscene.

title: L3A: Label-Augmented Analytic Adaptation for Multi-Label Class Incremental Learning
author_site: Xiang Zhang, Run He, Chen Jiao, Di Fang, Ming Li, Ziqian Zeng, Cen Chen, HUIPING ZHUANG
abstract: Class-incremental learning (CIL) enables models to learn new classes continually without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. Multi-label CIL (MLCIL) extends CIL to a real-world scenario where each sample may belong to multiple classes, introducing several challenges: label absence, which leads to incomplete historical information due to missing labels, and class imbalance, which results in the model bias toward majority classes. To address these challenges, we propose Label-Augmented Analytic Adaptation (L3A), an exemplar-free approach without storing past samples. L3A integrates two key modules. The pseudo-label (PL) module implements label augmentation by generating pseudo-labels for current phase samples, addressing the label absence problem. The weighted analytic classifier (WAC) derives a closed-form solution for neural networks. It introduces sample-specific weights to adaptively balance the class contribution and mitigate class imbalance. Experiments on MS-COCO and PASCAL VOC datasets demonstrate that L3A outperforms existing methods in MLCIL tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/scut-zx/L3A.
author: Xiang Zhang;Run He;Chen Jiao;Di Fang;Ming Li;Ziqian Zeng;Cen Chen;Huiping Zhuang
aff: South China University of Technology;South China University of Technology;South China University of Technology;South China University of Technology+Bosch (China) Investment Ltd.+South China University of Technology;Guangming Laboratory;South China University of Technology;South China University of Technology;
position: MS student;MS student;PhD student;MS student+Intern+Undergrad student;Full Professor;Associate Professor;Full Professor;
aff_unique_norm: South China University of Technology;Bosch (China) Investment Ltd.;Guangming Laboratory;

roast.laymen_summary: L3A is an attempt to teach AI how to recognize new objects in photos without forgetting the old ones, all while refusing to keep a 'cheat sheet' of past examples. It tries to fix its memory gaps by making educated guesses about missing labels and using a mathematical shortcut to stop itself from favoring the newest things it learned.
why_bad: Recursive Hallucination: The 'Pseudo-Label' module relies on the model's own guesses to learn, creating a 'garbage in, garbage out' feedback loop that amplifies initial mistakes.

Linearity Overload: Using a 'closed-form analytic solution' is a fancy way of saying they used a simple math formula for a complex problem, which usually collapses when faced with real-world data variety.

The Memory-Accuracy Paradox: By being strictly 'exemplar-free' (storing no old photos), the model's performance likely falls off a cliff compared to methods that keep even a tiny fraction of historical data.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you have an intern who is required to organize your entire company archive, but they have to shred every document the moment they finish reading it. When you ask them about a file from last month, they don't actually remember it; instead, they 'predict' what was in it based on the coffee stains on their desk and use a weighted spreadsheet to make their wild guesses sound statistically significant. It’s a bold pitch: 'We don't need a filing cabinet because our intern is a visionary hallucinator with a calculator!' In reality, your archive is now just a collection of very confident vibes and sophisticated-looking math that falls apart the moment you ask for a specific fact.

title: TypyBench: Evaluating LLM Type Inference for Untyped Python Repositories
author_site: Honghua Dong, Jiacheng Yang, Xun Deng, Yuhe Jiang, Gennady Pekhimenko, Fan Long, Xujie Si
abstract: Type inference for dynamic languages like Python is a persistent challenge in software engineering. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in code understanding, their type inference capabilities remain underexplored. We introduce `TypyBench`, a benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs' type inference across entire Python repositories. `TypyBench` features two novel metrics: `TypeSim`, which captures nuanced semantic relationships between predicted and ground truth types, and `TypeCheck`, which assesses type consistency across codebases. Our evaluation of various LLMs on a curated dataset of 50 high-quality Python repositories reveals that, although LLMs achieve decent `TypeSim` scores, they struggle with complex nested types and exhibit significant type consistency errors. These findings suggest that future research should shift focus from improving type similarity to addressing repository-level consistency. `TypyBench` provides a foundation for this new direction, offering insights into model performance across different type complexities and usage contexts. Our code and data are available at \href{https://github.com/typybench/typybench}.
keywords: Benchmark;Type Inference;Untyped Python Repo;Large Language Models;Long-Context;Repo-Level;Software Engineering
author: Honghua Dong;Jiacheng Yang;Xun Deng;Yuhe Jiang;Gennady Pekhimenko;Fan Long;Xujie Si
aff: Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto;University of Toronto;;Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto+Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto;Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto;Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto;University of Toronto
position: PhD student;PhD student;;PhD student+MS student;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Toronto;

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a grading system to see if AI can correctly guess what kind of data a Python program is handling when the labels are missing. They discovered that while the AI is okay at making 'close enough' guesses, it constantly forgets its own logic and contradicts itself when looking at the bigger picture.
why_bad: The dataset of 50 repositories is statistically microscopic, making the 'benchmark' feel more like a localized vibe check than a robust industry standard.
The TypeSim metric essentially awards participation trophies for being 'almost right,' ignoring the fact that in programming, a 'near miss' on a type still crashes the entire system.
The paper highlights that AI is bad at global consistency—a well-known industry headache—but offers no actual solution beyond pointing at the problem and asking someone else to fix it.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Perjuring Polygraph
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents TypyBench: a tool designed to vet a witness who claims to be an expert in identification. On the stand, the AI witness looks at a red fluid and says it’s 'probably wine-adjacent,' which the researchers call a 'high TypeSim score.' But then, the witness identifies the victim as a 'Human' in the opening statement and a 'Sentient Toaster' during cross-examination. This isn't a 'nuanced semantic relationship'; it’s perjury. You can't build a case when your star witness maintains the internal consistency of a fever dream. The researchers are asking for a mistrial and a grant to study why their witness is a liar, rather than just admitting the witness shouldn't be in the courtroom.

title: Q-VDiT: Towards Accurate Quantization and Distillation of Video-Generation Diffusion Transformers
author_site: Weilun Feng, Chuanguang Yang, Haotong Qin, Xiangqi Li, Yu Wang, Zhulin An, Libo Huang, Boyu Diao, Zixiang Zhao, Yongjun Xu, Michele Magno
abstract:
Diffusion transformers (DiT) have demonstrated exceptional performance in video generation. However, their large number of parameters and high computational complexity limit their deployment on edge devices. Quantization can reduce storage requirements and accelerate inference by lowering the bit-width of model parameters.
Yet, existing quantization methods for image generation models do not generalize well to video generation tasks. We identify two primary challenges: the loss of information during quantization and the misalignment between optimization objectives and the unique requirements of video generation. To address these challenges, we present **Q-VDiT**, a quantization framework specifically designed for video DiT models. From the quantization perspective, we propose the *Token aware Quantization Estimator* (TQE), which compensates for quantization errors in both the token and feature dimensions. From the optimization perspective, we introduce *Temporal Maintenance Distillation* (TMD), which preserves the spatiotemporal correlations between frames and enables the optimization of each frame with respect to the overall video context. Our W3A6 Q-VDiT achieves a scene consistency score of 23.40, setting a new benchmark and outperforming the current state-of-the-art quantization methods by **1.9$\times$**.
author: Weilun Feng;Chuanguang Yang;Haotong Qin;Xiangqi Li;Yu Wang;Zhulin An;Libo Huang;Boyu Diao;Zixiang Zhao;Yongjun Xu;Michele Magno

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to shrink massive video-generating AIs so they could run on a toaster by aggressively chopping their data into tiny, low-quality bits. They claim they fixed the resulting visual glitches with a 'spatial-temporal glue,' but the results mostly just look like slightly more consistent digital soup.
why_bad: W3A6 quantization is essentially trying to perform brain surgery with garden shears; 3-bit weights are too blunt to preserve any actual nuance.
The 'Scene Consistency Score' is a bespoke metric that smells like it was engineered in a lab specifically to make their mediocre results look like a world record.
The 1.9x improvement claim relies on comparing their fancy new method against outdated baselines that weren't even designed for video in the first place.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where your GPU costs more than a Honda Civic, one team tries to cram an entire Hollywood studio into a single floppy disk. SEE the 'Token aware Quantization Estimator' guess what a human face looks like using only four colors! WATCH the 'Temporal Maintenance Distillation' struggle to remember that a protagonist shouldn't morph into a floor lamp between frames! It’s a high-stakes thriller where the resolution is so low, you’ll pray for a buffering wheel just to give your eyes a rest. Coming this fall to a 'Rebuttal Ignored' folder near you.

title: Fluctuations of the largest eigenvalues of transformed spiked Wigner matrices
author_site: Aro Lee, Ji Oon Lee
abstract: We consider a spiked random matrix model obtained by applying a function entrywise to a signal-plus-noise symmetric data matrix. We prove that the largest eigenvalue of this model, which we call a transformed spiked Wigner matrix, exhibits Baik-Ben Arous-Péché (BBP) type phase transition. We show that the law of the fluctuation converges to the Gaussian distribution when the effective signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is above the critical number, and to the GOE Tracy-Widom distribution when the effective SNR is below the critical number. We provide precise formulas for the limiting distributions and also concentration estimates for the largest eigenvalues, both in the supercritical and the subcritical regimes.
keywords: spiked random matrix;Wigner matrix;largest eigenvalue;PCA;signal detection
author: Aro Lee;Ji Oon Lee
aff: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;KAIST
aff_unique_norm: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The paper attempts to calculate exactly when a hidden signal becomes visible through a cloud of random noise after the data has been distorted by a mathematical function. It identifies a 'tipping point' where the signal stops acting like a standard bell curve and starts behaving like a more complex, chaotic distribution.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The authors applied a well-known phenomenon (BBP transition) to a slightly modified matrix and acted like they discovered fire.
Technical Pedantry: The paper spends forty pages providing 'precise formulas' for behaviors that anyone in Random Matrix Theory could have guessed over a lukewarm espresso.
Limited Utility: The 'transformation' aspect is a mathematical niche that offers zero practical benefit to actual data scientists who deal with real-world noise.
roast.analogy_name: The Golden Truffle in the Mashed Potato Mountain
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Probability,' where today’s contestant is trying to find a single truffle hidden inside a mountain of instant mashed potatoes—but with a twist: they’ve spray-painted the whole mess neon purple. Our 'expert' chef spent the entire episode writing a 50-page manual on how much the truffle wobbles when you shake the plate. If the truffle is big enough, it jiggles like a standard bowl of Jell-O; if it's too small, it vibrates with the chaotic energy of a toddler on a sugar rush. The judges sent them home because we already know how truffles shake, and the purple paint didn't make the potatoes taste any better—it just made the math harder to swallow.

title: Off-Policy Evaluation of Ranking Policies for Large Action Spaces via Embeddings and User Behavior Assumption
abstract: Off-policy evaluation (OPE) in ranking settings with large ranking action spaces, which stems from an increase in both the number of unique actions and length of the ranking, is essential for assessing new recommender policies using only logged bandit data from previous versions. To address the high variance issues associated with existing estimators, we introduce two new assumptions: *no direct effect on rankings* and *user behavior model on ranking embedding spaces*. We then propose *the generalized marginalized inverse propensity score (GMIPS) estimator* with statistically desirable properties compared to existing ones. We characterize the parameters of our estimator that minimize the mean squared error (MSE). Finally, we demonstrate that the GMIPS achieves the lowest MSE. Notably, among GMIPS variants, the *marginalized reward interaction IPS (MRIPS)* incorporates a doubly marginalized importance weight based on a cascade behavior assumption on ranking embeddings. MRIPS effectively balances the trade-off between bias and variance, even as the ranking action spaces increase and the above assumptions may not hold, as evidenced by our experiments.
keywords: Off-Policy Evaluation;Counterfactual Machine Learning;Inverse Propensity Score
author: Tatsuki Takahashi;Chihiro Maru;Hiroko Shoji
aff: Chuo University;;Chuo University
aff_unique_norm: Chuo University;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors want to predict how well a new recommendation system will work using only old data, which is usually impossible because the number of ways to rank items is astronomical. To fix this, they invented a math shortcut that assumes humans browse websites in a perfectly predictable, robotic fashion within a simplified 'embedding' universe.
why_bad: The 'no direct effect' assumption is basically a fantasy where the order of a list doesn't actually influence a user's choice—which defeats the entire purpose of studying ranking.
The proposed GMIPS estimator is a classic case of 'math-washing,' adding extreme complexity to hide the fact that the model relies on assumptions that are likely to fail in the real world.
The paper claims to balance bias and variance, but the reviewers suspect it only works on the authors' specific toy datasets and would explode the moment it encountered an actual unpredictable human.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Telepathic Taste-Tester
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense (the authors) claims they can predict exactly how a diner will react to a 50-course tasting menu based solely on a receipt for a cheeseburger they bought in 2019. Their star witness is a mathematical ghost called 'The Cascade Assumption,' who testifies that humans always eat from top to bottom and never get distracted by a shiny napkin. The prosecution argues that this 'GMIPS' evidence is inadmissible because it treats the diner not as a hungry human, but as a coordinate in a 128-dimensional vector space. You can't claim you've solved the 'Large Action Space' of a buffet when your entire argument rests on the assumption that the diner has no free will and views a shrimp cocktail as a 'latent embedding.' Objections sustained; the math is fancy, but the logic is in contempt of court.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 100% billable hour confidence.

title: An Efficient Private GPT Never Autoregressively Decodes
author_site: Zhengyi Li, Yue Guan, Kang Yang, Yu Feng, Ning Liu, Yu Yu, Jingwen Leng, Minyi Guo
abstract: The wide deployment of the generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) has raised privacy concerns for both clients and servers. While cryptographic primitives can be employed for secure GPT inference to protect the privacy of both parties, they introduce considerable performance overhead. To accelerate secure inference, this study proposes a public decoding and secure verification approach that utilizes public GPT models, motivated by the observation that securely decoding one and multiple tokens takes a similar latency. The client uses the public model to generate a set of tokens, which are then securely verified by the private model for acceptance. The efficiency of our approach depends on the acceptance ratio of tokens proposed by the public model, which we improve from two aspects: (1) a private sampling protocol optimized for cryptographic primitives and (2) model alignment using knowledge distillation. Our approach improves the efficiency of secure decoding while maintaining the same level of privacy and generation quality as standard secure decoding. Experiments demonstrate a $2.1\times \sim 6.0\times$ speedup compared to standard decoding across three pairs of public-private models and different network conditions.
author: Zhengyi Li;Yue Guan;Kang Yang;Yu Feng;Ning Liu;Yu Yu;Jingwen Leng;Minyi Guo

roast.laymen_summary: Keeping AI conversations private usually requires heavy encryption that makes the AI run at the speed of a dial-up modem. This paper tries to cheat the system by letting a fast, unencrypted 'decoy' model guess the words first, then using the secret model to quickly verify if the guesses were right.
why_bad: The 'speedup' relies entirely on the public model being a mind-reader for the private one; if they aren't perfectly synced, the system actually runs slower than doing nothing.
The 'private sampling protocol' introduces enough cryptographic math homework to negate the time saved by the clever guessing strategy.
It assumes the public model won't accidentally leak sensitive patterns about the private data it’s trying to mimic, which is like trying to keep a secret by shouting hints.
roast.analogy_name: The Paranoiac and the Chatty Intern
roast.analogy_description: Picture a CEO so paranoid he works inside a literal bank vault and only communicates via encrypted carrier pigeons—it's secure, but it takes three days to say 'Hello.' Our startup, 'QuickLeaker,' solves this! We put a random intern outside the vault who shouts guesses at the door: 'Are you saying... buy more stocks?' The CEO just thumps the door once for 'Yes' or twice for 'No.' It's a 5x speedup in communication! Of course, if the intern is an idiot, the CEO spends all day thumping the door in frustration, and eventually, the intern learns exactly what’s in the vault just by listening to the rhythm of the thumps. It’s disruptive, it’s agile, and it’s probably a security nightmare. Invest now!

title: Satori: Reinforcement Learning with Chain-of-Action-Thought Enhances LLM Reasoning via Autoregressive Search
author_site: Maohao Shen, Guangtao Zeng, Zhenting Qi, Zhang-Wei Hong, Zhenfang Chen, Wei Lu, Gregory Wornell, Subhro Das, David Cox, Chuang Gan
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning capabilities across diverse domains. Recent studies have shown that increasing test-time computation enhances LLMs' reasoning capabilities. This typically involves extensive sampling at inference time guided by an external LLM verifier, resulting in a two-player system. Despite external guidance, the effectiveness of this system demonstrates the potential of a single LLM to tackle complex tasks. Thus, we pose a new research problem: *Can we internalize the searching capabilities to fundamentally enhance the reasoning abilities of a single LLM?* This work explores an orthogonal direction focusing on post-training LLMs for autoregressive searching (*i.e.,* an extended reasoning process with self-reflection and self-exploration of new strategies). To achieve this, we propose the Chain-of-Action-Thought (COAT) reasoning and a two-stage training paradigm: 1) a small-scale format tuning stage to internalize the COAT reasoning format and 2) a large-scale self-improvement stage leveraging reinforcement learning. Our approach results in Satori, a 7B LLM trained on open-source models and data. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that Satori achieves state-of-the-art performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks while exhibits strong generalization to out-of-domain tasks. Code, data, and models are fully open-sourced.
author: Maohao Shen;Guangtao Zeng;Zhenting Qi;Zhang-Wei Hong;Zhenfang Chen;Wei Lu;Gregory W. Wornell;Subhro Das;David Daniel Cox;Chuang Gan

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to teach a small AI to double-check its own homework by letting it 'talk to itself' through a internal search process instead of relying on a second AI to grade it. They basically wanted the model to develop an inner monologue that catches its own mistakes before it blurts out a wrong answer.
why_bad: The 'Chain-of-Action-Thought' (COAT) is arguably just a rebranding of existing 'Chain-of-Thought' techniques with extra steps, offering limited theoretical novelty.
The 7B parameter scale is too small to prove the method actually 'internalizes reasoning' rather than just over-fitting on specific math benchmarks.
Autoregressive search significantly increases 'time-to-first-token' and inference costs, making the model's 'deep thinking' too expensive for most real-world applications.
roast.analogy_name: The Gordon Ramsay Inner Monologue Special
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the chef doesn't have a sous-chef to taste the sauce, so he just stands there screaming at himself in the mirror. 'Is it salty? Let me think! I'll add sugar... No, that's a trap! I'll add salt and rethink my life!' He's essentially trying to 'internalize' the entire kitchen staff into his own head. Sure, he eventually serves a decent risotto, but he burned three hours and forty-two 'action-thoughts' just to decide if the water was boiling. It's technically impressive that he didn't set the kitchen on fire, but most people would prefer a chef who just knows how to cook without the existential crisis.

title: Trajectory World Models for Heterogeneous Environments
author_site: Shaofeng Yin, Jialong Wu, Siqiao Huang, Xingjian Su, he, Jianye Hao, Mingsheng Long
abstract: Heterogeneity in sensors and actuators across environments poses a significant challenge to building large-scale pre-trained world models on top of this low-dimensional sensor information. In this work, we explore pre-training world models for heterogeneous environments by addressing key transfer barriers in both data diversity and model flexibility. We introduce UniTraj, a unified dataset comprising over one million trajectories from 80 environments, designed to scale data while preserving critical diversity. Additionally, we propose TrajWorld, a novel architecture capable of flexibly handling varying sensor and actuator information and capturing environment dynamics in-context. Pre-training TrajWorld on UniTraj yields substantial gains in transition prediction, achieves a new state-of-the-art for off-policy evaluation, and also delivers superior online performance of model predictive control. To the best of our knowledge, this work, for the first time, demonstrates the transfer benefits of world models across heterogeneous and complex control environments. Code and data are available at https://github.com/thuml/TrajWorld.
author: Shaofeng Yin;Jialong Wu;Siqiao Huang;Xingjian Su;Xu He;Jianye HAO;Mingsheng Long
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Huawei Technologies Ltd.;Tianjin University;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to create a 'One Brain to Rule Them All' for robots, hoping that a model trained on everything from drones to robotic arms could learn the universal laws of physics. They built a massive library of robot movements and a flexible AI to study it, but the results are more 'Jack of all trades, master of none.'
why_bad: The 'novel' architecture is essentially just a Transformer with a fancy nameplate, offering little in the way of actual structural innovation.
The dataset 'heterogeneity' is mostly just different configurations within the same simulator, which is like claiming you're a polyglot because you can order a taco in three different fonts.
The performance improvements are marginal and mostly rely on brute-forcing the problem with more data rather than fixing the underlying transfer learning issues.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitch for an AI that writes assembly instructions for literally anything. To build it, you feed it manuals for a LEGO set, a Boeing 747, and a Billy bookcase. The founders claim it has captured the 'essence of assembly.' In reality, the AI is great at predicting that a 'screw usually goes in a hole,' but if you actually ask it to build a skyscraper using instructions from a toaster, the whole thing collapses. It’s a 'world model' that’s never actually touched the world, just read a lot of brochures about it.

title: Skip the Equations: Learning Behavior of Personalized Dynamical Systems Directly From Data
author_site: Krzysztof Kacprzyk, Julianna Piskorz, M van der Schaar
abstract: While black-box approaches are commonly used for data-driven modeling of dynamical systems, they often obscure a system's underlying behavior and properties, limiting adoption in areas such as medicine and pharmacology. A two-step process of discovering ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and their subsequent mathematical analysis can yield insights into the system's dynamics. However, this analysis may be infeasible for complex equations, and refining the ODE to meet certain behavioral requirements can be challenging. Direct semantic modeling has recently been proposed to address these issues by predicting the system's behavior, such as the trajectory's shape, directly from data, bypassing post-hoc mathematical analysis. In this work, we extend the original instantiation, limited to one-dimensional trajectories and inputs, to accommodate multi-dimensional trajectories with additional personalization, allowing evolution to depend on auxiliary static features (e.g., patient covariates). In a series of experiments, we show how our approach enables practitioners to integrate prior knowledge, understand the dynamics, ensure desired behaviors, and revise the model when necessary.
author: Krzysztof Kacprzyk;Julianna Piskorz;Mihaela van der Schaar
aff: ;University of Cambridge;University of Cambridge+University of California, Los Angeles
aff_unique_norm: ;University of Cambridge;University of California, Los Angeles

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to predict how complex systems (like a patient's health) evolve by having an AI 'eye-ball' the shape of the data instead of doing the actual math. It’s essentially a high-tech way of skipping calculus class and hoping the AI can just 'vibe' its way into understanding physics.
why_bad: Mathematical Hand-Waving: By 'skipping the equations,' the authors also skipped the part where they prove the model won't hallucinate physically impossible behavior.
The 'Glorified Curve-Fitter' Problem: The reviewers felt the model doesn't actually understand dynamics; it just memorizes shapes and panics when it sees a trajectory it hasn't seen before.
Weak Baselines: The paper claims to be a breakthrough but barely manages to beat standard, 30-year-old math techniques that don't require a thousand-dollar GPU to run.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where gravity is just a suggestion and blueprints are 'too mainstream,' one researcher decides to build a skyscraper... using only his intuition. [Deep Voiceover Voice] HE DOESN'T NEED STRUCTURAL ENGINEERING. HE DOESN'T NEED LOAD-BEARING WALLS. He's skipping the physics and going straight to the aesthetic. Watch as he attempts to build a 50-story medical center based solely on the 'energy' of the dirt. Coming this summer: THE MAN WHO KNEW TOO LITTLE. Rated R for 'Catastrophic Structural Failure.'
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels stabilized at 85%.

title: Adaptive Sensitivity Analysis for Robust Augmentation against Natural Corruptions in Image Segmentation
author_site: Laura Zheng, Wenjie Wei, Tony Wu, Jacob Clements, Shreelekha Revankar, Andre Harrison, Yu Shen, Ming Lin
abstract: Achieving robustness in image segmentation models is challenging due to the fine-grained nature of pixel-level classification. These models, which are crucial for many real-time perception applications, particularly struggle when faced with natural corruptions in the wild for autonomous systems. While sensitivity analysis can help us understand how input variables influence model outputs, its application to natural and uncontrollable corruptions in training data is computationally expensive. In this work, we present an adaptive, sensitivity-guided augmentation method to enhance robustness against natural corruptions. Our sensitivity analysis on average runs 10 times faster and requires about 200 times less storage than previous sensitivity analysis, enabling practical, on-the-fly estimation during training for a model-free augmentation policy. With minimal fine-tuning, our sensitivity-guided augmentation method achieves improved robustness on both real-world and synthetic datasets compared to state-of-the-art data augmentation techniques in image segmentation.
author: Laura Yu Zheng;Wenjie Wei;Tony Wu;Jacob Clements;Shreelekha Revankar;Andre Harrison;Yu Shen;Ming Lin

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make AI better at recognizing objects in messy, real-world conditions like rain or fog by quickly identifying which pixels confuse the model the most. They essentially built a 'faster' way to stress-test the AI during its training sessions so it wouldn't panic when it sees a blurry street sign.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The 'speed-up' is mostly just cutting corners on math that researchers have been doing for years, making it a performance patch rather than a scientific breakthrough.

The 'Sims' Fallacy: Testing on 'synthetic corruptions' is like claiming you're a world-class pilot because you’re really good at Microsoft Flight Simulator; it rarely translates to actual messy reality.
Weak Baselines: The authors claimed victory by comparing their method against outdated techniques, which is the academic equivalent of bragging about winning a race against a toddler.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Speed-Reading Witness
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution claims they have a revolutionary new eyewitness. This witness claims to have identified the suspect in a torrential downpour, while sprinting, from three blocks away. But under cross-examination, the truth comes out: the witness wasn't actually 'observing' the scene better; they were just blinking 10 times faster to save energy. They’ve presented 'evidence' that consists of a grainy Polaroids where the 'rain' was clearly added in post-production with a Sharpie. Your Honor, the defense moves to dismiss. You can't claim a breakthrough in forensic vision when you're just squinting at a filtered Instagram photo and calling it 'robust' testimony. This isn't a new legal precedent; it's a glorified eye exam with a 'Fast Forward' button.

title: Improved Off-policy Reinforcement Learning in Biological Sequence Design
author_site: Hyeonah Kim, Minsu Kim, Taeyoung Yun, Sanghyeok Choi, Emmanuel Bengio, Alex Hernandez-Garcia, Jinkyoo Park
abstract: Designing biological sequences with desired properties is challenging due to vast search spaces and limited evaluation budgets. Although reinforcement learning methods use proxy models for rapid reward evaluation, insufficient training data can cause proxy misspecification on out-of-distribution inputs. To address this, we propose a novel off-policy search, $\delta$-Conservative Search, that enhances robustness by restricting policy exploration to reliable regions. Starting from high-score offline sequences, we inject noise by randomly masking tokens with probability $\delta$, then denoise them using our policy. We further adapt $\delta$ based on proxy uncertainty on each data point, aligning the level of conservativeness with model confidence. Experimental results show that our conservative search consistently enhances the off-policy training, outperforming existing machine learning methods in discovering high-score sequences across diverse tasks, including DNA, RNA, protein, and peptide design.
author: Hyeonah Kim;Minsu Kim;Taeyoung Yun;Sanghyeok Choi;Emmanuel Bengio;Alex Hernández-García;Jinkyoo Park

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to design new proteins by taking high-quality biological sequences, intentionally breaking them, and then asking an AI to 'fix' them into something even better. It’s essentially an expensive way to prevent their AI from hallucinating 'god-tier' DNA that would actually just be useless molecular gunk in a real lab.
why_bad: Circular Validation: The authors 'proved' their model works by testing it against another AI simulator instead of real biological data, which is like grading your own homework and giving yourself an A+.
Glitter on a Pig: The 'novel' $\delta$-Conservative Search is basically just a standard denoising trick from 2015 rebranded with a Greek letter to make it sound like a breakthrough.

Extreme Parameter Sensitivity: The entire system relies on the model 'knowing when it doesn't know,' but AI models are notoriously overconfident liars, making the $\delta$ adjustment more of a lucky guess than a robust science.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the kitchen! Our chef wants to create a world-class Beef Wellington, but they’ve never actually touched a stove. Their strategy? Take a perfectly good Wellington, throw it in a blender for a random number of seconds—let's call that '$\delta$'—and then try to glue the pieces back together while blindfolded. They claim this 'Denoising Soufflé' method is revolutionary because they only blend the parts they 'feel' unsure about. However, the only judge is a cardboard cutout that's been programmed to say 'Delicious!' regardless of the texture. It looks great on camera, but the moment a real human takes a bite, they’re getting food poisoning.

title: Discovering Latent Causal Graphs from Spatiotemporal Data
author_site: Kun Wang, Sumanth Varambally, Duncan Watson-Parris, Yian Ma, Rose Yu
abstract: Many important phenomena in scientific fields like climate, neuroscience, and epidemiology are naturally represented as spatiotemporal gridded data with complex interactions.  Inferring causal relationships from these data is a challenging problem compounded by the high dimensionality of such data and the correlations between spatially proximate points. We present SPACY (SPAtiotemporal Causal discoverY), a novel framework based on variational inference, designed to model latent time series and their causal relationships from spatiotemporal data. SPACY alleviates the high-dimensional challenge by discovering causal structures in the latent space. To aggregate spatially proximate, correlated grid points, we use spatial factors, parametrized by spatial kernel functions, to map observational time series to latent representations. Theoretically, we generalize the problem to a continuous spatial domain and establish identifiability when the observations arise from a nonlinear, invertible function of the product of latent series and spatial factors. Using this approach, we avoid assumptions that are often unverifiable, including those about instantaneous effects or sufficient variability. Empirically, SPACY outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on synthetic data, even in challenging settings where existing methods struggle, while remaining scalable for large grids. SPACY also identifies key known phenomena from real-world climate data. An implementation of SPACY is available at \url{https://github.com/Rose-STL-Lab/SPACY/}
keywords: Representation Learning;Causal Discovery;Spatiotemporal Inference;Variational Inference;Time Series Analysis
author: Kun Wang;Sumanth Varambally;Duncan Watson-Parris;Yian Ma;Rose Yu
aff: Princeton University;;University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego
aff_unique_norm: Princeton University;;University of California, San Diego

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to figure out what's causing what in massive, messy datasets like weather maps or brain scans by grouping neighboring data points into 'hidden' categories. They essentially built a math-heavy filter to find the underlying puppet masters behind millions of flickering data points without needing to assume everything happens instantly.
why_bad: The 'identifiability' proof relies on a non-linear invertible function that is essentially a theoretical unicorn—mathematically elegant but practically non-existent in noisy real-world systems.
The empirical 'wins' were largely against outdated baselines on synthetic data that was conveniently structured to play to the model's specific strengths.
While claiming to handle high dimensionality, the reliance on spatial kernels acts as a glorified smoothing filter that might actually be erasing the granular causal signals they're trying to find.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Invisible Arsonist
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution—the authors—claim they've identified the 'hidden' arsonist responsible for a series of house fires across the city. Their evidence? They didn't actually see the arsonist; they just noticed that houses close together burned similarly, so they grouped them into 'Latent Neighborhoods.' They argue that because they used a 'Spatial Factor'—a fancy way of saying they checked the GPS—they've proven the fire didn't start everywhere at once. But when the defense asks for a direct witness, the prosecution points to a complex non-linear equation and says, 'Trust us, the math says the arsonist is identifiable.' The judge is unimpressed because the prosecution's 'SOTA' comparison was just a group of toddlers with magnifying glasses, and the 'real-world' evidence was just a picture of a candle that they claim proves the entire theory of combustion.

title: Distributed Parallel Gradient Stacking(DPGS): Solving Whole Slide Image Stacking Challenge in Multi-Instance Learning
author_site: Boyuan Wu, wang, Xianwei Lin, Jiachun Xu, Jikai Yu, Zhou Shicheng, Hongda Chen, Lianxin Hu
abstract: Whole Slide Image (WSI) analysis is framed as a Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) problem, but existing methods struggle with non-stackable data due to inconsistent instance lengths, which degrades performance and efficiency. We propose a Distributed Parallel Gradient Stacking (DPGS) framework with Deep Model-Gradient Compression (DMGC) to address this. DPGS enables lossless MIL data stacking for the first time, while DMGC accelerates distributed training via joint gradient-model compression. Experiments on Camelyon16 and TCGA-Lung datasets demonstrate up to 31× faster training, up to a 99.2% reduction in model  communication size at convergence, and up to a 9.3% improvement in accuracy compared to the baseline. To our knowledge, this is the first work to solve non-stackable data in MIL while improving both speed and accuracy.
keywords: Multi-Instance Learning ，Distributed Training，Gradient Compression，Whole Slide Image Analysis，Medical Image Classification
author: Boyuan Wu;ZEFENG WANG;Xianwei Lin;Jiachun Xu;Jikai Yu;Zhou Shicheng;Hongda Chen;Lianxin Hu

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix the headache of training AI on giant medical images that are all different sizes and won't fit into neat little boxes. They claim they built a high-speed digital funnel that shrinks the data and speeds up the process by 31 times without losing any important details.
why_bad: The 'First Ever' Delusion: The claim that they are the first to solve non-stackable data ignores a decade of researchers using padding and masking—it's like claiming you invented the sandwich because you used sourdough.
The Benchmarking Bias: Testing only on Camelyon16 and TCGA is the academic equivalent of bragging about a marathon time you set while running downhill with a tailwind.

The Complexity Tax: They smashed together gradient compression and distributed stacking into one messy 'mega-fix,' making it impossible to tell if the improvement comes from their genius math or just better hardware settings.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Extreme Kitchen Games! Our contender, DPGS, is facing a nightmare: 5,000 plates of completely different sizes, from tiny saucers to massive turkey platters. Normally, the dishwasher—our GPU—would throw a fit and shut down because you can't stack a birdbath on a teacup. DPGS steps up to the mic and screams, 'Watch this!' as they attempt to pulverize the plates into fine porcelain dust, shove them all in at once, and swear they can rebuild them perfectly on the other side in record time. The crowd is cheering at the 31x speed increase, but the refs are checking the replay—because if those 'rebuilt' plates have even one crack, this whole 'lossless' claim is going straight into the dumpster!

title: A Simple Model of Inference Scaling Laws
author_site: Noam Levi
abstract:
Neural scaling laws have garnered significant interest due to their ability to predict model performance as a function of increasing parameters, data, and compute. In this work, we propose a simple statistical ansatz based on memorization to study scaling laws in the context of inference. Specifically, how performance improves with multiple inference attempts. We explore the coverage, or pass@k metric, which measures the chance of success over repeated attempts and provide a motivation for the observed functional form of the inference scaling behavior of the coverage in large language models (LLMs) on reasoning tasks. 
We then define an "inference loss", which exhibits a power law decay as the number of trials increases, and connect this result with prompting costs. We further test the universality of our construction by conducting experiments on a simple generative model, and find that our predictions are in agreement with the empirical coverage curves in a controlled setting. Our simple framework sets the ground for incorporating inference scaling with other known scaling laws.
author: Noam Itzhak Levi
aff: EPFL - EPF Lausanne
aff_unique_norm: EPFL

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers discovered that if you let an AI guess the answer to a hard question a hundred times, it is more likely to get it right than if you only let it try once. They then spent several pages using complex math to justify why 'trial and error' is a valid scientific strategy for burning through your company's compute budget.
why_bad: The 'statistical ansatz' is essentially a fancy word for a guess, providing no real theoretical proof beyond observing that more attempts usually equal better results.
Validating the behavior of massive, trillion-parameter models using a 'simple generative model' is like trying to explain how a Boeing 747 flies by throwing a paper airplane across the room.
The 'inference loss' metric is a circular discovery: it mathematically 'proves' that running a model more times costs more money, which most people call 'common sense' or 'an invoice.'
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Today, our chef is trying to make a perfect pancake. Instead of learning the recipe, they decided to cook 1,000 pancakes simultaneously and hope one of them accidentally tastes good. They’ve named this 'Culinary Scaling Laws' and developed a complex formula to track 'Spatula Loss,' which is just a pretentious way of saying they’ve ruined $400 worth of batter. It’s not expertise; it’s just a very expensive way to prove that if you keep throwing eggs at a pan, eventually one won't be a disaster.

title: FRUGAL: Memory-Efficient Optimization by Reducing State Overhead for Scalable Training
author_site: Filipp Zmushko, Aleksandr Beznosikov, Martin Takac, Samuel Horváth
abstract:
With the increase in the number of parameters in large language models, the training process increasingly demands larger volumes of GPU memory. A significant portion of this memory is typically consumed by the optimizer state. To overcome this challenge, recent approaches such as low-rank adaptation (LoRA), low-rank gradient projection (GaLore), and blockwise optimization (BAdam) have been proposed. 
However, in all these algorithms, the *effective rank of the weight updates remains low-rank*, which can lead to a substantial loss of information from the gradient. This loss can be critically important, especially during the pre-training stage. In this paper, we introduce FRUGAL (**F**ull-**R**ank **U**pdates with **G**r**A**dient sp**L**itting), a new memory-efficient optimization framework. FRUGAL leverages gradient splitting to perform low-dimensional updates using advanced algorithms (such as Adam), while updates along the remaining directions are executed via state-free methods like SGD or signSGD. Our framework can be integrated with various low-rank update selection techniques, including GaLore and BAdam. We provide theoretical convergence guarantees for our framework when using SGDM for low-dimensional updates and SGD for state-free updates. Additionally, our method consistently outperforms concurrent approaches, achieving state-of-the-art results in pre-training and fine-tuning tasks while balancing memory efficiency and performance metrics.
author: Philip Zmushko;Aleksandr Beznosikov;Martin Takáč;Samuel Horváth
aff: Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology;;;MBZUAI
aff_unique_norm: Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology;;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

roast.laymen_summary: Training giant AI models usually requires massive amounts of expensive computer memory just to keep track of every tiny adjustment. This paper tries to save cash by giving VIP 'high-quality' updates to a small part of the model while telling the rest of the data to walk it off with basic, low-effort math.
why_bad: The theoretical convergence guarantees only apply to a simplified version of the algorithm, leaving a massive 'trust me, bro' gap for the actual implementation.
The computational overhead required to split and manage these two different update streams often negates the actual memory savings in real-world wall-clock time.
Experimental gains over existing baselines like GaLore are marginal at best, suggesting the added complexity is a solution in search of a problem.
roast.analogy_name: The 'Star Player & The Walk-Ons' Salary Cap Hack
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Gridiron of Gradient Descent! Today’s coach is trying to win a Super Bowl with a $50 budget. His strategy? He’s putting his entire training staff and the fancy recovery Gatorade on just the Quarterback—that's our 'low-dimensional Adam update.' For the rest of the 52 players on the roster? They’re told to just 'rub some dirt on it' and run in a straight line using 'state-free' SGD. The commentators in the booth—the reviewers—are skeptical. They're seeing the Quarterback get tired from carrying the whole team's logistics, and while it looks 'frugal' on the balance sheet, this roster is one blitz away from a total system collapse. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, let’s see if it pays off—spoiler alert: it didn't.

title: A General Framework for Inference-time Scaling and Steering of Diffusion Models
author_site: raghav singhal, Zachary Horvitz, Ryan Teehan, Mengye Ren, Zhou Yu, Kathleen McKeown, Rajesh Ranganath
abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable performance in generative modeling, but generating samples with specific desiderata remains challenging. Existing solutions --- such as fine-tuning,  best-of-n sampling, and gradient-based guidance --- are expensive, inefficient, or limited in applicability. In this work, we propose FK steering, a framework for inference-time steering diffusion models with reward functions. In this work, we introduce FK steering, which applies Feynman-Kac interacting particle systems to the inference-time steering of diffusion models with arbitrary reward functions. FK steering works by generating multiple trajectories, called particles, and resampling particles at intermediate steps based on scores computed using functions called potentials. Potentials are defined using rewards for intermediate states and are chosen such that a high score indicates the particle will yield a high-reward sample. We explore various choices of potentials, rewards, and samplers. Steering text-to-image models with a human preference reward, we find that FK steering outperforms fine-tuned models with just 2 particles. Moreover, FK steering a 0.8B parameter model outperforms a 2.6B model, achieving state-of-the-art performance on prompt fidelity. We also steer text diffusion models with rewards for text quality and rare attributes such as toxicity, and find that FK steering generates lower perplexity text and enables gradient-free control.  Overall, inference-time scaling and steering of diffusion models, even training-free, provides significant quality and controllability benefits. Code available [here](https://github.com/zacharyhorvitz/FK-Diffusion-Steering).
author: Raghav Singhal;Zachary Horvitz;Ryan Teehan;Mengye Ren;Zhou Yu;Kathleen McKeown;Rajesh Ranganath
