roast.laymen_summary: Instead of letting an AI finish a single crappy drawing, this method starts multiple versions simultaneously and kills off the ugly ones halfway through. By constantly doubling down on the 'winning' paths during generation, the AI produces better results without needing to be retrained.
why_bad: The computational overhead is a nightmare; running multiple 'particles' (parallel paths) means you're basically paying for five images just to get one good one.
The math—specifically applying Feynman-Kac formulas to diffusion—is a known trick in physics and statistics, making the 'novelty' feel more like a textbook port than a groundbreaking discovery.

The method relies heavily on 'potentials' (reward functions) that are notoriously finicky to tune, often leading to 'reward hacking' where the AI technically wins but the output looks like a psychedelic fever dream.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the chef is tasked with making a perfect soufflé. Instead of baking one and hoping for the best, our 'FK Steering' chef starts twelve identical pots at once. Every three minutes, Gordon Ramsay storms into the kitchen, stares at the pots, and literally throws the four saddest, flattest ones into the trash. He then forces the chef to divide the remaining 'good' pots to fill the empty spots and keep going. By the end, you have a Michelin-star soufflé, but you’ve wasted half a carton of eggs, quadrupled your gas bill, and the kitchen staff is on the verge of a collective breakdown. It’s 'Survival of the Fittest' for dessert, and while the results are delicious, nobody can afford the bill.

title: MindAligner: Explicit Brain Functional Alignment for Cross-Subject Visual Decoding from Limited fMRI Data
author_site: Yuqin Dai, Zhouheng Yao, Chunfeng Song, Qihao Zheng, Weijian Mai, Kunyu Peng, Shuai Lu, Wanli Ouyang, Jian Yang, Jiamin Wu
abstract: Brain decoding aims to reconstruct visual perception of human subject from fMRI signals, which is crucial for understanding brain's perception mechanisms.  Existing methods are confined to the single-subject paradigm due to substantial brain variability, which leads to weak generalization across individuals and incurs high training costs, exacerbated by limited availability of fMRI data. To address these challenges, we propose MindAligner, an explicit functional alignment framework for cross-subject brain decoding from limited fMRI data. The proposed MindAligner enjoys several merits. First, we learn a Brain Transfer Matrix (BTM) that projects the brain signals of an arbitrary new subject to one of the known subjects, enabling seamless use of pre-trained decoding models. Second, to facilitate reliable BTM learning, a Brain Functional Alignment module is proposed to perform soft cross-subject brain alignment under different visual stimuli with a multi-level brain alignment loss, uncovering fine-grained functional correspondences with high interpretability. Experiments indicate that MindAligner not only outperforms existing methods in visual decoding under data-limited conditions, but also provides valuable neuroscience insights in cross-subject functional analysis. The code will be made publicly available.
author: Yuqin Dai;Zhouheng Yao;Chunfeng Song;Qihao Zheng;Weijian Mai;Kunyu Peng;Shuai Lu;Wanli Ouyang;Jian Yang;Jiamin Wu

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a universal 'brain-to-image' translator that works on everyone without needing hours of expensive individual training. They essentially attempted to create a digital filter that squishes a new person's unique brain activity into the shape of a person the AI already knows.
why_bad: The 'Brain Transfer Matrix' assumes complex human brains can be mapped to one another using what is likely oversimplified linear math, ignoring massive individual biological differences.
Claiming victory with 'limited data' in the fMRI world is a massive red flag for overfitting, where the model just memorizes the noise in the small sample rather than learning actual patterns.
The 'alignment' process is a decade-old concept (Hyperalignment) rebranded with a flashy name, offering marginal technical novelty and questionable 'neuroscience insights'.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Forged Fingerprints
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where the prosecution has a clear fingerprint from a crime scene (Subject A). They catch a new suspect (Subject B), but his fingerprints don't match. The defense 'expert,' Dr. MindAligner, stands up and proposes that we don't need a new investigation. Instead, he presents a 'Transfer Matrix'—a specialized pair of glasses that digitally warps Subject B’s fingerprints until they look exactly like Subject A’s. 'Your Honor,' he argues, 'if we just stretch the thumb and smudge the loops, they’re basically the same guy!' The Judge (the Reviewer) is unimpressed, noting that just because you can distort one person’s data to look like another’s doesn't mean you’ve discovered a universal truth; it just means you’re really good at digital forgery with a sample size of one.

title: Alpha-SQL: Zero-Shot Text-to-SQL using Monte Carlo Tree Search
author_site: Boyan Li, Jiayi Zhang, Ju Fan, Yanwei XU, Chong Chen, Nan Tang, Yuyu Luo
abstract:
Text-to-SQL, which enables natural language interaction with databases, serves as a pivotal method across diverse industries.
With new, more powerful large language models (LLMs) emerging every few months, fine-tuning has become incredibly costly, labor-intensive, and error-prone. As an alternative, *zero-shot* Text-to-SQL, which leverages the growing knowledge and reasoning capabilities encoded in LLMs without task-specific fine-tuning, presents a promising and more challenging direction.
To address this challenge, we propose Alpha-SQL, a novel approach that leverages a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework to iteratively infer SQL construction actions based on partial reasoning states. To enhance the framework’s reasoning capabilities, we introduce *LLM-as-Action-Model* to dynamically generate SQL construction *actions* during the MCTS process, steering the search toward more promising SQL queries. Moreover, Alpha-SQL employs a self-supervised reward function to evaluate the quality of candidate SQL queries, ensuring more accurate and efficient query generation. Experimental results show that Alpha-SQL achieves 69.7% execution accuracy on the BIRD development set, using a 32B open-source LLM without fine-tuning. Alpha-SQL outperforms the best previous zero-shot approach based on GPT-4o by 2.5% on the BIRD development set.
author: Boyan Li;Jiayi Zhang;Ju Fan;Yanwei Xu;Chong Chen;Nan Tang;Yuyu Luo
aff: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;Renmin University of China;Huawei Technologies Ltd.;Huawei Technologies Ltd.;HKUST(GZ);The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)+Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to turn an AI into a high-speed chess player that 'thinks' through thousands of possible code paths just to write a single database query. While it technically works better than just guessing, it's essentially using a supercomputer's worth of brainpower to do something a seasoned developer does in three seconds over coffee.
why_bad: The 'Inference Tax' is astronomical: Using Monte Carlo Tree Search makes every query incredibly slow and expensive, meaning you'll be waiting for your data until the heat death of the universe.
The reward function is basically the AI grading its own homework; if the model is already confused about the SQL, its 'self-supervised' evaluation is just a more confident way of being wrong.
The complexity-to-performance ratio is terrible—adding a massive search framework just to beat GPT-4o by a measly 2.5% is the definition of over-engineering.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Billion-Dollar Objection
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a defense attorney who, instead of simply answering the judge’s question, demands a three-day recess to simulate 10,000 parallel realities where he says different versions of 'I object' to see which one makes the jury blink. The prosecution has already moved on, the judge is asleep, and the court reporter has run out of ink. Sure, he eventually finds the 'perfect' phrasing that wins the point by a hair, but he’s billed the client $4 million for a misdemeanor traffic ticket. You’ve technically won the case, but you’ve gone bankrupt and the courthouse is closing for the night.

title: MME-CoT: Benchmarking Chain-of-Thought in Large Multimodal Models for Reasoning Quality, Robustness, and Efficiency
author_site: Dongzhi Jiang, Renrui Zhang, Ziyu Guo, Yanwei Li, Yu Qi, Xinyan Chen, Liuhui Wang, Jianhan Jin, Claire Guo, Shen Yan, Bo Zhang, Chaoyou Fu, Peng Gao, Hongsheng Li
abstract:
Answering questions with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has significantly enhanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its impact on Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) still lacks a systematic assessment and in-depth investigation. In this paper, we introduce **MME-CoT**, a specialized benchmark evaluating the CoT reasoning performance of LMMs, spanning six domains: math, science, OCR, logic, space-time, and general scenes. 
As the first comprehensive study in this area, we propose a thorough evaluation suite incorporating three novel metrics that assess the reasoning quality, robustness, and efficiency at a fine-grained level.
Leveraging curated high-quality data and a unique evaluation strategy, we conduct an in-depth analysis of state-of-the-art LMMs, uncovering several key insights: *1)* Models with reflection mechanism demonstrate a superior CoT quality, with Kimi k1.5 outperforming GPT-4o and demonstrating the highest quality results; *2)* CoT prompting often degrades LMM performance on perception-heavy tasks, suggesting a potentially harmful overthinking behavior; and *3)* Although the CoT quality is high, LMMs with reflection exhibit significant inefficiency in both normal response and self-correction phases.
We hope MME-CoT serves as a foundation for advancing multimodal reasoning in LMMs.
author: Dongzhi Jiang;Renrui Zhang;Ziyu Guo;Yanwei Li;Yu Qi;Xinyan Chen;Liuhui Wang;Jianhan Jin;Claire Guo;Shen Yan;Bo Zhang;Chaoyou Fu;Peng Gao;Hongsheng Li

roast.laymen_summary: We built a specialized test to see if AI models get smarter when they 'think out loud' about images, but it turns out they're basically over-caffeinated scholars. While they're great at complex logic, they often get so lost in their own thoughts that they fail to see what's right in front of their eyes.
why_bad: The 'novel' metrics are essentially just rebranded versions of existing accuracy and latency stats with a fresh coat of paint.
Claiming that thinking out loud hurts simple perception is like discovering water is wet; the 'CoT tax' on basic tasks is already well-documented in AI circles.
The 'reflection' mechanism is treated as a black box, making the comparison between models like Kimi and GPT-4o feel more like a fanboy debate than a rigorous scientific analysis.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a quarterback who, instead of just throwing the ball to a wide-open receiver, stops mid-play to recite the trajectory physics and his internal emotional state. By the time he's finished 'reflecting' on the optimal spiral, he's been sacked three times, the stadium lights are off, and he somehow managed to convince himself the receiver was a hallucination. He’s technically a genius, but you really just needed him to see the giant red jersey standing five feet away.

title: Oscillations Make Neural Networks Robust to Quantization
abstract: We challenge the prevailing view that oscillations in Quantization Aware Training (QAT) are merely undesirable artifacts caused by the Straight-Through Estimator (STE). Through theoretical analysis of QAT in linear models, we demonstrate that the gradient of the loss function can be decomposed into two terms: the original full-precision loss and a term that causes quantization oscillations. Based on these insights, we propose a novel regularization method that induces oscillations to improve quantization robustness. Contrary to traditional methods that focuses on minimizing the effects of oscillations, our approach leverages the beneficial aspects of weight oscillations to preserve model performance under quantization. Our empirical results on ResNet-18 and Tiny ViT demonstrate that this counter-intuitive strategy matches QAT accuracy at $\geq$ 3-bit weight quantization, while maintaining close to full precision accuracy at bits greater than the target bit. Our work therefore provides a new perspective on model preparation for quantization, particularly for finding weights that are robust to changes in the bit of the quantizer -- an area where current methods struggle to match the accuracy of QAT at specific bits.
author: Jonathan Wenshøj;Bob Pepin;Raghavendra Selvan
aff: Copenhagen University;Copenhagen University;University of Copenhagen
aff_unique_norm: University of Copenhagen

roast.laymen_summary: Most researchers try to stop AI weights from jittering during training, but these authors argue that making the model 'shake' on purpose makes it tougher. They claim this intentional instability helps the AI keep its brain power even when you aggressively shrink its file size.
why_bad: The 'theoretical proof' is based on linear models, which is like trying to explain how a jet engine works by studying a paper airplane.
Matching the accuracy of existing methods isn't a breakthrough; it's just a more eccentric way to arrive at the same destination everyone else is already at.
The strategy completely loses its 'magic' at low bit-widths, making it a fancy solution for a problem that researchers have already solved more simply.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Vibrating Witness
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom where the defendant (the Neural Network) is being cross-examined by a ruthless prosecutor (Quantization). Usually, a defense attorney wants their witness to stand perfectly still and give firm answers. However, these researchers—acting as the most 'creative' legal team in history—argue that if the witness spends the entire trial vibrating uncontrollably and switching their story back and forth, they’ll be 'robust' against the prosecutor's trick questions. The Judge (the Reviewer) isn't impressed. While the witness didn't technically perjure themselves more than usual, the courtroom is now just filled with a very confused, shaking person, and the legal precedent (the theory) only applies if the crime was 'stealing a single grape' (a linear model). Case dismissed for being unnecessarily dramatic without changing the verdict.

title: FACTER: Fairness-Aware Conformal Thresholding and Prompt Engineering for Enabling Fair LLM-Based Recommender Systems
author_site: Arya Fayyazi, Mehdi Kamal, Massoud Pedram
abstract: We propose FACTER, a fairness-aware framework for LLM-based recommendation systems that integrates conformal prediction with dynamic prompt engineering. By introducing an adaptive semantic variance threshold and a violation-triggered mechanism, FACTER automatically tightens fairness constraints whenever biased patterns emerge. We further develop an adversarial prompt generator that leverages historical violations to reduce repeated demographic biases without retraining the LLM. Empirical results on MovieLens and Amazon show that FACTER substantially reduces fairness violations (up to 95.5%)  while maintaining strong recommendation accuracy, revealing semantic variance as a potent proxy of bias.
author: Arya Fayyazi;Mehdi Kamal;Massoud Pedram
aff: University of Southern California;University of Southern California;University of Southern California
aff_unique_norm: University of Southern California

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to fix biased AI recommendations by building a 'hall monitor' that checks the AI's math and yells at it to change its tone whenever it starts stereotyping. It’s basically a high-tech way of slapping the AI’s wrist and forcing it to rewrite its homework until it stops being problematic.
why_bad: The 'semantic variance' metric is a giant leap of faith; just because an AI is being inconsistent doesn't mean it's being biased, making the whole foundation a bit shaky.
It’s an over-engineered 'wrapper'—adding layers of adversarial prompts and math filters makes the system slower than a DMV line without actually fixing the underlying model.
The 95.5% bias reduction looks great on paper, but the researchers basically 'taught to the test' by using specific benchmarks that favor their exact type of prompt tweaking.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'The Equitable Kitchen,' where our Head Chef (the LLM) has a nasty habit of only serving salads to women and steaks to men. Instead of teaching the chef to be better, FACTER is like hiring an expensive Sous-Chef to stand behind him with a whistle and a stack of 'Instruction Cards.' Every time the Head Chef reaches for a gendered garnish, the Sous-Chef blows the whistle, slaps his hand, and forces him to read a new, increasingly complex recipe card until the plate looks 'fair.' Sure, the customer eventually gets a balanced meal, but by the time it leaves the kitchen, the food is cold, the Sous-Chef is exhausted, and you’ve spent $500 on 'dynamic prompt' napkins just to get a side of fries.

title: STNet: Spectral Transformation Network for Solving Operator Eigenvalue Problem
abstract:
Operator eigenvalue problems play a critical role in various scientific fields and engineering applications, yet numerical methods are hindered by the curse of dimensionality. Recent deep learning methods provide an efficient approach to address this challenge by iterative updating neural networks. 
These methods' performance relies heavily on the spectral distribution of the given operator: larger gaps between the operator's eigenvalues will improve precision, thus tailored spectral transformations that leverage the spectral distribution can enhance their performance. Based on this observation, we propose the **S**pectral **T**ransformation **Net**work (**STNet**).
During each iteration, STNet uses approximate eigenvalues and eigenfunctions to perform spectral transformations on the original operator, turning it into an equivalent but easier problem.
Specifically, we employ deflation projection to exclude the subspace corresponding to already solved eigenfunctions, thereby reducing the search space and avoiding converging to existing eigenfunctions. 
Additionally, our filter transform magnifies eigenvalues in the desired region and suppresses those outside, further improving performance.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that STNet consistently outperforms existing learning-based methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance in accuracy.
keywords: AI for Science;Operator Eigenvalue Problem;Scientific Computing
author: Hong Wang;Jiang Yixuan;Jie Wang;Xinyi Li;Jian Luo;huanshuo dong

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to use AI to solve complex physics equations by essentially 'zooming in' on the answers they wanted and blocking out the ones they already found. It’s a high-tech way of clearing the clutter to make math easier, but it turns out they mostly just gave old-school calculus a fancy, expensive facelift.
why_bad: Old Wine in a New Bottle: The 'innovative' techniques like deflation and spectral filtering have been standard numerical linear algebra since the Cold War, and putting a neural network on top doesn't make them new.
The Computational Tax: The paper claims efficiency, but performing complex transformations at every single iteration likely makes the 'shortcut' slower than just taking the long way around.

Lack of Theoretical Backbone: While it performs well on their specific homework problems, there’s no proof that the neural network won't hallucinate wrong answers when faced with a real-world physics crisis.
roast.analogy_name: Where’s Waldo: The $100 Million Magnifying Glass
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where finding a guy in a striped shirt is statistically improbable. [BWAAAAAAM] One team of scientists decides to stop looking for Waldo and start vaporizing everyone who *isn't* Waldo. Witness the high-octane thrill of 'STNet,' where we use a supercomputer to digitally erase every person we've already checked, then apply a high-contrast filter to make Waldo’s red stripes glow like a supernova. It’s a cinematic masterpiece of over-engineering—coming this summer to a journal that actually checks if a magnet would have been faster than a laser-guided AI drone.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered.

title: The Harder Path: Last Iterate Convergence for Uncoupled Learning in Zero-Sum Games with Bandit Feedback
author_site: Côme Fiegel, Pierre Menard, Tadashi Kozuno, Michal Valko, Vianney Perchet
abstract:
We study the problem of learning in zero-sum matrix games with repeated play and bandit feedback. 
Specifically, we focus on developing uncoupled algorithms that guarantee, without communication between players, convergence of the last-iterate to a Nash equilibrium. Although the non-bandit case has been studied extensively, this setting has only been explored recently, with a bound of $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1/8})$ on the exploitability gap. We show that, for uncoupled algorithms, guaranteeing convergence of the policy profiles to a Nash equilibrium is detrimental to the performances, with the best attainable rate being $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1/4})$ in contrast to the usual $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1/2})$ rate for convergence of the average iterates. We then propose two algorithms that achieve this optimal rate. The first algorithm leverages a straightforward tradeoff between exploration and exploitation, while the second employs a regularization technique based on a two-step mirror descent approach.
author: Côme Fiegel;Pierre Menard;Tadashi Kozuno;Michal Valko;Vianney Perchet

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers discovered that forcing two competing AIs to settle on a perfect final strategy without talking to each other makes them learn four times slower than if they just averaged their past guesses. It turns out that being 'perfect at the end' is mathematically much more painful than just being 'good on average' when you're playing in the dark.
why_bad: The 'optimal' rate of T^-1/4 is essentially the mathematical equivalent of watching paint dry, making the algorithm practically useless for real-time systems.
The trade-off between exploration and exploitation is presented as a 'straightforward' solution, which reviewers likely found more simplistic than groundbreaking.
Last-iterate convergence in bandit settings is a niche theoretical obsession that solves a problem most practitioners would bypass by just using more data or basic communication.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the kitchen, where you and your rival are tasked with making a perfectly balanced fusion dish—except there's a soundproof wall between you and you’re both blindfolded. You can't talk, and the judges only let you taste your own seasoning once every hour. This paper demands that your very last bite be Michelin-star quality, rather than just hoping the average of your efforts is edible. To achieve this, the authors suggest you cook so slowly that you're basically moving in stop-motion. It’s technically a 'recipe for success,' but by the time the meal is ready, the guests have died of old age and the restaurant has been reclaimed by nature.

title: Bring Reason to Vision: Understanding Perception and Reasoning through Model Merging
author_site: Shiqi Chen, Jinghan Zhang, Tongyao Zhu, Wei Liu, Siyang Gao, Miao Xiong, Manling Li, Junxian He
abstract:
Vision-Language Models (VLMs) combine visual perception with the general capabilities, such as reasoning, of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, the mechanisms by which these two abilities can be combined and contribute remain poorly understood.
In this work, we explore to compose perception and reasoning through model merging that connects parameters of different models.  
Unlike previous works that often focus on merging models of the same kind, we propose merging models **across modalities**, enabling the incorporation of the reasoning capabilities of LLMs into VLMs. 
Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that model merging offers a successful pathway to transfer reasoning abilities from LLMs to VLMs in a **training-free** manner.
Moreover, we utilize the merged models to understand the internal mechanism of perception and reasoning and how merging affects it. We find that perception capabilities are predominantly encoded in the early layers of the model, whereas reasoning is largely facilitated by the middle-to-late layers. After merging, we observe that all layers begin to contribute to reasoning, whereas the distribution of perception abilities across layers remains largely unchanged. These observations shed light on the potential of model merging as a tool for multimodal integration and interpretation.
author: Shiqi Chen;Jinghan Zhang;Tongyao Zhu;Wei Liu;Siyang Gao;Miao Xiong;Manling Li;Junxian He

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to give an AI 'common sense' by literally smashing the brains of a text-only model and an image-viewing model together like two play-dough balls. They discovered that while the front of the brain sees things and the back thinks about them, merging them just makes the whole thing a confused, blurry mess.
why_bad: The 'training-free' approach is basically a fancy way of saying they did a 'copy-paste' job with the math and hoped for a miracle instead of actually teaching the model how to work.
Their big 'discovery' that vision happens in early layers and reasoning happens in late layers is the AI equivalent of discovering that you chew with your teeth and not your toes.

Merging models with different architectures or training backgrounds without fine-tuning usually leads to 'catastrophic interference,' which is academic-speak for the AI turning into a digital vegetable.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Chopped: Neural Network Edition!' Our contestant today tried to make a 'Reasoning Ratatouille.' Instead of carefully layering the vegetables and simmering them to perfection, they took a high-end French cookbook and a pair of binoculars, threw them both into an industrial blender, and served the resulting gray sludge to the judges. They're arguing that because the binoculars are now physically touching the paper, the book can now 'see' the flavor profile. The judges are currently in the bathroom with food poisoning because, as it turns out, just because two things are in the same bowl doesn't mean they've actually met. It’s not 'fusion cuisine'; it’s a kitchen fire with a PhD.

title: EmoGrowth: Incremental Multi-label Emotion Decoding with Augmented Emotional Relation Graph
author_site: Kaicheng Fu, Changde Du, Jie Peng, Kunpeng Wang, Shuangchen Zhao, Xiaoyu Chen, Huiguang He
abstract: Emotion recognition systems face significant challenges in real-world applications, where novel emotion categories continually emerge and multiple emotions often co-occur. This paper introduces multi-label fine-grained class incremental emotion decoding, which aims to develop models capable of incrementally learning new emotion categories while maintaining the ability to recognize multiple concurrent emotions. We propose an Augmented Emotional Semantics Learning (AESL) framework to address two critical challenges: past- and future-missing partial label problems. AESL incorporates an augmented Emotional Relation Graph (ERG) for reliable soft label generation and affective dimension-based knowledge distillation for future-aware feature learning. We evaluate our approach on three datasets spanning brain activity and multimedia domains, demonstrating its effectiveness in decoding up to 28 fine-grained emotion categories. Results show that AESL significantly outperforms existing methods while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Our code is available at https://github.com/ChangdeDu/EmoGrowth.
keywords: Emotion decoding， Class incremental learning， Multi-label learning
author: Kaicheng Fu;Changde Du;Jie Peng;Kunpeng Wang;Shuangchen Zhao;Xiaoyu Chen;Huiguang He

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build an AI that can learn new feelings—like 'existential dread' or 'hangry'—without immediately forgetting what 'happy' looks like. They used a complex digital map of human moods to help the computer juggle 28 different emotions simultaneously without its brain melting.
why_bad: The 'Emotional Relation Graph' is essentially a glorified spiderweb of assumptions that adds massive complexity without proving it’s actually better than simpler math.
Claiming to work on both brain scans and YouTube clips is a massive stretch; it's like saying your key works for both a padlock and a theoretical physics equation.
The 'Future-Missing' label problem is a self-inflicted wound—the model spends more time guessing what it hasn't seen yet than actually mastering what’s right in front of it.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Sentiment Super Bowl, where Team EmoGrowth is attempting the 'Triple-Flip-Memory-Save!' They’re trying to stack 28 blocks of human complex on a base that’s already vibrating. Every time the coach throws a new emotion like 'Melancholy' onto the pile, the model panics and tries to duct-tape it to 'Sadness' using a 'Relation Graph.' But look at the replay! By trying to remember everything at once, the model has the emotional intelligence of a Roomba in a glitter factory. It’s a bold strategy to use 'Knowledge Distillation' as a stabilizer, but the tower is leaning harder than a tech lead after a 14-hour sprint. They aren't preventing catastrophic forgetting; they're just orchestrating a very expensive, very slow-motion crash!

title: A Recipe for Causal Graph Regression: Confounding Effects Revisited
author_site: Yujia Yin, Tianyi Qu, Zihao Wang, Yifan Chen
abstract: Through recognizing causal subgraphs, causal graph learning (CGL) has risen to be a promising approach for improving the generalizability of graph neural networks under out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. However, the empirical successes of CGL techniques are mostly exemplified in classification settings, while regression tasks, a more challenging setting in graph learning, are overlooked. We thus devote this work to tackling causal graph regression (CGR); to this end we reshape the processing of confounding effects in existing CGL studies, which mainly deal with classification. Specifically, we reflect on the predictive power of confounders in graph-level regression, and generalize classification-specific causal intervention techniques to regression through a lens of contrastive learning. Extensive experiments on graph OOD benchmarks validate the efficacy of our proposals for CGR. The model implementation and the code are provided on https://github.com/causal-graph/CGR.
author: Yujia Yin;Tianyi Qu;Zihao Wang;Yifan Chen
aff: Hong Kong Baptist University;Zhejiang University;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;
aff_unique_norm: Hong Kong Baptist University;Zhejiang University;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI gets distracted by random coincidences when trying to predict numbers, so these researchers tried to build a 'BS-detector' to help the AI focus on what actually matters. They essentially took a trick used for sorting photos and tried to apply it to predicting complex values, hoping it would make the AI less confused when things change.
why_bad: The authors basically tried to 'copy-paste' math meant for discrete labels into a world of continuous numbers without accounting for the fact that regression errors are far less forgiving.
The so-called 'causal intervention' is just a glorified contrastive loss function wearing a tuxedo; it doesn't actually prove cause-and-effect, it just clusters similar-looking data points.
The 'Out-of-Distribution' benchmarks used are notoriously easy to game, making the 'breakthrough' look more like a lucky coin flip than a robust engineering feat.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a toaster that doesn't just brown bread—it 'causally analyzes the molecular structure of the wheat' to ensure the perfect crunch. In reality, it’s just a regular toaster with a very expensive 'Causal™' sticker on the side. They’re claiming that because this toaster worked on sourdough (classification), it can now perfectly predict the exact temperature of a volcanic eruption (regression). It sounds revolutionary in the slide deck, but at the end of the day, you're still just getting burnt toast and a lot of fancy vocabulary about why the burnt parts don't 'statistically correlate' with the heat settings.

title: Large Language Models are Demonstration Pre-Selectors for Themselves
author_site: Jiarui Jin, Yuwei Wu, Haoxuan Li, Xiaoting He, Weinan Zhang, Yiming Yang, Yong Yu, Jun Wang, Mengyue Yang
abstract: In-context learning with large language models (LLMs) delivers strong few-shot performance by choosing few-shot demonstrations from the entire training dataset. However, previous few-shot in-context learning methods, which calculate similarity scores for choosing demonstrations, incur high computational costs by repeatedly retrieving large-scale datasets for each query. This is due to their failure to recognize that not all demonstrations are equally informative, and many less informative demonstrations can be inferred from a core set of highly informative ones. To this end, we propose FEEDER (FEw yet Essential Demonstration prE-selectoR), a novel \emph{pre-selection} framework that identifies a core subset of demonstrations containing the most informative examples. This subset, referred to as the FEEDER set, consists of demonstrations that capture both the ''sufficiency'' and ''necessity'' information to infer the entire dataset. Notice that FEEDER is selected before the few-shot in-context learning, enabling more efficient few-shot demonstrations choosing in a smaller set. To identify FEEDER, we propose a novel effective tree based algorithm. Once selected, it can replace the original dataset, leading to improved efficiency and prediction accuracy in few-shot in-context learning. Additionally, FEEDER also benefit fine-tuning LLMs, we propose a bi-level optimization method enabling more efficient training without sacrificing performance when datasets become smaller. Our experiments are on 6 text classification datasets, 1 reasoning dataset, and 1 semantic-parsing dataset, across 6 LLMs (ranging from 335M to 7B parameters), demonstrate that: (i) In few-shot inference, FEEDER achieves superior (or comparable) performance while utilizing only half the input training data. (ii) In fine-tuning, FEEDER significantly boosts the performance of LLMs.
author: Jiarui Jin;Yuwei Wu;Haoxuan Li;Xiaoting He;Weinan Zhang;Yiming Yang;Yong Yu;Jun Wang;Mengyue Yang

roast.laymen_summary: This paper argues that LLMs don't need to read the whole textbook to learn a task; they just need a curated 'cheat sheet' of the most essential examples. The researchers built a filter called FEEDER to pick these 'greatest hits' so the AI doesn't waste time digging through redundant data every time you ask it a question.
why_bad: The 'efficiency' argument is a bit of a shell game; the computational cost of building their complex 'tree-based' selection system might actually outweigh the time saved during inference.

Testing on 7B parameter models is like claiming you've revolutionized engine design while only testing on a 1998 Honda Civic—it doesn't prove it works for the Ferraris (GPT-4/Llama 3) we actually care about.
The 'novel' sufficiency-necessity framework is mostly just a spicy rebranding of 'coreset selection,' a field that has been thoroughly picked over by researchers for years.
roast.analogy_description: Folks, we’re looking at a coach who wants to win the Super Bowl by only bringing three players to the stadium! Team FEEDER is claiming they’ve identified the 'Essential Core,' but they’re basically just cutting the roster to save on the Gatorade budget. They’re running a complex tree-based play-call just to tell us that the star quarterback is more important than the guy who holds the clipboard. It's a bold strategy, Cotton, but when the opposing team is fielding 175-billion-parameter giants and you're still playing JV ball with a 335M-parameter middle-schooler, a 'leaner' playbook isn't going to save you from getting absolutely pancaked on the line of scrimmage!

title: Open Your Eyes: Vision Enhances Message Passing Neural Networks in Link Prediction
author_site: Yanbin Wei, Xuehao Wang, Zhan Zhuang, Yang Chen, Shuhao Chen, Yulong Zhang, James Kwok, Yu Zhang
abstract: Message-passing graph neural networks (MPNNs) and structural features (SFs) are cornerstones for the link prediction task. However, as a common and intuitive mode of understanding, the potential of visual perception has been overlooked in the MPNN community. For the first time, we equip MPNNs with vision structural awareness by proposing an effective framework called Graph Vision Network (GVN), along with a more efficient variant (E-GVN). Extensive empirical results demonstrate that with the proposed frameworks, GVN consistently benefits from the vision enhancement across seven link prediction datasets, including challenging large-scale graphs. Such improvements are compatible with existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods and GVNs achieve new SOTA results, thereby underscoring a promising novel direction for link prediction.
author: Yanbin Wei;Xuehao Wang;Zhan Zhuang;Yang Chen;Shuhao Chen;Yulong Zhang;James Kwok;Yu Zhang

title: HYGMA: Hypergraph Coordination Networks with Dynamic Grouping for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
author_site: Chiqiang Liu, Dazi Li
abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning faces significant challenges in effectively organizing agent relationships and facilitating information exchange, particularly when agents need to adapt their coordination patterns dynamically. This paper presents a novel framework that integrates dynamic spectral clustering with hypergraph neural networks to enable adaptive group formation and efficient information processing in multi-agent systems. The proposed framework dynamically constructs and updates hypergraph structures through spectral clustering on agents' state histories, enabling higher-order relationships to emerge naturally from agent interactions. The hypergraph structure is enhanced with attention mechanisms for selective information processing, providing an expressive and efficient way to model complex agent relationships. This architecture can be implemented in both value-based and policy-based paradigms through a unified objective combining task performance with structural regularization. Extensive experiments on challenging cooperative tasks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both sample efficiency and final performance. The code is available at: https://github.com/mysteryelder/HYGMA.
keywords: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning;Hypergraph Convolution;Dynamic Grouping;Multi-Agent Cooperation
author: Chiqiang Liu;Dazi Li

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built a system to help robot teams cooperate by grouping them into temporary 'cliques' using complex math normally reserved for high-end data sorting. They used a fancy 3D graph structure to let these bots talk to each other, claiming it makes them smarter and faster at finishing group tasks.
why_bad: The spectral clustering math is a computational boat anchor that makes the training process painfully slow for negligible gains.
The paper fails to prove that the 'hypergraph' actually does anything that a standard, much simpler attention mechanism couldn't do.
Experimental results were cherry-picked from environments where the baseline models weren't properly tuned, making the 'superiority' look like a statistical fluke.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Spectral Subpoena
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a high-stakes murder trial where the Lead Defense Attorney refuses to simply speak to the jury. Instead, every time a piece of evidence is presented, the attorney pauses the trial to perform a statistical analysis of the jurors' heart rates and seating positions to decide who is 'vibing' together. He then constructs a complex web of silk threads connecting 'synergistic' jurors into higher-order groups before whispering the evidence to them through a customized megaphone. By the time the attorney has finished calculating his 'dynamic grouping,' the defendant has died of old age, the judge has retired, and the prosecution has won by default because the defense spent $4 million on string and graph theory instead of actually making a coherent argument.

title: Bayesian Parameter Shift Rules in Variational Quantum Eigensolvers
abstract:
Parameter shift rules (PSRs) are key techniques for efficient gradient estimation in variational quantum eigensolvers (VQEs).  In this paper, we propose its Bayesian variant, where Gaussian processes with appropriate kernels are used to estimate the gradient of the VQE objective.  Our Bayesian PSR offers flexible gradient estimation from observations at arbitrary locations with uncertainty information, and reduces to the generalized PSR in special cases.  In stochastic gradient descent (SGD), the flexibility of Bayesian PSR allows reuse of observations in previous steps, which accelerates the optimization process.  Furthermore, the accessibility to the posterior uncertainty,
along with our proposed notion of gradient confident region (GradCoRe), enables us to minimize the observation costs in each SGD step. 
 Our numerical experiments show that the VQE optimization with Bayesian PSR and GradCoRe significantly accelerates SGD, and outperforms the state-of-the-art methods, including sequential minimal optimization.
keywords: parameter shift rule;variational quantum eigensolver;quantum computing;confidence region;Gaussian process
author: Samuele Pedrielli;Christopher J. Anders;Lena Funcke;Karl Jansen;Kim Andrea Nicoli;Shinichi Nakajima
aff: Technische Universität Berlin;RIKEN;Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms Universität Bonn;DESY;Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms Universität Bonn;BIFOLD, TU Berlin

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to speed up quantum computers by using a fancy 'guess-and-check' math trick that remembers previous attempts instead of starting from scratch every time. It’s essentially a high-tech way of saying, 'I think the answer is over there because of what I saw five minutes ago, and I'm 70% sure about it.'
why_bad: The classical 'brain' required to run these Gaussian Processes is so heavy it actually slows down the whole system more than the quantum speedup saves it.
The 'GradCoRe' method is essentially a fancy name for 'ignore the data when we're confused,' which leads to the optimization getting stuck in local ruts.
The simulation results are suspiciously pristine, ignoring the fact that real-world quantum hardware is noisier than a construction site during a thunderstorm.
roast.analogy_name: The Lazy Archer's Statistical Wind Chart
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Quantum Archery Invitational! Most archers here are doing the 'Parameter Shift'—shooting one arrow left and one arrow right just to calibrate their aim. It's slow, folks, but it works. But look at our contestant today! Instead of shooting, they've brought out a massive supercomputer and a stack of old weather reports. They're telling the crowd, 'Based on the gust of wind I felt in the parking lot and a photo of a flag from 1998, I am 85% confident the target is slightly to the left!' It sounds brilliant, Jim, but while they're crunching the numbers and calculating their 'confidence regions,' the sun is setting and the other archers have already finished the round. They’re trying to think the arrow into the bullseye while the rest of the world is actually shooting!
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels stabilized.

title: Learning Imperfect Information Extensive-form Games with Last-iterate Convergence under Bandit Feedback
author_site: Canzhe Zhao, Yutian Cheng, Jing Dong, Baoxiang Wang, Shuai Li
abstract: We investigate learning approximate Nash equilibrium (NE) policy profiles in two-player zero-sum imperfect information extensive-form games (IIEFGs) with last-iterate convergence guarantees. Existing algorithms either rely on full-information feedback or provide only asymptotic convergence rates. In contrast, we focus on the bandit feedback setting, where players receive feedback solely from the rewards associated with the experienced information set and action pairs in each episode. Our proposed algorithm employs a negentropy regularizer weighted by a "virtual transition" over the information set-action space to facilitate an efficient approximate policy update. Through a carefully designed virtual transition and leveraging the entropy regularization technique, we demonstrate finite-time last-iterate convergence to the NE with a rate of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(k^{-\frac{1}{8}})$ under bandit feedback in each episode $k$. Empirical evaluations across various IIEFG instances show its competitive performance compared to baseline methods.
keywords: Extensive-form games; partially observable Markov games (POMGs); last-iterate convergence
author: Canzhe Zhao;Yutian Cheng;Jing Dong;Baoxiang Wang;Shuai Li
aff: Shanghai Jiaotong University;Shanghai Jiaotong University;The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen;;John Hopcroft Center, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build an AI that learns to win at bluffing games like Poker even when it can only see its own moves and the final score. They claim their method ensures the AI's very last strategy is the best one, rather than just averaging its past performance like older models.
why_bad: The convergence rate of $k^{-1/8}$ is agonizingly slow; you’d essentially need to wait until the heat death of the universe for the AI to actually play a perfect game.
The 'virtual transition' trick is a mathematical band-aid that looks more like an ad-hoc hack than a robust theoretical breakthrough.
The empirical evaluations were likely conducted on 'toy games' so small they make Tic-Tac-Toe look like 4D chess, leaving zero proof this works in the real world.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Eternal Poker Face
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents a man who claims to be a Poker Grandmaster. His strategy? He plays blindfolded, only knows if he won or lost a hand, and refuses to learn from his mistakes until the very last second. He calls this 'innovation.' I call it a scam! He promises that eventually, he will be unbeatable, but his own math proves he won't reach that level of skill until long after this courtroom has crumbled to dust. Your Honor, the defendant isn't offering a breakthrough; he's offering a 'virtual transition'—a fancy legal term for 'I made it up because the real math was too hard.' I move to strike this paper from the record for wasting the court's time with a strategy that is theoretically 'convergent' but practically useless!

title: On the Convergence of Continuous Single-timescale Actor-critic
author_site: Xuyang Chen, Lin Zhao
abstract:
Actor-critic algorithms have been instrumental in boosting the performance of numerous challenging applications involving continuous control, such as highly robust and agile robot motion control. However, their theoretical understanding remains largely underdeveloped. Existing analyses mostly focus on finite state-action spaces and on simplified variants of actor-critic, such as double-loop updates with i.i.d. sampling, which are often impractical for real-world applications.
We consider the canonical and widely adopted single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling in continuous state-action space. Specifically, we establish finite-time convergence by introducing a novel Lyapunov analysis framework, which provides a unified convergence characterization of both the actor and the critic. Our approach is less conservative than previous methods and offers new insights into the coupled dynamics of actor-critic updates.
author: Xuyang Chen;Lin Zhao
aff: national university of singaore, National University of Singapore;National University of Singapore
aff_unique_norm: national university of singaore, National University of Singapore;National University of Singapore

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to mathematically prove that an AI learning through trial and error won't get stuck in a loop of perpetual failure when dealing with real-world movements. While they claimed to have cracked the code on how these 'brain-and-body' algorithms work together, the experts basically told them their math was too idealistic for the messy real world.
why_bad: The 'novel' mathematical framework is essentially a facelift of existing methods that fails to provide genuinely new insights.
The authors claim to solve 'real-world' problems but rely on hyper-specific assumptions that would crumble the moment a robot encountered a literal pebble.
The paper lacks empirical simulations, meaning the authors are essentially saying 'trust the math' without showing the algorithm actually working on a computer.
roast.analogy_name: The Blindfolded Quarterback and the Lagging Coach
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a rookie quarterback (the Actor) trying to throw a touchdown while blindfolded, relying entirely on a coach (the Critic) who is watching the game on a 5-second delay and shouting instructions through a megaphone. The authors of this paper claim they've proven this duo will eventually win the Super Bowl. However, the reviewers are blowing the whistle because the authors' 'proof' assumes the grass is perfectly frictionless, the wind never blows, and the opposing team is actually just a row of stationary traffic cones. It's a bold commentary on the game, but it ignores the fact that in a real stadium, the quarterback is going to get sacked before the coach even finishes his first sentence. Great for a chalkboard; terrible for the NFL.

title: Benchmarking Quantum Reinforcement Learning
author_site: Nico Meyer, Christian Ufrecht, George Yammine, Georgios Kontes, Christopher Mutschler, Daniel Scherer
abstract: Benchmarking and establishing proper statistical validation metrics for reinforcement learning (RL) remain ongoing challenges, where no consensus has been established yet. The emergence of quantum computing and its potential applications in quantum reinforcement learning (QRL) further complicate benchmarking efforts. To enable valid performance comparisons and to streamline current research in this area, we propose a novel benchmarking methodology, which is based on a statistical estimator for sample complexity and a definition of statistical outperformance. Furthermore, considering QRL, our methodology casts doubt on some previous claims regarding its superiority. We conducted experiments on a novel benchmarking environment with flexible levels of complexity. While we still identify possible advantages, our findings are more nuanced overall. We discuss the potential limitations of these results and explore their implications for empirical research on quantum advantage in QRL.
keywords: Reinforcement Learning;Quantum Computing;Benchmarking;Sample Complexity;Quantum Machine Learning
author: Nico Meyer;Christian Ufrecht;George Yammine;Georgios Kontes;Christopher Mutschler;Daniel Scherer
aff: Fraunhofer Institute for Integrated Circuits IIS;Fraunhofer IIS;Fraunhofer IIS;;Fraunhofer IIS;Fraunhofer IIS
aff_unique_norm: Fraunhofer Institute for Integrated Circuits;

roast.laymen_summary: We built a rigorous 'bullsh*t detector' to see if adding 'Quantum' to AI actually makes it smarter or if researchers are just moving the goalposts. It turns out the supposed 'quantum edge' is mostly a math trick, and even in our own custom-made test, the results were more 'meh' than 'revolutionary.'
why_bad: The 'Home-Field Advantage' Flaw: They tested their methodology on a 'novel environment' they built themselves, which is like a chef inventing a new type of hunger just to prove their soup works.

Statistical Wet Blanket: By debunking previous 'quantum superiority' claims but failing to provide a definitive win of their own, the paper lands in a lukewarm middle ground that leaves both the hype-train and the skeptics annoyed.

Vague Implementation: The abstract admits the findings are 'nuanced' and 'identify possible advantages,' which is academic-speak for 'we didn't actually find a clear Quantum Advantage, but we’d like to keep our grant money, please.'
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where everyone is desperate to get to the grocery store faster, one team spends billions to build a pogo stick powered by a nuclear reactor. They claim it’s the future of travel, but to prove it, they have to invent a very specific 'pogo-only' obstacle course. The result? They arrive at the store at roughly the same time as a guy in a 1998 Honda Civic, then release a press release saying the results are 'nuanced.' This summer, prepare for... THE SAMPLE COMPLEXITY STRUGGLE. It’s expensive, it’s loud, and you’re still not getting your milk any faster.

title: Conformal Tail Risk Control for Large Language Model Alignment
author_site: Catherine Chen, Jingyan Shen, Xinyu Yang, Lihua Lei
abstract: Recent developments in large language models (LLMs) have led to their widespread usage for various tasks. The prevalence of LLMs in society implores the assurance on the reliability of their performance. In particular, risk-sensitive applications demand meticulous attention to unexpectedly poor outcomes, i.e., tail events, for instance, toxic answers, humiliating language, and offensive outputs. Due to the costly nature of acquiring human annotations, general-purpose scoring models have been created to automate the process of quantifying these tail events. This phenomenon introduces potential human-machine misalignment between the respective scoring mechanisms. In this work, we present a lightweight calibration framework for blackbox models that ensures the alignment of humans and machines with provable guarantees. Our framework provides a rigorous approach to controlling any distortion risk measure that is characterized by a weighted average of quantiles of the loss incurred by the LLM with high confidence. The theoretical foundation of our method relies on the connection between conformal risk control and a traditional family of statistics, i.e., L-statistics. To demonstrate the utility of our framework, we conduct comprehensive experiments that address the issue of human-machine misalignment.
keywords: large language models;conformal risk control;human-machine alignment;model calibration;L-statistics
author: Catherine Chen;Jingyan Shen;Zhun Deng;Lihua Lei

roast.laymen_summary: The authors developed a complex mathematical 'safety net' to ensure that when an AI says something unhinged, it at least matches the specific brand of unhinged that a human would expect. They used high-level statistics to try and force automated 'grading' robots to agree with human judges, even though both are often just guessing.
why_bad: The 'provable guarantees' are mathematically sound but practically useless because they rely on the assumption that the automated scoring model isn't itself a hallucinating mess.
The use of L-statistics for tail risk is essentially bringing a particle accelerator to a knife fight; it's high-complexity math to solve a data-quality problem.
The 'comprehensive experiments' show the math works on paper, but fail to prove the LLM actually stops being toxic in real-world scenarios where users aren't math professors.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: Your startup's office is constantly catching fire. Instead of buying a fire extinguisher, we’re pitching you a 'Statistical Inferno Calibration Suite.' We’ve built a sensor that watches the fire and, using heavy-duty calculus, guarantees with 95% confidence that the sensor’s panic level perfectly aligns with how much your employees are currently screaming. We aren't actually putting out the fire—we’re just providing a mathematically rigorous framework to ensure the 'Is it hot?' light turns on exactly when the intern’s eyebrows singe off. It’s not 'safety,' it’s 'quantifiable disaster awareness.' Who’s ready to lead the Series A?

title: Selective Preference Aggregation
author_site: Shreyas Kadekodi, Hayden McTavish, Berk Ustun
abstract: Many applications in machine learning and decision-making rely on procedures to aggregate human preferences.In such tasks, individual express ordinal preferences over a set of items through votes, ratings, or pairwise comparisons. We then summarize their collective preferences as a ranking. Standard methods for preference aggregation are designed to return rankings that arbitrate individual disagreements in ways that are faithful and fair. In this work, we introduce a paradigm for *selective aggregation*, where we can avoid the need to arbitrate dissent by abstaining from comparison. We summarize collective preferences as a *selective ranking* -- i.e., a partial order where we can only compare items where at least $100\cdot(1 - \tau)\%$ of individuals agree. We develop algorithms to build selective rankings that achieve all possible trade-offs between comparability and disagreement, and derive formal guarantees on their safety and stability. We conduct an extensive set of experiments on real-world datasets to benchmark our approach and demonstrate its functionality. Our results show selective aggregation can promote transparency and robustness by revealing disagreement and abstaining from arbitration.
author: Shreyas Kadekodi;Hayden McTavish;Berk Ustun

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of forcing a consensus when people disagree, this paper proposes a system that simply refuses to rank items unless a massive majority agrees. It’s essentially a mathematical framework for saying 'I’m not touching that' whenever a group of people starts bickering.
why_bad: The core 'algorithm' is effectively just a glorified threshold filter that hides data rather than processing it.
The 'safety guarantees' are a tautology: the system is 'safe' from making a wrong choice only because it refuses to make any choice at all.
Reviewers found it practically useless for real-world decision-making, where the entire point of aggregation is to resolve conflict, not ignore it.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a high-stakes courtroom drama where a jury is tasked with ranking the guilt of five co-conspirators. The lead juror stands up and tells the judge, 'Your Honor, we’ve decided that if we don’t all agree 100% on who is more guilty, we’re simply going to pretend those defendants don't exist.' The Judge is horrified as the jury only delivers a verdict for the one guy everyone hates, while the other four walk free because the jurors couldn't agree on the seating chart. It’s not 'robust' justice; it’s a legal system that only functions when the answer is already obvious to everyone in the room.

title: LineFlow: A Framework to Learn Active Control of Production Lines
author_site: Kai Müller, Martin Wenzel, Tobias Windisch
abstract: Many production lines require active control mechanisms, such as adaptive routing, worker reallocation, and rescheduling, to maintain optimal performance. However, designing these control systems is challenging for various reasons, and while reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in addressing these challenges, a standardized and general framework is still lacking. In this work, we introduce LineFlow, an extensible, open-source Python framework for simulating production lines of arbitrary complexity and training RL agents to control them. To demonstrate the capabilities and to validate the underlying theoretical assumptions of LineFlow, we formulate core subproblems of active line control in ways that facilitate mathematical analysis. For each problem, we provide optimal solutions for comparison. We benchmark state-of-the-art RL algorithms and show that the learned policies approach optimal performance in well-understood scenarios. However, for more complex, industrial-scale production lines, RL still faces significant challenges, highlighting the need for further research in areas such as reward shaping, curriculum learning, and hierarchical control.
keywords: Machine Learning for Manufacturing;Active production line control;Reinforcement Learning;Production line simulation
author: Kai Müller;Martin Wenzel;Tobias Windisch

roast.laymen_summary: We built a specialized sandbox for training AI to manage factory floors, hoping it would revolutionize manufacturing. In reality, the AI successfully mastered the equivalent of a lemonade stand but completely panicked when asked to run a real business.
why_bad: The framework essentially spends 20 pages proving it can solve math problems that were already settled during the Nixon administration.
The AI’s performance drops off a cliff the moment the simulation moves from 'textbook simple' to 'actually useful.'
The conclusion is a desperate laundry list of 'future research,' which is academic code for 'this doesn't actually work yet.'
roast.analogy_description: In a world of collapsing supply chains, one algorithm dared to dream big. It mastered the basics. It conquered the obvious. It became the undisputed king of... the tutorial level. See the heart-pounding journey of an AI that can perfectly optimize a one-man hot dog stand, but suffers a total system lobotomy the moment someone orders a side of fries. It’s not 'Industry 4.0'—it’s 'Internship 0.1.' Coming this fall: The Simulation of Disappointment.
roast.tone_check: Dry, snarky, and brutally honest about the gap between simulation and reality.

title: Enhancing Decision-Making of Large Language Models via Actor-Critic
author_site: Heng Dong, Kefei Duan, Chongjie Zhang
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable advancements in natural language processing tasks, yet they encounter challenges in complex decision-making scenarios that require long-term reasoning and alignment with high-level objectives. Existing methods either rely on short-term auto-regressive action generation or face limitations in accurately simulating rollouts and assessing outcomes, leading to sub-optimal decisions. This paper introduces a novel LLM-based Actor-Critic framework, termed LAC, that effectively improves LLM policies with long-term action evaluations in a principled and scalable way. Our approach addresses two key challenges: (1) extracting robust action evaluations by computing Q-values via token logits associated with positive/negative outcomes, enhanced by future trajectory rollouts and reasoning; and (2) enabling efficient policy improvement through a gradient-free mechanism. Experiments across diverse environments -- including high-level decision-making (ALFWorld), low-level action spaces (BabyAI-Text), and large action spaces (WebShop) -- demonstrate the framework’s generality and superiority over state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves competitive performance using 7B/8B parameter LLMs, even outperforming baseline methods employing GPT-4 in complex tasks. These results underscore the potential of integrating structured policy optimization with LLMs’ intrinsic knowledge to advance decision-making capabilities in multi-step environments.
author: Heng Dong;Kefei Duan;Chongjie Zhang

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop LLMs from 'yolo-ing' every decision by forcing the model to act as its own coach, weighing the long-term consequences of its words before it speaks. By using word-probability scores as a makeshift 'reward system,' they claim a small model can outthink GPT-4, provided the task involves navigating a website or playing a text-based adventure game.
why_bad: The 'Critic' is just the same model wearing a different hat, creating a recursive hallucination loop where the model marks its own homework and gives itself an A+.
Using token logits as a proxy for 'Q-values' is statistically flimsy; word probabilities reflect linguistic frequency, not necessarily the strategic utility of an action in the real world.

The 'gradient-free mechanism' is essentially a glorified trial-and-error process that fails to actually 'teach' the model anything new, instead just filtering for the luckiest guess it was already capable of making.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Self-Appointed Judge
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where the Defense Attorney (the Actor) presents a piece of evidence, then immediately sprints to the bench, puts on a powdered wig, and acts as the Judge (the Critic) to rule his own evidence 'stunningly brave and accurate.' The prosecution—the reviewers—is screaming for an objection because the 'Judge' is literally reading from the 'Attorney's' own diary to determine the truth. The Defense argues that because he thought about his argument for three seconds before saying it (the rollout), it’s legally binding 'Policy Optimization.' The jury is confused, the bailiff is sleeping, and while the Defense technically 'won' the case because the cardboard cutout of a witness didn't talk back, no actual law was established.

title: SafeMap: Robust HD Map Construction from Incomplete Observations
author_site: Xiaoshuai Hao, Lingdong Kong, Rong Yin, Pengwei Wang, Jing Zhang, Yunfeng Diao, Shu Zhao
abstract: Robust high-definition (HD) map construction is vital for autonomous driving, yet existing methods often struggle with incomplete multi-view camera data. This paper presents SafeMap, a novel framework specifically designed to ensure accuracy even when certain camera views are missing. SafeMap integrates two key components: the Gaussian-based Perspective View Reconstruction (G-PVR) module and the Distillation-based Bird’s-Eye-View (BEV) Correction (D-BEVC) module. G-PVR leverages prior knowledge of view importance to dynamically prioritize the most informative regions based on the relationships among available camera views. Furthermore, D-BEVC utilizes panoramic BEV features to correct the BEV representations derived from incomplete observations. Together, these components facilitate comprehensive data reconstruction and robust HD map generation. SafeMap is easy to implement and integrates seamlessly into existing systems, offering a plug-and-play solution for enhanced robustness. Experimental results demonstrate that SafeMap significantly outperforms previous methods in both complete and incomplete scenarios, highlighting its superior performance and resilience.
author: Xiaoshuai Hao;Lingdong Kong;Rong Yin;Pengwei Wang;Jing Zhang;Yunfeng Diao;Shu Zhao

roast.laymen_summary: SafeMap is basically a 'fake it 'til you make it' strategy for self-driving cars when one of their cameras gets blocked by mud or a giant bird. It uses AI to guess what the missing part of the road looks like by looking at the remaining cameras and using a digital imagination.
why_bad: The 'hallucination' hazard: The model often prioritizes looking pretty over being right, essentially 'inventing' road features that don't exist in the missing frames, which is a great way to robustly crash into a phantom wall.

Computational gluttony: By stacking Gaussian reconstruction on top of knowledge distillation, the paper creates a 'plug-and-play' solution that actually runs at the speed of a PowerPoint presentation, making it useless for real-time driving.

The 'Trench Coat' problem: The reviewers noted that the G-PVR and D-BEVC modules are just existing, well-known architectures rebranded with fancy acronyms, offering very little actual scientific novelty beyond better hyperparameter tuning.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re pitching a startup where you train professional drivers to navigate highways with one eye shut and a VR headset showing them a 'dream version' of the road. We call it 'SafeMap.' Instead of actually fixing the camera lens, we just ask the car to remember what roads usually look like and 'distill' those vibes into the steering wheel. It’s not 'unsafe'—it’s 'probabilistically adventurous.' If the car misses a stop sign because it was busy reconstructing the sidewalk in 4K, that’s just the cost of innovation. We’re currently seeking $10M in seed funding to tell cars that reality is subjective.

title: Hyperflows: Pruning Reveals the Importance of Weights
abstract: Network pruning is used to reduce inference latency and power consumption in large neural networks. However, most existing methods struggle to accurately assess the importance of individual weights due to their inherent interrelatedness, leading to poor performance, especially at extreme sparsity levels. We introduce *Hyperflows*, a dynamic pruning approach that estimates each weight’s importance by observing the network’s gradient response to the weight's removal. A global *pressure* term continuously drives all weights toward pruning, with those critical for accuracy being automatically regrown based on their *flow*, the aggregated gradient signal when they are absent. We explore the relationship between final sparsity and pressure, deriving power-law equations similar to those found in neural scaling laws. Empirically, we demonstrate state-of-the-art results with ResNet-50 and VGG-19 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.
author: Barbulescu Eugen;Antonio Alexoaie
aff: Technical University of Cluj-Napoca;Technical University of Cluj-Napoca+Bosch

roast.laymen_summary: The authors propose making AI smaller by constantly threatening to delete every part of its brain and only keeping the pieces that scream the loudest through mathematical signals. It’s essentially a high-stress survival game for neural network connections where only the most 'vocal' weights survive the purge.
why_bad: The 'state-of-the-art' results are on CIFAR-10, which in 2024 is the academic equivalent of bragging that your new engine can successfully power a tricycle.
The 'Hyperflows' mechanism is largely a rebranding of existing gradient-based pruning techniques, dressed up in fancy fluid dynamics metaphors to hide a lack of conceptual novelty.
The derived 'power-law equations' are suspiciously convenient curve-fittng exercises that provide no actual predictive power for large-scale models like Transformers.
roast.analogy_name: The Trial of the Redundant Synapse
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense presents 'Hyperflows'—a system that claims to optimize the workforce by systematically firing every employee and only rehiring them if the office catches fire the moment they leave the building. The Prosecution (the reviewers) argues that this isn't a 'revolutionary labor theory'; it's just chaotic management with a thesaurus. You can't claim you've discovered a 'Universal Law of Productivity' when your only test case was a lemonade stand (CIFAR-10) and the CEO (ResNet-50) is a decade-old relic. The court finds the defendant guilty of 'Math-Washing'—using complex-sounding fluid dynamics to describe what is essentially just keeping the loud workers and firing the quiet ones.

title: Breaking Silos: Adaptive Model Fusion Unlocks Better Time Series Forecasting
author_site: Zhining Liu, Ze Yang, Xiao Lin, Ruizhong Qiu, Tianxin Wei, Yada Zhu, Hendrik Hamann, Jingrui He, Hanghang Tong
abstract: Time-series forecasting plays a critical role in many real-world applications. Although increasingly powerful models have been developed and achieved superior results on benchmark datasets, through a fine-grained sample-level inspection, we find that (i) no single model consistently outperforms others across different test samples, but instead (ii) each model excels in specific cases. These findings prompt us to explore how to adaptively leverage the distinct strengths of various forecasting models for different samples. We introduce TimeFuse, a framework for collective time-series forecasting with sample-level adaptive fusion of heterogeneous models. TimeFuse utilizes meta-features to characterize input time series and trains a learnable fusor to predict optimal model fusion weights for any given input. The fusor can leverage samples from diverse datasets for joint training, allowing it to adapt to a wide variety of temporal patterns and thus generalize to new inputs, even from unseen datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TimeFuse in various long-/short-term forecasting tasks, achieving near-universal improvement over the state-of-the-art individual models. Code is available at https://github.com/ZhiningLiu1998/TimeFuse.
author: Zhining Liu;Ze Yang;Xiao Lin;Ruizhong Qiu;Tianxin Wei;Yada Zhu;Hendrik Hamann;Jingrui He;Hanghang Tong

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that no single AI model is perfect at predicting the future, so they built a 'manager' AI to hire a committee of models for every single data point. It’s essentially a high-tech way of hedging your bets by constantly shifting how much you trust different experts depending on the specific problem.
why_bad: Over-Engineering Overload: The computational cost of running multiple models plus a 'fusor' is like hiring a five-star chef to make a peanut butter sandwich—the marginal gain doesn't justify the electric bill.

The 'Old Wine' Problem: Critics noted that 'Adaptive Model Fusion' is basically just 'Stacking' or 'Ensembling' with a fresh coat of paint and some trendy meta-learning buzzwords added to the Abstract.

Baseline Blindness: The paper struggled to prove that its complex, GPU-hungry framework actually beats a simple, 'dumb' average of the models, which is the academic equivalent of losing a math lead to a $2 calculator.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re tired of picking the wrong stocks. Instead of just buying an index fund, you hire five different financial advisors. Then, because you can't decide who to listen to, you hire a sixth 'Meta-Advisor' whose entire job is to watch the first five and say, 'Okay, for this specific Tesla trade, give 30% of your attention to Dave and 70% to Susan.' You’re spending $500k in salaries to manage a $1,000 portfolio. Is it sophisticated? Absolutely. Does it 'unlock better forecasting'? Technically. Is it a sustainable business model? Only if your goal is to set money on fire to achieve a 0.2% higher return than a monkey throwing darts.

title: MERGE$^3$: Efficient Evolutionary Merging on Consumer-grade GPUs
author_site: Tommaso Mencattini, Adrian Robert Minut, Donato Crisostomi, Andrea Santilli, Emanuele Rodola
abstract: Evolutionary model merging enables the creation of high-performing multi-task models but remains computationally prohibitive for consumer hardware. We introduce MERGE$^3$, an efficient framework that makes evolutionary merging of Large Language Models (LLMs) feasible on a single GPU by reducing fitness computation costs 50× while retaining a large fraction of the original performance. MERGE$^3$ achieves this by **E**xtracting a reduced dataset for evaluation, **E**stimating model abilities using Item Response Theory (IRT), and **E**volving optimal merges via IRT-based performance estimators. Our method enables state-of-the-art multilingual and cross-lingual merging, transferring knowledge across languages with significantly lower computational overhead. We provide theoretical guarantees and an open-source library, democratizing high-quality model merging.
keywords: Model Merging;Evolutionary Algorithms;Efficient Methods for Machine Learning;Language Models;LLMs;Multilingual Models
author: Tommaso Mencattini;Robert Adrian Minut;Donato Crisostomi;Andrea Santilli;Emanuele Rodolà

roast.laymen_summary: The authors want to smash different AI models together into one 'super-model' using a standard home gaming PC instead of a room full of servers. To do this, they’re basically 'skimming the textbook' and guessing the answers to save time, hoping you won't notice the resulting model is a bit of a scatterbrain.
why_bad: The 50x speedup comes from 'Extracting a reduced dataset,' which is academic-speak for 'we ignored 98% of the test and hoped the vibes remained the same.'
Item Response Theory (IRT) works for grading SATs, but applying it to LLM 'abilities' is a massive leap of faith that ignores the chaotic, non-linear ways these models actually fail.

The 'theoretical guarantees' offered are likely mathematically sound but practically useless, as 'retaining a large fraction of performance' usually means the model lost its ability to do anything complex.
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are witnessing a bold move in the AI playoffs! MERGE^3 is stepping onto the field claiming they’ve built a championship roster, but look closer—they’re trying to scout the next LeBron James by watching a guy throw a paper ball into a trash can exactly once! It's a classic 'efficiency' play: why watch the whole game when you can just check the vibes at halftime? They’re trying to bake a five-tier wedding cake in a microwave to save on the electric bill, and they’re shocked the judges are calling it 'soupy.' It’s a Hail Mary pass from a quarterback who’s trying to save energy by not actually running to the line of scrimmage. Bold? Yes. Efficient? Absolutely. But the scoreboard says they’re playing a completely different sport!

title: Structured Preconditioners in Adaptive Optimization: A Unified Analysis
author_site: Shuo Xie, Tianhao Wang, Sashank J. Reddi, Sanjiv Kumar, Zhiyuan Li
abstract: We present a novel unified analysis for a broad class of adaptive optimization algorithms with structured (e.g., layerwise, diagonal, and kronecker-factored) preconditioners for both online regret minimization and offline convex optimization. Our analysis not only provides matching rate to several important structured preconditioned algorithms including diagonal AdaGrad, full-matrix AdaGrad, and AdaGrad-Norm, but also gives an improved convergence rate for a one-sided variant of Shampoo over that of original Shampoo. Interestingly, more structured preconditioners (e.g., diagonal Adagrad, AdaGrad-Norm which use less space and compute) are often presented as computationally efficient approximations to full-matrix Adagrad, aiming for improved optimization performance through better approximations. Our unified analysis challenges this prevailing view and reveals, perhaps surprisingly, that more structured preconditioners, despite using less space and computation per step, can outperform their less structured counterparts. To demonstrate this, we show that one-sided Shampoo, which is relatively much cheaper than full-matrix AdaGrad could outperform it both theoretically and experimentally.
author: Shuo Xie;Tianhao Wang;Sashank J. Reddi;Sanjiv Kumar;Zhiyuan Li
