roast.laymen_summary: We analyzed a bunch of different math shortcuts used to speed up AI training and found that the 'cheap' versions aren't just budget approximations of the expensive ones. It turns out that doing less math can actually make the AI learn faster and better, which is like finding out a flip phone outruns a supercomputer.
why_bad: The 'unified analysis' is essentially a mathematical quilt of existing proofs that fails to offer a truly novel theoretical breakthrough.
The 'improved' convergence rate for one-sided Shampoo is a marginal theoretical gain that doesn't necessarily translate to real-world training stability.
The paper attempts to overturn decades of intuition about approximation theory with experimental evidence that critics found too narrow to be conclusive.
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where every scientist believes you need a $50,000 telescope to see the stars, one team claims they can see more with a pair of scratched sunglasses. Watch as they try to convince the elite that 'more detail' is actually just 'more noise.' It's the story of a 'unified' theory that’s really just three smaller theories in a trench coat, trying to sneak into the Hall of Innovation. This summer, prepare for the ultimate paradox: where the less math you do, the more the reviewers ask you to explain yourself.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically grounded snark delivered with 99% confidence intervals.

title: Learning Configurations for Data-Driven Multi-Objective Optimization
author_site: Zhiyang Chen, Hailong Yao, Xia Yin
abstract: Multi-objective optimization problems arise widely in various fields. In practice, multi-objective optimization is generally solved by heuristics with tunable parameters that are highly application-specific. Tuning parameters based on real-world instances (a.k.a. algorithm configuration) are generally empirical without theoretical guarantees. In this work, we establish the theoretical foundation of data-driven multi-objective optimization through the lens of machine learning theory. We provide generalization guarantees on selecting parameters for multi-objective optimization algorithms based on sampled problem instances. Moreover, if the performance metric of the algorithm is the Pareto volume, we can PAC-learn the approximately optimal configuration in polynomial time. We apply our framework to various algorithms, including approximation algorithms, local search, and linear programming. Experiments on multiple problems verify our theoretical findings.
author: Zhiyang Chen;Hailong Yao;Xia Yin
aff: Tsinghua University;University of Science and Technology Beijing;, Tsinghua University
aff_unique_norm: Tsinghua University;University of Science and Technology Beijing

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to use fancy math to prove exactly how to tune the 'hidden knobs' on complex software that has to juggle multiple goals at once. Basically, we’re claiming we found a way to stop guessing which settings work and started using machine learning to guarantee the best results.
why_bad: The theoretical 'guarantees' rely on the assumption that the future will look exactly like the past, which is a bold-faced lie in any real-world data scenario.
The 'polynomial time' complexity is technically true but practically useless, featuring constants so massive that the universe would end before the algorithm finishes its first cup of coffee.
The gap between the high-level math and the actual experiments is wide enough to fly a Boeing 747 through; the theory handles 'abstract distributions' while the code handles 'toy problems'.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re at a high-end buffet where you want to maximize both lobster intake and dessert space without getting sick. Our startup provides a 400-page mathematical proof that tells you exactly how many centimeters to open your mouth for the optimal bite. Sure, our 'generalization guarantee' only works if the buffet never changes its menu and you breathe at a constant rate of 12 inhalations per minute, but look at these graphs! We aren't just eating; we're Disrupting the Gastronomic Multi-Objective Space with a scalable, data-driven fork.

title: Fair Class-Incremental Learning using Sample Weighting
abstract: Model fairness is becoming important in class-incremental learning for Trustworthy AI. While accuracy has been a central focus in class-incremental learning, fairness has been relatively understudied. However, naively using all the samples of the current task for training results in unfair catastrophic forgetting for certain sensitive groups including classes. We theoretically analyze that forgetting occurs if the average gradient vector of the current task data is in an "opposite direction'' compared to the average gradient vector of a sensitive group, which means their inner products are negative. We then propose a fair class-incremental learning framework that adjusts the training weights of current task samples to change the direction of the average gradient vector and thus reduce the forgetting of underperforming groups and achieve fairness. For various group fairness measures, we formulate optimization problems to minimize the overall losses of sensitive groups while minimizing the disparities among them. We also show the problems can be solved with linear programming and propose an efficient Fairness-aware Sample Weighting (FSW) algorithm. Experiments show that FSW achieves better accuracy-fairness tradeoff results than state-of-the-art approaches on real datasets.
author: Jaeyoung Park;Minsu Kim;Steven Euijong Whang
aff: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology
aff_unique_norm: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: When AI learns new tricks over time, it tends to develop 'digital amnesia' for older information, often unfairly dumping data about minority groups first. This paper tries to fix that by micromanaging which data points the AI pays attention to, effectively trying to balance the scales so no group gets left behind in the memory wipe.
why_bad: The 'theoretical discovery' that opposing gradients cause forgetting is basically the AI equivalent of saying 'water is wet'—it's been well-known in the field for years.
Using Linear Programming for every training step is computationally suicidal; it’s like trying to solve a Rubik's cube every time you take a single step forward.
The trade-off between accuracy and fairness is handled with such heavy-handed sample weighting that the model likely ends up being mediocre at everything instead of good at anything.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're a chef on a show where you have to learn a new recipe every hour. The problem is, every time you learn to bake a soufflé, you completely forget how to make a vegan salad. To stay 'fair,' the authors suggest you spend four hours doing complex calculus to decide exactly how many milligrams of salt to put on each vegetable so you don't offend the salad lovers while whisking the eggs. By the time you've finished your 'Fairness-aware' math, the kitchen is on fire, the soufflé has collapsed, and the judges have already disqualified you for being slower than a dial-up modem.

title: Optimal and Practical Batched Linear Bandit Algorithm
author_site: Sanghoon Yu, Min-hwan Oh
abstract: We study the linear bandit problem under limited adaptivity, known as the batched linear bandit. While existing approaches can achieve near-optimal regret in theory, they are often computationally prohibitive or underperform in practice. We propose BLAE, a novel batched algorithm that integrates arm elimination with regularized G-optimal design, achieving the minimax optimal regret (up to logarithmic factors in $T$) in both large-$K$ and small-$K$ regimes for the first time, while using only $O(\log\log T)$ batches. Our analysis introduces new techniques for batch-wise optimal design and refined concentration bounds. Crucially, BLAE demonstrates low computational overhead and strong empirical performance, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in extensive numerical evaluations. Thus, BLAE is the first algorithm to combine provable minimax-optimality in all regimes and practical superiority in batched linear bandits.
author: Sanghoon Yu;Min-hwan Oh
aff: Seoul National University;Seoul National University
aff_unique_norm: Seoul National University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to solve the problem of making the best possible decisions while only being allowed to check your results a handful of times. The authors claim they've built a 'perfect' system that works fast and never fails, even when you have a million choices to pick from.
why_bad: The 'optimal' math relies on hidden constants so massive they effectively render the speed gains imaginary in real-world scenarios.
The G-optimal design step is a computational nightmare that the authors hand-waved away as 'efficient' without actually proving it scales.
The empirical benchmarks compared the new algorithm against 'state-of-the-art' methods that were intentionally handicapped or outdated.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where you have to win a 500-lap race but you're only allowed to touch the steering wheel three times... one algorithm thinks it can take the gold. Witness 'BLAE'—the mathematical equivalent of trying to parallel park a semi-truck using only a telescope and a calendar. It promises the moon, delivers a spreadsheet, and forgets that 'theoretical speed' doesn't mean anything if the engine explodes before the first turn. Coming this fall to a GitHub repo that hasn't been updated in six months: The Fast and the Finitary.

title: Semantic Shift Estimation via Dual-Projection and Classifier Reconstruction for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning
author_site: Run He, Di Fang, Yicheng Xu, Yawen Cui, Ming Li, Cen Chen, Ziqian Zeng, HUIPING ZHUANG
abstract: Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning (EFCIL) aims to sequentially learn from distinct categories without retaining exemplars but easily suffers from catastrophic forgetting of learned knowledge. While existing EFCIL methods leverage knowledge distillation to alleviate forgetting, they still face two critical challenges: semantic shift and decision bias. Specifically, the embeddings of old tasks shift in the embedding space after learning new tasks, and the classifier becomes biased towards new tasks due to training solely with new data, hindering the balance between old and new knowledge. To address these issues, we propose the Dual-Projection Shift Estimation and Classifier Reconstruction (DPCR) approach for EFCIL. DPCR effectively estimates semantic shift through a dual-projection, which combines a learnable transformation with a row-space projection to capture both task-wise and category-wise shifts. Furthermore, to mitigate decision bias, DPCR employs ridge regression to reformulate a classifier reconstruction process. This reconstruction exploits previous in covariance and prototype of each class after calibration with estimated shift, thereby reducing decision bias. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, on various datasets, DPCR effectively balances old and new tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art EFCIL methods. Our codes are available at https://github.com/RHe502/ICML25-DPCR.
author: Run He;Di Fang;Yicheng Xu;Yawen Cui;Ming Li;Cen Chen;Ziqian Zeng;Huiping Zhuang

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to teach AI new tricks without it immediately deleting its old memories, all while being forbidden from keeping any 'cheat sheets' of past data. The authors propose a mathematical guessing game to correct the AI's tendency to get obsessed with the latest thing it learned at the expense of everything else.
why_bad: Incremental novelty: The 'Dual-Projection' is essentially a fancy math band-aid on existing methods rather than a fundamental breakthrough in how AI learns.
Weak benchmarks: The authors claim victory by beating 'yesterday's news' models while conveniently ignoring more recent, high-performing competitors.
Over-engineering: The complexity of the 'Classifier Reconstruction' process adds significant computational baggage for a performance gain that is statistically underwhelming.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Shifting Alibi
roast.analogy_description: Picture a star witness who identifies a suspect on Monday, but by Friday—after being shown 50 more lineups—they start claiming the first suspect was actually a golden retriever. In this courtroom drama, the lawyer (the algorithm) isn't allowed to show the witness the original photos (no exemplars). Instead, the lawyer tries to use 'Dual-Projection' to convince the jury that if we account for how much the witness's brain has rotted since Monday, their testimony still counts. The Judge (the reviewer) slams their gavel, ruling that 'reconstructing' a witness's memory using ridge regression is just a desperate attempt to fix a witness who is fundamentally unreliable.

title: WyckoffDiff -- A Generative Diffusion Model for Crystal Symmetry
author_site: Filip Ekström Kelvinius, Oskar Andersson, Abhijith Parackal, Dong Qian, Rickard Armiento, Fredrik Lindsten
abstract: Crystalline materials often exhibit a high level of symmetry. However, most generative models do not account for symmetry, but rather model each atom without any constraints on its position or element. We propose a generative model, Wyckoff Diffusion (WyckoffDiff), which generates symmetry-based descriptions of crystals. This is enabled by considering a crystal structure representation that encodes all symmetry, and we design a novel neural network architecture which enables using this representation inside a discrete generative model framework. In addition to respecting symmetry by construction, the discrete nature of our model enables fast generation. We additionally present a new metric, Fréchet Wrenformer Distance, which captures the symmetry aspects of the materials generated, and we benchmark WyckoffDiff against recently proposed generative models for crystal generation. As a proof-of-concept study, we use WyckoffDiff to find new materials below the convex hull of thermodynamical stability.
keywords: generative modeling;materials science;materials generation;diffusion models;discrete diffusion models;Wyckoff
author: Filip Ekström Kelvinius;Oskar B. Andersson;Abhijith S Parackal;Dong Qian;Rickard Armiento;Fredrik Lindsten
aff: Linköping University;Linköping University;Linköping University;;Linköping University;Linköping University
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;;Associate Professor;Associate Professor
aff_unique_norm: Linköping University;

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of letting an AI toss atoms around like a toddler with a bucket of LEGOs, this model forces every atom to follow strict geometric rules of symmetry. It aims to generate realistic crystal structures faster by focusing on the 'blueprint' of the symmetry rather than the individual placement of every single particle.
why_bad: The model enforces symmetry so rigidly by design that it lacks the flexibility to discover novel, 'imperfect' materials that don't fit into its pre-defined boxes.
The authors introduced a custom metric (Fréchet Wrenformer Distance) which reviewers suspect was specifically engineered to make their own model look good while ignoring standard industry benchmarks.
The performance gains in stability are marginal, suggesting the complex mathematical overhead of 'Wyckoff positions' doesn't actually outperform simpler, less pretentious models.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the championship, folks, where WyckoffDiff is trying to revolutionize the game! He’s out on the pitch refusing to take a shot unless his teammates are standing in a perfectly mirrored formation. It’s bold, it’s beautiful, but he’s spent the entire first half measuring the grass with a protractor while the other team is actually, you know, scoring goals. He even tried to tell the ref that points should only count if the play looks like a snowflake. Stunning aesthetics, Cotton, but the scoreboard doesn't care about your geometry homework!

title: HEAP: Hyper Extended A-PDHG Operator for Constrained High-dim PDEs
author_site: Mingquan Feng, Weixin Liao, Yixin Huang, Yifan Fu, Qifu Zheng, Junchi Yan
abstract: Neural operators have emerged as a promising approach for solving high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs). However, existing neural operators often have difficulty in dealing with constrained PDEs, where the solution must satisfy additional equality or inequality constraints beyond the governing equations. To close this gap, we propose a novel neural operator, Hyper Extended Adaptive PDHG (HEAP) for constrained high-dim PDEs, where the learned operator evolves in the parameter space of PDEs. We first show that the evolution operator learning can be formulated as a quadratic programming (QP) problem, then unroll the adaptive primal-dual hybrid gradient (APDHG) algorithm as the QP-solver into the neural operator architecture. This allows us to improve efficiency while retaining theoretical guarantees of the constrained optimization. Empirical results on a variety of high-dim PDEs show that HEAP outperforms the state-of-the-art neural operator model.
author: Mingquan Feng;Weixin Liao;Yixin Huang;Yifan Fu;Qifu Zheng;Junchi Yan
aff: ;Shanghai Jiaotong University;Shanghai Jiaotong University;Shanghai Jiaotong University;Shanghai Jiaotong University;
aff_unique_norm: ;Shanghai Jiao Tong University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a smart math-solver that can handle complex physics problems while strictly following 'the rules' instead of just making lucky guesses. They basically took a classic, rigid optimization algorithm and shoved it inside a flexible neural network, hoping to get the best of both worlds.
why_bad: The 'Hyper Extended' prefix is mostly marketing fluff; adding layers of complexity to the PDHG algorithm made it computationally bloated without actually solving the underlying stability issues.
Unrolling a solver into a neural network sounds fancy, but if the initial conditions shift even slightly, the whole 'theoretical guarantee' collapses like a house of cards.
The empirical results were suspiciously 'cherry-picked,' comparing their shiny new HEAP against outdated models on trivial problems that don't represent the messy reality of high-dimensional physics.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Overfit Witness
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a high-stakes trial where a physics equation is accused of violating the Laws of Constraints. The authors enter the courtroom as defense attorneys with their star witness: The HEAP. They claim HEAP is an expert because it has memorized the entire legal code (the QP-solver). However, as soon as the prosecution presents a piece of evidence not found in the training manual, HEAP begins to sweat hydraulic fluid. It turns out HEAP isn't 'reasoning' about the law; it’s just reading a teleprompter that the attorneys are frantically typing into behind the scenes. The 'theoretical guarantees' are nothing more than a pinky-promise that the witness won't lie, provided nobody asks a difficult question. The Judge—a cold, hard PDE—dismisses the case because the defense spent more time naming their witness than actually proving it could handle the truth.

title: Reflection-Window Decoding: Text Generation with Selective Refinement
author_site: Zeyu Tang, Zhenhao Chen, Xiangchen Song, Loka Li, Yunlong Deng, Yifan Shen, Guangyi Chen, Peter Spirtes, Kun Zhang
abstract: The autoregressive decoding for text generation in large language models (LLMs), while widely used, is inherently suboptimal due to the lack of a built-in mechanism to perform refinement and/or correction of the generated content. In this paper, we consider optimality in terms of the joint probability over the generated response, when jointly considering all tokens at the same time. We theoretically characterize the potential deviation of the autoregressively generated response from its globally optimal counterpart that is of the same length. Our analysis suggests that we need to be cautious when noticeable uncertainty arises during text generation, which may signal the sub-optimality of the generation history. To address the pitfall of autoregressive decoding for text generation, we propose an approach that incorporates a sliding reflection window and a pausing criterion, such that refinement and generation can be carried out interchangeably as the decoding proceeds. Our selective refinement framework strikes a balance between efficiency and optimality, and our extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
keywords: Autogressive Decoding;Text Generation;Reflection Window;Selective Refinement;Large Language Model
author: Zeyu Tang;Zhenhao Chen;Xiangchen Song;Loka Li;Yunlong Deng;Yifan Shen;Guangyi Chen;Peter Spirtes;Kun Zhang
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Carnegie Mellon University;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence+Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence+Carnegie Mellon University
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;MS student;PhD student;Postdoc+Postdoc;Full Professor;Professor+Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Standard AI writes like a drunk person who can't stop talking or delete their typos, leading to sentences that start strong but end in a train wreck. This paper proposes a 're-read' feature where the AI stops, looks back at its recent mistakes, and fixes them before moving on.
why_bad: The 'sliding window' approach adds massive computational overhead, making the AI generate text at the speed of a dial-up modem.
The 'pausing criterion' relies on the model's own uncertainty, but LLMs are famously overconfident even when they are hallucinating absolute nonsense.
The paper fails to prove this is actually better than just asking the model to 'think step-by-step' or doing a simple second pass.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every word is final... one AI decided to live in the past. Meet 'The Editor.' He’s on a first date, but every time he says something slightly awkward, he freezes time, pulls out a notebook, rewrites the last three sentences of his conversation, and then resumes as if nothing happened. It sounds brilliant—until you realize the date has been going on for fourteen hours, he’s still sweating, and the 'corrections' are just as weird as the original mistakes. Coming this fall: A story about why you should probably just think before you speak instead of trying to rewrite reality in real-time.

title: A Comparison of LLM fine-tuning Methods & Evaluation Metrics with Travel Chatbot Use Case
abstract: This research compared large language model (LLM) fine-tuning methods, including Quantized Low Rank Adapter (QLoRA), Retrieval Augmented fine-tuning (RAFT), and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), and additionally compared LLM evaluation methods including End to End (E2E) benchmark method of “Golden Answers”, traditional natural language processing (NLP) metrics, RAG Assessment (Ragas), OpenAI GPT-4 evaluation metrics, and human evaluation, using the travel chatbot use case. The travel dataset was sourced from the Reddit API by requesting posts from travel-related subreddits to get conversation prompts and personalized travel experiences, and augmented for each fine-tuning method. QLoRA and RAFT were applied to two pre-trained LLMs: LLaMa 2 7B and Mistral  7B. The best model according to human evaluation and some GPT-4 metrics was Mistral RAFT, so this underwent a Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) training pipeline, and ultimately was evaluated as the best model. Our main findings are that: 1) quantitative and Ragas metrics do not align with human evaluation, while Open AI GPT-4 evaluations do, 2) RAFT outperforms QLoRA, but still needs postprocessing, and 3) RLHF improves model performance significantly to outperform benchmark models.
author: Sonia Wei Meyer;Shreya Singh;Christopher Ton;Bertha Tam;Xingjian REN
aff: Kaiser Permanente;;;;
aff_unique_norm: Kaiser Permanente;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to see if they could turn chaotic Reddit travel advice into a functional chatbot by putting two small AI models through three different types of 'digital boot camp.' They concluded that traditional math tests for AI suck, GPT-4 is surprisingly good at judging other AIs, and layering multiple training techniques onto Mistral makes it slightly less likely to suggest a vacation in a dumpster.
why_bad: Using Reddit posts as 'Golden Answer' benchmarks is like using a shouting match in a pub as a legal encyclopedia; the data is too noisy and biased to be a reliable ground truth.

The 'revelation' that LLM-based evaluation aligns better with humans than traditional NLP metrics (like ROUGE or BLEU) is a dead horse that has been beaten into a fine powder by better papers two years ago.
Fine-tuning 7B-parameter models in 2024 is the research equivalent of 'My First Chemistry Set'; without testing on larger scales, the results lack the gravity needed for a top-tier publication.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Travel Bro
roast.analogy_description: Counsel, you stand before this court accused of scientific fluffery. You claim to have engineered a 'superior travel expert,' yet your star witness is a 7-billion-parameter model that you fed a diet of unverified Reddit rants and then asked its buddy, GPT-4, to vouch for its character. This isn't an evaluation; it's a circular firing squad of confirmation bias where the defendant is grading their own homework. You’ve presented a 'comprehensive study' that essentially concludes a specialized model is better than a random guess, a 'discovery' so obvious it shouldn't just be thrown out of court—it should be held in contempt of the entire field of Artificial Intelligence.

title: Zero-Shot Cyclic Peptide Design via Composable Geometric Constraints
author_site: Dapeng Jiang, Xiangzhe Kong, Jiaqi Han, Mingyu Li, Rui Jiao, Wenbing Huang, Stefano Ermon, Jianzhu Ma, Yang Liu
abstract: Cyclic peptides, characterized by geometric constraints absent in linear peptides, offer enhanced biochemical properties, presenting new opportunities to address unmet medical needs. However, designing target-specific cyclic peptides remains underexplored due to limited training data. To bridge the gap, we propose CP-Composer, a novel generative framework that enables zero-shot cyclic peptide generation via composable geometric constraints. Our approach decomposes complex cyclization patterns into unit constraints, which are incorporated into a diffusion model through geometric conditioning on nodes and edges. During training, the model learns from unit constraints and their random combinations in linear peptides, while at inference, novel constraint combinations required for cyclization are imposed as input. Experiments show that our model, despite trained with linear peptides, is capable of generating diverse target-binding cyclic peptides, reaching success rates from 38\% to 84\% on different cyclization strategies.
keywords: Cyclic peptide design;diffusion model;geometrically constrained generation
author: Dapeng Jiang;Xiangzhe Kong;Jiaqi Han;Mingyu Li;Rui Jiao;Wenbing Huang;Stefano Ermon;Jianzhu Ma;Yang Liu

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach an AI to design circular protein drugs by only showing it examples of straight ones and hoping it could 'math' its way into a loop. It’s an attempt to build a wedding ring using only the logic of a toothpick and a lot of wishful thinking.
why_bad: Physics Blindness: Training on linear peptides ignores the massive internal strain and unique energy landscapes that actually make a molecule stay in a circle.

Geometric Hallucination: The 'success rates' are likely based on the AI making something that *looks* like a ring, regardless of whether those atoms would actually tolerate being that close in the real world.
The 'Zero-Shot' Mirage: Claiming high performance without training data is usually academic code for 'our model is just overfitting to the rigid geometric constraints we manually forced into it.'
roast.analogy_name: The Spaghetti Lasso: Infinite Loop Edition
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where medicine is a straight line, one algorithm is tired of playing by the rules. Witness the AI trained exclusively on photos of dry spaghetti as it attempts to engineer the world's most complex pretzel. It’s got the nodes. It’s got the edges. It’s got absolutely no idea how gravity works. This summer, prepare for 'The Geometric Constraint'—a high-stakes thriller where 'Zero-Shot' means 'Zero Clue' if the molecule will actually bind or just explode on contact. If you liked 'Linear Regression,' you’ll love this circular logic.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained: Diffusion models conditioned on node/edge constraints often produce geometrically plausible but biologically inert structures if the underlying physics of cyclization isn't explicitly modeled.

title: Smoothed Normalization for Efficient Distributed Private Optimization
abstract: Federated learning enables training machine learning models while preserving the privacy of participants. Surprisingly, there is no differentially private distributed method for smooth non-convex optimization problems. The reason is that standard privacy techniques require bounding the participants' contributions, usually enforced via *clipping* of the updates. Existing literature typically ignores the effect of clipping by assuming the boundedness of gradient norms or analyzes distributed algorithms with clipping but ignores DP constraints. In this work, we study an alternative approach via *smoothed normalization* of the updates motivated by its favorable performance in the centralized setting. By integrating smoothed normalization with an error-feedback mechanism, we design a new distributed algorithm $\alpha$-${\sf NormEC}$. We prove that our method achieves a superior convergence rate over prior works. By extending $\alpha$-${\sf NormEC}$ to the DP setting, we obtain the first differentially private distributed optimization algorithm with provable convergence guarantees. Finally, we support our theoretical findings with experiments on practical machine learning problems.
author: Egor Shulgin;Sarit Khirirat;Peter Richtárik
aff: KAUST;King Abdullah University of Science and Technology;
aff_unique_norm: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology;

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to train AI on private data from thousands of phones without letting anyone 'peek' at the sensitive bits, a process that usually breaks if the data updates are too wild. We proposed a new way to 'smooth out' those updates rather than just cutting them off, claiming we finally solved a math problem everyone else was ignoring.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially a rebranding of existing error-feedback tricks, making the contribution feel like putting a new spoiler on a 2005 Honda Civic.
The claim of being the 'first' to provide provable guarantees for this problem ignores a mountain of existing Differential Privacy literature that the authors seemingly forgot to Google.
The theoretical 'superior convergence' only holds under highly specific, idealized conditions that would never actually survive the chaos of a real-world distributed network.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Top Chef: Privacy Edition.' You’re making a communal stew where 100 people contribute ingredients, but for 'privacy,' no one can know exactly how much salt anyone else added. Usually, the head chef just throws away any handful of salt that looks 'too big' (clipping), which makes the stew taste like sad water. These authors walk into the kitchen claiming they’ve revolutionized cooking with 'Smoothed Normalization.' Their big idea? Instead of throwing away the salt, they gently shake the hand of the person holding it and keep a secret notebook to track how much they think spilled (error-feedback). It sounds brilliant until the judges realize the 'new' technique is just a complicated way of doing what every grandma has done for years, and the 'mathematical proof' that it tastes better only works if the salt is invisible and the pot is infinitely large.

title: Understanding Generalization in Physics Informed Models through Affine Variety Dimensions
abstract:
In recent years, physics-informed machine learning has gained significant attention for its ability to enhance statistical performance and sample efficiency by integrating physical structures into machine learning models. These structures, such as differential equations, conservation laws, and symmetries, serve as inductive biases that can improve the generalization capacity of the hybrid model. However, the mechanisms by which these physical structures enhance generalization capacity are not fully understood, limiting the ability to guarantee the performance of the models. 
In this study, we show that the generalization performance of linear regressors incorporating differential equation structures is determined by the dimension of the associated affine variety, rather than the number of parameters. This finding enables a unified analysis of various equations, including nonlinear ones. We introduce a method to approximate the dimension of the affine variety and provide experimental evidence to validate our theoretical insights.
author: Takeshi Koshizuka;Issei Sato
aff: The University of Tokyo;The University of Tokyo
aff_unique_norm: University of Tokyo

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to explain why adding physics rules to AI makes it smarter by measuring the 'abstract geometric shape' of the rules rather than just counting the number of variables. While they claim this provides a universal yardstick for AI performance, it currently only really works for the math equivalent of a toy tricycle.
why_bad: The theoretical framework is strictly limited to linear regressors, making it largely irrelevant to the complex, non-linear neural networks used in actual physics-informed ML.
The 'unified analysis' for nonlinear equations is less of a rigorous proof and more of a 'trust me, it probably works' based on a sketchy approximation method.
The metric they propose (affine variety dimension) is notoriously difficult to calculate in practice, meaning the 'solution' is more complicated than the original problem.
roast.analogy_name: The Affine Alibi in the First Degree
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where a massive AI model is on trial for 'Gross Overfitting.' The prosecution presents evidence that the model has 175 billion parameters and no common sense. The defense attorney, an eccentric geometry professor, leaps to his feet and shouts, 'Objection! My client's innocence is not determined by his 175 billion personalities, but by the d-dimensional affine variety of his soul!' The professor then proceeds to draw complex 4D shapes on a chalkboard to prove that, geometrically, the crime was impossible. The judge looks at the chalkboard, then at the professor, and asks: 'Does this apply to anything more complex than a toaster?' When the professor admits it only applies to objects with zero moving parts, the judge bangs his gavel. 'Counselor, you aren't defending an AI; you're describing a rock. Case dismissed, and don't come back until you've discovered non-linearity.'

title: LEAD: Large Foundation Model for EEG-Based Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG) provides a non-invasive, highly accessible, and cost-effective solution for Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) detection. However, existing methods, whether based on manual feature extraction or deep learning, face two major challenges: the lack of large-scale datasets for robust feature learning and evaluation, and poor detection performance due to inter-subject variations. To address these challenges, we curate an EEG-AD corpus containing 813 subjects, which forms the world’s largest EEG-AD dataset to the best of our knowledge. Using this unique dataset, we propose **LEAD**, the first large foundation model for EEG-based AD detection. Our method encompasses an entire pipeline, from data selection and preprocessing to self-supervised contrastive pretraining, fine-tuning, and key setups such as subject-independent evaluation and majority voting for subject-level detection. We pre-train the model on 11 EEG datasets (4 AD and 7 non-AD) and unified fine-tune it on 5 AD datasets. Our self-supervised pretraining design includes sample-level and subject-level contrastive learning to extract useful general EEG features. Fine-tuning is performed on 5 channel-aligned datasets together. The backbone encoder incorporates temporal and channel embeddings to capture features across both temporal and spatial dimensions. Our method demonstrates outstanding AD detection performance, achieving up to a 9.86% increase in F1 score at the sample level and up to a 9.31% improvement at the subject level compared to state-of-the-art methods. The results of our model strongly confirm the effectiveness of subject-level contrastive pretraining and channel-aligned multi-dataset fine-tuning for addressing inter-subject variation. The source code is at \url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LEAD-3B51}.
author: Yihe Wang;Nan Huang;Nadia Mammone;Marco Cecchi;Xiang Zhang
aff: University of North Carolina at Charlotte;University of North Carolina at Charlotte;University of Reggio Calabria;Cognision;University of North Carolina at Charlotte

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'universal translator' for brain waves to spot Alzheimer’s, hoping that more data would fix the fact that everyone's brain signals are uniquely messy. While they brag about having the world's largest dataset, in the AI world, their 'massive' collection is still just a drop in the bucket compared to the models that power things like ChatGPT.
why_bad: Scale Inflation: Calling a model trained on 813 people a 'Large Foundation Model' is like calling a lemonade stand a 'Global Fortune 500 Corporation'—it's missing a few zeros to justify the hype.
Incremental Novelty: The 'unique' training techniques are actually standard AI recipes that have been used in computer vision for years, just rebranded for brain waves without much original spice.

The Generalization Gap: The paper claims to solve 'inter-subject variation,' but critics suspect the model is just memorizing the specific quirks of the recording equipment rather than actually understanding Alzheimer’s.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Draft, folks! Today we’re looking at 'LEAD,' a rookie who’s calling himself the next Tom Brady because he dominated five local high school teams. He’s calling his playbook a 'Foundation Strategy,' but let’s be real: he’s running basic slant routes we’ve seen in the pros for a decade! He’s touting a 9% increase in passing accuracy, but he’s playing against a JV defense of only 813 subjects while the real 'Large Models' are out here training against the population of a small country. He says he’s mastered the 'spatial dimensions' of the field, but we suspect he’s just lucky the wind was blowing the same way during every home game. It’s a flashy highlight reel, but this kid isn't ready for the Sunday Night lights just yet. Back to you, Jim!

title: DiffusionVLA: Scaling Robot Foundation Models via Unified Diffusion and Autoregression
author_site: Junjie Wen, Yichen Zhu, Minjie Zhu, Zhibin Tang, Jinming Li, Zhongyi Zhou, Xiaoyu Liu, Chaomin Shen, Yaxin Peng, Feifei Feng
abstract:
In this paper, we present DiffusionVLA, a novel framework that integrates autoregressive reasoning with diffusion policies to address the limitations of existing methods: while autoregressive Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models lack precise and robust action generation, diffusion-based policies inherently lack reasoning capabilities. Central to our approach is autoregressive reasoning — a task decomposition and explanation process enabled by a pre-trained VLM — to guide diffusion-based action policies. To tightly couple reasoning with action generation, we introduce a reasoning injection module that directly embeds self-generated reasoning phrases into the policy learning process. The framework is simple, flexible, and efficient, enabling seamless deployment across diverse robotic platforms.

We conduct extensive experiments using multiple real robots to validate the effectiveness of DiVLA. Our tests include a challenging factory sorting task, where DiVLA successfully categorizes objects, including those not seen during training. The reasoning injection module enhances interpretability, enabling explicit failure diagnosis by visualizing the model’s decision process. Additionally, we test DiVLA on a zero-shot bin-picking task, achieving \textbf{63.7\% accuracy on 102 previously unseen objects}. Our method demonstrates robustness to visual changes, such as distractors and new backgrounds, and easily adapts to new embodiments. Furthermore, DiVLA can follow novel instructions and retain conversational ability. Notably, DiVLA is data-efficient and fast at inference; our smallest DiVLA-2B runs 82Hz on a single A6000 GPU. Finally, we scale the model from 2B to 72B parameters, showcasing improved generalization capabilities with increased model size.
author: Junjie Wen;Yichen Zhu;Minjie Zhu;Zhibin Tang;Jinming Li;Zhongyi Zhou;Xiaoyu Liu;Chaomin Shen;Yaxin Peng;Feifei Feng

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to fix clumsy robots by duct-taping a 'thinking' chat model to a 'doing' movement model so the robot could talk itself through a task. While they claim the robot is now a genius at sorting trash, it's mostly just a very expensive way to achieve a mediocre success rate.
why_bad: The 'reasoning' is largely performative, as there is no proof the movement policy actually listens to the text prompts rather than just reacting to the pixels.
The 63.7% accuracy rate is essentially a coin flip with extra steps, hardly justifying the massive 72B parameter overhead.
The architecture lacks true innovation, simply gluing two pre-existing techniques together without solving the fundamental lag between thinking and acting.
roast.analogy_name: The Michelin-Star Philosopher and the Drunk Sous-Chef
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the kitchen. Today, we’re watching a world-class food critic explain the existential necessity of a perfect sear while a blindfolded toddler actually handles the stove. The authors think that if the critic narrates the recipe loud enough, the toddler will magically stop burning the scallops. It’s a 72-billion-dollar meal where the chef explains the 'why' of the dish perfectly, but the 'how' involves dropping the plate 40% of the time. Bone appétit, if you don't mind the glass shards.

title: Mean-Shift Distillation for Diffusion Mode Seeking
abstract:
We present *mean-shift distillation*, a novel diffusion distillation technique that provides a provably good proxy for the gradient of the diffusion output distribution. This is derived directly from mean-shift mode seeking on the distribution, and we show that its extrema are aligned with the modes. We further derive an efficient product distribution sampling procedure to evaluate the gradient.

Our method is formulated as a drop-in replacement for score distillation sampling (SDS), requiring neither model retraining nor extensive modification of the sampling procedure. We show that it exhibits superior mode alignment as well as improved convergence in both synthetic and practical setups, yielding higher-fidelity results when applied to both text-to-image and text-to-3D applications with Stable Diffusion.
author: Vikas Thamizharasan;Nikitas Chatzis;Iliyan Georgiev;Matthew Fisher;Difan Liu;Nanxuan Zhao;Evangelos Kalogerakis;Michal Lukáč

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix blurry AI images by forcing the model to stop hedging its bets and finally pick the single most likely version of an object. They claim their new math trick finds the 'perfect' version of an image without extra training, but in reality, it just aggressively leans into the most generic, average result possible.
why_bad: The 'provably good' mathematical derivation relies on heuristic assumptions that fall apart the moment you move from simple shapes to actual complex images.
The claimed 'efficiency' of the sampling procedure is largely theoretical, as the computational overhead makes it slower than the industry standards it's trying to replace.
Experimental results are suspiciously cherry-picked, showing 'improved fidelity' that looks suspiciously like high-contrast over-saturation rather than actual detail.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Top Chef: Latent Space.' Our contestant today tried to revolutionize the kitchen by creating a 'Mean-Shift Jus.' Instead of balancing a complex broth, they decided to boil 50 gallons of generic beef stock down to a single, salty teaspoon, claiming it represents the 'purest essence' of flavor. They told the judges this syrup could replace seasoning entirely, but the result is a one-note salt bomb that ruins the texture of the steak. The judges are sending them home because 'distilling the mode' in cooking just means you're serving a plate of bouillon cubes and calling it a gourmet breakthrough.

title: Stochastic Poisson Surface Reconstruction with One Solve using Geometric Gaussian Processes
author_site: Sidhanth Holalkere, David S Bindel, Silvia Sellán, Alexander Terenin
abstract: Poisson Surface Reconstruction is a widely-used algorithm for reconstructing a surface from an oriented point cloud. To facilitate applications where only partial surface information is available, or scanning is performed sequentially, a recent line of work proposes to incorporate uncertainty into the reconstructed surface via Gaussian process models. The resulting algorithms first perform Gaussian process interpolation, then solve a set of volumetric partial differential equations globally in space, resulting in a computationally expensive two-stage procedure. In this work, we apply recently-developed techniques from geometric Gaussian processes to combine interpolation and surface reconstruction into a single stage, requiring only one linear solve per sample. The resulting reconstructed surface samples can be queried locally in space, without the use of problem-dependent volumetric meshes or grids. These capabilities enable one to (a) perform probabilistic collision detection locally around the region of interest, (b) perform ray casting without evaluating points not on the ray's trajectory, and (c) perform next-view planning on a per-ray basis. They also do not requiring one to approximate kernel matrix inverses with diagonal matrices as part of intermediate computations, unlike prior methods. Results show that our approach provides a cleaner, more-principled, and more-flexible stochastic surface reconstruction pipeline.
keywords: stochastic poisson surface reconstruction;geometric gaussian processes;computer graphics
author: Sidhanth Holalkere;David Bindel;Silvia Sellán;Alexander Terenin
aff: Cornell University;Cornell University;Columbia University+Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Cornell University
aff_unique_norm: Cornell University;Columbia University;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to turn messy 3D point clouds into smooth surfaces by using fancy probability math to guess where the 'missing' parts are. Instead of a clunky two-step process that eats up your computer's memory, the authors use a one-step shortcut that lets you zoom in on specific parts of an object without rendering the whole thing at once.
why_bad: The computational cost of Gaussian Processes scales cubically, meaning the math becomes a nightmare the moment you have more than a handful of data points.
The paper claims efficiency but ignores the fact that their 'single solve' is actually a massive, dense matrix inversion that would make a supercomputer sweat.
It fails to provide a convincing comparison against standard Poisson Surface Reconstruction, which is significantly faster and doesn't require a PhD in statistics to implement.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Over-Engineered Eyewitness
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution presents a witness who didn't actually see the crime, but wants to 'statistically infer' the suspect's face based on the vibe of the room. Instead of just drawing a sketch like a normal person—which the defense admits is a 'two-stage process'—this 'expert' insists on a single, 48-hour testimony where they calculate the probability of every photon's trajectory. The witness claims this is 'more principled' and allows the jury to ask about the suspect's left earlobe specifically, but by the time the 'single solve' is finished, the statute of limitations has expired and the courtroom has run out of coffee. Objection: Over-complicated; sustained.

title: Inverse Optimization via Learning Feasible Regions
author_site: Ke Ren, Peyman Mohajerin Esfahani, Angelos Georghiou
abstract: We study inverse optimization (IO), where the goal is to use a parametric optimization program as the hypothesis class to infer relationships between input-decision pairs. Most of the literature focuses on learning only the objective function, as learning the constraint function (i.e., feasible regions) leads to nonconvex training programs. Motivated by this, we focus on learning feasible regions for known linear objectives, and introduce two training losses along with a hypothesis class to parameterize the  constraint function. Our hypothesis class surpasses the previous objective-only method by naturally capturing discontinuous behaviors in input-decision pairs. We introduce a customized block coordinate descent algorithm with a smoothing technique to solve the training problems, while for further restricted hypothesis classes, we reformulate the training optimization as a tractable convex program or mixed integer linear program. Synthetic experiments and two power system applications including comparisons with state-of-the-art approaches showcase and validate the proposed approach.
author: Ke Ren;Peyman Mohajerin Esfahani;Angelos Georghiou
aff: Amazon;University of Toronto+Delft University of Technology;University of Cyprus
aff_unique_norm: Amazon;University of Toronto;Delft University of Technology;University of Cyprus

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to figure out the hidden rules someone is following by looking at the choices they make, assuming we already know what their end goal is. It’s like trying to map out an invisible maze by watching where a mouse bumps its head, then using some very fancy math to smooth out the bruises.
why_bad: The 'known linear objective' assumption is a massive crutch; in the real world, we rarely know exactly what someone is trying to optimize, making this a solution in search of a problem.
The 'customized block coordinate descent' is essentially a glorified game of 'hot or cold' that struggles to reach a global optimum in complex scenarios.
The 'tractable' reformulations are only tractable for tiny, simplified toy models, likely collapsing under the weight of actual big data like a cardboard chair at a powerlifting meet.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a color-blind commentator trying to explain a soccer match where the pitch lines are invisible. They know the players want to score (the 'known objective'), so every time a player stops running, the commentator screams, 'Aha! The touchline must be exactly under his left cleat!' They’re using a 'smoothing technique'—which is basically just squinting until the chaos looks like a strategy—to draw the field. But the moment the game speeds up or a player does something slightly unpredictable, the commentator’s 'tractable model' trips over its own shoelaces. It's a bold attempt to play ball without a rulebook, but the referees are blowing the whistle because the math is out of bounds.

title: OD³: Optimization-free Dataset Distillation for Object Detection
abstract: Training large neural networks on large-scale datasets requires substantial computational resources, particularly for dense prediction tasks such as object detection. Although dataset distillation (DD) has been proposed to alleviate these demands by synthesizing compact datasets from larger ones, most existing work focuses solely on image classification, leaving the more complex detection setting largely unexplored. In this paper, we introduce **OD³**, a novel optimization-free data distillation framework specifically designed for object detection. Our approach involves two stages: first, a candidate selection process in which object instances are iteratively placed in synthesized images based on their optimal locations, and second, a candidate screening process using a pre-trained observer model to remove low-confidence objects. We perform our data synthesis framework on MS COCO and PASCAL VOC, two popular detection datasets, with compression ratios ranging from 0.25% to 5%. Compared to the prior solely existing dataset distillation method on detection and conventional core set selection methods, **OD³** delivers superior accuracy, establishes new state-of-the-art results, surpassing prior best method by more than 14% on MS COCO at a compression ratio of 1.0%. The code is in the supplementary material.
keywords: dataset distillation;object detection;data-centric AI;efficient machine learning
author: Salwa K. Al Khatib;Ahmed Elhagry;Shitong Shao;Zhiqiang Shen
aff: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;
aff_unique_norm: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of training AI on millions of images, the authors tried to cram all the important bits into a tiny 'Greatest Hits' dataset by copy-pasting objects onto new backgrounds. It's essentially an attempt to teach a computer to recognize the world using nothing but a very dense, weirdly organized sticker book.
why_bad: The 'optimization-free' approach is basically a fancy way of saying they used trial-and-error placement instead of actual math, leading to datasets that look like digital junk drawers.
Using a pre-trained 'observer' model to pick the best images is a circular logic trap: you need a smart AI to create the data to train a smart AI, which defeats the purpose of efficient learning.
The method is less about 'distilling' knowledge and more about sophisticated copy-pasting, failing to provide the theoretical depth required for a top-tier machine learning breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where training AI costs more than a private jet, one team dares to do the unthinkable: skip the hard work. [BASS DROP] They didn't want to calculate gradients. They didn't want to optimize. They just wanted to... PASTE. Watch as they take the entire MS COCO dataset and squash it into a tiny folder like a frat boy packing for a weekend in Vegas. They call it 'OD³.' The reviewers call it 'Microsoft Paint on steroids.' This summer, witness the story of an AI that tries to learn what a 'cat' is by looking at a picture of a kitten floating in the middle of a freeway. It’s not 'distillation'—it's a collage from hell.

title: Compositional Generalization via Forced Rendering of Disentangled Latents
author_site: Qiyao Liang, Daoyuan Qian, Liu Ziyin, Ila R. Fiete
abstract: Composition—the ability to generate myriad variations from finite means—is believed to underlie powerful generalization. However, compositional generalization remains a key challenge for deep learning. A widely held assumption is that learning disentangled (factorized) representations naturally supports this kind of extrapolation. Yet, empirical results are mixed, with many generative models failing to recognize and compose factors to generate out-of-distribution (OOD) samples. In this work, we investigate a controlled 2D Gaussian "bump" generation task with fully disentangled $(x,y)$ inputs, demonstrating that standard generative architectures still fail in OOD regions when training with partial data, by re-entangling latent representations  in subsequent layers. By examining the model's learned kernels and manifold geometry, we show that this failure reflects a "memorization" strategy for generation via data superposition rather than via composition of the true factorized features. We show that when models are forced—through architectural modifications with regularization or curated training data—to render the disentangled latents into the full-dimensional representational (pixel) space, they can be highly data-efficient and effective at composing in OOD regions. These findings underscore that disentangled latents in an abstract representation are insufficient and show that if models can represent disentangled factors directly in the output representational space, it can achieve robust compositional generalization.
author: Qiyao Liang;Daoyuan Qian;Liu Ziyin;Ila R Fiete
aff: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;University of Cambridge;Massachusetts Institute of Technology+NTT Research;Massachusetts Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;University of Cambridge;NTT Research

roast.laymen_summary: Just because you give an AI perfectly organized building blocks doesn't mean it won't immediately mash them into a confusing, unfixable sludge. To fix this, the researchers basically had to handcuff the AI and force it to keep its 'peas' and 'carrots' separate all the way to the final plate.
why_bad: The '2D Gaussian bump' task is so simple it’s practically a math joke; solving it doesn't mean you've solved real AI, it means you've solved a dot.
Forcing the model to render latents directly is essentially 'cheating' by hard-coding the answer into the architecture, which defeats the purpose of learning.
The method is about as scalable as a tricycle on a highway—it works for coordinates, but it would explode if you tried to apply it to a cat or a car.
roast.analogy_name: The Toddler and the Bento Box: Requiem for a Salad
roast.analogy_description: In a world where you gave your toddler a perfectly partitioned Bento box—salmon in one square, rice in the other—you expected a gourmet meal. But once the lights go out, the toddler (your neural network) decides that 'compositional generalization' actually means smashing everything into a single, moist sphere of chaos. These researchers tried to save dinner by literally gluing the dividers to the kid’s hands so they couldn't ruin the organization. This summer, witness the high-stakes drama of a child being forced to act like a very expensive, very rigid kitchen utensil, all while the parents claim it's 'revolutionary behavior.'

title: MoHAVE: Mixture of Hierarchical Audio-Visual Experts for Robust Speech Recognition
author_site: Sungnyun Kim, Kangwook Jang, Sangmin Bae, Sungwoo Cho, Se-Young Yun
abstract: Audio-visual speech recognition (AVSR) has become critical for enhancing speech recognition in noisy environments by integrating both auditory and visual modalities. However, existing AVSR systems struggle to scale up without compromising computational efficiency. In this study, we introduce MoHAVE (Mixture of Hierarchical Audio-Visual Experts), a novel robust AVSR framework designed to address these scalability constraints. By leveraging a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, MoHAVE activates modality-specific expert groups, ensuring dynamic adaptation to various audio-visual inputs with minimal computational overhead. Key contributions of MoHAVE include: (1) a sparse MoE framework that efficiently scales AVSR model capacity, (2) a hierarchical gating mechanism that dynamically utilizes the expert groups based on input context, enhancing adaptability and robustness, and (3) remarkable performance across robust AVSR benchmarks, including LRS3 and MuAViC transcription and translation tasks, setting a new standard for scalable speech recognition systems.
author: Sungnyun Kim;Kangwook Jang;Sangmin Bae;Sungwoo Cho;Se-Young Yun
aff: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;Korea Advanced Institute of Technology;Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;KAIST

aff_unique_norm: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: MoHAVE tries to read lips and listen to voices simultaneously by using a 'smart' switchboard that only wakes up specific specialists for each task. It’s essentially a high-tech filter designed to stop the AI from getting overwhelmed by background noise or its own massive size.
why_bad: The 'novel' hierarchical gating is basically a glorified flow chart that adds more complexity and latency than it actually saves in compute.
The benchmarks used, like LRS3, feature perfectly centered talking heads, meaning the model likely chokes the moment a user eats a sandwich or turns their head.
The paper claims efficiency, but the memory overhead of storing dozens of 'experts' makes it a nightmare to deploy on anything smaller than a literal server farm.
roast.analogy_description: In a world screaming with static, one model dared to hire a thousand experts... but only one desk. Witness 'MoHAVE,' the summer’s most over-engineered thriller! See a lip-reading specialist argue with a sound engineer while a 'Hierarchical Manager' spends twenty minutes deciding who gets to speak. It’s a race against time where the AI spends more energy managing its staff than actually hearing what you said. Coming this fall: The experts are ready, the GPU is on fire, and the transcript is still 'Error 404'.

title: SymMaP: Improving Computational Efficiency in Linear Solvers through Symbolic Preconditioning
abstract:
Matrix preconditioning is a critical technique to accelerate the solving of linear systems, where performance heavily depends on the selection of preconditioning parameters.
Traditional parameter selection approaches often define fixed constants for specific scenarios.
However, they rely on domain expertise and fail to consider the instance-wise features for individual problems, limiting their performance. 
In contrast, machine learning (ML) approaches, though promising, are hindered by high inference costs and limited interpretability. 
To combine the strengths of both approaches, we propose a symbolic discovery framework—namely, **Sym**bolic **Ma**trix **P**reconditioning (**SymMaP**)—to learn efficient symbolic expressions for preconditioning parameters. 
Specifically, we employ a large neural network to search the high-dimensional discrete space for expressions that can accurately predict the optimal parameters. 
The learned expression allows for high inference efficiency and excellent interpretability (expressed in concise symbolic formulas), making it simple and reliable for deployment. 
Experimental results show that SymMaP consistently outperforms traditional strategies across various benchmarks.
author: Hong Wang;Jie Wang;Minghao Ma;Haoran Shao;Haoyang Liu
aff: University of Science and Technology of China;University of Science and Technology of China;University of Science and Technology of China;University of Science and Technology of China;University of Science and Technology of China
position: PhD student;Full Professor;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;MS student
aff_unique_norm: University of Science and Technology of China

roast.laymen_summary: The authors used a massive, expensive AI to hunt for tiny math formulas that help computers solve big equations slightly faster. It's essentially hiring a NASA engineering team to design a slightly better paperclip and claiming you've revolutionized the office supply industry.
why_bad: The 'Search Tax' Paradox: The authors claim efficiency, but the computational cost of using a massive neural network to find a 'simple' formula is often higher than just solving the original problem using standard methods.

The Generalization Gap: While the formulas look 'interpretable,' they are essentially overfitted artifacts that crumble the moment they encounter data that wasn't in the specific, narrow training set.

Reinventing the Wheel with a Jet Engine: Using high-dimensional symbolic search to find parameters that domain experts have already optimized via calculus is a classic case of 'AI for AI's sake' without adding actual mathematical value.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re tired of squeezing lemons by hand (Traditional Solvers), and the industrial press is too bulky (Standard ML). So, we built a $10,000 smart-press that uses a cloud-connected neural network to analyze the lemon's 'vibe' and calculate the perfect 3D-printed plastic insert to squeeze it. It’s 'interpretable' because at the end, it prints out a little card that says 'Press Harder.' Does it work? Sure, on these three specific lemons we bought in Palo Alto. Is it faster? Well, if you ignore the 45 minutes it took to 'search' for the plastic insert, you saved exactly half a second on the juice. We’re currently pre-seed, looking for investors who don't understand how lemons work.

title: Exploiting Presentative Feature Distributions for Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning of Large Language Models
author_site: Xin Cheng, Jiabo Ye, Haiyang Xu, Ming Yan, Ji Zhang, Feng Liu, Fei Huang, Lei Feng
abstract: Endowing large language models (LLMs) with continual learning (CL) capacities is practically important, which enables them to dynamically acquire new knowledge over time. Although many effective methods have been proposed for CL of LLMs, they did not consider online scenarios, thereby sharing a common problem: information leakage (IL), where the task-related information of learned tasks is accessed or reused again. IL not only imposes potential risks on data privacy protection but also significantly hinders the deployment of LLMs in real-world scenarios. To avoid IL while maintaining outstanding CL performance, we propose a novel CL method for LLMs, which first characterizes a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) block by a presentative feature distribution, and then dynamically selects the appropriate PEFT blocks for each instance based on its similarity with the presentative feature distributions. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our method on the CL of LLM, showcasing its potential to enhance both privacy and adaptability in practical applications.
author: Xin Cheng;Jiabo Ye;Haiyang Xu;Ming Yan;Ji Zhang;Feng Liu;Fei Huang;Lei Feng
aff: Chongqing University;;Alibaba Group;Alibaba Group;Alibaba Group;University of Melbourne;Alibaba Group US;Southeast University
position: MS student;;Researcher;Instructor;Senior Staff Engineer;Assistant Professor;Senior Research Director;Full Professor
aff_unique_norm: Chongqing University;;Alibaba Group;University of Melbourne;Southeast University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to stop AI from getting its wires crossed when learning new tasks by creating a 'security guard' that decides which specific brain-patch to use for every new question. Essentially, we’re claiming that 'remembering things' is a privacy bug, so we built a fancy filing system to keep your old math homework away from your new poetry assignments.
why_bad: The 'Information Leakage' problem is largely a strawman; the authors confuse basic model generalization with a privacy violation to make their work seem more urgent.
'Presentative feature distribution' is just a high-calorie term for a simple similarity check, making a basic routing mechanism sound like a breakthrough in statistical physics.
The method adds significant computational overhead by forcing the model to play 'Guess Who?' with its own internal modules before answering every single prompt.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Gridiron of Generalization! The coach is terrified that if the QB remembers the playbook from last week, the defense might read his mind—total information leakage! So, the strategy today? Every single play, they’re swapping out the entire offensive line based on the 'vibe' of the turf. 'Oh, the grass looks a bit damp? Bring in the Mud-Specialist PEFT block!' It’s a bold move, folks, but while they’re busy checking the 'feature distribution' of the Gatorade, the play clock has expired and the actual AI hasn't moved the ball an inch. They’re playing 4D chess against a problem that doesn't exist while the fans are just wondering why the model is suddenly buffering!

title: Understanding the Logic of Direct Preference Alignment through Logic
author_site: Kyle Richardson, Vivek Srikumar, Ashish Sabharwal
abstract: Recent direct preference alignment algorithms (DPA), such as DPO, have shown great promise in aligning large language models to human preferences. While this has motivated the development of many new variants of the original DPO loss, understanding the differences between these recent proposals, as well as developing new DPA loss functions, remains difficult given the lack of a technical and conceptual framework for reasoning about the underlying semantics of these algorithms. In this paper, we attempt to remedy this by formalizing DPA losses in terms of discrete reasoning problems. Specifically, we ask: Given an existing DPA loss, can we systematically derive a symbolic program that characterizes its semantics? We propose a novel formalism for characterizing preference losses for single model and reference model based approaches, and identify symbolic forms for a number of commonly used DPA variants. Further, we show how this formal view of preference learning sheds new light on both the size and structure of the DPA loss landscape, making it possible to not only rigorously characterize the relationships between recent loss proposals but also to systematically explore the landscape and derive new loss functions from first principles. We hope our framework and findings will help provide useful guidance to those working on human AI alignment.
author: Kyle Richardson;Vivek Srikumar;Ashish Sabharwal
aff: Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence;University of Utah;Allen Institute for AI
aff_unique_norm: Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence;University of Utah;Allen Institute for AI

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to turn the math formulas used to train AI into a rigid set of logic puzzles to see if they could 'calculate' the perfect way to make models behave. Essentially, they built a complex dictionary to translate simple AI training tricks into high-brow symbolic code, hoping to find a secret recipe for better alignment.
why_bad: Extreme Over-Engineering: You've taken a straightforward mathematical loss function and dressed it up in formal logic like a toddler in a tuxedo—it looks fancy, but it doesn't actually help the AI learn any better.

The 'So What?' Problem: The paper spends 20 pages building a massive 'conceptual framework' to describe existing tools, but fails to provide a single empirical result showing that their 'newly derived' formulas actually outperform the old ones.

Tautological Reasoning: The title literally promises to explain logic through logic; it’s a circular academic exercise that describes the 'what' in excruciating detail while completely ignoring the 'how' of real-world implementation.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to *Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition*. Today, our 'chef' isn't actually cooking; they're presenting a 50-page technical manual on the 'Boolean Logic of Salsa Viscosity.' Instead of just heating up the burrito—which represents the AI—and checking if it tastes good, they've spent the entire afternoon creating a 'Symbolic Framework' to prove that a bean is, logically speaking, a legume. They claim they can 'derive new recipes from first principles,' but when the judges asked for a sample, the chef just handed them a flowchart. You can’t eat a flowchart, Gordon! It’s all garnish, no protein, and the 'landscape' they’re exploring is just a messy kitchen floor covered in abstract math.

title: One Example Shown, Many Concepts Known! Counterexample-Driven Conceptual Reasoning in Mathematical LLMs
author_site: Yinghui Li, Jiayi Kuang, Haojing Huang, Zhikun Xu, Xinnian Liang, Yi Yu, Wenlian Lu, Yangning Li, Xiaoyu Tan, Chao Qu, Ying Shen, Hai-Tao Zheng, Philip Yu
abstract: Leveraging mathematical Large Language Models (LLMs) for proof generation is a fundamental topic in LLMs research. We argue that the ability of current LLMs to prove statements largely depends on whether they have encountered the relevant proof process during training. This reliance limits their deeper understanding of mathematical theorems and related concepts. Inspired by the pedagogical method of "proof by counterexamples" commonly used in human mathematics education, our work aims to enhance LLMs’ ability to conduct mathematical reasoning and proof through counterexamples. Specifically, we manually create a high-quality, university-level mathematical benchmark, COUNTERMATH, which requires LLMs to prove mathematical statements by providing counterexamples, thereby assessing their grasp of mathematical concepts. Additionally, we develop a data engineering framework to automatically obtain training data for further model improvement. Extensive experiments and detailed analyses demonstrate that COUNTERMATH is challenging, indicating that LLMs, such as OpenAI o1, have insufficient counterexample-driven proof capabilities. Moreover, our exploration into model training reveals that strengthening LLMs' counterexample-driven conceptual reasoning abilities is crucial for improving their overall mathematical capabilities. We believe that our work offers new perspectives on the community of mathematical LLMs.
author: Yinghui Li;Jiayi Kuang;Haojing Huang;Zhikun Xu;Xinnian Liang;Yi Yu;Wenlian Lu;Yangning Li;Xiaoyu Tan;Chao Qu;Ying Shen;Hai-Tao Zheng;Philip S. Yu
