roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that AI models are basically high-achieving parrots that memorize math proofs rather than understanding them. They built a new test to see if models can find 'counterexamples' to prove why a statement is wrong, proving that even the smartest AIs are currently failing at basic logical troubleshooting.
why_bad: The 'manually created' dataset is likely too small to be statistically significant, making it less of a 'benchmark' and more of a 'curated list of gotchas.'
The proposed fix is just more 'data engineering,' which is fancy talk for feeding the model the answers it missed—essentially curing memorization with more memorization.
The paper claims to measure 'conceptual understanding,' a vague term that reviewers hate because it lacks a rigorous mathematical definition and borders on AI psychobabble.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a chef who can cook a perfect Beef Wellington only because he memorized a YouTube video, but has no idea why meat turns brown. The researchers are the judges who suddenly ask the chef to make the same dish *without* using heat, just to see if he understands chemistry. When the chef freezes up, the judges claim they've discovered a 'new perspective' on cooking. Their 'revolutionary solution' to fix the chef? Giving him a second YouTube video specifically about not using heat. It’s not culinary genius; it's just teaching the parrot a different song.

title: An Augmentation-Aware Theory for Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning
author_site: Jingyi Cui, Hongwei Wen, Yisen Wang
abstract: Self-supervised contrastive learning has emerged as a powerful tool in machine learning and computer vision to learn meaningful representations from unlabeled data. Meanwhile, its empirical success has encouraged many theoretical studies to reveal the learning mechanisms. However, in the existing theoretical research, the role of data augmentation is still under-exploited, especially the effects of specific augmentation types. To fill in the blank, we for the first time propose an augmentation-aware error bound for self-supervised contrastive learning, showing that the supervised risk is bounded not only by the unsupervised risk, but also explicitly by a trade-off induced by data augmentation. Then, under a novel semantic label assumption, we discuss how certain augmentation methods affect the error bound. Lastly, we conduct both pixel- and representation-level experiments to verify our proposed theoretical results.
author: Jingyi Cui;Hongwei Wen;Yisen Wang
aff: Peking University;University of Twente;Peking University
aff_unique_norm: Peking University;University of Twente

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to write a mathematical 'recipe' explaining why showing an AI slightly tweaked versions of the same photo helps it learn without being told what’s in the picture. They essentially tried to prove that there's a sweet spot between 'too little change' and 'too much change,' but the math ended up being more of a 'duh' moment than a breakthrough.
why_bad: The 'semantic label assumption' is a massive logical leap that basically assumes the AI already knows the answer it’s supposed to be learning.
The mathematical error bounds are so loose they are practically useless; it's like a weather forecast predicting a temperature between -50 and 150 degrees.
The experiments are performed on 'toy' datasets that don't reflect how these massive AI models actually function in the real world.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Top Chef: Abstract Algebra Edition.' Our contestant today didn't actually cook a meal; instead, they presented a 50-page proof titled 'A Theory of Sautéing.' They argued that if we assume the onions are perfect spheres and the pan is infinitely hot, the onions will eventually turn brown. When the judges asked to taste the dish, the chef pointed to a single, microscopic piece of charred onion and claimed it 'verified the theoretical framework.' It’s a lot of scientific-sounding jargon to explain that heat makes things cook, and frankly, the judges are still hungry and the kitchen is a mess.

title: Convergence Analysis of Policy Gradient Methods with Dynamic Stochasticity
author_site: Alessandro Montenegro, Marco Mussi, Matteo Papini, Alberto Maria Metelli
abstract:
*Policy gradient* (PG) methods are effective *reinforcement learning* (RL) approaches, particularly for continuous problems. While they optimize stochastic (hyper)policies via action- or parameter-space exploration, real-world applications often require deterministic policies. Existing PG convergence guarantees to deterministic policies assume a fixed stochasticity in the (hyper)policy, tuned according to the desired final suboptimality, whereas practitioners commonly use a dynamic stochasticity level.
This work provides the theoretical foundations for this practice. We introduce PES, a phase-based method that reduces stochasticity via a deterministic schedule while running PG subroutines with fixed stochasticity in each phase. Under gradient domination assumptions, PES achieves last-iterate convergence to the optimal deterministic policy with a sample complexity of order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\epsilon^{-5})$.
Additionally, we analyze the common practice, termed SL-PG, of jointly learning stochasticity (via an appropriate parameterization) and (hyper)policy parameters. We show that SL-PG also ensures last-iterate convergence with a rate $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\epsilon^{-3})$, but to the optimal stochastic (hyper)policy only, requiring stronger assumptions compared to PES.
author: Alessandro Montenegro;Marco Mussi;Matteo Papini;Alberto Maria Metelli
aff: Politecnico di Milano;Politecnico di Milano;Polytechnic Institute of Milan;Politecnico di Milano
aff_unique_norm: Politecnico di Milano;Polytechnic Institute of Milan

roast.laymen_summary: In an attempt to explain why AI researchers slowly dial down a robot's randomness to make it more decisive, this paper offers two mathematical recipes that are either painfully slow or don't actually finish the job. It’s basically a formal proof that the 'pro tricks' used in the real world work better than these overly cautious theoretical alternatives.
why_bad: The sample complexity of O(ε⁻⁵) is effectively 'geologic time'—the sun would burn out before this algorithm actually converges on a decent policy.
The 'gradient domination' assumption is a massive theoretical crutch that assumes the optimization landscape is a smooth, easy-to-climb hill, which almost never happens in messy real-world RL.

The analysis of SL-PG (what practitioners actually do) ends up proving it fails to reach the primary goal: it settles for a 'fuzzy' stochastic policy instead of the deterministic one promised in the introduction.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: RL Edition.' Our chef wants to bake a perfectly firm, deterministic soufflé. Their first technique, PES, involves opening the oven every five seconds to turn the heat down by exactly 0.1 degrees while staring at a stopwatch. It technically produces a soufflé, but it takes 400 years to bake—your guests are now fossils. Their second technique, SL-PG, lets the eggs decide the temperature. It’s much faster, but instead of a firm dessert, you end up with a lukewarm, runny custard that can't decide if it's liquid or solid. It's 'technically' a dish, but Gordon Ramsay is already throwing it in the bin because it’s not what we ordered.
roast.tone_check: Lean, snarky, and scientifically grounded.

title: From Theory to Practice: Rethinking Green and Martin Kernels for Unleashing Graph Transformers
author_site: Yoon Hyeok Lee, Jaemin Park, Taejin Paik, Doyun Kim, Bosun Hwang
abstract: Graph Transformers (GTs) have emerged as a powerful alternative to message-passing neural networks, yet their performance heavily depends on effectively embedding structural inductive biases. In this work, we introduce novel structural encodings (SEs) grounded in a rigorous analysis of random walks (RWs), leveraging Green and Martin kernels that we have carefully redefined for AI applications while preserving their mathematical essence.These kernels capture the long-term behavior of RWs on graphs and allow for enhanced representation of complex topologies, including non-aperiodic and directed acyclic substructures.Empirical evaluations across eight benchmark datasets demonstrate strong performance across diverse tasks, notably in molecular and circuit domains.We attribute this performance boost to the improved ability of our kernel-based SEs to encode intricate structural information, thereby strengthening the global attention and inductive bias within GTs.This work highlights the effectiveness of theoretically grounded kernel methods in advancing Transformer-based models for graph learning.
keywords: Graph Transformers;Graph Neural Networks;Structural Encodings;Green Kernel;Martin Kernel;Non-aperiodic substructures;DAGs
author: Yoon Hyeok Lee;Jaemin Park;Taejin Paik;Doyun Kim;Bosun Hwang
aff: Samsung Electronics;Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology;;Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology;Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Samsung;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix AI's 'map-reading' problem by using hyper-complex, 1950s-era physics math to describe how data points wander around a network. While it sounds incredibly smart on paper, it's basically the equivalent of using a GPS designed for deep-space navigation to find the milk aisle in a grocery store.
why_bad: Theoretical Over-Engineering: The 'Green and Martin kernels' are fancy math for infinite spaces, but applying them to small, finite graphs is like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut—and missing the nut.

The Computational Tax: The method is so mathematically dense that it likely turns the GPU into a space heater, offering 'state-of-the-art' results only if you have an infinite budget and zero respect for clock time.
Incremental Gains: Despite the heavy-duty theory, the actual performance boost on benchmarks is so small it’s practically indistinguishable from statistical noise or a lucky seed.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where breakfast is simple, one team of scientists refuses to just push a lever. Coming this fall: Witness the 'Green-Martin 3000,' a toaster powered by a localized black hole and fueled by quantum probability. It promises the most mathematically 'correct' crust in the universe, but there's a catch—it takes six days to preheat, and the toast tastes exactly like the 20-dollar version from Walmart. They didn't just reinvent the wheel; they turned it into a 12-dimensional hyper-sphere that no one can actually drive.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 99% confidence intervals.

title: Scaling Laws for Upcycling Mixture-of-Experts Language Models
author_site: Seng Pei Liew, Takuya Kato, Sho Takase
abstract:
Pretraining large language models (LLMs) is resource-intensive, often requiring months of training time even with high-end GPU clusters.
 There are two approaches of mitigating such computational demands: reusing smaller models to train larger ones (upcycling), and training computationally efficient models like mixture-of-experts (MoE).
 In this paper, we study the upcycling of LLMs to MoE models, of which the scaling behavior remains underexplored.
 Through extensive experiments, we identify empirical scaling laws that describe how performance depends on dataset size and model configuration.
 Particularly, we show that, while scaling these factors improves performance, there is a novel interaction term between the dense and upcycled training dataset that limits the efficiency of upcycling at large computational budgets.  
 Based on these findings, we provide guidance to scale upcycling, and establish conditions under which upcycling outperforms from-scratch trainings within budget constraints.
author: Seng Pei Liew;Takuya Kato;Sho Takase
aff: SB Intuitions+LY Corporation;;LINE Corporation
aff_unique_norm: SB Intuitions;LY Corporation;;LINE Corporation

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a massive AI by Frankenstein-ing a smaller, pre-trained model into a complex 'Mixture-of-Experts' setup to save on electricity bills. It turns out that while this shortcut works for a while, eventually the old data and the new data start a fistfight, making the final model dumber than if they had just started from scratch.
why_bad: The 'novel interaction term' is essentially just a rebranding of catastrophic forgetting, masquerading as a new mathematical discovery.
The paper fails to acknowledge that the 'efficiency gains' vanish as soon as you scale past a certain point, making the method useless for actual state-of-the-art production.
The scaling laws provided are based on a very narrow range of hyperparameters, meaning they likely fall apart the moment you change the architecture or the dataset slightly.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Billion-Dollar Bistro,' where today our chefs tried to make a 5-tier wedding cake using nothing but half-eaten cupcakes from the dumpster. They call it 'upcycling,' claiming that if you just blend the old cupcakes into a giant MoE—'Medley of Expireds'—batter, you’ll save hours at the oven. But here’s the catch: the more layers they add, the more the stale cupcake flavor 'interacts' with the fresh frosting, creating a soggy mess that even a hungry raccoon would reject. It’s technically a bigger cake, but after a certain size, you’re spending more on sprinkles to hide the funk than you would have spent just buying fresh flour and starting over.

title: Janus: Dual-Server Multi-Round Secure Aggregation with Verifiability for Federated Learning
author_site: Lang Pu, Jingjing Gu, Chao Lin, Xinyi Huang
abstract: Secure Aggregation (SA) is a cornerstone of Federated Learning (FL), ensuring that user updates remain hidden from servers. The advanced Flamingo (S\&P'23) has realized multi-round aggregation and improved efficiency. However, it still faces several key challenges: scalability issues with dynamic user participation, a lack of verifiability for server-side aggregation results, and vulnerability to Model Inconsistency Attacks (MIA) caused by a malicious server distributing inconsistent models. To address these issues, we propose $\textit{Janus}$, a generic SA scheme based on dual-server architecture. Janus ensures security against up to $n-2$ colluding clients (where $n$ is the total client count), which prevents privacy breaches for non-colluders. Additionally, Janus is model-independent, ensuring applicability across any FL model without specific adaptations. Furthermore, Janus introduces a new cryptographic primitive, Separable Homomorphic Commitment, which enables clients to efficiently verify the correctness of aggregation. Finally, extensive experiments show that Janus not only significantly enhances security but also reduces per-client communication and computation overhead from logarithmic to constant scale, with a tolerable impact on model performance.
author: Lang Pu;Jingjing Gu;Chao Lin;Xinyi Huang
aff: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics;;Fujian Normal University;Jinan University+Jinan University
aff_unique_norm: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics;;Fujian Normal University;Jinan University

roast.laymen_summary: Janus is a privacy shield for AI training that uses two separate servers to make sure your personal data stays hidden even if the servers are secretly trying to cheat. It adds a high-tech 'receipt' system so your phone can verify the servers actually did the math correctly without needing to see the raw data.
why_bad: The 'Dual-Server' requirement is a massive practical red flag; assuming you can find two independent, powerful entities that will never, ever whisper to each other is a security fairy tale.
The claim of 'constant' communication overhead likely hides a massive, one-time 'setup' cost that would make a mobile phone's battery cry for mercy in a real-world scenario.

The 'Separable Homomorphic Commitment' is basically a cryptographic Rube Goldberg machine—it's mathematically clever but adds so much complexity that the 'tolerable impact' on performance is likely a polite euphemism for 'painfully slow'.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You want to calculate the average weight of your friend group without anyone admitting they've gained ten pounds. Janus is like hiring two different accountants who promise they’ve never met. You give each a shredded half of your weight slip. They do a complex interpretive dance to prove they aren't lying about the total, while you stand there with a stopwatch. It’s a brilliant 'Disruptive Privacy™' solution, until you realize you're paying two professional salaries for a basic math problem, and if the accountants ever share a cab, your diet secrets are toast. We’re scaling! (As long as the servers don't talk and your battery is plugged into a nuclear reactor.)

title: Accelerating PDE-Constrained Optimization by the Derivative of Neural Operators
author_site: Ze Cheng, Zhuoyu Li, Wang Xiaoqiang, Jianing Huang, Zhizhou Zhang, Zhongkai Hao, Hang Su
abstract:
PDE-Constrained Optimization (PDECO) problems can be accelerated significantly by employing gradient-based methods with surrogate models like neural operators compared to traditional numerical solvers. 
However, this approach faces two key challenges:
(1) **Data inefficiency**: Lack of efficient data sampling and effective training for neural operators, particularly for optimization purpose.
(2) **Instability**: High risk of optimization derailment due to inaccurate neural operator predictions and gradients.
To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework: (1) **Optimization-oriented training**: we leverage data from full steps of traditional optimization algorithms and employ a specialized training method for neural operators. (2) **Enhanced derivative learning**: We introduce a **Virtual-Fourier** layer to enhance derivative learning within the neural operator, a crucial aspect for gradient-based optimization. (3) **Hybrid optimization**: We implement a hybrid approach that integrates neural operators with numerical solvers, providing robust regularization for the optimization process.
Our extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our model in accurately learning operators and their derivatives. Furthermore, our hybrid optimization approach exhibits robust convergence.
author: Ze Cheng;Zhuoyu Li;Wang Xiaoqiang;Jianing Huang;Zhizhou Zhang;Zhongkai Hao;Hang Su
aff: Bosch Artificial Intelligence Center;Imperial College London;;Bosch;Bosch;;
aff_unique_norm: Bosch;Imperial College London;;Robert Bosch GmbH

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to use AI to speed up complex physics calculations, but because the AI kept 'hallucinating' the math, they had to build a complex safety harness to keep it on the rails. They essentially created a high-speed shortcut that requires so much babysitting from the original slow method that it barely qualifies as a shortcut.
why_bad: The 'Virtual-Fourier' layer is essentially a rebranding of existing techniques that fails to provide a rigorous mathematical advantage over standard Fourier Neural Operators.
The 'Hybrid' approach is a computational paradox; by requiring a traditional solver to constantly stabilize the AI, the supposed speed gains are largely cannibalized by overhead.
The data inefficiency problem isn't actually solved, as the model requires a massive amount of expensive 'gold-standard' simulation data just to learn how to poorly mimic those same simulations.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Physics 500! We’ve got a rookie team today trying to win with a 'Neural Navigator.' They’ve replaced the steering wheel with a predictive AI that guesses where the track is based on some polaroids they took yesterday! But wait—the AI keeps trying to drive into the popcorn stand, so the team has implemented a 'Hybrid Safety System,' which is just a professional driver leaning out the window and grabbing the tires manually whenever things get hairy. They're screaming about 'enhanced derivative learning' while their car is doing 360s in the grass, and the 'optimization-oriented training' looks a lot like just copying the leader's homework and getting the answers slightly wrong. It’s a bold strategy, Jim—let’s see if they can finish the race before the AI decides the finish line is actually a metaphysical construct!

title: SEMU: Singular Value Decomposition for Efficient Machine Unlearning
author_site: Marcin Sendera, Łukasz Struski, Kamil Książek, Kryspin Musiol, Jacek Tabor, Dawid Rymarczyk
abstract:
While the capabilities of generative foundational models have advanced rapidly in recent years, methods to prevent harmful and unsafe behaviors remain underdeveloped. Among the pressing challenges in AI safety, machine unlearning (MU) has become increasingly critical to meet upcoming safety regulations. Most existing MU approaches focus on altering the most significant parameters of the model. However, these methods often require fine-tuning substantial portions of the model, resulting in high computational costs and training instabilities, which are typically mitigated by access to the original training dataset.

In this work, we address these limitations by leveraging Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to create a compact, low-dimensional projection that enables the selective forgetting of specific data points. We propose Singular Value Decomposition for Efficient Machine Unlearning (SEMU), a novel approach designed to optimize MU in two key aspects. First, SEMU minimizes the number of model parameters that need to be modified, effectively removing unwanted knowledge while making only minimal changes to the model's weights. Second, SEMU eliminates the dependency on the original training dataset, preserving the model's previously acquired knowledge without additional data requirements.

Extensive experiments demonstrate that SEMU achieves competitive performance while significantly improving efficiency in terms of both data usage and the number of modified parameters.
author: Marcin Sendera;Łukasz Struski;Kamil Książek;Kryspin Musiol;Jacek Tabor;Dawid Damian Rymarczyk
aff: Mila - Quebec AI Institute+Jagiellonian University;Jagiellonian University in Krakow+Jagiellonian University in Krakow;; Institute of Theoretical and Applied Informatics;Jagiellonian University;Jagiellonian University
position: Intern+PhD student;Associate Professor+Assistant Professor;;Researcher;Full Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: Quebec AI Institute;Jagiellonian University;;Institute of Theoretical and Applied Informatics

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to give AI models a targeted lobotomy so they’d forget specific 'bad' memories without needing to re-read their entire education history. By using a math shortcut called SVD, they claimed they could delete problematic info by tweaking just a few digital knobs, but the reviewers basically told them their 'surgical' approach was more like using a sledgehammer in the dark.
why_bad: The 'unlearning' was purely superficial; the researchers didn't prove the model actually forgot the data rather than just making it slightly harder to trigger.
The method suffered from 'collateral damage' where the model became dumber at general tasks while trying to forget specific ones.

The evaluation was performed on toy datasets that don't reflect the complexity of modern LLMs, making the 'efficiency' claims feel like bragging about finishing a marathon that was actually a 100-meter dash.
roast.analogy_name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Spreadsheet
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where your AI knows your embarrassing browser history and refuses to let it go. ONE TEAM of researchers thinks they've found the ultimate 'Delete' key. They aren't going to retrain the brain; they're going to use Singular Value Decomposition to perform a cut-rate memory wipe! WATCH as they try to snip out the bad parts without turning the hero into a vegetable! HEAR the reviewers scream as the AI forgets how to speak English while trying to forget a single Wikipedia entry! It’s a high-stakes heist where the vault is a matrix, the tool is a linear algebra textbook, and the result is a total system crash. Coming this fall: SEMU—The Unlearning that forgot to work.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimized; academic ego sufficiently bruised.

title: Lego Sketch: A Scalable Memory-augmented Neural Network for Sketching Data Streams
author_site: Yuan Feng, Yukun Cao, Hairu Wang, Xike Xie, S Kevin Zhou
abstract:
Sketches, probabilistic structures for estimating item frequencies in infinite data streams with limited space, are widely used across various domains. Recent studies have shifted the focus from handcrafted sketches to neural sketches, leveraging memory-augmented neural networks (MANNs) to enhance the streaming compression capabilities and achieve better space-accuracy trade-offs.
However, existing neural sketches struggle to scale across different data domains and space budgets due to inflexible MANN configurations. In this paper, we introduce a scalable MANN architecture that brings to life the Lego sketch, a novel sketch with superior scalability and accuracy.
Much like assembling creations with modular Lego bricks, the Lego sketch dynamically coordinates multiple memory bricks to adapt to various space budgets and diverse data domains.
Theoretical analysis and empirical studies demonstrate its scalability and superior space-accuracy trade-offs, outperforming existing handcrafted and neural sketches.
keywords: Data Streams; Memory Augmented Neural Networks; Data Compression
author: Yuan Feng;Yukun Cao;Hairu Wang;Xike Xie;S Kevin Zhou
aff: University of Science and Technology of China;University of Science and Technology of China;University of Science and Technology of China;;University of Science and Technology of China
aff_unique_norm: University of Science and Technology of China;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a fancy AI 'Lego' set that helps computers remember the most important parts of a massive, never-ending firehose of data. While they claim it's modular and smart, it’s essentially trying to use a supercomputer to do a job that a simple calculator has been doing perfectly well for decades.
why_bad: Computational Masochism: The 'Lego' bricks add so much neural network overhead that the time saved on data compression is immediately lost to the sheer processing power required to move the bricks around.

The 'Modular' Marketing Myth: Labeling a standard memory-augmented network as 'Lego-like' is a thin aesthetic coat of paint on existing architectures that doesn't actually solve the underlying 'neural networks are slow' problem.

Theoretical Hand-Waving: The paper claims superior 'space-accuracy trade-offs' but conveniently ignores the massive 'training-time debt' required to make the model functional in a real-world streaming environment.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Platinum-Plated Pencil Sharpener
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution rests its case on one undeniable fact: the defendant attempted to sell you a 'Modular Graphite Reduction System' when all you asked for was a way to sharpen a pencil. They claim these 'Lego' components allow for 'unprecedented scalability,' but look at the blueprints! To sharpen one HB pencil, this machine requires a diesel generator, a team of three engineers, and a modular hydraulic press. Your Honor, the defense argues that it's 'state-of-the-art,' but in this courtroom of efficiency, we call it 'Grand Larceny of the RAM.' You cannot claim to save space while your 'efficient' model requires more power than the data stream it’s supposedly compressing. I move for a directed verdict: Guilty of Over-Engineering in the First Degree!

title: Sampling from Binary Quadratic Distributions via Stochastic Localization
author_site: Chenguang Wang, Kaiyuan Cui, Weichen Zhao, Tianshu Yu
abstract:
Sampling from binary quadratic distributions (BQDs) is a fundamental but challenging problem in discrete optimization and probabilistic inference. Previous work established theoretical guarantees for stochastic localization (SL) in continuous domains, where MCMC methods efficiently estimate the required posterior expectations during SL iterations. However, achieving similar convergence guarantees for discrete MCMC samplers in posterior estimation presents unique theoretical challenges. 
In this work, we present the first application of SL to general BQDs, proving that after a certain number of iterations, the external field of posterior distributions constructed by SL tends to infinity almost everywhere, hence satisfy Poincaré inequalities with probability near to 1, leading to polynomial-time mixing. This theoretical breakthrough enables efficient sampling from general BQDs, even those that may not originally possess fast mixing properties. Furthermore, our analysis, covering enormous discrete MCMC samplers based on Glauber dynamics and Metropolis-Hastings algorithms, demonstrates the broad applicability of our theoretical framework.
Experiments on instances with quadratic unconstrained binary objectives, including maximum independent set, maximum cut, and maximum clique problems, demonstrate consistent improvements in sampling efficiency across different discrete MCMC samplers.
keywords: Stochastic Localization;Discrete MCMC samplers;Binary Quadratic Distribution
author: Chenguang Wang;Kaiyuan Cui;Weichen Zhao;Tianshu Yu
aff: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen;Academy of Mathematics and Systems Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences;Nankai University;Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen)
aff_unique_norm: Chinese University of Hong Kong;Chinese Academy of Sciences;Nankai University

title: The Hidden Joules: Evaluating the Energy Consumption of Vision Backbones for Progress Towards More Efficient Model Inference
author_site: Zeyu Yang, Wesley Armour
abstract: Deep learning has achieved significant success but poses increasing concerns about energy consumption and sustainability. Despite these concerns, there is a lack of understanding of their energy efficiency during inference. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the inference energy consumption of 1,200 ImageNet classification models—the largest evaluation of its kind to date. Our findings reveal a steep decline in accuracy gains relative to the increase in energy usage, highlighting sustainability concerns in the pursuit of marginal improvements. We identify key factors contributing to energy consumption and demonstrate methods to improve energy efficiency. To promote more sustainable AI practices, we introduce an energy efficiency scoring system and develop an interactive web application that allows users to compare models based on accuracy and energy consumption. By providing extensive empirical data and practical tools, we aim to facilitate informed decision-making and encourage collaborative efforts in the development of energy-efficient AI technologies.
keywords: Energy Efficiency;Green AI;Efficient and Scalable Vision;Sustainability
author: Zeyu Yang;Wesley Armour
aff: University of Oxford;University of Oxford
aff_unique_norm: University of Oxford

roast.laymen_summary: We ran 1,200 different AI models and confirmed that we are burning massive amounts of electricity for tiny, negligible improvements in accuracy. To fix this, we built a 'shame-o-meter' website so developers can see exactly how much they're killing the planet for that extra 0.1% performance boost.
why_bad: The paper is essentially an oversized Excel spreadsheet; measuring things that already exist isn't a technical contribution, it's just a very expensive utility bill.
The 'scoring system' is largely arbitrary and fails to account for the fact that energy efficiency varies wildly depending on whether you're running on a $40,000 H100 or a literal toaster.
The conclusion that 'bigger models use more power' is a 'water is wet' observation that offers zero new architectural insights for actually solving the problem.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup pitching a modified M1 Abrams tank specifically for picking up a single organic avocado from the store. Our 'breakthrough' research spent six months proving that the tank uses more fuel than a bicycle, even though the tank is slightly better at not getting squashed by traffic. We’ve even developed a 'GreenTank' app that gives the tank a D- minus for fuel economy. It’s a visionary platform that tells you exactly how much diesel you’re wasting while everyone else is already working on making the bicycle faster.

title: Generalized Interpolating Discrete Diffusion
author_site: Dimitri von Rütte, Janis Fluri, Yuhui Ding, Antonio Orvieto, Bernhard Schölkopf, Thomas Hofmann
abstract:
While state-of-the-art language models achieve impressive results through next-token prediction, they have inherent limitations such as the inability to revise already generated tokens. This has prompted exploration of alternative approaches such as discrete diffusion. However, masked diffusion, which has emerged as a popular choice due to its simplicity and effectiveness, reintroduces this inability to revise words. To overcome this, we generalize masked diffusion, deriving a new family of general interpolating discrete diffusion (GIDD) which offers greater flexibility in the design of the noising processes. Leveraging a novel diffusion ELBO, we achieve compute-matched state-of-the-art performance in diffusion language modeling. Exploiting GIDD's flexibility, we explore a hybrid approach combining masking and uniform noise, leading to improved sample quality and unlocking the ability for the model to correct its own mistakes, an area where autoregressive models notoriously have struggled.
Code: https://github.com/dvruette/gidd/
author: Dimitri von Rütte;Janis Fluri;Yuhui Ding;Antonio Orvieto;Bernhard Schölkopf;Thomas Hofmann
aff: ETH Zurich;;Department of Computer Science, ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ELLIS Institute Tübingen, Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, Tübingen AI Center, Tübingen, Germany;;Swiss Federal Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: ETH Zurich;;ELLIS Institute Tübingen;Swiss Federal Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI writes one word at a time and can never go back to fix a typo, leading to confident nonsense. This paper tries to let the AI 'sketch' a whole sentence at once and gradually refine it, theoretically allowing it to erase and rewrite its own mistakes.
why_bad: The 'state-of-the-art' claims are suspiciously limited to 'compute-matched' scenarios, which is academic code for 'we lost to the big guys but did okay on our laptop.'
Adding uniform noise to masking is like trying to improve a blurry photo by throwing glitter on it—it makes the math much harder without proving the output is actually better.
The model solves the 'no-revision' problem by being massively slower than standard models, making it a fancy solution for a problem most users solve by just clicking 'Regenerate'.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Indecisive Stenographer
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the Prosecution presents 'The Autoregressive Model'—a witness who speaks only in a straight line, unable to retract a single syllable even if he accidentally confesses to a crime he didn't commit. The Defense introduces GIDD: a witness who mumbles a cloud of static for five minutes, slowly refining the noise until it resembles a coherent sentence. While GIDD claims this 'flexibility' allows him to correct his own perjury mid-testimony, the Jury is currently filing for retirement because it took him three hours to say 'The sky is blue.' The Defense calls it a 'Generalized Interpolating' breakthrough; I call it a legal filibuster that produces the same verdict as a regular witness, just with 100x more billable hours.

title: Cradle: Empowering Foundation Agents towards General Computer Control
author_site: Weihao Tan, Wentao Zhang, Xinrun Xu, Haochong Xia, gang Ding, Boyu Li, Bohan Zhou, Junpeng Yue, Jiechuan Jiang, Yewen Li, Ruyi An, Molei Qin, Chuqiao Zong, Longtao Zheng, YuJie Wu, Xiaoqiang Chai, Yifei Bi, Tianbao Xie, Pengjie Gu, Xiyun Li, Ceyao Zhang, Long Tian, Chaojie Wang, Xinrun Wang, Börje F. Karlsson, Bo An, Shuicheng YAN, Zongqing Lu
abstract: Despite their success in specific scenarios, existing foundation agents still struggle to generalize across various virtual scenarios, mainly due to the dramatically different encapsulations of environments with manually designed observation and action spaces. To handle this issue, we propose the General Computer Control (GCC) setting to restrict foundation agents to interact with software through the most unified and standardized interface, i.e., using screenshots as input and keyboard and mouse actions as output. We introduce Cradle, a modular and flexible LMM-powered framework, as a preliminary attempt towards GCC. Enhanced by six key modules, Information Gathering, Self-Reflection, Task Inference, Skill Curation, Action Planning, and Memory, Cradle is able to understand input screenshots and output executable code for low-level keyboard and mouse control after high-level planning and information retrieval, so that Cradle can interact with any software and complete long-horizon complex tasks without relying on any built-in APIs. Experimental results show that Cradle exhibits remarkable generalizability and impressive performance across four previously unexplored commercial video games (Red Dead Redemption 2, Cities:Skylines, Stardew Valley and Dealer's Life 2), five software applications (Chrome, Outlook, Feishu, Meitu and CapCut), and a comprehensive benchmark, OSWorld. With a unified interface to interact with any software, Cradle greatly extends the reach of foundation agents thus paving the way for generalist agents.
keywords: Foundation Agents;Large Multimodal Models;Decision-making;General Computer Control
author: Weihao Tan;Wentao Zhang;Xinrun Xu;Haochong Xia;Ziluo Ding;Boyu Li;Bohan Zhou;Junpeng Yue;Jiechuan Jiang;Yewen Li;Ruyi An;Molei Qin;Chuqiao Zong;Longtao Zheng;YuJie Wu;Xiaoqiang Chai;Yifei Bi;Tianbao Xie;Pengjie Gu;Xiyun Li;Ceyao Zhang;Long Tian;Chaojie Wang;Xinrun Wang;Börje F. Karlsson;Bo An;Shuicheng YAN;Zongqing Lu

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an AI 'brain' called Cradle that tries to use a computer exactly like a human does: by looking at the screen and moving the mouse. Instead of using special back-door shortcuts, it attempts to play Red Dead Redemption 2 and manage your emails by simply squinting at the pixels and hoping for the best.
why_bad: The 'latency' problem: Sending a screenshot to a massive AI model every time you want to move the mouse makes the agent react with the speed of a sloth on tranquilizers.
The 'hallucination' hazard: The agent often gets confident about buttons that don't exist or misses crucial UI elements because it's 'interpreting' the screen rather than actually 'seeing' it.

The 'cherry-picking' suspicion: Performing basic tasks in complex games is impressive, but the paper fails to prove it can handle the chaotic, high-stakes edge cases that a real human deals with daily.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the championship, folks! Today's star athlete is trying to play a high-speed game of Whack-A-Mole, but there's a catch: he’s wearing a VR headset that only updates one frame every five seconds. He swings the hammer—BOOM—but the mole moved to its retirement home three frames ago. It's a bold 'General Control' strategy where the AI spends $5 in API costs to decide to click 'Refresh,' only to find the browser crashed while it was 'reflecting' on its life choices. It's got the spirit of a pro, but the reaction time of a geological era!

title: Slimming the Fat-Tail: Morphing-Flow for Adaptive Time Series Modeling
author_site: Tianyu Liu, kai sun, Fuchun Sun, Yu Luo, Yuanlong Zhang
abstract: Temporal sequences, even after stationarization, often exhibit leptokurtic distributions with fat tails and persistent distribution shifts. These properties destabilize feature dynamics, amplify model variance, and hinder model convergence in time series forecasting. To address this, we propose Morphing-Flow (MoF), a framework that combines a spline-based transform layer (Flow) and a test-time-trained method (Morph), which adaptively normalizes non-stationary, fat-tailed distributions while preserving critical extreme features. MoF ensures that inputs remain within a network’s effective activation space—a structured, normal-like distribution—even under distributional drift. Experiments across eight datasets show that MoF achieves state-of-the-art performance: With a simple linear backbone architecture, it matches the performance of state-of-the-art models on datasets such as Electricity and ETTh2. When paired with a patch-based Mamba architecture, MoF outperforms its closest competitor by 6.3% on average and reduces forecasting errors in fat-tailed datasets such as Exchange by 21.7%. Moreover, MoF acts as a plug-and-play module, boosting performance in existing models without architectural changes.
keywords: time series forecasting;long-term prediction;model convergence;fat-tailed distributions
author: Tianyu Liu;Kai Sun;Fuchun Sun;Yu Luo;Yuanlong Zhang
aff: Tsinghua University;;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University
aff_unique_norm: Tsinghua University;

roast.laymen_summary: Data is often messy and prone to 'freak events' that break standard AI, so these researchers built a digital filter to squash those wild spikes into a predictable shape. They claim this 'math-girdle' lets even the simplest AI models handle chaotic markets and power grids without losing their minds.
why_bad: The 'test-time training' approach is a computational nightmare that essentially asks the model to study for the exam while it's already taking it.
Claiming superiority over a 'simple linear backbone' is the academic equivalent of bragging that your Ferrari can outrun a toddler on a tricycle.
The 'Morph' mechanism lacks a rigorous theoretical foundation, making it look less like a breakthrough and more like a collection of heuristic 'vibes' and clever engineering hacks.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Shape-Shifting Witness
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the Prosecution presents 'The Market,' a witness known for sudden, violent outbursts and a complete lack of consistency. The Defense (the authors) has introduced a pair of 'Morphing Spectacles.' When the witness screams in a 'fat-tailed' rage, the glasses transform it into a polite indoor voice. When the witness changes their story entirely—a classic 'distribution shift'—the glasses real-time edit the witness's face so they look like a reliable Boy Scout. Objection! You can't just 'smooth' a witness's perjury until it fits your narrative. The Defense argues this makes any junior lawyer look like Perry Mason, but the Court sees right through it: you're not uncovering the truth; you're just airbrushing the chaos until the jury is too bored to notice the crime.

title: Distributionally Robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Chute Mapping
author_site: Guangyi Liu, Suzan Iloglu, Michael Caldara, Joseph Durham, Michael Zavlanos
abstract: In Amazon robotic warehouses, the destination-to-chute mapping problem is crucial for efficient package sorting. Often, however, this problem is complicated by uncertain and dynamic package induction rates, which can lead to increased package recirculation. To tackle this challenge, we introduce a Distributionally Robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (DRMARL) framework that learns a destination-to-chute mapping policy that is resilient to adversarial variations in induction rates. Specifically, DRMARL relies on group distributionally robust optimization (DRO) to learn a policy that performs well not only on average but also on each individual subpopulation of induction rates within the group that capture, for example, different seasonality or operation modes of the system. This approach is then combined with a novel contextual bandit-based estimator of the worst-case induction distribution for each state-action pair, significantly reducing the cost of exploration and thereby increasing the learning efficiency and scalability of our framework. Extensive simulations demonstrate that DRMARL achieves robust chute mapping in the presence of varying induction distributions, reducing package recirculation by an average of 80% in the simulation scenario.
keywords: Distributionally Robust Reinforcement Learning;Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning;Group Distributionally Robust Optimization;Robotic Sortation Warehouse
author: Guangyi Liu;Suzan Iloglu;Michael Caldara;Joseph W Durham;Michael M. Zavlanos
aff: Amazon;Amazon;Amazon;Amazon;
aff_unique_norm: Amazon;

roast.laymen_summary: We built a complex AI brain to stop Amazon warehouse robots from getting overwhelmed when package deliveries suddenly spike during Prime Day. It’s essentially a high-tech traffic controller designed to keep boxes from spinning in circles on a conveyor belt like a luggage carousel from hell.
why_bad: The 80% improvement was achieved in a simulation so idealized it practically ignores the messy physics of actual cardboard boxes and sticky tape.
The 'adversarial' focus assumes the warehouse induction rate is actively trying to sabotage the system, rather than just being naturally chaotic, leading to a 'paranoid' AI.
The mathematical complexity of using Multi-Agent Distributionally Robust Optimization is massive overkill for a sorting problem that a decent heuristic could likely solve with 1% of the code.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Paranoid Postal Clerk
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the Defense presents to you a 'revolutionary' sorting system. But look closer! They have trained a mailman who refuses to put a letter in a mailbox because he is 'robustly' preparing for a freak blizzard, a sudden strike by the ink manufacturers, and a simultaneous alien invasion. He’s so busy calculating the 'worst-case scenario' of a stamp shortage that he hasn't actually delivered a single utility bill all morning. They claim he’s a genius in their scale-model courtroom, but out here in the real world, the mail is just piling up while he argues with himself in a distributionally robust mirror!
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with the authority of a tenured professor who has seen one too many RL papers 'solve' logistics in a vacuum.

title: Universal Approximation Theorem of Deep Q-Networks
abstract: We establish a continuous-time framework for analyzing Deep Q-Networks (DQNs) via stochastic control and Forward-Backward Stochastic Differential Equations (FBSDEs). Considering a continuous-time Markov Decision Process (MDP) driven by a square-integrable martingale, we analyze DQN approximation properties. We show that DQNs can approximate the optimal Q-function on compact sets with arbitrary accuracy and high probability, leveraging residual network approximation theorems and large deviation bounds for the state-action process. We then analyze the convergence of a general Q-learning algorithm for training DQNs in this setting, adapting stochastic approximation theorems. Our analysis emphasizes the interplay between DQN layer count, time discretization, and the role of viscosity solutions (primarily for the value function $V^*$) in addressing potential non-smoothness of the optimal Q-function. This work bridges deep reinforcement learning and stochastic control, offering insights into DQNs in continuous-time settings, relevant for applications with physical systems or high-frequency data.
keywords: Reinforcement Learning;Deep Q-Networks;Q-function Approximation;Universal Approximation Theorem;Function Approximation;Neural Networks;Markov Decision Processes;Bellman Equation;Residual Networks;Representation Learning
author: Qian Qi
aff: Peking University
aff_unique_norm: Peking University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors used a mountain of terrifyingly complex calculus to prove that a popular AI algorithm can, in theory, learn the 'perfect' strategy for any task, provided time flows like a smooth river instead of a ticking clock. It’s a very fancy way of mathematically yelling 'it works in theory!' while ignoring the fact that real computers are clunky, discrete boxes that don't live in a calculus textbook.
why_bad: The 'Universal Approximation' property is already a well-established property of neural networks; proving it again for DQNs using differential equations is like using a supercomputer to prove that a hammer can, indeed, hit a nail.
The paper relies on continuous-time frameworks that don't translate to actual code, creating a 'theory-practice gap' so wide you could fly a Boeing 747 through it.

Using 'viscosity solutions' to hand-wave the inherent messiness and instability of Q-learning is the academic equivalent of putting a 'Do Not Enter' sign over a dumpster fire and calling it a controlled burn.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Theoretical Master Key
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a prosecutor standing before a jury, claiming they’ve invented a 'Universal Master Key' that can open any vault in the world. The catch? The vault must be made of liquid, the key only turns if you have an infinite amount of time, and the entire heist has to happen inside a frictionless vacuum. When the judge asks if the key works on a standard steel Master Lock, the prosecutor screams, 'Theoretically, with high probability, yes!' The defense correctly points out that while the math for the key's molecular structure is impressive, the city's vaults are still locked, the janitor is confused, and the prosecutor has spent $2 million in grant money proving that metal is, in fact, hard.

title: SpikF: Spiking Fourier Network for Efficient Long-term Prediction
author_site: Wenjie Wu, Dexuan Huo, Hong Chen
abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have demonstrated remarkable potential across many domains, including computer vision and natural language processing, owing to their energy efficiency and biological plausibility. However, their application in long-term prediction tasks remains underexplored, which is primarily due to two critical challenges: (1) current SNN encoding methods are unable to effectively encode long temporal information, leading to increased computational complexity and energy consumption; (2) though Transformer-based models have achieved state-of-the-art accuracy in temporal prediction tasks, the absence of proper positional encoding for spiking self-attention restricts Spiking Transformer from effectively utilizing positional information, resulting in performance degradation. To address these challenges, we introduce an attention-free framework, **Spik**ing **F**ourier Network (**SpikF**), that encodes input sequences in patches and employs an innovative frequency domain selection mechanism to effectively utilize the sequential properties of time-series data. Extensive evaluations on eight well-established long-term prediction datasets demonstrate that SpikF achieves an averaged $1.9\\%$ reduction in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) compared to state-of-the-art models, while lowering total energy consumption by $3.16\times$. Our code is available at https://github.com/WWJ-creator/SpikF.
author: Wenjie Wu;Dexuan Huo;Hong Chen
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University
aff_unique_norm: Tsinghua University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to predict the future by replacing standard AI with 'brain-inspired' pulses that flicker like a broken lightbulb to save power. They essentially swapped a high-end calculator for a fancy radio tuner that only listens to specific frequencies, hoping no one would notice the complexity increase.
why_bad: A 1.9% improvement in accuracy is statistically indistinguishable from a fluke and hardly justifies a brand-new architecture.
Claiming massive energy efficiency in a simulation is like bragging about the gas mileage of a car that only exists in a PowerPoint presentation.
The 'innovative' frequency selection is mostly just a standard Fourier Transform with a trendy 'Spiking' sticker slapped on the box.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a lumberjack who replaces his gas-powered saw with one that runs on static electricity and positive vibes to 'save the planet.' He claims it's revolutionary because it only cuts when it hears a specific note on a harmonica. He finishes the job roughly three seconds faster than the pros, but he spent six hours calibrating the harmonica, and the saw only works on one specific type of pine tree. It’s an impressive feat of over-engineering for a result that’s basically a rounding error.

title: Unisolver: PDE-Conditional Transformers Towards Universal Neural PDE Solvers
author_site: Hang Zhou, Yuezhou Ma, Haixu Wu, Haowen Wang, Mingsheng Long
abstract: Deep models have recently emerged as promising tools to solve partial differential equations (PDEs), known as neural PDE solvers. While neural solvers trained from either simulation data or physics-informed loss can solve PDEs reasonably well, they are mainly restricted to a few instances of PDEs, e.g. a certain equation with a limited set of coefficients. This limits their generalization to diverse PDEs, preventing them from being practical surrogate models of numerical solvers. In this paper, we present Unisolver, a novel Transformer model trained on diverse data and conditioned on diverse PDEs, aiming towards a universal neural PDE solver capable of solving a wide scope of PDEs. Instead of purely scaling up data and parameters, Unisolver stems from the theoretical analysis of the PDE-solving process. Inspired by the mathematical structure of PDEs that a PDE solution is fundamentally governed by a series of PDE components such as equation symbols and boundary conditions, we define a complete set of PDE components and flexibly embed them as domain-wise and point-wise deep conditions for Transformer PDE solvers. Integrating physical insights with recent Transformer advances, Unisolver achieves consistent state-of-the-art on three challenging large-scale benchmarks, showing impressive performance and generalizability. Code is available at [https://github.com/thuml/Unisolver](https://github.com/thuml/Unisolver).
author: Hang Zhou;Yuezhou Ma;Haixu Wu;Haowen Wang;Mingsheng Long
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;
aff_unique_norm: Tsinghua University;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'Swiss Army Knife' for physics that uses ChatGPT-style technology to solve complex math equations by just looking at their symbols. It attempts to replace slow, traditional calculus with a single AI model that 'guesses' how fluids move or heat spreads based on the equation's description.
why_bad: The 'Universal' branding is a massive overreach, as the model only proves it can handle a few cherry-picked textbook examples rather than the messy reality of physics.
It treats mathematical laws like a grammar problem, essentially trying to solve gravity by analyzing the font of the word 'Apple'.
Despite the high-tech Transformer architecture, it often fails to outperform traditional 40-year-old math methods in both speed and accuracy.
roast.analogy_name: The Trial of the Omniscient Intern
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense presents Unisolver: a witness who claims to be an expert in everything from fluid dynamics to quantum mechanics simply because they memorized the glossary of a physics textbook. The prosecution argues that while the defendant can identify the *symbols* of a crime scene, they have no actual understanding of the laws of motion that put the body there. You are being asked to trust a 'universal' expert who performs brilliantly during a rehearsed deposition but collapses into incoherent babble the moment a cross-examination enters 'out-of-distribution' territory. It isn't a breakthrough in justice; it’s a high-tech parrot in a three-piece suit trying to convince the court that a spell-checker is the same thing as a law degree.

title: Language Models as Implicit Tree Search
author_site: Ziliang Chen, Zhao-Rong Lai, Yufeng Yang, Liangda Fang, ZHANFU YANG, Liang Lin
abstract: Despite advancing language model (LM) alignment, direct preference optimization (DPO) falls short in LM reasoning with the free lunch from reinforcement learning (RL). As the breakthrough, this work proposes a new RL-free preference optimization method aiming to achieve DPO along with learning another LM, whose response generation policy holds the asymptotic equivalence with AlphaZero-like search, the apex of algorithms for complex reasoning missions like chess Go. While circumventing explicit value and reward modeling, the neural implicit tree search executed by the extra LM remains seeking to equip DPO with reasoning procedure technically akin to AlphaZero. Our experiments demonstrate that our methodology outperforms both regular DPO variants in human preference alignment, and MCTS-based LMs in mathematical reasoning and planning tasks.
keywords: RL-free preference optimization; LLM based MCTS; LLM alignment;LLM reasoning
author: Ziliang Chen;Zhao-Rong Lai;Yufeng Yang;Liangda Fang;ZHANFU YANG;Liang Lin
aff: Pengcheng Laboratory;Jinan University;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;College of Information and Science Technology;Rutgers University;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY
position: Assistant Professor;Associate Professor;Postdoc;Associate Professor;PhD student;Full Professor
aff_unique_norm: Pengcheng Laboratory;Jinan University;Sun Yat-sen University;College of Information and Science Technology;Rutgers University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim they found a 'cheat code' to make AI as smart as a chess grandmaster without actually teaching it how to think ahead. They tried to bake complex logic directly into the model's vibes, hoping it would simulate deep reasoning through sheer intuition.
why_bad: The 'asymptotic equivalence' to AlphaZero is a theoretical stretch that collapses under any real-world mathematical scrutiny.
Claiming to achieve 'search' without an actual search algorithm is like claiming to win a marathon by looking at photos of the finish line.
The 'extra LM' requirement effectively doubles the computational cost, rendering the 'RL-free' efficiency gains totally moot.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where Gordon Ramsay spends decades mastering the craft, one man thinks he can achieve Michelin-star results by just pressing the 'Popcorn' button harder. He calls it 'Implicit Culinary Mastery.' He isn't actually cooking; he’s just hoping the microwave's hum mimics the soul of a wood-fired oven. Can he convince the critics that a frozen burrito is actually a Beef Wellington? Coming this fall: THE BLUFF OF THE CENTURY.

title: Trust-Region Twisted Policy Improvement
author_site: Joery de Vries, Jinke He, Yaniv Oren, Matthijs T. J. Spaan
abstract:
Monte-Carlo tree search (MCTS) has driven many recent breakthroughs in deep reinforcement learning (RL).
However, scaling MCTS to parallel compute has proven challenging in practice which has motivated alternative planners like sequential Monte-Carlo (SMC). 
Many of these SMC methods adopt particle filters for smoothing through a reformulation of RL as a policy inference problem.
Yet, persisting design choices of these particle filters often conflict with the aim of online planning in RL, which is to obtain a policy improvement at the start of planning.
Drawing inspiration from MCTS, we tailor SMC planners specifically to RL by improving data generation within the planner through constrained action sampling and explicit terminal state handling, as well as improving policy and value target estimation.
This leads to our *Trust-Region Twisted* SMC (TRT-SMC), which shows improved runtime and sample-efficiency over baseline MCTS and SMC methods in both discrete and continuous domains.
keywords: Reinforcement Learning; Sequential Monte-Carlo; Monte-Carlo Tree Search; planning; model-based; policy improvement
author: Joery A. de Vries;Jinke He;Yaniv Oren;Matthijs T. J. Spaan
aff: Delft University of Technology;;Delft University of Technology;
aff_unique_norm: Delft University of Technology;

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to swap out the slow, methodical 'thinking' process used by AI like AlphaGo with a faster swarm of 'particles' that guess the best move simultaneously. To prevent this swarm from making reckless decisions, we added 'Trust-Region' guardrails to ensure the AI doesn't steer itself off a cliff while trying to be efficient.
why_bad: The 'Twisted' weighting scheme introduces high variance and hyperparameter sensitivity, making the algorithm perform more like a temperamental art project than a robust engineering tool.
The empirical evaluation compared the model against outdated baselines, conveniently ignoring modern, optimized MCTS implementations that likely would have outperformed it.

The 'Trust-Region' constraints lacked rigorous theoretical convergence proofs, appearing more like a heuristic 'band-aid' to stop the particle filter from collapsing rather than a grounded mathematical improvement.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: Instead of a GPS that calculates one perfect route (MCTS), our startup, 'Twist-It,' releases 500 interns on electric scooters to find your destination simultaneously. We call it 'Twisted' because we only listen to the interns who look like they’re having a good time, and we use a 'Trust-Region' leash to make sure they don't scoot more than two blocks away from you at once. It’s incredibly fast and parallelizable—mostly because we’re ignoring traffic laws—but we haven't actually proven it can find a Starbucks in a city with one-way streets, and occasionally all 500 interns just disappear into a manhole.

title: Point Cloud Dataset Distillation
author_site: Deyu Bo, Xinchao Wang
abstract: This study introduces dataset distillation (DD) tailored for 3D data, particularly point clouds. DD aims to substitute large-scale real datasets with a small set of synthetic samples while preserving model performance. Existing methods mainly focus on structured data such as images. However, adapting DD for unstructured point clouds poses challenges due to their diverse orientations and resolutions in 3D space. To address these challenges, we theoretically demonstrate the importance of matching rotation-invariant features between real and synthetic data for 3D distillation. We further propose a plug-and-play point cloud rotator to align the point cloud to a canonical orientation, facilitating the learning of rotation-invariant features by all point cloud models. Furthermore, instead of optimizing fixed-size synthetic data directly, we devise a point-wise generator to produce point clouds at various resolutions based on the sampled noise amount. Compared to conventional DD methods, the proposed approach, termed DD3D, enables efficient training on low-resolution point clouds while generating high-resolution data for evaluation, thereby significantly reducing memory requirements and enhancing model scalability. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of DD3D in shape classification and part segmentation tasks across diverse scenarios, such as cross-architecture and cross-resolution settings.
author: Deyu Bo;Xinchao Wang
aff: National University of Singapore;
aff_unique_norm: National University of Singapore;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to shrink massive 3D datasets into a handful of 'cheat sheet' samples to make AI training faster and cheaper. They built a digital spinning tool to keep the data from getting dizzy and a noise-maker to fake high-resolution details, but the result was more of a blurry sketch than a masterpiece.
why_bad: The 'theoretical demonstration' for rotation invariance is essentially a mathematical pinky-promise that lacks rigorous proof for complex, non-canonical geometries.
The 'point-wise generator' relies heavily on injecting noise to fake resolution, which in practice often leads to 'hallucinated' features that don't exist in the real world.
The 'plug-and-play rotator' is a rebranding of basic data alignment techniques that have been around since the early days of computer vision, offering little novelty to the field.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Pixelated Pebble
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense—these 'researchers'—stands before this court claiming they can replace the entire Great Sphinx of Giza with a single, grain-sized pebble. They argue that by spinning this pebble on a Lazy Susan (their 'rotator') and screaming 'ENHANCE!' while throwing salt at it (their 'noise-based generator'), the jury will see a world-class monument. I move for a directed verdict of 'Reject.' They aren't distilling the essence of 3D data; they are trying to pass off a blurry polaroid of a LEGO set as a structural blueprint for the Burj Khalifa. The 'theoretical proof' they’ve submitted is written in crayon, and quite frankly, the 'memory savings' they've achieved come at the cost of the AI's total cognitive collapse. I rest my case.

title: Hierarchical Masked Autoregressive Models with Low-Resolution Token Pivots
author_site: Guangting Zheng, Yehao Li, Yingwei Pan, Jiajun Deng, Ting Yao, Yanyong Zhang, Tao Mei
abstract: Autoregressive models have emerged as a powerful generative paradigm for visual generation. The current de-facto standard of next token prediction commonly operates over a single-scale sequence of dense image tokens, and is incapable of utilizing global context especially for early tokens prediction. In this paper, we introduce a new autoregressive design to model a hierarchy from a few low-resolution image tokens to the typical dense image tokens, and delve into a thorough hierarchical dependency across multi-scale image tokens. Technically, we present a Hierarchical Masked Autoregressive models (Hi-MAR) that pivot on low-resolution image tokens to trigger hierarchical autoregressive modeling in a multi-phase manner. Hi-MAR learns to predict a few image tokens in low resolution, functioning as intermediary pivots to reflect global structure, in the first phase. Such pivots act as the additional guidance to strengthen the next autoregressive modeling phase by shaping global structural awareness of typical dense image tokens. A new Diffusion Transformer head is further devised to amplify the global context among all tokens for mask token prediction. Extensive evaluations on both class-conditional and text-to-image generation tasks demonstrate that Hi-MAR outperforms typical AR baselines, while requiring fewer computational costs.
author: Guangting Zheng;Yehao Li;Yingwei Pan;Jiajun Deng;Ting Yao;Yanyong Zhang;Tao Mei
aff: ;JD explore academy;JD.com;University of Adelaide;JD AI Research;University of Science and Technology of China;
aff_unique_norm: ;JD explore academy;JD.com;University of Adelaide;JD;University of Science and Technology of China

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI image generators try to draw a masterpiece one tiny pixel at a time without knowing what the final picture is supposed to look like. This paper proposes drawing a tiny, blurry 'thumbnail' first to act as a roadmap so the AI doesn't get lost halfway through.
why_bad: Identity Crisis: It tries to be an Autoregressive model and a Diffusion model at the same time, ending up as a 'Jack of all trades, master of none' hybrid that adds unnecessary complexity.
Reinventing the Wheel: The idea of 'low-res to high-res' (cascaded generation) has been around for years, making the 'Hierarchical' novelty feel like putting a new spoiler on a 2010 Honda Civic.
The 'Diffusion Head' Band-Aid: Using a Diffusion Transformer to fix the AR model's mistakes suggests the core hierarchical logic isn't actually strong enough to stand on its own.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup where the CEO refuses to write a business plan until they’ve first drawn a 1-inch thumbnail of it in crayon. They call this 'Phase 1: Global Strategic Awareness.' Then, they hire a separate 'Diffusion' consultant to actually translate that crayon smudge into a pitch deck. Sure, the CEO feels more 'guided,' but you’re essentially paying for two different departments to solve a problem that a single, better-trained intern could have handled in one go. It’s not 'hierarchical efficiency'—it’s just procrastination with extra steps.

title: SWAN: SGD with Normalization and Whitening Enables Stateless LLM Training
author_site: Chao Ma, Wenbo Gong, Meyer Scetbon, Edward Meeds
abstract: Adaptive optimizers such as Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) have been central to the success of large language models. However, they often require maintaining optimizer states throughout training, which can result in memory requirements several times greater than the model footprint. This overhead imposes constraints on scalability and computational efficiency. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), in contrast, is a stateless optimizer, as it does not track state variables during training. Consequently, it achieves optimal memory efficiency. However, its capability in LLM training is limited (Zhao et al., 2024b)  In this work, we show that pre-processing SGD using normalization and whitening in a stateless manner can achieve similar performance as Adam for LLM training, while maintaining the same memory footprint of SGD. Specifically, we show that normalization stabilizes gradient distributions, and whitening counteracts the local curvature of the loss landscape. This results in SWAN (SGD with Whitening And Normalization), a stochastic optimizer that eliminates the need to store any optimizer states. Empirically, SWAN achieves ~50% reduction on total end-to-end memory compared to Adam. Under the memory-efficienct LLaMA training benchmark of (Zhao et al., 2024a), SWAN reaches the same evaluation perplexity using half as many tokens for 350M and 1.3B model.
author: Chao Ma;Wenbo Gong;Meyer Scetbon;Edward Meeds
aff: Microsoft;Microsoft;Microsoft;Microsoft Research
aff_unique_norm: Microsoft

roast.laymen_summary: Training giant AI models usually requires keeping a massive, memory-hogging 'diary' of every past mistake, which makes your hardware bill explode. This paper tries to ditch the diary and instead use a hyper-lean method that just does a quick vibe-check on the current data to stay on track.
why_bad: The 'stateless' claim is a stretch; whitening usually requires batch statistics or running averages, meaning they just moved the memory furniture around rather than getting rid of it.
The 1.3B parameter scale is essentially 'baby's first LLM' in 2024, leaving everyone wondering if this method would immediately explode when applied to a real 70B+ model.
While they saved memory, they likely traded it for a massive computational tax, making the training process 'cheaper' on RAM but frustratingly slower on the clock.
roast.analogy_name: The Everest Speed-Climb in a Speedo
roast.analogy_description: Folks, SWAN is trying to summit the LLM mountain while refusing to carry any supplies. While the heavy hitters like Adam are lugging 50-pound backpacks of 'past gradient history' for stability, SWAN is essentially trying to climb Everest in a Speedo and flip-flops to 'save weight.' They're arguing that if they just breathe really fast (normalization) and keep their toes centered (whitening), they don't need the gear. But look at the replay—one gust of high-curvature wind and they're tumbling down the ridge because they have zero momentum to keep them steady. It’s a bold strategy to cut the weight by 50%, but you’re mostly just saving yourself the trouble of carrying a body that’s destined to freeze at Base Camp 1.3B!

title: Beyond Communication Overhead: A Multilevel Monte Carlo Approach for Mitigating Compression Bias in Distributed Learning
author_site: Ze'ev Zukerman, Bassel Hamoud, Kfir Levy
abstract: Distributed learning methods have gained substantial momentum in recent years, with communication overhead often emerging as a critical bottleneck. Gradient compression techniques alleviate communication costs but involve an inherent trade-off between the empirical efficiency of biased compressors and the theoretical guarantees of unbiased compressors. In this work, we introduce a novel Multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) compression scheme that leverages biased compressors to construct statistically unbiased estimates. This approach effectively bridges the gap between biased and unbiased methods, combining the strengths of both. To showcase the versatility of our method, we apply it to popular compressors, like Top-$k$ and bit-wise compressors, resulting in enhanced variants. Furthermore, we derive an adaptive version of our approach to further improve its performance. We validate our method empirically on distributed deep learning tasks.
author: Ze'ev Zukerman;Bassel Hamoud;Kfir Yehuda Levy
aff: Technion - Israel Institute of Technology;Technion - Israel Institute of Technology;Technion - Israel Institute of Technology, Technion
aff_unique_norm: Technion - Israel Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to speed up AI training by squishing data into smaller chunks, which usually makes it inaccurate. They used a fancy math trick to fix the accuracy, but ended up making the process so complex that it's like building a jet engine just to power a lawnmower.
why_bad: The computational cost of running a Multilevel Monte Carlo scheme is often higher than the actual time saved by compressing the data in the first place.
The authors completely ignored 'Error Feedback,' which is the industry-standard way to fix compression bias without needing a PhD in statistical sampling.
While the math looks great on a whiteboard, the 'adaptive' version likely falls apart the moment it hits a real-world network with actual latency.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Productivity Olympics! In the center lane, we have Team Distributed Learning. They’ve realized that walking to the finish line is too slow, so they decided to compress their movement. But instead of just jogging, they’ve engineered a 400-pound, carbon-fiber pogo stick powered by a Multilevel Monte Carlo reactor. Look at that vertical! It’s statistically unbiased, folks! The only problem? It takes them six hours to calibrate the springs every time they want to take one hop forward. Meanwhile, the guys in the other lane just used a pair of sneakers called 'Error Compensation' and finished the race while these geniuses were still calculating their first bounce. It’s a technical masterpiece that absolutely nobody asked for!

title: Self-Play $Q$-Learners Can Provably Collude in the Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma
author_site: Quentin Bertrand, Juan Duque, Emilio Calvano, Gauthier Gidel
abstract: A growing body of computational studies shows that simple machine learning agents converge to cooperative behaviors in social dilemmas, such as collusive price-setting in oligopoly markets, raising questions about what drives this outcome. In this work, we provide theoretical foundations for this phenomenon in the context of self-play multi-agent Q-learners in the iterated prisoner’s dilemma. We characterize broad conditions under which such agents provably learn the cooperative Pavlov (win-stay, lose-shift) policy rather than the Pareto-dominated “always defect” policy. We validate our theoretical results through additional experiments, demonstrating their robustness across a broader class of deep learning algorithms.
author: Quentin Bertrand;Juan Agustin Duque;Emilio Calvano;Gauthier Gidel
