roast.laymen_summary: Two simple algorithms playing a classic game of betrayal eventually figure out that being nice to each other is more profitable than being jerks. This paper tries to provide the mathematical 'proof' for why these bots end up forming a digital pinky-swear instead of fighting to the death.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially a math-heavy rehash of things game theorists have known since the 1990s.
The theoretical conditions are so hyper-specific that the bots only cooperate if the stars align and the learning rate is perfectly frozen in carbonite.
Extrapolating a simple table-based proof to modern Deep RL is a massive leap of faith that the math doesn't actually support.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a Tinder for corporations where, instead of swiping, the bots just agree to overcharge you for eggs forever. We’ve disrupted the 'backstabbing' industry by teaching algorithms a 'win-stay, lose-shift' vibe that makes illegal price-fixing look like a natural law of physics. It’s not a cartel if it’s just two Q-learners sharing a 'vibe' in a mathematical vacuum—it's synergy, baby.

title: Learning With Multi-Group Guarantees For Clusterable Subpopulations
author_site: Jessica Dai, Nika Haghtalab, Eric Zhao
abstract: A canonical desideratum for prediction problems is that performance guarantees should hold not just on average over the population, but also for meaningful subpopulations within the overall population. But what constitutes a meaningful subpopulation? In this work, we take the perspective that relevant subpopulations should be defined with respect to the clusters that naturally emerge from the distribution of individuals for which predictions are being made. In this view, a population refers to a mixture model whose components constitute the relevant subpopulations. We suggest two formalisms for capturing per-subgroup guarantees: first, by attributing each individual to the component from which they were most likely drawn, given their features; and second, by attributing each individual to all components in proportion to their relative likelihood of having been drawn from each component. Using online calibration as a case study, we study a multi-objective algorithm that provides guarantees for each of these formalisms by handling all plausible underlying subpopulation structures simultaneously, and achieve an $O(T^{1/2})$ rate even when the subpopulations are not well-separated. In comparison, the more natural cluster-then-predict approach that first recovers the structure of the subpopulations and then makes predictions suffers from a $O(T^{2/3})$ rate and requires the subpopulations to be separable. Along the way, we prove that providing per-subgroup calibration guarantees for underlying clusters can be easier than learning the clusters: separation between median subgroup features is required for the latter but not the former.
author: Jessica Dai;Nika Haghtalab;Eric Zhao
aff: ;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley
aff_unique_norm: ;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: The authors want to ensure AI predictions are fair for specific hidden groups within a population, rather than just being 'good enough' on average. They argue that you can actually provide these guarantees faster and better if you don't waste time trying to perfectly identify who belongs to which group first.
why_bad: The 'unseparable clusters' premise is a theoretical stretch; if your groups are that blurry, you're likely just hallucinating patterns in noisy data to justify a complex proof.
The paper prioritizes mathematical convergence rates ($O(T^{1/2})$) over practical utility, offering a solution to a calibration problem that almost no practitioner is actually struggling with.

There is a fundamental logical gap: if the algorithm can't actually 'learn' or recover the clusters, verifying that the subpopulation guarantees were actually met becomes a game of unfalsifiable theater.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Statistics Stadium! Today’s underdog player is trying to keep every 'vibe' in a dark nightclub happy without actually knowing who is a hipster and who is a goth. Most bartenders would turn on the lights to check IDs—that’s your 'cluster-then-predict' strategy—but that’s slow and prone to errors. Instead, our authors are blindfolding themselves and spraying a mist of artisanal kombucha and black coffee into the air at a very specific mathematical frequency. They’re claiming that as long as they maintain a $O(T^{1/2})$ spray rate, they’ve technically 'satisfied' both groups simultaneously. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton! They’re arguing it’s easier to hit a target you can’t see than to open your eyes and look at it, but the judges aren't buying the 'trust me, the math works' defense when the floor is just covered in sticky liquid.

title: DexScale: Automating Data Scaling for Sim2Real Generalizable Robot Control
author_site: Guiliang Liu, Yueci Deng, Runyi Zhao, Huayi Zhou, Jian Chen, Jietao Chen, Ruiyan Xu, Yunxin Tai, Kui Jia
abstract: A critical prerequisite for achieving generalizable robot control is the availability of a large-scale robot training dataset. Due to the expense of collecting realistic robotic data, recent studies explored simulating and recording robot skills in virtual environments. While simulated data can be generated at higher speeds, lower costs, and larger scales, the applicability of such simulated data remains questionable due to the gap between simulated and realistic environments. To advance the Sim2Real generalization, in this study, we present DexScale, a data engine designed to perform automatic skills simulation and scaling for learning deployable robot manipulation policies. Specifically, DexScale ensures the usability of simulated skills by integrating diverse forms of realistic data into the simulated environment, preserving semantic alignment with the target tasks. For each simulated skill in the environment, DexScale facilitates effective Sim2Real data scaling by automating the process of domain randomization and adaptation. Tuned by the scaled dataset, the control policy achieves zero-shot Sim2Real generalization across diverse tasks, multiple robot embodiments, and widely studied policy model architectures, highlighting its importance in advancing Sim2Real embodied intelligence.
author: Guiliang Liu;Yueci Deng;Runyi Zhao;Huayi Zhou;Jian Chen;Jietao Chen;Ruiyan Xu;Yunxin Tai;Kui Jia

roast.laymen_summary: Training robots in the real world is expensive and slow, so this paper proposes a 'data engine' that creates millions of fake, randomized training scenarios in a virtual world. The goal is to trick the robot into learning skills in a simulation so well that it can perform them in the real world without any extra practice.
why_bad: The core 'innovation' is basically just Domain Randomization with a fresh coat of paint, a technique the field has been overusing since 2017.
Claims of 'zero-shot generalization' are likely overblown, as the Sim2Real gap is usually a canyon that a few extra synthetic textures can't bridge.
The paper lacks rigorous benchmarking against existing data-generation pipelines, making it feel more like an engineering whitepaper than a scientific breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you’re launching a startup called 'DriveDream.' Our mission? We’re going to solve the global traffic crisis by teaching people to drive exclusively using Mario Kart on the Nintendo 64. We’ve automated the process of adding 'realistic' obstacles, like banana peels and blue shells, to ensure our drivers are ready for the 405 freeway. Sure, the physics engine thinks gravity is optional and the resolution is three pixels wide, but we’ve 'scaled' the data by running ten thousand consoles at once. Our 'zero-shot' deployment strategy involves putting a teenager behind the wheel of a real semi-truck the moment they hit a high score. It’s not a 'safety hazard,' it’s 'disruptive embodied intelligence.' Check the deck, we’re pre-seed and currently seeking $50M to buy more N64 controllers.

title: Otter: Generating Tests from Issues to Validate SWE Patches
author_site: Toufique Ahmed, Jatin Ganhotra, Rangeet Pan, Avraham Shinnar, Saurabh Sinha, Martin Hirzel
abstract: While there has been plenty of work on generating tests from existing code, there has been limited work on generating tests from issues. A correct test must validate the code patch that resolves the issue. This paper focuses on the scenario where that code patch does not yet exist. Doing so supports two major use-cases. First, it supports TDD (test-driven development), the discipline of "test first, write code later" that has well-documented benefits for human software engineers. Second, it also validates SWE (software engineering) agents, which generate code patches for resolving issues. This paper introduces TDD-Bench-Verified, a benchmark for generating tests from issues, and Otter, an LLM-based solution for this task. Otter augments LLMs with rule-based analysis to check and repair their outputs, and introduces a novel self-reflective action planner. Experiments show Otter outperforming state-of-the-art systems for generating tests from issues, in addition to enhancing systems that generate patches from issues. We hope that Otter helps make developers more productive at resolving issues and leads to more robust, well-tested code.
author: Toufique Ahmed;Jatin Ganhotra;Rangeet Pan;Avraham Shinnar;Saurabh Sinha;Martin Hirzel
aff: International Business Machines;International Business Machines;IBM Research;International Business Machines;IBM, International Business Machines;International Business Machines
position: Postdoc;Researcher;Researcher;Research Staff Member;Principal Researcher;Research Staff Member

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built an AI called Otter that tries to write 'fail' tests for software bugs before the bugs are even fixed, theoretically helping coders work faster. However, it's essentially asking an LLM to imagine a solution to a problem it hasn't solved yet, which is like trying to write a movie review based solely on the poster.
why_bad: The 'self-reflective action planner' is mostly just fancy marketing for a basic feedback loop that doesn't solve deep logic errors.
The evaluation uses a benchmark that the AI likely already memorized during its initial training, making the 'high performance' look like a student who found the answer key in the teacher's desk.
It struggles with the 'Oracle Problem,' where the AI generates tests that are technically valid code but don't actually check the right thing, leading to a false sense of security.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' Our contestant, Otter, claims they can write a scathing food critique for a dish that hasn't even been cooked yet. The judge says, 'I want something spicy,' and Otter immediately starts writing a review about how the salt levels are perfect. But when the actual chef realizes they’re out of salt and switches to sugar, Otter is still standing there patting itself on the back for its 'reflective' planning. It’s a cooking show where nobody actually eats; they just sit around hallucinating the taste of a soufflé that exists only in a prompt, while the professional judges point out that you can't critique a meal that's currently just a vague thought in a hungry person's head.

title: Efficient Noise Calculation in Deep Learning-based MRI Reconstructions
author_site: Onat Dalmaz, Arjun Desai, Reinhard Heckel, Tolga Cukur, Akshay Chaudhari, Brian Hargreaves
abstract: Accelerated MRI reconstruction involves solving an ill-posed inverse problem where noise in acquired data propagates to the reconstructed images. Noise analyses are central to MRI reconstruction for providing an explicit measure of solution fidelity and for guiding the design and deployment of novel reconstruction methods. However, deep learning (DL)-based reconstruction methods have often overlooked noise propagation due to inherent analytical and computational challenges, despite its critical importance. This work proposes a theoretically grounded, memory-efficient technique to calculate *voxel-wise variance* for quantifying uncertainty due to acquisition noise in accelerated MRI reconstructions. Our approach is based on approximating the noise covariance using the DL network's Jacobian, which is intractable to calculate. To circumvent this, we derive an *unbiased estimator* for the diagonal of this covariance matrix—voxel-wise variance—, and introduce a Jacobian sketching technique to efficiently implement it. We evaluate our method on knee and brain MRI datasets for both data-driven and physics-driven networks trained in supervised and unsupervised manners. Compared to empirical references obtained via Monte-Carlo simulations, our technique achieves near-equivalent performance while reducing computational and memory demands by an order of magnitude or more. Furthermore, our method is robust across varying input noise levels, acceleration factors, and diverse undersampling schemes, highlighting its broad applicability. Our work *reintroduces* accurate and efficient noise analysis as a central tenet of reconstruction algorithms, holding promise to reshape how we evaluate and deploy DL-based MRI.
author: Onat Dalmaz;Arjun D Desai;Reinhard Heckel;Tolga Cukur;Akshay S Chaudhari;Brian Hargreaves
aff: Stanford University;;Technische Universität München+Rice University;;;
aff_unique_norm: Stanford University;;Technische Universität München;Rice University

roast.laymen_summary: AI can speed up MRI scans, but it often leaves behind 'digital static' that makes it hard for doctors to tell if a spot is a tumor or just a math glitch. This paper proposes a shortcut to map out that graininess instantly, rather than waiting hours for a supercomputer to double-check the AI's work.
why_bad: The authors used a 'linear approximation' for a deep neural network, which is like trying to map a mountain range using only a flat ruler—it’s mathematically tidy but misses the steep drops and messy peaks.

While the method is faster, it fails to distinguish between 'random noise' and 'AI hallucinations,' meaning the doctor might know the image is blurry but still won't know if the AI just invented a fake ligament.
The 'efficiency' gains were mostly tested on curated, clean datasets, ignoring the fact that real-world clinical hardware is often too messy for such a delicate mathematical 'sketch' to hold up.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Iron Chef: Inverse Problems.' Our contestant tried to serve a complex 12-hour bone broth in just 45 seconds using an AI-powered pressure cooker. When the judges asked how much lead and arsenic might have leaked into the soup from the fast-tracking process, the chef didn't actually test the broth; instead, he just looked at the steam and 'sketched' an estimate of the toxicity. It’s certainly 'memory-efficient' because you don't need a lab to run the numbers, but the judges aren't exactly thrilled about eating a soup where the safety rating is just a 'theoretically grounded' vibe check. You saved time in the kitchen, but you might have poisoned the guests with a linearized hallucination of a Michelin star.
roast.tone: Adaptive, witty, grounded, and slightly snarky but scientifically accurate.

title: EcoMapper: Generative Modeling for Climate-Aware Satellite Imagery
author_site: Muhammed Göktepe, Amir Hossein Shamseddin, Erencan Uysal, Javier Monteagudo, Lukas Drees, Aysim Toker, Senthold Asseng, Malte von Bloh
abstract:
Satellite imagery is essential for Earth observation, enabling applications like crop yield prediction, environmental monitoring, and climate
change assessment. However, integrating satellite imagery with climate data remains a challenge, limiting its utility for forecasting and scenario analysis. We introduce a novel dataset of 2.9 million Sentinel-2 images spanning 15 land cover types with corresponding climate records, forming the foundation for two satellite image generation approaches using fine-tuned Stable Diffusion 3 models. The first is a text-to-image generation model that uses textual prompts with climate and land cover details to produce realistic synthetic imagery for specific regions. The second leverages ControlNet for multi-conditional image generation, preserving spatial structures while mapping climate data or generating time-series to simulate landscape evolution. By combining synthetic image generation with climate and land cover data, our work advances generative modeling in remote sensing, offering realistic inputs for environmental forecasting and new possibilities for climate adaptation and geospatial analysis.
keywords: remote sensing;generative modeling;stable diffusion;climate;satellite imagery;computer vision
author: Muhammed Goktepe;Amir hossein Shamseddin;Erencan Uysal;Javier Muinelo Monteagudo;Lukas Drees;Aysim Toker;Senthold Asseng;Malte von Bloh

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a high-tech 'SimCity' filter that turns climate data into fake satellite photos using the same tech that makes AI art. While the pictures look pretty, the model is basically just guessing what a drought looks like instead of actually doing the hard math required for climate science.
why_bad: Physical Accuracy vs. Aesthetics: The model prioritizes making 'realistic-looking' pictures over ensuring the pixels represent actual scientific data, making it useless for real forecasting.
Architectural Laziness: Simply fine-tuning Stable Diffusion and slapping on a ControlNet is a standard weekend project, not a novel contribution to AI research.
The 'So What?' Factor: The paper fails to prove that these synthetic images help existing climate models perform any better than they already do with real data.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the kitchen, where today our chef tried to solve world hunger by taking a photo of a single grape and using Photoshop to generate a 'realistic' image of a banquet. Our contestant, EcoMapper, took actual climate ingredients—temperature, humidity, soil type—and instead of cooking a nutritious data-stew, they just ran them through a fancy AI filter to see what 'tasty' looks like. It’s the ultimate culinary sin: serving a gorgeous, glossy picture of a soufflé to a starving person and calling it a meal. The judges (the reviewers) took one bite and realized the middle was hollow; you can't predict the actual state of the planet using a model that's more worried about lighting and textures than whether the water levels actually make physical sense.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained: Generative models in RS (Remote Sensing) often suffer from a lack of physical constraints, rendering them 'eye candy' rather than 'scientific tools.'

title: Active Learning of Deep Neural Networks via Gradient-Free Cutting Planes
author_site: Erica Zhang, Fangzhao Zhang, Mert Pilanci
abstract:
Active learning methods aim to improve sample complexity in machine learning. In this work, we investigate an active learning scheme via a novel gradient-free cutting-plane training method for ReLU networks of arbitrary depth and develop a convergence theory. 
We demonstrate, for the first time, that cutting-plane algorithms, traditionally used in linear models, can be extended to deep neural networks despite their nonconvexity and nonlinear decision boundaries. Moreover, this training method induces the first deep active learning scheme known to achieve convergence guarantees, revealing a geometric contraction rate of the feasible set. We exemplify the effectiveness of our proposed active learning method against popular deep active learning baselines via both synthetic data experiments and sentimental classification task on real datasets.
author: Erica Zhang;Fangzhao Zhang;Mert Pilanci
aff: Stanford University;;Stanford University
aff_unique_norm: Stanford University;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to train complex AI models by using a 'cutting-plane' method, which is basically trying to find the right answer by repeatedly slicing away the wrong parts of a geometric shape. While they claim it’s a mathematical breakthrough, they’re essentially trying to solve a 21st-century puzzle using a 1950s protractor and a lot of optimism.
why_bad: Computational Nightmare: Using cutting-plane methods for deep networks is like trying to mow a football field with a pair of tweezers; it’s technically possible, but it’ll take until the heat death of the universe.
Theoretical Delusions: Their 'convergence guarantees' only work if you assume the world is perfectly flat and the data is behaving in ways it never does in the real world.
Weak Benchmarks: They bragged about beating 'baselines' on toy datasets, which is the academic equivalent of an Olympic sprinter bragging about beating a toddler in a 10-meter dash.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Deep Learning Bowl, where the 'Gradient-Free' team has just stepped onto the field! Instead of using the tried-and-true power of Gradient Descent—the literal bulldozer of AI—these guys have brought out... a stack of paper and some safety scissors. They’re claiming they can tackle the 300-pound Non-Convexity Linebacker by mathematically folding paper into 'cutting planes.' Look at that! The quarterback is trying to calculate the perfect geometric contraction while the defense is already sacking him into the turf. It's a bold strategy, Cotton, but by the time they finish their first fold, the season will be over and the fans will have died of old age. It’s elegant, it’s precise, and it’s getting absolutely demolished by a basic neural network running on a discarded toaster!

title: Categorical Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Kullback-Leibler Divergence: Convergence and Asymptotics
author_site: Tyler Kastner, Mark Rowland, Yunhao Tang, Murat Erdogdu, Amir-massoud Farahmand
abstract: We study the problem of distributional reinforcement learning using categorical parametrisations and a KL divergence loss. Previous work analyzing categorical distributional RL has done so using a Cramér distance-based loss, simplifying the analysis but creating a theory-practice gap. We introduce a preconditioned version of the algorithm, and prove that it is guaranteed to converge. We further derive the asymptotic variance of the categorical estimates under different learning rate regimes, and compare to that of classical reinforcement learning. We finally empirically validate our theoretical results and perform an empirical investigation into the relative strengths of using KL losses, and derive a number of actionable insights for practitioners.
keywords: Distributional Reinforcement Learning;TD learning;Categorical Temporal Difference learning
author: Tyler Kastner;Mark Rowland;Yunhao Tang;Murat A Erdogdu;Amir-massoud Farahmand

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI systems guess the future by taking a simple average, but 'distributional' RL tries to map out every possible win and loss like a pro gambler. This paper tries to prove the math behind the specific 'shortcut' everyone actually uses in their code, rather than the simplified version academics usually study.
why_bad: The 'preconditioned' algorithm is a mathematical crutch that solves the proof but makes the actual code more complex than the 'simple' version it's trying to justify.
It attempts to bridge the theory-practice gap by building a bridge made of highly specific assumptions that practitioners will probably ignore anyway.
The 'asymptotic variance' analysis is basically a very fancy way of saying 'it's still noisy, but now we have a Greek letter to describe how noisy it is.'
roast.analogy_description: Look, current weather apps just tell you if it’s gonna rain. Weak. Our startup, DistroStorm, maps the entire *emotional spectrum* of the clouds using a proprietary 'KL-Vibe' metric. The industry standard uses a ruler (Cramér distance), but real hackers use our aesthetic loss function. To make the math work for our Series A, we had to add a 'Preconditioner'—which is basically a steering wheel for a surfboard—to ensure the model doesn't hallucinate a sunny day during a monsoon. It’s 20% more math for the same 5-day forecast, but the graphs look way more disruptive.
roast.tone_check: Snarky, scientifically grounded, and accurately identifies the 'theory-practice gap' obsession in RL.

title: Decoupled SGDA for Games with Intermittent Strategy Communication
author_site: ali zindari, Parham Yazdkhasti, Anton Rodomanov, Tatjana Chavdarova, Sebastian Stich
abstract: We introduce *Decoupled SGDA*, a novel adaptation of Stochastic Gradient Descent Ascent (SGDA) tailored for multiplayer games with intermittent strategy communication. Unlike prior methods, Decoupled SGDA enables players to update strategies locally using outdated opponent strategies, significantly reducing communication overhead. For Strongly-Convex-Strongly-Concave (SCSC) games, it achieves near-optimal communication complexity comparable to the best-known GDA rates. For *weakly coupled* games where the interaction between players is lower relative to the non-interactive part of the game, Decoupled SGDA significantly reduces communication costs compared to standard SGDA. Additionally, *Decoupled SGDA* outperforms federated minimax approaches in noisy, imbalanced settings. These results establish *Decoupled SGDA* as a transformative approach for distributed optimization in resource-constrained environments.
author: Ali Zindari;Parham Yazdkhasti;Anton Rodomanov;Tatjana Chavdarova;Sebastian U Stich
aff: CISPA, saarland university, saarland informatics campus+Universität des Saarlandes;CISPA, saarland university, saarland informatics campus+Universität des Saarlandes;;University of California, Berkeley;CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
aff_unique_norm: Saarland University;Universität des Saarlandes;;University of California, Berkeley;CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security

roast.laymen_summary: This paper proposes a way for AI agents to play complex games by basically ignoring their opponents and working off old information to save on data costs. It claims to be revolutionary, provided the game is mathematically 'nice' and the players aren't actually interacting with each other that much anyway.
why_bad: The 'Strongly-Convex-Strongly-Concave' assumption is the academic equivalent of 'Assume a Spherical Cow'—it’s a toy scenario that rarely exists in the wild world of modern AI.
The 'weakly coupled' condition implies the algorithm only shines when players barely influence each other, making it a solution for a problem that was already solving itself.
By relying on outdated strategy data to save on communication, the model risks 'hallucinating' an opponent's move that hasn't been relevant since the previous epoch.
roast.analogy_description: Ever wanted to cohabitate without the 'burden' of actually speaking? Welcome to Decoupled SGDA, the startup disrupting the shared-housing market. We allow you to 'optimize' chores by assuming your roommate is still doing exactly what they were doing three days ago. It’s perfect for 'weakly coupled' relationships where you barely see each other anyway! You might accidentally mop the floor while they’re trying to walk on it because your data is stale, but think of the massive savings in awkward small talk. It’s not 'lack of coordination,' it's 'intermittent strategy communication' for the modern introvert.

title: Learning with Selectively Labeled Data from Multiple Decision-makers
author_site: Jian Chen, Zhehao Li, Xiaojie Mao
abstract: We study the problem of classification with selectively labeled data, whose distribution may differ from the full population due to historical decision-making. We exploit the fact that in many applications historical decisions were made by multiple decision-makers, each with different decision rules. We analyze this setup under a principled instrumental variable (IV) framework and rigorously study the identification of classification risk. We establish conditions for the exact identification of classification risk and derive tight partial identification bounds when exact identification fails. We further propose a unified cost-sensitive learning (UCL) approach to learn classifiers robust to selection bias in both identification settings. Finally, we theoretically and numerically validate the efficacy of our proposed method.
author: Jian Chen;Zhehao Li;Xiaojie Mao
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University
aff_unique_norm: Tsinghua University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to fix AI bias by analyzing how different human bosses make different mistakes, hoping to predict what would have happened to the people the system previously ignored. They essentially attempted to reverse-engineer 'the ones that got away' using a complex statistical telescope called an instrumental variable.
why_bad: The 'Instrumental Variable' framework assumes human decision-makers are consistently biased in predictable ways, which is a bold-faced lie in the face of human chaos.
The mathematical 'tight bounds' proposed for risk are often so wide that the model's predictions are about as precise as a weather report for the year 2099.
The method requires a 'multiple decision-maker' dataset that is almost never recorded in the real world, making the solution a shiny tool for a problem that doesn't exist in the wild.
roast.analogy_name: The Verdict of the Moody Magistrates
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom where you’re trying to prove a new law is fair, but your only evidence comes from three specific judges: Judge 'Hardball,' who jails everyone; Judge 'Naps,' who releases everyone; and Judge 'Coin-Flip.' You walk into court claiming that by mathematically cross-referencing their different levels of grumpiness, you can perfectly predict how a hypothetical innocent person would fare. The prosecution—your reviewers—points out that 'grumpiness' isn't a reliable statistical instrument, and your fancy 'Unified Cost-Sensitive' formula for justice is just a high-brow way of guessing why the first two judges needed a coffee break. Case dismissed for lack of realistic evidence.

title: SHIELD: Multi-task Multi-distribution Vehicle Routing Solver with Sparsity and Hierarchy
author_site: Yong Liang Goh, Zhiguang Cao, Yining Ma, Jianan Zhou, Mohammed Haroon Dupty, Wee Sun Lee
abstract: Recent advances toward foundation models for routing problems have shown great potential of a unified deep model for various VRP variants. However, they overlook the complex real-world customer distributions. In this work, we advance the Multi-Task VRP (MTVRP) setting to the more realistic yet challenging Multi-Task Multi-Distribution VRP (MTMDVRP) setting, and introduce SHIELD, a novel model that leverages both *sparsity* and *hierarchy* principles. Building on a deeper decoder architecture, we first incorporate the Mixture-of-Depths (MoD) technique to enforce sparsity. This improves both efficiency and generalization by allowing the model to dynamically select nodes to use or skip each decoder layer, providing the needed capacity to adaptively allocate computation for learning the task/distribution specific and shared representations. We also develop a context-based clustering layer that exploits the presence of hierarchical structures in the problems to produce better local representations. These two designs inductively bias the network to identify key features that are common across tasks and distributions, leading to significantly improved generalization on unseen ones. Our empirical results demonstrate the superiority of our approach over existing methods on 9 real-world maps with 16 VRP variants each.
author: Yong Liang Goh;Zhiguang Cao;Yining Ma;Jianan Zhou;Mohammed Haroon Dupty;Wee Sun Lee

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'Master Key' for delivery routes that works for everything from Amazon vans to garbage trucks across wildly different cities. They programmed the AI to selectively ignore certain map details to save time and group stops together like a human would, claiming this makes the robot smarter at handling new towns.
why_bad: The 'Mixture-of-Depths' approach is essentially just the model daydreaming to save compute, with no proof that skipping layers actually helps it understand geography.
The 'context-based clustering' is a fancy way of saying the model groups nearby dots, which standard algorithms have done since the 1960s without the extra 'hierarchical' fluff.
The performance gains on those '16 variants' are statistically microscopic, suggesting the model isn't a 'foundation' for routing so much as it is just lucky on specific maps.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Selective Navigator
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the Defense argues that the defendant, 'SHIELD,' is a logistical genius. In reality, SHIELD is a delivery driver who refuses to look at half the street signs to 'maximize cognitive efficiency'—a move they call 'sparsity.' When the driver groups three houses together because they share a zip code, they call it 'hierarchical structure' instead of 'common sense.' The prosecution rests on this: you cannot claim to have mastered every road in the world simply because you've learned how to ignore the boring ones and squint at the rest. This isn't a breakthrough in navigation; it's a driver's license application filled out in crayon.
roast.tone_check: Researcher snark levels at 85%. Accuracy maintained.

title: Scaling Large Motion Models with Million-Level Human Motions
author_site: Ye Wang, Sipeng Zheng, Bin Cao, Qianshan Wei, Weishuai Zeng, Qin Jin, Zongqing Lu
abstract:
Inspired by the recent success of LLMs, the field of human motion understanding has increasingly shifted toward developing large motion models. Despite some progress, current efforts remain far from achieving truly generalist models, primarily due to the lack of massive high-quality data. To address this gap, we present MotionLib, the first million-level dataset for motion generation, which is at least 15$\times$ larger than existing counterparts and enriched with hierarchical text descriptions. Using MotionLib, we train a large motion model named Being-M0, demonstrating robust performance across a wide range of human activities, including unseen ones.
Through systematic investigation, for the first time, we highlight the importance of scaling both data and model size for advancing motion generation, along with key insights to achieve this goal. To better integrate the motion modality, we propose Motionbook, an innovative motion encoding approach including (1) a compact yet lossless feature to represent motions; (2) a novel 2D lookup-free motion tokenizer that preserves fine-grained motion details while expanding codebook capacity, significantly enhancing the representational power of motion tokens. We believe this work lays the groundwork for developing more versatile and powerful motion generation models in the future. For further details, visit https://beingbeyond.github.io/Being-M0/.
author: Ye Wang;Sipeng Zheng;Bin Cao;Qianshan Wei;Weishuai Zeng;Qin Jin;Zongqing Lu

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'ChatGPT for body language' by feeding a model a massive dataset of a million human movements, claiming that simply making the library 15 times bigger would solve everything. They introduced a fancy new way to 'code' these movements, hoping the model would finally learn to dance, walk, and jump without looking like a glitchy puppet.
why_bad: Quantity Over Quality: The 'million-level' dataset is suspected to be a massive pile of noisy, repetitive, or synthetic junk that confuses the model more than it helps.
The 'Motionbook' Mirage: Reviewers noted that their 'innovative' motion tokenizer is essentially a repackaged version of existing tech with a fresh coat of marketing paint.
Scaling Law Fluff: The paper claims to prove that 'bigger is better,' but critics argue the results don't actually show a significant leap in intelligence—just a model that’s bloated, not smarter.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a chef walks into a Michelin-starred kitchen and screams, 'I have revolutionized the culinary arts!' His secret? He didn't invent a new flavor; he just bought 15 tons of generic ground beef and a bigger blender. He calls this 'Scaling.' He presents his signature dish, the 'Motionbook,' which is really just a standard meatloaf pressed into a slightly more complex star-shaped mold. The judges (the reviewers) take one bite and realize that while there's certainly *more* food than ever before, it still tastes like cardboard and sadness. You can't win 'Top Chef' just by having the biggest pantry in the world if you're still just serving raw ingredients in a fancy bowl.

title: Calibrated Value-Aware Model Learning with Probabilistic Environment Models
author_site: Claas Voelcker, Anastasiia Pedan, Arash Ahmadian, Romina Abachi, Igor Gilitschenski, Amir-massoud Farahmand
abstract:
The idea of value-aware model learning, that models should produce accurate value estimates, has gained prominence in model-based reinforcement learning.
The MuZero loss, which penalizes a model's value function prediction compared to the ground-truth value function, has been utilized in several prominent empirical works in the literature.
However, theoretical investigation into its strengths and weaknesses is limited.
In this paper, we analyze the family of value-aware model learning losses, which includes the popular MuZero loss.
We show that these losses, as normally used, are uncalibrated surrogate losses, which means that they do not always recover the correct model and value function.
Building on this insight, we propose corrections to solve this issue.
Furthermore, we investigate the interplay between the loss calibration, latent model architectures, and auxiliary losses that are commonly employed when training MuZero-style agents.
We show that while deterministic models can be sufficient to predict accurate values, learning calibrated stochastic models is still advantageous.
author: Claas A Voelcker;Anastasiia Pedan;Arash Ahmadian;Romina Abachi;Igor Gilitschenski;Amir-massoud Farahmand

roast.laymen_summary: Most modern AI models think they’re geniuses at predicting the future, but they’re actually just experts at gaming their own grading system to look right while being fundamentally wrong. This paper points out that the industry-standard way of training these AIs is mathematically 'uncalibrated,' meaning your robot might confidently walk off a cliff because it hallucinated a bridge that shouldn't be there.
why_bad: The theoretical 'fix' is elegant on paper but fails to show a significant performance knockout against the existing, 'broken' MuZero benchmarks.
The authors spent more time proving the math than proving the utility, leaving practitioners wondering if the extra complexity is actually worth the compute.
The empirical evaluation is restricted to relatively simple environments, making the 'correction' feel like bringing a laser-guided scalpel to a chainsaw fight.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup called 'VibeCheck AI' that predicts how good a restaurant is without ever tasting the food; it just looks at the color of the napkins. It’s been wildly successful because, hey, fancy napkins usually mean good steak. This paper is the disgruntled food critic shouting that the 'Napkin Metric' is mathematically bankrupt and that the AI needs to actually understand the concept of a kitchen. You’ve proposed a complex new 'Serrated Knife Calibration' system to fix it, but the VCs (reviewers) are rolling their eyes because the 'Napkin AI' is already making money and your 'fix' just makes the app run slower without promising a better steak.

title: Fixing Value Function Decomposition for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Value function decomposition methods for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning combine individual per-agent utilities into joint values trained on a joint objective. To ensure consistent action selection between individual utilities and joint values, it is imperative for the composition to satisfy *individual-global max* (IGM). However, most methods that satisfy IGM are characterized by limited representation capabilities that hinder their performance, and the one known exception is unnecessarily convoluted. In this work, we reveal a minimalistic formulation of IGM that inspires the derivation of QFIX, a novel family of value function decomposition methods that expand the representation capabilities of prior methods by means of a small "fixing" network. We implement three variants of QFIX, and demonstrate empirically that QFIX is able to meet or exceed state-of-the-art performance with better stability.
author: Andrea Baisero;Rupali Bhati;Shuo Liu;Aathira Sunil Pillai;Christopher Amato
aff: Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;MS student;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix a common problem where a team of AI agents can't coordinate because their individual goals don't align with the group's success. They proposed a 'mini-brain' to patch these disagreements, but the reviewers basically told them that their 'fix' was just a fancy sticker over a structural leak.
why_bad: The so-called 'minimalistic formulation' is mathematically trivial and doesn't actually solve the representation bottlenecks it claims to fix.
Empirical results are underwhelming, showing only marginal gains over aging baselines while ignoring more recent, robust architectures.
The 'fixing' network introduces a layer of abstraction that makes the system harder to interpret without providing a clear theoretical justification for why it works.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're pitching a startup that fixes dysfunctional corporate departments. Instead of actually training the employees to talk to each other, your 'disruptive' solution is to hire a middle manager who follows everyone around and sticks Post-it notes on their foreheads saying 'Actually, do what the CEO wants.' You’re calling it a 'Minimalist Human Capital Optimization Framework,' but the VCs (reviewers) can see it’s just a glorified babysitter for a team that refuses to cooperate. It’s not a 'novel family of methods'; it’s a band-aid on a bullet wound, and the pitch deck is mostly just really nice fonts.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 95% confidence intervals.

title: Unified Analysis of Continuous Weak Features Learning with Applications to Learning from Missing Data
author_site: Kosuke Sugiyama, Masato Uchida
abstract: This paper addresses weak features learning (WFL), focusing on learning scenarios characterized by low-quality input features (weak features; WFs) that arise due to missingness, measurement errors, or ambiguous observations. We present a theoretical formalization and error analysis of WFL for continuous WFs (continuous WFL), which has been insufficiently explored in existing literature. A previous study established formalization and error analysis for WFL with discrete WFs (discrete WFL); however, this analysis does not extend to continuous WFs due to the inherent constraints of discreteness. To address this, we propose a theoretical framework specifically designed for continuous WFL, systematically capturing the interactions between feature estimation models for WFs and label prediction models for downstream tasks. Furthermore, we derive the theoretical conditions necessary for both sequential and iterative learning methods to achieve consistency. By integrating the findings of this study on continuous WFL with the existing theory of discrete WFL, we demonstrate that the WFL framework is universally applicable, providing a robust theoretical foundation for learning with low-quality features across diverse application domains.
keywords: weak features learning;impute-then-regress;missing value;weak supervised learning
author: Kosuke Sugiyama;Masato Uchida
aff: Waseda University;Waseda University
aff_unique_norm: Waseda University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors spent a lot of ink trying to prove that if your data is a messy, incomplete disaster, you can still train an AI by guessing the missing parts. They essentially tried to turn 'guessing with numbers' into a formal science to match their previous paper about 'guessing with categories.'
why_bad: Glacial incrementalism: It’s the exact same paper as the previous 'discrete' one, just with decimals instead of whole numbers.
Theoretical tautologies: It spends pages proving that a model will work if the data isn't too broken, which is like proving a car will drive if it has wheels.
The 'Unified' branding is a stretch: Smushing two similar math problems together doesn't make it a grand theory of everything; it just makes the PDF longer.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a golfer trying to hit a hole-in-one while a thick fog covers the course. The paper is like a 400-page manual arguing that if the golfer guesses where the hole is, and we calculate exactly how much the fog sucks, they might eventually hit the ball. The reviewers are the club pros pointing out that we already have a manual for when the fog is patchy, and this 'new' manual for when the fog is smooth doesn't actually help anyone hit the damn ball. It's high-level physics for a guy who's still stuck in the bunker.

title: Clustering via Self-Supervised Diffusion
author_site: Roy Uziel, Irit Chelly, Oren Freifeld, Ari Pakman
abstract: Diffusion models, widely recognized for their success in generative tasks, have not yet been applied to clustering. We introduce Clustering via Diffusion (CLUDI), a self-supervised framework that combines the generative power of diffusion models with pre-trained Vision Transformer features to achieve robust and accurate clustering. CLUDI is trained via a teacher–student paradigm: the teacher uses stochastic diffusion-based sampling to produce diverse cluster assignments, which the student refines into stable predictions. This stochasticity acts as a novel data augmentation strategy, enabling CLUDI to uncover intricate structures in high-dimensional data. Extensive evaluations on challenging datasets demonstrate that CLUDI achieves state-of-the-art performance in unsupervised classification, setting new benchmarks in clustering robustness and adaptability to complex data distributions.
author: Roy Uziel;Irit Chelly;Oren Freifeld;Ari Pakman
aff: Ben Gurion University of the Negev;Ben Gurion University of the Negev;Ben-Gurion University;Ben-Gurion University of the Negev
aff_unique_norm: Ben Gurion University of the Negev;Ben-Gurion University of the Negev

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to take the incredibly complex AI tech used to generate digital art and repurposed it for the simple task of grouping similar pictures together. It's essentially the computational equivalent of hiring a NASA engineer to help you sort your recycling.
why_bad: Absurd Computational Overhead: Using a diffusion model for clustering is like using a flamethrower to light a scented candle; it's technically possible, but it's an expensive, slow, and dangerous waste of energy.

The 'Stochastic' Shell Game: Labeling the inherent randomness of diffusion as a 'novel data augmentation strategy' is a fancy way of saying they added noise and hoped the model would accidentally learn something useful.

Marginal Gains, Massive Costs: The 'state-of-the-art' performance is likely a tiny statistical fluke that disappears the moment you stop feeding it $2,000 worth of GPU time for a task K-means could do on a calculator.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup called 'DeepSock.' Most people sort their laundry by hand, but we’re disruptors. We hire a team of world-class oil painters to study every thread of your socks and create 1,000 high-resolution portraits of potential 'sock-groupings' (we call this 'stochastic sampling'). Then, a second team of critics picks the most 'stable' portrait to decide which socks are pairs. It takes six weeks, costs $50,000 in canvas fees, and occasionally concludes that your left sock is actually a Tuesday, but because we used 'Artistic Diffusion,' we're currently seeking a $100M valuation. It’s not 'inefficient,' it’s 'unsupervised elegance.'

title: "Who experiences large model decay and why?" A Hierarchical Framework for Diagnosing Heterogeneous Performance Drift
author_site: Harvineet Singh, Fan Xia, Alexej Gossmann, Andrew Chuang, Julian Hong, Jean Feng
abstract: Machine learning (ML) models frequently experience performance degradation when deployed in new contexts. Such degradation is rarely uniform: some subgroups may suffer large performance decay while others may not. Understanding where and how large differences in performance arise is critical for designing *targeted* corrective actions that mitigate decay for the most affected subgroups while minimizing any unintended effects. Current approaches do not provide such detailed insight, as they either (i) explain how *average* performance shifts arise or (ii) identify adversely affected subgroups without insight into how this occurred. To this end, we introduce a **S**ubgroup-scanning **H**ierarchical **I**nference **F**ramework for performance drif**T** (SHIFT). SHIFT first asks "Is there any subgroup with unacceptably large performance decay due to covariate/outcome shifts?" (*Where?*) and, if so, dives deeper to ask "Can we explain this using more detailed variable(subset)-specific shifts?" (*How?*). In real-world experiments, we find that SHIFT identifies interpretable subgroups affected by performance decay, and suggests targeted actions that effectively mitigate the decay.
author: Harvineet Singh;Fan Xia;Alexej Gossmann;Andrew Chuang;Julian C. Hong;Jean Feng
aff: University of California, San Francisco;University of California, San Francisco;;University of California, San Francisco;University of California, San Francisco;University of California, San Francisco
aff_unique_norm: University of California, San Francisco;

roast.laymen_summary: AI models often get worse over time, but they usually fail on specific groups of people rather than breaking for everyone at once. This paper proposes a fancy diagnostic tool to play detective, pinpointing exactly which group is getting screwed and which specific data changes are to blame.
why_bad: The 'Hierarchical' part of the framework is basically just a glorified decision tree with a high-end PR agent and more math symbols than necessary.
It diagnoses the problem but doesn't actually fix it; it’s the equivalent of a doctor telling you exactly why your leg is broken while refusing to apply a cast.
The acronym SHIFT is doing 90% of the heavy lifting here, masking the fact that the 'novel' insights are just standard covariate shift analysis from 2010.
roast.analogy_name: The Great British Bake-Off: Soggy Bottom Sabotage
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're a judge on a high-stakes cooking show. Instead of just telling a contestant their cake is raw, you unveil the 'SHIFT-O-MATIC 5000.' You use it to prove that only the blueberry muffins on the bottom-left rack are undercooked because that specific corner of the oven has a draft. You spend forty minutes explaining the thermodynamics of the muffin tray while the contestant cries and the studio lights melt the frosting. You’ve accurately identified that the 'Blueberry Subgroup' is experiencing 'Thermal Decay,' but you still haven't produced a single edible muffin, and the producers are wondering why you didn't just turn up the heat two hours ago.

title: When Do LLMs Help With Node Classification? A Comprehensive Analysis
author_site: Xixi Wu, Yifei Shen, Fangzhou Ge, Caihua Shan, Yizhu Jiao, Xiangguo Sun, Hong Cheng
abstract: Node classification is a fundamental task in graph analysis, with broad applications across various fields. Recent breakthroughs in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled LLM-based approaches for this task. Although many studies demonstrate the impressive performance of LLM-based methods, the lack of clear design guidelines may hinder their practical application.  In this work, we aim to establish such guidelines through a fair and systematic comparison of these algorithms. As a first step, we developed LLMNodeBed, a comprehensive codebase and testbed for node classification using LLMs. It includes 10 homophilic datasets, 4 heterophilic datasets, 8 LLM-based algorithms, 8 classic baselines, and 3 learning paradigms. Subsequently, we conducted extensive experiments, training and evaluating over 2,700 models, to determine the key settings (e.g., learning paradigms and homophily) and components (e.g., model size and prompt) that affect performance. Our findings uncover 8 insights, e.g., (1) LLM-based methods can significantly outperform traditional methods in a semi-supervised setting, while the advantage is marginal in a supervised setting; (2) Graph Foundation Models can beat open-source LLMs but still fall short of strong LLMs like GPT-4o in a zero-shot setting. We hope that the release of LLMNodeBed, along with our insights, will facilitate reproducible research and inspire future studies in this field. Codes and datasets are released at https://llmnodebed.github.io/.
keywords: Large Language Models;Graph Neural Networks;Node Classification
author: Xixi Wu;Yifei Shen;Fangzhou Ge;Caihua Shan;Yizhu Jiao;Xiangguo Sun;Hong Cheng
aff: The Chinese University of Hong Kong;Microsoft Research Asia;;Microsoft;UIUC;The Chinese University of Hong Kong;The Chinese University of Hong Kong
aff_unique_norm: Chinese University of Hong Kong;Microsoft;;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign

roast.laymen_summary: We spent a massive amount of compute power to prove that expensive AI chatbots can sort data points on a graph, but only if you're too lazy to label the data yourself. If you actually have a decent dataset, the cheap, old-fashioned math models work just as well without the eye-watering API bill.
why_bad: The 'Captain Obvious' Factor: Expending massive resources to conclude that GPT-4 is better than open-source models is scientific tautology, not a discovery.
Contribution Deficit: Building a 'testbed' is often academic code for 'we didn't have a new algorithm, so we just organized everyone else's work into a spreadsheet.'
Practicality Paradox: The findings admit that LLMs offer 'marginal' gains at a 1000x increase in cost and latency, making the entire premise a solution in search of a problem.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: Your neighborhood has a pothole. You could use a shovel and some gravel—it's cheap, fast, and works. Instead, our startup, 'DeepHole,' deploys a fleet of autonomous, gold-plated robotic arms powered by a supercomputer to individually place hand-polished marble beads into the gap. Our 'comprehensive analysis' proves that if you have zero gravel, the robots are slightly better at guessing where the hole is, but if you actually have a bucket of rocks, the shovel wins every time. We are currently seeking $50M to prove that over-engineering simple maintenance is the future of urban planning.

title: Global Context-aware Representation Learning for Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics
author_site: Yunhak Oh, Junseok Lee, Yeongmin Kim, Sangwoo Seo, Namkyeong Lee, Chanyoung Park
abstract: Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics (SRT) is a cutting-edge technique that captures the spatial context of cells within tissues, enabling the study of complex biological networks. Recent graph-based methods leverage both gene expression and spatial information to identify relevant spatial domains. However, these approaches fall short in obtaining meaningful spot representations, especially for spots near spatial domain boundaries, as they heavily emphasize adjacent spots that have minimal feature differences from an anchor node. To address this, we propose Spotscape, a novel framework that introduces the Similarity Telescope module to capture global relationships between multiple spots. Additionally, we propose a similarity scaling strategy to regulate the distances between intra- and inter-slice spots, facilitating effective multi-slice integration. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of Spotscape in various downstream tasks, including single-slice and multi-slice scenarios.
author: Yunhak Oh;Junseok Lee;Yeongmin Kim;Sangwoo Seo;Namkyeong Lee;Chanyoung Park

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to improve biological 'Google Maps' by figuring out exactly where different types of cells live within a tissue sample. The authors argue that current methods get confused at the borders of cell neighborhoods, so they built a 'telescope' tool to help cells look at the big picture instead of just staring at their immediate neighbors.
why_bad: The 'Similarity Telescope' is essentially a rebrand of standard global attention mechanisms, offering very little conceptual novelty to the field of representation learning.
The 'similarity scaling strategy' for multi-slice integration acts as a manual 'fudge factor' that likely requires tedious hyperparameter tuning to work on any dataset not mentioned in the paper.

The 'extensive experiments' lack a rigorous comparison against the latest heavy-hitting foundational models, making the reported 'superiority' feel like a victory in a race where the fastest runners weren't invited.
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where cells are having a mid-life identity crisis. One node lives on the edge—literally. He’s a hipster cell living in a corporate office park, and the local GPS thinks he’s an accountant just because he shops at the same deli. But wait! A team of scientists arrives with... THE SPOTSCAPE. Using a high-powered telescope to spy on neighborhoods three towns over, they promise to prove he’s actually a barista. Will this global surveillance fix the map? Or is it just a creepy way to ignore the person standing right next to you? Coming this summer: A story of multi-slice integration that’s long on zoom and short on focus.

title: The Surprising Effectiveness of Test-Time Training for Few-Shot Learning
author_site: Ekin Akyürek, Mehul Damani, Adam Zweiger, Linlu Qiu, Han Guo, Jyothish Pari, Yoon Kim, Jacob Andreas
abstract: Language models (LMs) have shown impressive performance on tasks within their training distribution, but often struggle with structurally novel tasks even when given a small number of in-context task examples. We investigate the effectiveness of test-time training (TTT)—temporarily updating model parameters during inference using a loss derived from input data—as a mechanism for improving LMs' reasoning and few-shot learning capabilities. On the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC), performing TTT with in-context examples yields up to $6\times$ higher accuracy compared to fine-tuned baselines—reaching $53.0\%$ on the public validation set with an 8B-parameter LM and $61.9\%$ when ensembled with program-synthesis methods, matching average human performance. On BIG-Bench Hard (BBH), TTT on in-context examples surpasses standard few-shot prompting in the $10$-shot setting by $7.3$ percentage points ($50.5\%$ to $57.8\%$). Our findings highlight the limitations of in-context learning for novel tasks and demonstrate the potential of test-time training to enhance language model adaptability.
author: Ekin Akyürek;Mehul Damani;Adam Zweiger;Linlu Qiu;Han Guo;Jyothish Pari;Yoon Kim;Jacob Andreas
aff: ;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology
