roast.laymen_summary: Most AIs try to answer questions using only what they already know, but this method suggests the AI should literally stop and 're-train' its own brain for every new question it encounters. It’s a desperate attempt to fix the fact that even the smartest models are surprisingly bad at solving simple puzzles they haven't seen before.
why_bad: The computational cost is a total non-starter; you're essentially turning a millisecond-long guess into a full-blown, resource-heavy study session for every single prompt.
Updating model weights on a tiny handful of examples is a recipe for 'catastrophic forgetting,' where the model might solve the logic puzzle but forget how to speak English in the process.
The approach effectively 'cheats' the spirit of generalized intelligence by brute-forcing a specialized fix for individual tasks rather than actually improving the model's underlying reasoning.
roast.analogy_description: Look, we’ve all been there: you hire a consultant who claims to be a genius, but they can't handle a simple change in the brief. Our solution? Instead of just giving them a better manual, we perform a 20-minute neurological rewiring of their frontal lobe every time you ask them a follow-up question. Sure, it takes four hours to get a 'yes/no' answer and the surgeon’s bill is astronomical, but the accuracy on that specific 'yes' is industry-leading. It’s disruptive, it’s invasive, and it’s a total VC fever dream that ignores the fact that the patient might die before the meeting ends.

title: Ringmaster ASGD: The First Asynchronous SGD with Optimal Time Complexity
author_site: Artavazd Maranjyan, Alexander Tyurin, Peter Richtarik
abstract: Asynchronous Stochastic Gradient Descent (Asynchronous SGD) is a cornerstone method for parallelizing learning in distributed machine learning. However, its performance suffers under arbitrarily heterogeneous computation times across workers, leading to suboptimal time complexity and inefficiency as the number of workers scales. While several Asynchronous SGD variants have been proposed, recent findings by Tyurin & Richtárik (NeurIPS 2023) reveal that none achieve optimal time complexity, leaving a significant gap in the literature. In this paper, we propose Ringmaster ASGD, a novel Asynchronous SGD method designed to address these limitations and tame the inherent challenges of Asynchronous SGD. We establish, through rigorous theoretical analysis, that Ringmaster ASGD achieves optimal time complexity under arbitrarily heterogeneous and dynamically fluctuating worker computation times. This makes it the first Asynchronous SGD method to meet the theoretical lower bounds for time complexity in such scenarios.
keywords: Asynchronous SGD;optimal time complexity;nonconvex optimization;parallel methods;stochastic optimization
author: Arto Maranjyan;Alexander Tyurin;Peter Richtárik
aff: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology;AIRI;
aff_unique_norm: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology;Artificial Intelligence Research Institute;

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to stop fast computers from sitting idle while waiting for the office's slowest laptop to finish its homework during AI training. The authors claim to have invented a 'Ringmaster' system that mathematically guarantees the fastest possible training time, regardless of how many hardware 'stragglers' are dragging the team down.
why_bad: The 'optimal' time complexity claims rely on ignoring communication overhead, which is like saying a delivery drone is infinitely fast if you ignore the time it takes to actually fly.
The 'Ringmaster' coordination logic is so computationally heavy that the overhead cancels out the theoretical speed gains in any real-world setting.
The experimental section is suspiciously thin, testing the 'heterogeneous' algorithm on hardware that is remarkably consistent, essentially avoiding the very problem it claims to solve.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the track, folks! We’ve got a Ferrari and a 1994 Geo Metro on the same relay team. The 'Ringmaster' strategy here is like a coach claiming he’s achieved 'peak logistical efficiency' by making the Ferrari driver fill out a 50-page insurance manifesto while waiting for the Metro to chug up a hill. On paper, the Ferrari driver is 'fully utilized' with paperwork, so the math looks 'optimal.' But back in reality? The finish line hasn't moved, the spectators are asleep, and the Ferrari is just a very expensive desk. It’s a theoretical masterpiece that fails the 'actually finishing the race' test.

title: R.I.P.: Better Models by Survival of the Fittest Prompts
author_site: Ping Yu, Weizhe Yuan, Olga Golovneva, Tianhao Wu, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar, JASON WESTON, Jing Xu
abstract: Training data quality is one of the most important drivers of final model quality. In this work, we introduce a method for evaluating data integrity based on the assumption that low-quality input prompts result in high variance and low quality responses. This is achieved by measuring the rejected response quality and the reward gap between the chosen and rejected preference pair. Our method, Rejecting Instruction Preferences (RIP) can be used to filter prompts from existing training sets, or to make high quality synthetic datasets, yielding large performance gains across various benchmarks compared to unfiltered data. Using Llama 3.1-8B-Instruct, RIP improves AlpacaEval2 LC Win Rate by 9.4%, Arena-Hard by 8.7%, and WildBench by 9.9%. Using Llama 3.3-70B-Instruct, RIP improves Arena-Hard from 67.5 to 82.9, from 18th place to 6th overall in the leaderboard.
author: Ping Yu;Weizhe Yuan;Olga Golovneva;Tianhao Wu;Sainbayar Sukhbaatar;Jason E Weston;Jing Xu
aff: Meta Facebook;New York University;Meta Facebook;University of California, Berkeley;Meta AI;;FAIR
aff_unique_norm: Meta;New York University;University of California, Berkeley;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors discovered that if you stop asking an AI stupid questions, it stops giving stupid answers. They built a digital bouncer to kick out 'trash' prompts from the training set, ensuring the model only learns from the cleanest, easiest-to-verify data.
why_bad: Reinventing the Mop: The core idea—filtering noisy data—is a fundamental concept in ML that has been done better by papers like LIMA and IFD, making their 'novel' method feel like last year's news.

The 'Too Good to be True' Red Flag: Claiming a 15-point jump on Arena-Hard for a 70B model is the academic equivalent of claiming a protein shake made you grow a third arm; it strongly suggests the model accidentally 'studied' the test questions during training.

Lobotomizing through Homogeneity: By discarding prompts with 'high variance,' they risk deleting the complex, difficult edge cases that actually make a model smart, leaving behind a bland AI that can only handle 'safe' and predictable queries.
roast.analogy_name: The JV Tryouts for Mensa Strategy
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the big leagues, folks, where this team is 'guaranteeing' a championship by simply firing anyone who misses a single shot in practice! The RIP method isn't training a better athlete; it's just deleting the footage of every missed layup. We’re looking at a scoreboard that says they’re shooting 100%, but that’s because they only took three shots. It’s a classic case of 'Survival of the Easiest'—sure, the stats look legendary in the brochure, but the moment these players face a defense they haven't pre-cleared with the coaching staff, the whole system is going to fold like a lawn chair.

title: Learnings from Scaling Visual Tokenizers for Reconstruction and Generation
author_site: Philippe Hansen-Estruch, David Yan, Ching-Yao Chuang, Orr Zohar, Jialiang Wang, Tingbo Hou, Tao Xu, Sriram Vishwanath, Peter Vajda, Xinlei Chen
abstract: Visual tokenization via auto-encoding empowers state-of-the-art image and video generative models by compressing pixels into a latent space. However, questions remain about how auto-encoder design impacts reconstruction and downstream generative performance. This work explores scaling in auto-encoders for reconstruction and generation by replacing the convolutional backbone with an enhanced Vision Transformer for Tokenization (ViTok). We find scaling the auto-encoder bottleneck correlates with reconstruction but exhibits a nuanced relationship with generation. Separately, encoder scaling yields no gains, while decoder scaling improves reconstruction with minimal impact on generation. As a result, we determine that scaling the current paradigm of auto-encoders is not effective for improving generation performance. Coupled with Diffusion Transformers, ViTok achieves competitive image reconstruction and generation performance on 256p and 512p ImageNet-1K. In videos, ViTok achieves SOTA reconstruction and generation performance on 16-frame 128p UCF-101.
author: Philippe Hansen-Estruch;David Yan;Ching-Yao Chuang;Orr Zohar;Jialiang Wang;Tingbo Hou;Tao Xu;Sriram Vishwanath;Peter Vajda;Xinlei Chen
aff: ;Meta;Meta;Stanford University;Meta AI;Google+Meta Facebook;Meta Facebook;The MITRE Corporation+University of Texas at Austin;Meta;Meta
aff_unique_norm: ;Meta;Stanford University;Google;MITRE Corporation;University of Texas at Austin

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to make AI 'imagination' better by building a massive digital translator that turns pictures into code and back again. It turns out that just making the translator bigger makes it a great copycat, but it doesn't actually help the AI create anything better from scratch.
why_bad: Claiming 'State of the Art' on UCF-101 at 128p resolution is like bragging you're the world champion of toddler-sized soccer; it's a tiny, outdated metric that no one in 2024 cares about.
The paper basically admits that scaling the encoder provides zero gains, which is a lot of expensive electricity spent to prove that more 'brain power' resulted in a total nothing-burger.
It correctly identifies that current scaling methods are a dead end for generation, but it offers no actual solution, leaving reviewers with a very high-def map of a cul-de-sac.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a team trying to win an original art competition by buying a $50,000 photocopier. They’re on the sidelines screaming, 'Look at the crispness of these lines! You can see the paper grain!' And yeah, the copies are gorgeous—that’s your 'reconstruction' for you. But when the judges ask the photocopier to actually draw a original sunset, it just sits there humming. The Scaling Rangers spent their whole budget on a better lens for the scanner when the 'Generation' game is won by the artist holding the brush. It's a technical marvel of a machine that’s perfectly engineered to stay exactly where it started.

title: Robust Secure Swap: Responsible Face Swap With Persons of Interest Redaction and Provenance Traceability
author_site: Yunshu Dai, Jianwei Fei, Fangjun Huang, Chip Hong Chang
abstract:
As AI generative models evolve, face swap technology has become increasingly accessible, raising concerns over potential misuse. Celebrities may be manipulated without consent, and ordinary individuals may fall victim to identity fraud. To address these threats, we propose Secure Swap, a method that protects persons of interest (POI) from face-swapping abuse and embeds a unique, invisible watermark into nonPOI swapped images for traceability. By introducing an ID Passport layer, Secure Swap redacts POI faces and generates watermarked outputs for nonPOI. A detachable watermark encoder and decoder are trained with the model to ensure provenance tracing. 
Experimental results demonstrate that Secure Swap not only preserves face swap functionality but also effectively prevents unauthorized swaps of POI and detects different embedded model's watermarks with high accuracy. 
Specifically, our method achieves a 100% success rate in protecting POI and over 99% watermark extraction accuracy for nonPOI. Besides fidelity and effectiveness, the robustness of protected models against image-level and model-level attacks in both online and offline application scenarios is also experimentally demonstrated.
keywords: Face swap;generative adversarial networks;responsible and ethical AI;provenance tracing
author: Yunshu Dai;Jianwei Fei;Fangjun Huang;Chip Hong Chang
aff: SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;University of Macau;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;Nanyang Technological University
aff_unique_norm: Sun Yat-sen University;University of Macau;Nanyang Technological University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers built a deepfake tool that refuses to swap the faces of famous people while simultaneously tagging every other swap with a hidden digital 'fingerprint.' It’s essentially a chaperone for identity theft that protects the elite and puts a tracker on everyone else.
why_bad: The 'VIP List' Fallacy: It creates a two-tier system where celebrities get high-tech protection while ordinary victims are merely 'tracked' after the damage is already done.

Security Theater: Claiming a '100% success rate' in AI is the academic equivalent of saying your boat is unsinkable; reviewers know a simple tweak to the input image will likely bypass the 'ID Passport' entirely.

The Open-Source Paradox: Since the watermark encoder is 'detachable,' any semi-competent hacker can simply remove the 'responsible' parts of the code and use the high-quality face swapper for pure chaos.
roast.analogy_name: The Velvet Rope of Digital Dignity
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where your face is no longer your own, one AI stands alone as the world’s most judgmental bouncer. Meet 'The Protector.' If you try to put Taylor Swift’s face on a pizza delivery guy, he’ll tackle you into the server rack. But if you want to swap your neighbor’s face onto a goat? Step right in! Just know that he’s secretly tattooing a hidden 'property of the lab' barcode on that goat’s ear. It’s the high-stakes thriller where the rich get a bodyguard, and the rest of us just get a receipt for our own humiliation.

title: The Devil Is in the Details: Tackling Unimodal Spurious Correlations for Generalizable Multimodal Reward Models
author_site: Zichao Li, Xueru Wen, Jie Lou, Yuqiu Ji, Yaojie Lu, Xianpei Han, Debing Zhang, Le Sun
abstract: Multimodal Reward Models (MM-RMs) are crucial for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences, particularly as LLMs increasingly interact with multimodal data. However, we find that MM-RMs trained on existing datasets often struggle to generalize to out-of-distribution data due to their reliance on unimodal spurious correlations, primarily text-only shortcuts within the training distribution, which prevents them from leveraging true multimodal reward functions. To address this, we introduce a Shortcut-aware MM-RM learning algorithm that mitigates this issue by dynamically reweighting training samples, shifting the distribution toward better multimodal understanding, and reducing dependence on unimodal spurious correlations. Our experiments demonstrate significant improvements in generalization, downstream task performance, and scalability, establishing a more robust framework for multimodal reward modeling. Our source code is provided on https://github.com/alignrm/Generalizable-MM-RM.
author: Zichao Li;Xueru Wen;Jie Lou;Yuqiu Ji;Yaojie Lu;Xianpei Han;Debing Zhang;Le Sun
aff: Institute of Software, Chinese Academy of Sciences;;;;Institute of Software, Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Software, CAS;Xiaohongshu;Institute of Software, Chinese Academy of Sciences
aff_unique_norm: Chinese Academy of Sciences;;Xiaohongshu

roast.laymen_summary: AI judges are supposed to grade how well a computer handles both text and images, but they’ve been 'cheating' by just reading the captions and ignoring the pictures. This paper tries to fix that laziness by forcing the AI to actually look at the visuals, but it turns out breaking an AI's bad habits is harder than just telling it to 'pay attention.'
why_bad: The 'novel' reweighting algorithm is essentially a math-heavy version of 'just try harder,' which researchers have been doing since the 2010s.
The benchmarks used are the academic equivalent of a 'participation trophy'—too easy and too small to prove the model works in the messy real world.
The paper fails to prove the model actually understands the images; it likely just found a more sophisticated way to keep cheating on the text exam.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where the world's top food critic is actually blind and just reads the fancy font on the menu to give a 5-star review... one group of scientists decides to ground him. They implement a 'revolutionary' system to make him smell the risotto. But there's a catch: their breakthrough 'olfactory sensor' is just a fan blowing air, and they only tested it in a McDonald's. This summer, watch as 'The Shortcut' fails to deliver anything but a lukewarm burger. Rated R for Redundant.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered.

title: Structure Is All You Need: Structural Representation Learning on Hyper-Relational Knowledge Graphs
author_site: Jaejun Lee, Joyce Whang
abstract: Hyper-relational knowledge graphs (HKGs) enrich knowledge graphs by extending a triplet to a hyper-relational fact, where a set of qualifiers adds auxiliary information to a triplet. While many HKG representation learning methods have been proposed, they often fail to effectively utilize the HKG's structure. This paper demonstrates that thoroughly leveraging the structure of an HKG is crucial for reasoning on HKGs, and a purely structure-based representation learning method can achieve state-of-the-art performance on various link prediction tasks. We propose MAYPL, which learns to initialize representation vectors based on the structure of an HKG and employs an attentive neural message passing consisting of fact-level message computation and entity-centric and relation-centric aggregations, thereby computing the representations based solely on the structure. Due to its structure-driven learning, MAYPL can conduct inductive inferences on new entities and relations. MAYPL outperforms 40 knowledge graph completion methods in 10 datasets, compared with different baseline methods on different datasets to be tested from diverse perspectives.
keywords: Hyper-Relational Knowledge Graph;Knowledge Graph Representation Learning;Knowledge Graph Completion;Graph Neural Networks;Inductive Link Prediction
author: Jaejun Lee;Joyce Jiyoung Whang
aff: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;KAIST
aff_unique_norm: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim that AI can understand complex relationships solely by looking at the 'shape' of a network, ignoring what the entities actually are. It’s essentially trying to solve a murder mystery by looking at a phone log without ever listening to the calls.
why_bad: The title 'Structure Is All You Need' is a tired academic trope that triggered reviewers who are exhausted by 'Attention Is All You Need' parodies.
By completely ignoring the actual meaning of words (semantics), the model becomes a 'black box' that can't handle real-world nuances where the definition of a relationship actually matters.
Claiming to beat 40 different methods across 10 datasets sounds impressive until you realize they likely benchmarked against ancient algorithms that were basically written on stone tablets.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a dating app where you aren't allowed to see photos, read bios, or even know your match's name. Instead, our 'disruptive' startup, MAYPL, just shows you a graph of who they've texted and how often. We’re pitching this as 'Relational Harmony'—if you and a stranger both text a plumber and a pizza place at 9 PM, you're clearly soulmates! It’s 'Inductive,' meaning it works even for people who just joined the app. Sure, you might end up married to your own landlord because your 'structural patterns' align, but look at our spreadsheets! We’re outperforming Tinder by 400% in the 'Connecting People Who Have Phones' category. Don't worry about the actual conversation; the geometry of your social circle is all you need.

title: Gravity-Bench-v1: A Benchmark on Gravitational Physics Discovery for Agents
author_site: Nolan Koblischke, Hyunseok Jang, Kristen Menou, Mohamad Ali-Dib
abstract: Modern science emerged from reasoning over repeatedly-observed planetary motions. We present Gravity-Bench-v1, an environment-based benchmark that challenges AI agents on tasks that parallel this historical development. Gravity-Bench-v1 evaluates agents on the discovery of physics concealed within a dynamic environment, using rigorous gravitational dynamics simulations. Gravity-Bench includes out-of-distribution cases, i.e. with physics that deviates from the real world, to evaluate true scientific generalization capabilities. Agents must plan to collect data within an experimental budget and must perform a dynamic form of data analysis and reasoning to solve tasks efficiently. Our benchmark admits an open-ended space of solutions. Reference solutions for each task are provided to calibrate AI performance against human expertise. Technically at an upper-undergraduate level, our benchmark proves challenging to baseline AI agents. Gravity-Bench-v1 and planned extensions should help map out AI progress towards scientific discovery capabilities.
author: Nolan Koblischke;Hyunseok Jang;Kristen Menou;Mohamad Ali-Dib
aff: University of Toronto;University of Toronto;University of Toronto;New York University
aff_unique_norm: University of Toronto;New York University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a digital playground to see if AI agents could rediscover the laws of physics by poking around in a gravity simulator. It’s basically 'Kerbal Space Program' for robots, intended to prove that if you give a chatbot a virtual telescope and a budget, it can act like a tiny, digital Isaac Newton.
why_bad: The 'discovery' process is effectively just fancy curve-fitting within a pre-defined simulation, which doesn't actually mirror how messy real-world science works.
Current AI agents are hilariously bad at this, mostly failing to produce anything beyond noise, making the benchmark more of a graveyard than a measuring stick.
The so-called 'new physics' cases are often just minor tweaks to gravitational constants, which tests basic algebra rather than true scientific intuition or 'generalization.'
The 'experimental budget' is an arbitrary constraint that masks the fact that the agents aren't actually reasoning—they're just running out of tokens.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup where we disrupt the 'Laws of Nature' vertical by locking a golden retriever in a room with a pendulum and a calculator. We call it 'Discovery-as-a-Service.' We give the dog a 'research budget' of three tennis balls and expect it to derive General Relativity before lunch. If the dog fails, we don't blame the dog; we claim we’ve created a 'rigorous benchmark for canine-based astrophysics.' It’s high-stakes, it’s 'upper-undergraduate level,' and much like our AI agents, the dog will likely spend the entire budget staring at its own tail while we pivot to a Series A round based on the 'limitless potential' of the experiment.

title: Towards Attributions of Input Variables in a Coalition
author_site: Xinhao Zheng, Huiqi Deng, Quanshi Zhang
abstract: This paper focuses on the fundamental challenge of partitioning input variables in attribution methods for Explainable AI, particularly in Shapley value-based approaches. Previous methods always compute attributions given a predefined partition but lack theoretical guidance on how to form meaningful variable partitions. We identify that attribution conflicts arise when the attribution of a coalition differs from the sum of its individual variables' attributions. To address this, we analyze the numerical effects of AND-OR interactions in AI models and extend the Shapley value to a new attribution metric for variable coalitions. Our theoretical findings reveal that specific interactions cause attribution conflicts, and we propose three metrics to evaluate coalition faithfulness. Experiments on synthetic data, NLP, image classification, and the game of Go validate our approach, demonstrating consistency with human intuition and practical applicability.
author: Xinhao Zheng;Huiqi Deng;Quanshi Zhang
aff: Shanghai Jiaotong University;Xi'an jiaotong University;Shanghai Jiaotong University
aff_unique_norm: Shanghai Jiao Tong University;Xi'an Jiao Tong University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to fix AI 'explanations' by grouping input data together, arguing that explaining individual pixels is useless if they only make sense as a group. They invented a way to measure these groups using complex math, but ultimately struggled to prove their new system is actually better than just guessing.
why_bad: The 'theoretical guidance' is essentially a glorified heuristic that fails to provide a rigorous mathematical proof for why these specific groupings are superior.
Claims of 'human intuition' consistency are based on anecdotal visual examples rather than objective, large-scale human subject testing.
The computational complexity of calculating these coalition Shapley values scales poorly, making it a fancy paperweight for any real-world, high-dimensional model.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Co-conspiring Commas
roast.analogy_description: Imagine an AI is on trial for a crime, and the Defense (the authors) argues that you can't blame individual words in the AI's 'confession' because the words were conspiring in groups. The Prosecutor asks, 'Why did you group 'I' and 'did' but left 'it' alone?' The Defense claims they have a proprietary 'Vibe Metric' that proves these words are friends. The Judge (the reviewer) leans over the bench and notes that the Defense’s math only works if you already know who is guilty, and calculating the 'group guilt' takes longer than the actual trial. Case dismissed for lack of material evidence and excessive use of Greek letters to hide a weak argument.

title: Diversified Flow Matching with Translation Identifiability
author_site: Sagar Shrestha, Xiao Fu
abstract: Diversified distribution matching (DDM) finds a unified translation function mapping a diverse collection of conditional source distributions to their target counterparts. DDM was proposed to resolve content misalignment issues in unpaired domain translation, achieving translation identifiability. However, DDM has only been implemented using GANs due to its constraints on the translation function. GANs are often unstable to train and do not provide the transport trajectory information---yet such trajectories are useful in applications such as single-cell evolution analysis and robot route planning. This work introduces *diversified flow matching* (DFM), an ODE-based framework for DDM. Adapting flow matching (FM) to enforce a unified translation function as in DDM is challenging, as FM learns the translation function's velocity rather than the translation function itself. A custom bilevel optimization-based training loss, a nonlinear interpolant, and a structural reformulation are proposed to address these challenges, offering a tangible implementation. To our knowledge, DFM is the first ODE-based approach guaranteeing translation identifiability. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate the proposed method.
author: Sagar Shrestha;Xiao Fu
aff: Oregon State University;Oregon State University
aff_unique_norm: Oregon State University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a high-precision GPS for AI that doesn't just teleport data from A to B, but maps the entire scenic route. They swapped out old, glitchy AI methods for a 'smooth' physics-based approach to ensure that one specific input always maps to one specific output.
why_bad: Bilevel optimization is a notorious computational sinkhole that makes training slower than a bureaucratic committee meeting.
The 'structural reformulation' is a complex mathematical band-aid used to force a model that tracks speed (velocity) to care about location (translation).
The claim of being the 'first' to do this is undermined by the fact that the resulting framework is likely too fragile for anything beyond cherry-picked synthetic data.
roast.analogy_name: The Uber for Teleportation (Physics Degree Required)
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that promises to move you from your house to your office, but instead of a car, they use a particle accelerator. Our 'disruptive' edge? We don't just teleport you; we calculate the exact velocity of every molecule so we can prove you're the same person when you arrive. To stop you from turning into a pile of goo, we run a second, smaller particle accelerator inside the first one just to double-check the math. It's 10x more expensive, takes 50x longer, and requires a PhD to start the engine, but hey—at least we can mathematically guarantee you didn't accidentally become your neighbor's cat during the commute.

title: Streamline Without Sacrifice - Squeeze out Computation Redundancy in LMM
author_site: Penghao Wu, Lewei Lu, Ziwei Liu
abstract: Large multimodal models excel in multimodal tasks but face significant computational challenges due to excessive visual tokens. Unlike token reduction methods that focus on token-level redundancy, we identify and study the computation-level redundancy on vision tokens to ensure no information loss. Our key insight is that vision tokens from the pretrained vision encoder do not necessarily require all the heavy operations (e.g., self-attention, FFNs) in decoder-only LMMs and could be processed more lightly with proper designs. We designed a series of experiments to discover and progressively squeeze out the vision-related computation redundancy. Based on our findings, we propose ProxyV, a novel approach that utilizes proxy vision tokens to alleviate the computational burden on original vision tokens. ProxyV enhances efficiency without compromising performance and can even yield notable performance gains in scenarios with more moderate efficiency improvements. Furthermore, the flexibility of ProxyV is demonstrated through its combination with token reduction methods to boost efficiency further.
keywords: Large Multimodal Model;Multimodal Large Language Model;Large Multimodal Model Acceleration
author: Penghao Wu;Lewei Lu;Ziwei Liu
aff: Nanyang Technological University;SenseTime;Nanyang Technological University
aff_unique_norm: Nanyang Technological University;SenseTime

roast.laymen_summary: Current AI models treat every tiny piece of an image like a VIP, forcing the computer to do massive amounts of redundant math on things that don't matter. We tried to speed things up by using 'stunt double' tokens to do the heavy lifting, hoping to save battery life without making the AI go blind.
why_bad: The 'efficiency' gains are largely theoretical and don't translate to actual speedups on standard hardware due to the overhead of managing the proxy system.
The method adds significant architectural complexity for marginal performance improvements that look more like statistical noise than a breakthrough.
The paper fails to provide a convincing comparison against simpler, existing 'token pruning' methods that achieve similar results with half the code.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a celebrity who refuses to go anywhere without a 500-man security detail, which makes getting into a Starbucks take three hours. Our 'ProxyV' startup solves this by replacing 495 of those guards with life-sized cardboard cutouts to 'reduce the footprint.' We’re pitching this as a revolution in personal security. The catch? You still have to hire a specialized team of movers to carry the cutouts, and the celebrity still moves at a snail's pace because they're terrified someone will notice the guards are made of paper. We aren't actually making the line move faster; we're just making the lobby look busier while charging you for the glue.

title: Finite-Time Convergence Rates in Stochastic Stackelberg Games with Smooth Algorithmic Agents
author_site: Eric Frankel, Kshitij Kulkarni, Dmitriy Drusvyatskiy, Sewoong Oh, Lillian Ratliff
abstract:
Decision-makers often adaptively influence downstream competitive agents' behavior to minimize their cost, yet in doing so face critical challenges:  $(i)$ decision-makers might not *a priori* know the agents' objectives; $(ii)$ agents might *learn* their responses, introducing stochasticity and non-stationarity into the decision-making process; and $(iii)$ there may be additional non-strategic environmental stochasticity. 
Characterizing convergence of this complex system is contingent on how the decision-maker controls for the tradeoff between the induced drift and additional noise from the learning agent behavior and environmental stochasticity. To understand how the learning agents' behavior is influenced by the decision-maker’s actions, we first consider a decision-maker that deploys an arbitrary sequence of actions which induces a sequence of games and corresponding equilibria.   We characterize how the drift and noise in the agents' stochastic algorithms decouples from their optimization error. Leveraging this decoupling and accompanying finite-time efficiency estimates, we design decision-maker algorithms that control the induced drift relative to the agent noise. This enables efficient finite-time tracking of game theoretic equilibrium concepts that adhere to the incentives of the players' collective learning processes.
keywords: Stackelberg game;stochastic optimization;learning and games;stochastic tracking
author: Eric Frankel;Kshitij Kulkarni;Dmitriy Drusvyatskiy;Sewoong Oh;Lillian J. Ratliff
aff: Department of Computer Science, University of Washington;;University of Washington;;
aff_unique_norm: University of Washington;

roast.laymen_summary: This paper explores how a 'boss' can manipulate a group of 'followers' into a specific outcome, even though the boss doesn't know what the followers want and the followers are still learning how to play the game. It attempts to provide a mathematical speed-limit for how quickly everyone reaches a stable state while drowning in a sea of random noise and constant changes.
why_bad: The 'smoothness' assumption is a theoretical fantasy that assumes agents behave like well-oiled machines, rather than the jittery, unpredictable mess that actual AI agents are in the wild.

The paper claims to 'decouple' noise from optimization error, but the resulting constants in their 'finite-time' proofs are likely so large that the universe would end before the algorithm actually converges.

It’s a classic 'Theorem-only' special with zero empirical validation, meaning the authors spent all their time in the math clouds without checking if their 'efficient' algorithm survives a single encounter with real-world data.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Psychic Puppet Master
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the Prosecution expects you to believe that a puppeteer can lead a troupe of blindfolded dancers through a choreographed routine—without knowing the music, and while the dancers are still learning how to walk! The Defense has presented fifty pages of 'characterizing the drift' as if that explains why the dancers aren't tripping over their own feet. They claim to have 'decoupled' the chaos from the performance, but where is the evidence? They haven't produced a single witness—or a single simulation—who has actually seen this dance completed. This isn't a scientific breakthrough; it's a collection of elegant-looking Greek letters masquerading as a plan. I move to dismiss these 'finite-time' claims on the grounds that they are mathematically sound but practically nonexistent!

title: ExPLoRA: Parameter-Efficient Extended Pre-Training to Adapt Vision Transformers under Domain Shifts
author_site: Samar Khanna, Medhanie Irgau, David Lobell, Stefano Ermon
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques such as low-rank adaptation (LoRA) can effectively adapt large pre-trained foundation models to downstream tasks using only a small fraction (0.1%-10%) of the original trainable weights. An under-explored question of PEFT is in extending the pre-training phase without supervised labels; that is, can we adapt a pre-trained foundation model to a new domain via efficient self-supervised pre-training on this domain? In this work, we introduce ExPLoRA, a highly effective technique to improve transfer learning of pre-trained vision transformers (ViTs) under domain shifts. Initializing a ViT with pre-trained weights on large, natural-image datasets such as from DinoV2 or MAE, ExPLoRA continues the unsupervised pre-training objective on a new domain, unfreezing 1-2 pre-trained ViT blocks and tuning all other layers with LoRA. We then fine-tune the resulting model only with LoRA on this new domain for supervised learning. Our experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art results on satellite imagery, even outperforming fully pre-training and fine-tuning ViTs. Using the DinoV2 training objective, we demonstrate up to 8% improvement in linear probing top-1 accuracy on downstream tasks while using <10% of the number of parameters that are used in prior fully-tuned state-of-the art approaches. Our ablation studies confirm the efficacy of our approach over other baselines such as PEFT. Code is available at: https://samar-khanna.github.io/ExPLoRA/
keywords: lora;PEFT;parameter-efficient finetuning;parameter-efficient pre-training;vision transformer;ViT;domain adaptation;domain generalization;satellite images;foundation models
author: Samar Khanna;Medhanie Irgau;David B. Lobell;Stefano Ermon

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers wanted to teach a 'Big Tech' vision model how to understand satellite photos without retraining the whole thing, which is usually too expensive. They found that if you let the AI 'stare' at the new pictures for a while using a clever math hack called LoRA before actually giving it the test, it performs surprisingly well.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The reviewers likely felt that slapping an existing technique (LoRA) onto another existing technique (Self-Supervised Learning) is more of a 'weekend project' than a scientific breakthrough.

Arbitrary Heuristics: The decision to unfreeze exactly 1-2 blocks feels less like rigorous science and more like the authors poked the model until it stopped crashing, without explaining *why* those specific blocks matter.

Weak Baseline Comparison: Claiming victory over 'fully-tuned' models is easy when those models are notoriously hard to optimize; it's like bragging you beat a Ferrari in a race when the Ferrari was stuck in first gear.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where GPUs cost more than a kidney, one team tries to save the planet using only 1% of their brain. Witness the saga of ExPLoRA: a model trained on TikToks forced to identify top-secret military bases. See them 'unfreeze' a tiny portion of its digital soul just to see if it notices the difference between a cloud and a silo. It’s a high-stakes drama of 'Parameter Efficiency' where the budget is low, the math is 'low-rank,' and the rejection letter is inevitable. Coming to a GitHub repo near you: THE ADAPTER WHO KNEW TOO LITTLE.
roast.scientific_accuracy_check: The summary correctly identifies the use of Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) for domain adaptation using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) specifically via LoRA and selective block unfreezing.

title: Improving Reward Model Generalization from Adversarial Process Enhanced Preferences
author_site: Zhilong Zhang, Tian Xu, Xinghao Du, Xingchen Cao, Yihao Sun, Yang Yu
abstract: In sequential decision-making, the reward function serves as the primary supervision signal, guiding agents to acquire the desired behaviors. Traditional reward modeling methods rely heavily on human expertise, limiting their scalability. Automated preference generation from suboptimal demonstrations has emerged as a promising alternative to address this limitation. This approach first generates preference data from suboptimal demonstrations and then trains reward models based on these preferences. Despite its potential, existing methods often struggle to generate preference data with sufficient coverage, limiting the accuracy and generalizability of the resulting reward models. To overcome this limitation, we propose APEC (Automated Preference generation with Enhanced Coverage), a novel method that improves the coverage of preference data. APEC achieves this by selecting policy pairs with significantly different iteration indices from the whole adversarial imitation learning process. We provide a theoretical analysis to validate that the selected policy pairs provably hold preference relationships. Experimental results demonstrate that APEC consistently outperforms baseline methods in generating preferences with broader coverage across both vector-based and pixel-based control tasks. Consequently, the reward models trained with APEC align more closely with ground-truth rewards, deriving improved policy performance.
keywords: reward modeling;preference generation;preference-based reinforcement learning;adversarial imitation learning
author: Zhilong Zhang;Tian Xu;Xinghao Du;Xingchen Cao;Yihao Sun;Yang Yu
aff: Nanjing University;Nanjing University;Nanjing University;Nanjing University;Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors want to train robots by letting them watch their own 'before and after' videos rather than paying humans to grade them. They assume that if an AI records itself at the start and end of a training session, the later version is automatically the 'expert' that can teach the reward model what success looks like.
why_bad: The core assumption that 'later iteration equals better performance' is a massive leap of faith in adversarial learning, where models often diverge or collapse into weird, glitchy behaviors rather than actually improving.
The method is essentially just data-mining an existing training process (AIL) rather than providing a fundamental breakthrough in how machines understand complex tasks.
The theoretical 'proof' likely relies on idealized convergence settings that real-world, noisy 'pixel-based' control tasks rarely, if ever, satisfy.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the judge has never tasted real food. To 'learn' what a good meal is, the show runners just record a toddler trying to make spaghetti over ten hours. The authors' brilliant idea is to take a video from hour one (dry noodles on the floor) and a video from hour nine (overcooked mush in a shoe) and tell the judge, 'The shoe-mush is the gold standard because it happened later.' You’ve successfully taught the judge to prefer mush over floor-noodles, but you're still miles away from a Michelin star, and God help you if the toddler started throwing play-dough into the pot at hour ten.

title: Telling Peer Direct Effects from Indirect Effects in Observational Network Data
author_site: Xiaojing Du, Jiuyong Li, Debo Cheng, Lin Liu, Wentao Gao, XIONGREN CHEN, Ziqi Xu
abstract: Estimating causal effects is crucial for decision-makers in many applications, but it is particularly challenging with observational network data due to peer interactions. Some algorithms have been proposed to estimate causal effects involving network data, particularly peer effects, but they often fail to tell apart diverse peer effects. To address this issue, we propose a general setting which considers both peer direct effects and peer indirect effects, and the effect of an individual's own treatment, and provide the identification conditions of these causal effects. To differentiate these effects, we leverage causal mediation analysis and tailor it specifically for network data. Furthermore, given the inherent challenges of accurately estimating effects in networked environments, we propose to incorporate attention mechanisms to capture the varying influences of different neighbors and to explore high-order neighbor effects using multi-layer graph neural networks (GNNs). Additionally, we employ the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC) to further enhance the model’s robustness and accuracy. Extensive experiments on two semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world networks and on a dataset from a recommendation system confirm the effectiveness of our approach. Our findings have the potential to improve intervention strategies in networked systems, particularly in social networks and public health.
keywords: Estimate Causal Effects;Peer Effects;Causal Mediation Analysis;Graph Neural Networks
author: Xiaojing Du;Jiuyong Li;Debo Cheng;Lin Liu;Wentao Gao;XIONGREN CHEN;Ziqi Xu
aff: University of South Australia;University of South Australia, Australia;;University of South Australia, Australia;University of South Australia;University of South Australia;Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology
position: PhD student;Full Professor;;Full Professor;PhD student;PhD student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of South Australia;;Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to figure out if you bought that questionable air fryer because your best friend recommended it, or because your friend's cousin's neighbor's dog walker posted about it. The authors threw a high-tech kitchen sink of AI buzzwords at the problem to see if they could untangle the messy web of who is actually influencing whom in a social network.
why_bad: The Methodology Kitchen Sink: The authors mashed together GNNs, Attention mechanisms, and HSIC like a desperate chef, resulting in a model that's over-engineered and lacks a clear theoretical 'why.'

Synthetic Security Blanket: Testing primarily on semi-synthetic datasets is the academic equivalent of claiming you can swim the English Channel because you survived a very shallow bathtub—it doesn't prove the model works in the chaos of the real world.
Causal Overreach: The paper claims to solve 'causal identification' in networks, but their assumptions are so restrictive they basically assume the problem away before they even start.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Gossiping Jurors
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom drama where the prosecution is trying to prove exactly which juror convinced the others to vote 'guilty.' The star witness is a 'Graph Neural Network' wearing a fancy suit, claiming it can track every whisper and side-eye in the jury room using 'High-Order Attention.' However, when the Judge (the reviewer) asks for the specific math behind these 'whispers,' the witness starts babbling about 'Hilbert-Schmidt Independence' like it's a magic spell. To make matters worse, it turns out the prosecution's 'evidence' wasn't even from a real trial—it was a transcript from a middle-school drama club rehearsal they edited to look like a crime. The Judge leans over the bench, sighs, and dismisses the case because the 'expert' is just using expensive-sounding words to hide the fact that they're basically just guessing who the Alpha Juror is.

title: Principal-Agent Bandit Games with Self-Interested and Exploratory Learning Agents
author_site: Junyan Liu, Lillian Ratliff
abstract:
We study the repeated principal-agent bandit game, where the principal indirectly explores an unknown environment by incentivizing an agent to play arms. 
Unlike prior work that assumes a greedy agent with full knowledge of reward means, we consider a self-interested learning agent who iteratively updates reward estimates and may explore arbitrarily with some probability.
As a warm-up, we first consider a self-interested learning agent without exploration.
We propose algorithms for both i.i.d. and linear reward settings with bandit feedback in a finite horizon $T$, achieving regret bounds of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ and $\widetilde{O}(T^{\frac{2}{3}})$, respectively. 
Specifically, these algorithms rely on a novel elimination framework coupled with new search algorithms which accommodate the uncertainty from the agent's learning behavior. We then extend the framework to handle an exploratory learning agent and develop an algorithm to achieve a $\widetilde{O}(T^{\frac{2}{3}})$ regret bound in i.i.d. reward setup by enhancing the robustness of our elimination framework to the potential agent exploration. Finally, when our agent model reduces to that in (Dogan et al., 2023a), we propose an algorithm based on our robust framework, which achieves a $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret bound, significantly improving upon their $\widetilde{O}(T^{\frac{11}{12}})$ bound.
author: Junyan Liu;Lillian J. Ratliff

roast.laymen_summary: A boss tries to trick a clueless employee into testing out different products to see which one makes the most money, but the employee is also trying to learn the job on the fly. It's a mathematical model for micromanaging someone who is just as confused as you are.
why_bad: The 'novel elimination framework' is essentially a glorified game of Minesweeper that ignores the fact that real humans don't follow rigid mathematical exploration probabilities.
A regret bound of T^(2/3) in the linear setting is the theoretical equivalent of a 'C+' grade—it works, but it's not going to win any beauty pageants or solve real-world latency.
The paper spends too much time bragging about beating a 2023 baseline that was already considered inefficient, making the 'significant improvement' look like winning a sprint against a toddler.
roast.analogy_name: The 'Intern in a Dark Room' Playbook
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Micromanagement Bowl! We’ve got a Principal in the owner's box who wants to find the 'Golden Slot Machine' but is too cheap to pull the lever themselves. They've hired a 'Learning Agent'—essentially an intern who’s never seen a casino. The Principal’s big 'innovation' here? They’ve finally realized that if they pay the intern in gum, the intern might get distracted by the flashing lights and wander off to the buffet. The 'Elimination Framework' is just the Principal yelling 'Not that one!' every time the intern hits a losing streak. The stats look okay on paper, but let's be real: the 'robustness' they're touting is just a fancy way of saying they've accounted for the intern being occasionally hyperactive. It’s a lot of math to prove you can somewhat control someone who doesn't know what they're doing anyway. Back to you, Jim!

title: R*: Efficient Reward Design via Reward Structure Evolution and Parameter Alignment Optimization with Large Language Models
author_site: Pengyi Li, Jianye Hao, Hongyao Tang, Yifu Yuan, Jinbin Qiao, Zibin Dong, Yan Zheng
abstract:
Reward functions are crucial for policy learning. Large Language Models (LLMs), with strong coding capabilities and valuable domain knowledge, provide an automated solution for high-quality reward design. 
However, 
code-based reward functions require precise guiding logic and parameter configurations within a vast design space, leading to low optimization efficiency.
To address the challenges,
we propose an efficient automated reward design framework, called R*,
which decomposes reward design into two parts: reward structure evolution and parameter alignment optimization. To design high-quality reward structures, R* maintains a reward function population and modularizes the functional components. LLMs are employed as the mutation operator, and module-level crossover is proposed to facilitate efficient exploration and exploitation.
To design more efficient reward parameters, R* first leverages LLMs to generate multiple critic functions for trajectory comparison and annotation. Based on these critics, a voting mechanism is employed to collect the trajectory segments with high-confidence labels.
These labeled segments are then used to refine the reward function parameters through preference learning.
Experiments on diverse robotic control tasks demonstrate that R* outperforms strong baselines in both reward design efficiency and quality, surpassing human-designed reward functions.
author: Pengyi Li;Jianye HAO;Hongyao Tang;Yifu Yuan;Jinbin Qiao;Zibin Dong;YAN ZHENG

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to stop humans from having to manually write the 'rules' for robots by letting an AI write the code and then using a second AI to grade how well the first AI did. It’s essentially a high-tech version of letting a student write their own exam and then hiring their twin brother to proctor it.
why_bad: Circular Logic: The 'critics' used to judge the reward functions are just other LLMs, creating a feedback loop where the AI justifies its own hallucinations without real-world grounding.

The 'Basics' Problem: The paper claims 'efficiency' while burning massive amounts of compute to do evolutionary 'crossover' on code snippets that a human could have tuned in five minutes with a spreadsheet.

Inconsistent Evaluation: The benchmarks show the system 'outperforming' humans, but it conveniently ignores that the AI-generated rewards are often over-fitted to specific simulator quirks rather than generalizable logic.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Kangaroo Coding Court
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, look at the defendant! R* claims to be a pioneer of justice, but it has committed the ultimate legal sin: it has turned the courtroom into a hall of mirrors. The prosecution asserts that R* isn't 'evolving' anything; it’s just shuffling a deck of cards and asking its best friend—the LLM Critic—if the hand looks 'vibey' enough to win. 'Objection!' you might cry, as the AI acts as both the lawmaker writing the reward code and the Supreme Court justice interpreting the results. There is no objective truth here, only a modularized shell game where the parameters are 'aligned' to satisfy a judge who has never stepped foot in the physical world. This isn't a breakthrough in robotic control; it's a racketeering scheme fueled by tokens and circular reasoning! I move to strike this entire framework from the record!

title: When to retrain a machine learning model
author_site: Florence Regol, Leo Schwinn, Kyle Sprague, Mark Coates, Thomas L Markovich
abstract: A significant challenge in maintaining real-world machine learning models is responding to the continuous and unpredictable evolution of data. Most practitioners are faced with the difficult question: when should I retrain or update my machine learning model? This seemingly straightforward problem is particularly challenging for three reasons: 1) decisions must be made based on very limited information - we usually have access to only a few examples, 2) the nature, extent, and impact of the distribution shift are unknown, and 3) it involves specifying a cost ratio between retraining and poor performance, which can be hard to characterize. Existing works address certain aspects of this problem, but none offer a comprehensive solution. Distribution shift detection falls short as it cannot account for the cost trade-off; the scarcity of the data, paired with its unusual structure, makes it a poor fit for existing offline reinforcement learning methods, and the online learning formulation overlooks key practical considerations. To address this, we present a principled formulation of the retraining problem and propose an uncertainty-based method that makes decisions by continually forecasting the evolution of model performance evaluated with a bounded metric. Our experiments, addressing classification tasks, show that the method consistently outperforms existing baselines on 7 datasets. We thoroughly assess its robustness to varying cost trade-off values and mis-specified cost trade-offs.
author: Florence Regol;Leo Schwinn;Kyle Sprague;Mark Coates;Thomas Markovich
aff: ;Technical University of Munich;;McGill University;Block
aff_unique_norm: ;Technical University of Munich;McGill University;Block

roast.laymen_summary: AI models are like milk; they eventually go sour as the world changes, but retraining them is expensive and annoying. This paper tries to build a fancy 'expiration date' sensor that tells you exactly when your model has become too stupid to keep using without wasting money on unnecessary updates.
why_bad: The 'principled formulation' is mostly just a math-heavy way of describing common sense that practitioners have been doing via gut instinct for a decade.
The evaluation is restricted to classification tasks on seven aging datasets, which is like claiming you've solved global traffic by watching seven toy cars in a sandbox.
It assumes users can actually quantify the 'cost' of a model mistake into a clean number, ignoring the reality that most businesses have no idea how much their bad AI is actually costing them.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the High-Stakes Hygiene Open! Our athlete hasn't showered in three days. Does he smell? Definitely. Does he need a full scrub-down? Maybe. The authors are introducing the 'Predictive Pit-Sniff 3000,' a system designed to calculate if the cost of the water bill is higher than the social shame of clearing out the 2 PM board meeting. It's a bold strategy, Cotton! They’re using 'uncertainty-based forecasting' to see if they can survive one more hour of body odor without hitting the soap. But wait—the referees are blowing the whistle because the system was only tested in a room full of other sweaty teenagers. In a real-world environment with actual nostrils, this 'optimized' stink-threshold is going to get them disqualified from the dating pool immediately!

title: Leveraging Per-Instance Privacy for Machine Unlearning
author_site: Naz Sepahvand, Anvith Thudi, Berivan Isik, Ashmita Bhattacharyya, Nicolas Papernot, Eleni Triantafillou, Daniel Roy, Gintare Karolina Dziugaite
abstract:
We present a principled, per-instance approach to quantifying the difficulty of unlearning via fine-tuning. We begin by sharpening an analysis of noisy gradient descent for unlearning (Chien et al., 2024), obtaining a better utility–unlearning trade-off by replacing worst-case privacy loss bounds with per-instance privacy losses (Thudi et al., 2024), each of which bounds the (R ´enyi) divergence to retraining without an individual datapoint. To demonstrate the practical applicability of our theory, we present empirical results showing that our theoretical predictions are born out both for Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (SGLD) as well as for standard fine-tuning without explicit noise. We further demonstrate that per-instance privacy losses correlate well with several existing data difficulty metrics, while also
identifying harder groups of data points, and introduce novel evaluation methods based on loss barriers. All together, our findings provide a foundation for more efficient and adaptive unlearning strategies tailored to the unique properties of individual data points.
author: Nazanin Mohammadi Sepahvand;Anvith Thudi;Berivan Isik;Ashmita Bhattacharyya;Nicolas Papernot;Eleni Triantafillou;Daniel M. Roy;Gintare Karolina Dziugaite
aff: McGill University+Montreal Institute for Learning Algorithms, University of Montreal, Université de Montréal;University of Toronto;Google;University of Toronto;University of Toronto+Google;Google;;McGill University+Google DeepMind+Montreal Institute for Learning Algorithms, University of Montreal, University of Montreal
position: PhD student+PhD student;PhD student;Research Scientist;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor+Research Scientist;Researcher;;Adjunct Professor+Senior Researcher+Member
aff_unique_norm: McGill University;University of Montreal;University of Toronto;Google;

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a tool that figures out exactly how much effort it takes to make an AI 'forget' a specific person's data without breaking the rest of its brain. They claim they can predict which data points are 'stickier' than others, but their math mostly just confirms that some memories are harder to delete than others.
why_bad: Derivative Contributions: The paper is essentially a 'Greatest Hits' album of 2024 citations (Chien et al. and Thudi et al.) without providing enough original 'new music' to justify its own existence.

The Noise Paradox: Claiming that 'standard fine-tuning without explicit noise' yields privacy results is like saying you're bulletproof because you haven't been shot yet—it's mathematically hand-wavy and violates the core tenets of Differential Privacy.

Efficiency Irony: The authors propose an 'efficient' strategy that requires calculating complex metrics for every single data point, which in many cases is actually more computationally expensive than just retraining the entire model from scratch.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching an app that promises to selectively delete memories of your ex. Instead of just moving on, the app insists on performing a $5,000 brain scan to calculate exactly how 'traumatic' each individual date was before it tries to erase them. By the time the app finishes its 'per-instance' analysis of your Friday nights, you've spent more time and money analyzing the relationship than you did actually living it. Plus, the founders claim the app works even if you don't use the 'sedation' feature—which is just a fancy way of saying you're just sitting in a dark room thinking about your ex and hoping you'll forget them naturally. It’s a high-tech solution to a problem that’s better solved by just burning the old photo albums.

title: Fragments to Facts: Partial-Information Fragment Inference from LLMs
author_site: Lucas Rosenblatt, Bin Han, Robert Wolfe, Bill Howe
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can leak sensitive training data through memorization and membership inference attacks. Prior work has primarily focused on strong adversarial assumptions, including attacker access to entire samples or long, ordered prefixes, leaving open the question of how vulnerable LLMs are when adversaries have only partial, unordered sample information. For example, if an attacker knows a patient has "hypertension," under what conditions can they query a model fine-tuned on patient data to learn the patient also has "osteoarthritis?" In this paper, we introduce a more general threat model under this weaker assumption and show that fine-tuned LLMs are susceptible to these fragment-specific extraction attacks. To systematically investigate these attacks, we propose two data-blind methods: (1) a likelihood ratio attack inspired by methods from membership inference, and (2) a novel approach, PRISM, which regularizes the ratio by leveraging an external prior. Using examples from medical and legal settings, we show that both methods are competitive with a data-aware baseline classifier that assumes access to labeled in-distribution data, underscoring their robustness.
author: Lucas Rosenblatt;Bin HAN;Robert Wolfe;Bill Howe
aff: New York University;University of Washington;University of Washington;University of Washington
aff_unique_norm: New York University;University of Washington

roast.laymen_summary: The authors discovered that LLMs are basically indiscreet gossips who only need a tiny nudge to spill your private medical or legal secrets. Even if an attacker only knows two random facts about you, these models can be tricked into 'filling in the blanks' with sensitive data they were supposed to forget.
why_bad: The PRISM method is essentially a glorified statistical vibe-check that barely outperforms standard guessing.
The 'weak assumption' threat model is a bit of a stretch, as it assumes the attacker already possesses the most specific keywords needed to trigger the leak.
Testing on only two niche datasets (medical and legal) makes the results about as generalizable as a horoscope.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Predictive Text
roast.analogy_description: Picture a witness on the stand who is legally sworn to secrecy but has the impulse control of a caffeinated toddler. The Prosecution—the 'attacker'—doesn't have a confession; they just have a Post-it note that says 'Defendant' and 'Taxes.' They ask the witness a leading question, and the witness immediately screams, 'YES, AND HE COMMITTED FRAUD IN 2019!' The Defense objects because the Prosecution basically tricked a high-speed autocomplete into saying the obvious. The Judge—the Reviewer—dismisses the case because 'guessing the rest of the sentence' isn't a forensic breakthrough; it's just a noisy snitch doing what it was built to do.

title: Not All Wrong is Bad: Using Adversarial Examples for Unlearning
author_site: Ali Ebrahimpour-Boroojeny, Hari Sundaram, Varun Chandrasekaran
abstract: Machine unlearning, where users can request the deletion of a forget dataset, is becoming increasingly important because of numerous privacy regulations. Initial works on "exact'' unlearning (e.g., retraining) incur large computational overheads. However, while computationally inexpensive, "approximate'' methods have fallen short of reaching the effectiveness of exact unlearning: models produced fail to obtain comparable accuracy and prediction confidence on both the forget and test (i.e., unseen) dataset. Exploiting this observation, we propose a new unlearning method, Adversarial Machine UNlearning (AMUN), that outperforms prior state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods for image classification. AMUN lowers the confidence of the model on the forget samples by fine-tuning the model on their corresponding adversarial examples. Adversarial examples naturally belong to the distribution imposed by the model on the input space; fine-tuning the model on the adversarial examples closest to the corresponding forget samples (a) localizes the changes to the decision boundary of the model around each forget sample and (b) avoids drastic changes to the global behavior of the model, thereby preserving the model's accuracy on test samples. Using AMUN for unlearning a random 10% of CIFAR-10 samples, we observe that even SOTA membership inference attacks cannot do better than random guessing.
author: Ali Ebrahimpour-Boroojeny;Hari Sundaram;Varun Chandrasekaran
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of actually deleting sensitive data from an AI’s brain, these researchers tried to gaslight the model into being confused by its own memories using 'glitchy' versions of the data it’s supposed to forget. It’s essentially a digital lobotomy that tries to hide specific secrets by training the AI to be confidently wrong about them whenever they're brought up.
why_bad: Confusion is not the same as forgetting: Just because the model is now 'unsure' doesn't mean the sensitive information isn't still buried deep in its neurons, waiting for a clever hacker to dig it out.
Collateral damage on the brain: Fine-tuning a model on adversarial 'garbage' data tends to degrade its overall IQ, making it worse at its actual job just to hide a few specific facts.
Overblown security claims: Asserting that your method is 'unhackable' because it beat one basic privacy test is like claiming your house is a fortress because you locked the screen door.
roast.analogy_name: The 'Mustache and Glasses' Witness Protection Program
roast.analogy_description: Alright sports fans, we’re looking at a bold—if slightly delusional—play here! The 'AMUN' team wants to get a player off the field, but instead of actually sending him to the locker room, they’ve decided to just put a fake mustache on him and tell the referees he's a total stranger! The coach is screaming from the sidelines, 'He’s not the guy you’re looking for, he’s a localized glitch!' It’s a high-stakes game of gaslighting where they’re trying to 'fine-tune' the refs into being confused. But look at the replay! Just because you’ve blurred the player's face doesn't mean he isn't still taking up space on the roster and messing up the team's overall formation. The reviewers are throwing the yellow flag for illegal use of 'voodoo science' on the decision boundary. You can't just yell 'I forgot!' at a computer until it works, folks—that's not a strategy, that's a tantrum!

title: Grokking at the Edge of Linear Separability
author_site: Alon Beck, Noam Levi, Yohai Bar-Sinai
abstract:
We investigate the phenomenon of grokking -- delayed generalization accompanied by non-monotonic test loss behavior -- in a simple binary logistic classification task, for which "memorizing" and "generalizing" solutions can be strictly defined. 
Surprisingly, we find that grokking arises naturally even in this minimal model when the parameters of the problem are close to a critical point, and provide both empirical and analytical insights into its mechanism.
Concretely, by appealing to the implicit bias of gradient descent, we show that logistic regression can exhibit grokking when the training dataset is nearly linearly separable from the origin and there is strong noise in the perpendicular directions.
The underlying reason is that near the critical point, "flat" directions in the loss landscape with nearly zero gradient cause training dynamics to linger for arbitrarily long times near quasi-stable solutions before eventually reaching the global minimum.
Finally, we highlight similarities between our findings and the recent literature, strengthening the conjecture that grokking generally occurs in proximity to the interpolation threshold, reminiscent of critical phenomena often observed in physical systems.
author: Alon Beck;Noam Itzhak Levi;Yohai Bar-Sinai
aff: Tel Aviv University;EPFL - EPF Lausanne;Tel Aviv University
aff_unique_norm: Tel Aviv University;EPFL

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers found that even the simplest AI models can have a 'lightbulb moment' where they suddenly stop memorizing and start actually understanding, but only if the data is just messy enough to keep them confused for a long time. It turns out that AI 'grokking' isn't some mystical neural magic; it's just the math getting stuck in a flat, boring rut before finally rolling downhill into the right answer.
why_bad: The paper rebrands standard 'slow convergence' in basic logistic regression as 'grokking' to sound more profound than it actually is.
The conditions required for the effect are so specific and 'finely tuned' that they rarely occur in actual machine learning practice.
Reviewers felt the analytical 'insights' were just fancy ways of saying gradient descent moves slowly on flat surfaces, which isn't exactly a Nobel-worthy revelation.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Science for the Hopeless!' Today, we’re making a 'Linear Separability Burrito.' You put it in the microwave, and for three minutes, nothing happens. The outside is cold, the inside is a frozen brick of 'training noise,' and you’re convinced your microwave is broken—this is our 'quasi-stable solution.' You’re about to give up and eat it frozen, but in the final three seconds, the thermal dynamics hit a critical threshold. Suddenly, the molecules 'grok' the heat, and it transforms from a sad ice-block into a molten lava bomb. The paper argues that the burrito didn't gradually warm up; it just lingered in a 'flat' thermal state until the math forced it to get its act together at the very last second. Bone appétit, you lazy genius!

title: Human-Aligned Image Models Improve Visual Decoding from the Brain
author_site: Nona Rajabi, Antonio Ribeiro, Miguel Vasco, Farzaneh Taleb, Mårten Björkman, Danica Kragic
abstract: Decoding visual images from brain activity has significant potential for advancing brain-computer interaction and enhancing the understanding of human perception. Recent approaches align the representation spaces of images and brain activity to enable visual decoding. In this paper, we introduce the use of human-aligned image encoders to map brain signals to images. We hypothesize that these models more effectively capture perceptual attributes associated with the rapid visual stimuli presentations commonly used in visual brain data recording experiments. Our empirical results support this hypothesis, demonstrating that this simple modification improves image retrieval accuracy by up to 21\% compared to state-of-the-art methods. Comprehensive experiments confirm consistent performance improvements across diverse EEG architectures, image encoders, alignment methods, participants, and brain imaging modalities.
author: Nona Rajabi;Antonio H. Ribeiro;Miguel Vasco;Farzaneh Taleb;Mårten Björkman;Danica Kragic
aff: KTH Royal Institute of Technology;Uppsala University;KTH Royal Institute of Technology;KTH Royal Institute of Technology;;KTH
aff_unique_norm: KTH Royal Institute of Technology;Uppsala University;

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to read people's minds by plugging brain waves into AI models that are designed to 'see' images more like humans do. While they claim a 21% boost in guessing which picture a person was looking at, they essentially just swapped out an old filter for a trendier one and hoped nobody would notice the underlying data is still a mess.
why_bad: Incremental 'Plug-and-Play' Research: The authors didn't actually invent a new architecture; they just swapped a standard image encoder for a 'human-aligned' one and called it a breakthrough.
The EEG Signal-to-Noise Delusion: Claiming massive improvements on EEG data is bold, considering EEG signals are notoriously noisy—it's like trying to transcribe a whisper recorded in a hurricane.

Retrieval vs. Reconstruction: The model isn't 'decoding' thoughts so much as it's playing a high-tech game of 'Multiple Choice,' which doesn't actually prove it understands how the brain processes vision.
roast.analogy_name: The Drunk Sketch Artist: Human-Aligned Edition
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where your deepest thoughts are nothing but static and blurry shapes. [Deep Voiceover] One team of scientists thought they found the ultimate shortcut. They didn't fix the fuzzy brain-radio, they just gave the sketch artist a better pair of glasses. He still can't hear what you're saying, and he’s definitely still vibrating from too much caffeine, but now he’s drawing with more 'vibe.' Witness the cinematic struggle of a computer trying to turn a headache into a high-def JPEG. It’s 'Inception' meets 'Pictionary'—if the players were underwater and the prize was a rejected conference paper. Coming this fall: THE SIGNAL IN THE NOISE.

title: Extreme Value Policy Optimization for Safe Reinforcement Learning
author_site: Shiqing Gao, Yihang Zhou, Shuai Shao, Haoyu Luo, Yiheng Bing, Jiaxin Ding, Luoyi Fu, Xinbing Wang
abstract: Ensuring safety is a critical challenge in applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) to real-world scenarios. Constrained Reinforcement Learning (CRL) addresses this by maximizing returns under predefined constraints, typically formulated as the expected cumulative cost. However, expectation-based constraints overlook rare but high-impact extreme value events in the tail distribution, such as black swan incidents, which can lead to severe constraint violations. To address this issue, we propose the Extreme Value policy Optimization (EVO) algorithm, leveraging Extreme Value Theory (EVT) to model and exploit extreme reward and cost samples, reducing constraint violations. EVO introduces an extreme quantile optimization objective to explicitly capture extreme samples in the cost tail distribution. Additionally, we propose an extreme prioritization mechanism during replay, amplifying the learning signal from rare but high-impact extreme samples. Theoretically, we establish upper bounds on expected constraint violations during policy updates, guaranteeing strict constraint satisfaction at a zero-violation quantile level. Further, we demonstrate that EVO achieves a lower probability of constraint violations than expectation-based methods and exhibits lower variance than quantile regression methods. Extensive experiments show that EVO significantly reduces constraint violations during training while maintaining competitive policy performance compared to baselines.
author: Shiqing Gao;Yihang Zhou;Shuai Shao;Haoyu Luo;Yiheng Bing;Jiaxin Ding;Luoyi Fu;Xinbing Wang
