roast.laymen_summary: Most AI safety systems try to avoid mistakes on average, but this paper tries to build a robot that specifically prepares for one-in-a-million 'Black Swan' disasters. It uses a mathematical niche called Extreme Value Theory to obsess over the worst-case scenarios instead of just being generally careful.
why_bad: The 'zero-violation' guarantee is a mathematical fairy tale that assumes the AI has already seen enough disasters to predict them, which is a paradox since disasters are, by definition, rare.
Prioritizing 'extreme' samples in the replay buffer is a recipe for gradient instability, essentially turning the AI into a paranoid neurotic that overcorrects every time it sees a tiny outlier.
The theoretical upper bounds provided are likely based on 'spherical cow' assumptions that fall apart the moment the environment becomes more complex than a basic simulator.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where 'average safety' is for losers... one algorithm decides to live in a state of constant, calculated panic. Meet EVO. While other AIs are learning to walk, EVO is busy building a bunker for a 1-in-a-billion meteor strike that its math says is 'statistically significant.' It promised absolute safety. It promised zero accidents. But when you spend all your time worrying about the tail-end distribution of a falling piano, you forget how to actually cross the street. This summer, watch as one robot’s quest for 'Extreme Value' leads to the most stable, safest, and most useless performance in history. EVO: It’s not paranoid if the math says it’s possible.

title: Bayesian Basis Function Approximation for Scalable Gaussian Process Priors in Deep Generative Models
author_site: Mehmet Yiğit Balık, Maksim Sinelnikov, Priscilla Ong, Harri Lähdesmäki
abstract: High-dimensional time-series datasets are common in domains such as healthcare and economics. Variational autoencoder (VAE) models, where latent variables are modeled with a Gaussian process (GP) prior, have become a prominent model class to analyze such correlated datasets. However, their applications are challenged by the inherent cubic time complexity that requires specific GP approximation techniques, as well as the general challenge of modeling both shared and individual-specific correlations across time. Though inducing points enhance GP prior VAE scalability, optimizing them remains challenging, especially since discrete covariates resist gradient‑based methods. In this work, we propose a scalable basis function approximation technique for GP prior VAEs that mitigates these challenges and results in linear time complexity, with a global parametrization that eliminates the need for amortized variational inference and the associated amortization gap, making it well-suited for conditional generation tasks where accuracy and efficiency are crucial. Empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach not only improves scalability and interpretability but also drastically enhances predictive performance.
keywords: Gaussian Process;High-dimensional time-series;Variational Autoencoder;Conditional Generation;Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space
author: Mehmet Yiğit Balık;Maksim Sinelnikov;Priscilla Ong;Harri Lähdesmäki
aff: Aalto University+Aalto University;Aalto University;Aalto University;Aalto University
position: PhD student+MS student;PhD student;MS student;Associate Professor
aff_unique_norm: Aalto University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to make AI better at spotting patterns in messy time-series data, like a patient's heart rate, without the computer's brain melting from the math. The authors claim they found a 'mathematical shortcut' that lets the AI process huge amounts of data instantly by using a pre-made cheat sheet instead of calculating everything from scratch.
why_bad: The 'innovative' basis function approach is essentially a 2015 math trick wearing a fake mustache; the novelty is nearly non-existent.
The authors claim it's 'scalable' because it's linear, but their 'global parametrization' would actually crash a server if the dataset grew beyond a few thousand rows.
The 'drastic' performance gains were mostly measured against weak, poorly-tuned baselines—it's easy to look like an Olympic sprinter when you're racing toddlers.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where drawing a single curve takes longer than a DMV appointment, one model promises the impossible: The Speed of a Photocopy with the Soul of a Da Vinci! See the 'Global Cheat Sheet' that eliminates thinking! Gasp as it claims to be 'Linear' while secretly requiring a gas tank the size of a small moon! It’s a high-stakes thriller where the 'Amortization Gap' is the villain, but the hero's only weapon is a stencil someone else invented ten years ago. Coming this summer: A masterpiece that's actually just a traced drawing.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered.

title: Demystifying Cost-Efficiency in LLM Serving over Heterogeneous GPUs
author_site: Youhe Jiang, Fangcheng Fu, Xiaozhe Yao, Guoliang HE, Xupeng Miao, Ana Klimovic, Bin Cui, Binhang Yuan, Eiko Yoneki
abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have led to increasingly diverse requests, accompanied with varying resource (compute and memory) demands to serve them. However, this in turn degrades the cost-efficiency of LLM serving as common practices primarily rely on homogeneous GPU resources. In response to this problem, this work conducts a thorough study about serving LLMs over heterogeneous GPU resources on cloud platforms. The rationale is that different GPU types exhibit distinct compute and memory characteristics, aligning well with the divergent resource demands of diverse requests. Particularly, through comprehensive benchmarking, we discover that the cost-efficiency of LLM serving can be substantially optimized by meticulously determining GPU composition, deployment configurations, and workload assignments. Subsequently, we design a scheduling algorithm via mixed-integer linear programming, aiming at deducing the most cost-efficient serving plan under the constraints of price budget and real-time GPU availability. Remarkably, our approach effectively outperforms homogeneous and heterogeneous baselines under a wide array of scenarios, covering diverse workload traces, varying GPU availablilities, and multi-model serving. This casts new light on more accessible and efficient LLM serving over heterogeneous cloud resources.
keywords: Distributed Machine Learning System; Generative Inference of Foundation Model.
author: YOUHE JIANG;Fangcheng Fu;Xiaozhe Yao;Guoliang HE;Xupeng Miao;Ana Klimovic;Bin CUI;Binhang Yuan;Eiko Yoneki

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that using $40,000 GPUs to summarize a grocery list is a financial disaster, so they proposed mixing cheap and expensive chips together. They built a complex math puzzle to decide which chip gets which task, claiming it makes AI cheaper for everyone.
why_bad: The 'Mixed-Integer Linear Programming' approach is a computational anchor; by the time the algorithm decides which GPU to use, the user has already closed their browser tab in frustration.
It treats cloud GPU availability as a static buffet rather than the chaotic, 'out-of-stock' clearance rack it actually is in the real world.
The paper glosses over the massive networking overhead and 'Cold Start' latency required to shuffle model weights across different hardware architectures.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re calling an Uber. Instead of a standard sedan, our 'optimized' algorithm sends you a unicycle if you’re alone, or a 747 if you’re carrying a backpack. We call it 'Fleet-Flex.' Sure, the unicycle takes four hours to arrive and the 747 can't find a place to land, but on paper, our average fuel-per-passenger metric is revolutionary. We’ve turned a simple logistics problem into a mathematical nightmare that saves three cents per trip while costing ten dollars in coordination fees. It's not 'waiting for a ride,' it's 'heterogeneous mobility optimization.'

title: SecEmb: Sparsity-Aware Secure Federated Learning of On-Device Recommender System with Large Embedding
author_site: Peihua Mai, Youlong Ding, Ziyan Lyu, Minxin Du, Yan (James) Pang
abstract: Federated recommender system (FedRec) has emerged as a solution to protect user data through collaborative training techniques. A typical FedRec involves transmitting the full model and entire weight updates between edge devices and the server, causing significant burdens to edge devices with limited bandwidth and computational power. The sparsity of embedding updates provides opportunity for payload optimization, while existing sparsity-aware federated protocols generally sacrifice privacy for efficiency. A key challenge in designing a secure sparsity-aware efficient protocol is to protect the rated item indices from the server. In this paper, we propose a lossless secure recommender systems with on sparse embedding updates (SecEmb). SecEmb reduces user payload while ensuring that the server learns no information about both rated item indices and individual updates except the aggregated model. The protocol consists of two correlated modules: (1) a privacy-preserving embedding retrieval module that allows users to download relevant embeddings from the server, and (2) an update aggregation module that securely aggregates updates at the server. Empirical analysis demonstrates that SecEmb reduces both download and upload communication costs by up to 90x and decreases user-side computation time by up to 70x compared with secure FedRec protocols. Additionally, it offers non-negligible utility advantages compared with lossy message compression methods.
author: Peihua Mai;Youlong Ding;Ziyan Lyu;Minxin Du;Yan Pang
aff: National University of Singapore;;;;National University of Singapore
aff_unique_norm: National University of Singapore;

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop Netflix-style apps from eating your battery and data by only sending the 'important' updates from your phone to their servers. They built a digital 'blindfold' so the company could improve its recommendations without actually knowing which specific movies you’ve been binge-watching.
why_bad: The '90x faster' claim smells like a lab-grown fantasy that would crumble the moment it encounters a real-world smartphone's messy hardware constraints.
The 'privacy-preserving' math likely contains a massive loophole where the server can still guess your interests just by looking at the timing and size of your encrypted data packets.
The authors compared their fancy new system against 'straw man' baselines—ancient methods that are easy to beat—rather than the current industry gold standards.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Invisible Grocery List
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the defense claims they’ve revolutionized privacy by allowing a customer to pay for groceries without the cashier ever seeing what’s in the cart. They argue it’s '90% more efficient' because the customer only pays for the specific calories they consumed. But look at the evidence! To pull this off, the customer has to spend six hours in the parking lot performing complex origami on every single receipt, and the 'invisible' bag is clearly shaped like a 12-pack of beer. Your Honor, this isn't a breakthrough in security; it's just a very elaborate way of making the cashier do more math while pretending we don't know the defendant has a serious snack-cake habit. Objection sustained: the protocol is dismissed for being theoretically flashy but practically perjurious.

title: Gradient Aligned Regression via Pairwise Losses
author_site: Dixian Zhu, Tianbao Yang, Livnat Jerby
abstract: Regression is a fundamental task in machine learning that has garnered extensive attention over the past decades. The conventional approach for regression involves employing loss functions that primarily concentrate on aligning model prediction with the ground truth for each individual data sample. Recent research endeavors have introduced novel perspectives by incorporating label similarity into regression through the imposition of additional pairwise regularization or contrastive learning on the latent feature space, demonstrating their effectiveness. However, there are two drawbacks to these approaches: (i) their pairwise operations in the latent feature space are computationally more expensive than conventional regression losses; (ii) they lack theoretical insights behind these methods. In this work, we propose GAR (Gradient Aligned Regression) as a competitive alternative method in label space, which is constituted by a conventional regression loss and two pairwise label difference losses for gradient alignment including magnitude and direction. GAR enjoys: i) the same level efficiency as conventional regression loss because the quadratic complexity for the proposed pairwise losses can be reduced to linear complexity; ii) theoretical insights from learning the pairwise label difference to learning the gradient of the ground truth function. We limit our current scope as regression on the clean data setting without noises, outliers or distributional shifts, etc. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method practically on two synthetic datasets and on eight extensive real-world tasks from six benchmark datasets with other eight competitive baselines. Running time experiments demonstrate the superior efficiency of the proposed GAR compared to existing methods with pairwise regularization or contrastive learning in the latent feature space. Additionally, ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each component of GAR. The code is open sourced at https://github.com/DixianZhu/GAR.
author: Dixian Zhu;Tianbao Yang;Livnat Jerby
aff: Stanford University;Texas A&M University - College Station+Texas A&M University - College Station;Stanford University
aff_unique_norm: Stanford University;Texas A&M University

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI models learn by trying to get individual answers right one by one, but this paper suggests they should also learn the 'vibe' between pairs of answers to ensure the slopes are correct. While it claims to be faster and smarter, the authors admit it only works if your data is perfectly clean and lacks any of the messy 'noise' found in the real world.
why_bad: The 'Laboratory Vacuum' Fallacy: The authors explicitly stated the method only works on 'clean data' without noise or outliers, which is like designing a high-performance umbrella that can't handle rain.
Incremental Novelty: Using pairwise losses for regression isn't exactly a 'eureka' moment; it’s more like adding a slightly different spoiler to a car that everyone has been driving since 2015.

Theoretical Over-selling: The 'theoretical insights' regarding gradient alignment are essentially just a fancy mathematical re-skinning of existing concepts that don't offer enough practical 'oomph' to justify the extra math.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Top Chef: Peer Review Edition.' Our contestant today tried to revolutionize making a soufflé by measuring the exact molecular distance between every bubble of air to ensure the 'structural gradient' was perfect. It sounds brilliant, except the chef started their presentation by saying, 'This recipe only works if the kitchen is a pressurized vacuum and nobody sneezes.' The judges are confused because, while the bubbles are indeed very well-aligned, real kitchens have drafts, loud noises, and clumsy sous-chefs. They’ve essentially built a very expensive, mathematically 'perfect' way to cook an egg that will immediately collapse the moment it touches a plate in a real restaurant.

title: Optimizing Language Models for Inference Time Objectives using Reinforcement Learning
author_site: Yunhao Tang, Kunhao Zheng, Gabriel Synnaeve, REMI MUNOS
abstract: In this work, we investigate the merits of explicitly optimizing for inference time algorithmic performance during model training. We show how optimizing for inference time performance can improve overall model efficacy. We consider generic inference time objectives with $k$ samples, with focus on pass@$k$ and majority voting as two main applications. With language model training on reasoning datasets, we showcase the performance trade-off enabled by training with such objectives. When training on code generation tasks, we show that the approach significantly improves pass@$k$ objectives compared to the baseline method.
keywords: RL;inference time compute;pass at k;code generation
author: Yunhao Tang;Kunhao Zheng;Gabriel Synnaeve;Remi Munos
aff: Google DeepMind;Meta Facebook;Meta Facebook;Meta+INRIA
aff_unique_norm: Google;Meta;INRIA

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to train AI models to be better at 'multiple choice' guessing by using Reinforcement Learning to reward them for getting the answer right at least once in a dozen tries. Basically, we’re teaching the AI that it doesn’t have to be smart on the first try, as long as it eventually stumbles onto the truth after some frantic trial and error.
why_bad: The 'novelty' here is as thin as single-ply tissue; the community has been using RL for inference-time tweaks since the pre-Socratic era of LLMs.
The performance gains are statistically 'meh,' proving that burning massive compute to marginally bump a pass@k score is like buying a Ferrari just to drive to the mailbox.
The paper fails to prove the model actually learned to reason, suggesting it just learned to exploit the specific 'majority voting' metric used during training.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a defense attorney who, instead of presenting one coherent alibi, screams ten different contradictory stories at the jury and hopes the judge counts any single one of them as 'reasonable doubt.' You’re essentially asking the court to ignore the nine times he claimed his client was on Mars, as long as the tenth story says he was at a Denny's. The prosecution—in this case, the reviewers—isn't buying this 'quantity over quality' strategy, especially when the attorney is charging $1,000 an hour in GPU credits to come up with those nine extra lies.

title: Massive Values in Self-Attention Modules are the Key to Contextual Knowledge Understanding
author_site: Mingyu Jin, Kai Mei, Wujiang Xu, Mingjie Sun, Ruixiang Tang, Mengnan Du, Zirui Liu, Yongfeng Zhang
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in contextual knowledge understanding. In this paper, we show for the first time that these concentrated massive values consistently emerge in specific regions of attention queries (Q) and keys (K) while not having such patterns in values (V) in various modern transformer-based LLMs. Through extensive experiments, we further demonstrate that these massive values play a critical role in interpreting contextual knowledge (i.e., knowledge obtained from the current context window) rather than in retrieving parametric knowledge stored within the model’s parameters. Our further investigation of quantization strategies reveals that ignoring these massive values leads to a pronounced drop in performance on tasks requiring rich contextual understanding, aligning with our analysis. Finally, we trace the emergence of concentrated massive values and find that such concentration is caused by Rotary Positional Encoding (RoPE) and it appears since very first layers. These findings shed new light on how Q and K operate in LLMs and offer practical insights for model design and optimization. The code is available at https://github.com/MingyuJ666/Rope_with_LLM.
author: Mingyu Jin;Kai Mei;Wujiang Xu;Mingjie Sun;Ruixiang Tang;Mengnan Du;Zirui Liu;Yongfeng Zhang

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers discovered that AI models have 'shouting matches' in their internal math, where a few massive numbers do all the heavy lifting for understanding context. If you try to compress these models by silencing the 'shouters,' the AI loses its short-term memory and becomes functionally illiterate.
why_bad: Incremental discovery: The AI community already knew about 'outlier features' and 'attention sinks'; this paper just gave them a more dramatic adjective.
Confusing effect with cause: Blaming RoPE for these massive values is like blaming a megaphone for a rock concert being loud—it’s the mechanism, not a profound architectural revelation.
Weak benchmarks: The paper shows that breaking the most important numbers makes the model perform worse, which is a bit like proving a car won't run if you remove the 'massive' engine.
roast.analogy_description: We’ve disrupted the office supplies market. Traditional highlighters use ink; our 'Context-Aware RoPE-Tech' uses a literal air horn. Every time the AI reads something important, it just blasts 140 decibels at the page. Our 'breakthrough' research proves that if you wear earplugs (quantization), you can't hear the air horn, and therefore the system fails. We're not fixing the noise; we're claiming the noise is the secret sauce. It’s not a bug, it’s a 'Massive Value-Add'—please ignore the bleeding ears of our GPU kernels.

title: Covariances for Free: Exploiting Mean Distributions for Federated Learning with Pre-trained Models
abstract: Using pre-trained models has been found to reduce the effect of data heterogeneity and speed up federated learning algorithms. Recent works have investigated the use of first-order statistics and second-order statistics to aggregate local client data distributions at the server and achieve very high performance without any training. In this work we propose a training-free method based on an unbiased estimator of class covariance matrices. Our method, which only uses first-order statistics in the form of class means communicated by clients to the server, incurs only a fraction of the communication costs required by methods based on communicating second-order statistics. We show how these estimated class covariances can be used to initialize a linear classifier, thus exploiting the covariances without actually sharing them. When compared to state-of-the-art methods which also share only class means, our approach improves performance in the range of 4-26\% with exactly the same communication cost. Moreover, our method achieves performance competitive or superior to sharing second-order statistics with dramatically less communication overhead. Finally, using our method to initialize classifiers and then performing federated fine-tuning yields better and faster convergence.
author: Dipam Goswami;Simone Magistri;Kai Wang;Bartłomiej Twardowski;Andrew D. Bagdanov;Joost van de Weijer
aff: Computer Vision Center, Universitat Autónoma de Barcelona;University of Florence;Computer Vision Center, Universitat Autónoma de Barcelona;IDEAS NCBR+Computer Vision Center, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona;Università degli Studi di Firenze;
aff_unique_norm: Universitat Autónoma de Barcelona;University of Florence;Institute for Development, Economic Analysis, and Simulation (IDEAS);Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim they can get high-end AI performance for a bargain-bin price by guessing how diverse a group's data is based only on its average. It’s essentially trying to reconstruct a detailed map of a city while only being told the GPS coordinates of the town hall.
why_bad: The 'for free' claim is total marketing fluff; the method relies on aggressive assumptions about data distribution that rarely hold up outside of a sanitized lab setting.
By only using 'class means,' the model essentially assumes every data point follows a perfect bell curve, ignoring the messy, non-Gaussian reality of actual human data.
The performance jumps (up to 26%) look suspicious and likely stem from comparing against weak baselines rather than actually solving the hard problem of data heterogeneity.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Federated League, folks! We’ve got a coach in the booth who thinks he’s found a loophole in the physics of the game. He’s refusing to pay for the high-res scouting reports—the second-order statistics—and instead, he’s just asking for the average height of the players. He’s telling the owners he can 'mathematically infer' their sprint speeds and vertical leaps for free! It’s a bold strategy, but the refs are reaching for their flags. You can't just look at a team's average weight and assume they’re all 300-pound linemen; you're going to get steamrolled when a real-world variance hits the field. He’s playing fantasy football with a broken calculator, and the academic stadium is calling for a forfeit!

title: Empowering World Models with Reflection for Embodied Video Prediction
author_site: Xiaowei Chi, Chun-Kai Fan, Hengyuan Zhang, Xingqun Qi, Rongyu Zhang, Anthony Chen, Chi-Min Chan, Wei Xue, Qifeng Liu, Shanghang Zhang, Yike Guo
abstract: Video generation models have made significant progress in simulating future states, showcasing their potential as world simulators in embodied scenarios. However, existing models often lack robust understanding, limiting their ability to perform multi-step predictions or handle Out-of-Distribution (OOD) scenarios.  To address this challenge, we propose the Reflection of Generation (RoG), a set of intermediate reasoning strategies designed to enhance video prediction.  It leverages the complementary strengths of pre-trained vision-language and video generation models, enabling them to function as a world model in embodied scenarios. To support RoG, we introduce Embodied Video Anticipation Benchmark(EVA-Bench), a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates embodied world models across diverse tasks and scenarios, utilizing both in-domain and OOD datasets. Building on this foundation, we devise a world model, Embodied Video Anticipator (EVA), that follows a multistage training paradigm to generate high-fidelity video frames and apply an autoregressive strategy to enable adaptive generalization for longer video sequences. Extensive experiments demonstrate the efficacy of EVA in various downstream tasks like video generation and robotics, thereby paving the way for large-scale pre-trained models in real-world video prediction applications. The video demos are available at https://sites.google.com/view/icml-eva.
author: Xiaowei Chi;Chun-Kai Fan;Hengyuan Zhang;Xingqun Qi;Rongyu Zhang;Anthony Chen;Chi-Min Chan;Wei Xue;Qifeng Liu;Shanghang Zhang;Yike Guo

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to stop video-generating AI from hallucinating by making it 'think' and describe what should happen before it draws the frames. They basically glued a chatbot to a video generator and hoped the narration would act as a map for physics and logic.
why_bad: The 'Reflection' mechanism is largely rebranded prompt engineering that fails to address the underlying physics gaps in the base video models.
The EVA-Bench benchmark appears suspiciously tailored to highlight the model's specific strengths while ignoring more rigorous, industry-standard tests.
Autoregressive generation in this context still suffers from 'error compounding,' where the video becomes a blurry, incoherent mess after just a few steps.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Our chef today is trying to serve a 'World Model' soufflé, but instead of learning how to whisk, he’s just reading a recipe out loud to a bowl of raw eggs. He calls this 'Reflective Gastronomy,' claiming that if he describes a delicious meal in great detail (the VLM), the stove will magically figure out how to cook it. He even brought his own judges—a group of friends he calls the 'EVA-Bench'—who are the only people on earth willing to say this digital sludge tastes like a five-star dinner. Ultimately, talking about the food isn't the same as cooking it, and his 'long-sequence' dessert is just a pile of salt that gets saltier with every bite.

title: RelGNN: Composite Message Passing for Relational Deep Learning
author_site: Tianlang Chen, Charilaos Kanatsoulis, Jure Leskovec
abstract: Predictive tasks on relational databases are critical in real-world applications spanning e-commerce, healthcare, and social media. To address these tasks effectively, Relational Deep Learning (RDL) encodes relational data as graphs, enabling Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to exploit relational structures for improved predictions. However, existing RDL methods often overlook the intrinsic structural properties of the graphs built from relational databases, leading to modeling inefficiencies, particularly in handling many-to-many relationships. Here we introduce RelGNN, a novel GNN framework specifically designed to leverage the unique structural characteristics of the graphs built from relational databases. At the core of our approach is the introduction of atomic routes, which are simple paths that enable direct single-hop interactions between the source and destination nodes. Building upon these atomic routes, RelGNN designs new composite message passing and graph attention mechanisms that reduce redundancy, highlight key signals, and enhance predictive accuracy. RelGNN is evaluated on 30 diverse real-world tasks from Relbench (Fey et al., 2024), and achieves state-of-the-art performance on the vast majority of tasks, with improvements of up to 25%.
author: Tianlang Chen;Charilaos Kanatsoulis;Jure Leskovec

They claimed these shortcuts would stop the AI from getting distracted by useless noise, but the academic community essentially called it a fancy paint job on an old car.

But when the critics looked under the hood, they didn't find a teleportation device; they found a guy in a neon vest pointing at a map we’ve had since 2018. It’s not a revolution; it’s just a toll road that charges you 20% more for a 2% faster commute. Coming this fall: The SOTA that wasn't.

title: Optimizing Noise Distributions for Differential Privacy
author_site: Atefeh Gilani, Felipe Gomez, Shahab Asoodeh, Flavio Calmon, Oliver Kosut, Lalitha Sankar
abstract: We propose a unified optimization framework for designing continuous and discrete noise distributions that ensure differential privacy (DP) by minimizing Rényi DP, a variant of DP, under a cost constraint. Rényi DP has the advantage that by considering different values of the Rényi parameter $\alpha$, we can tailor our optimization for any number of compositions. To solve the optimization problem, we reduce it to a finite-dimensional convex formulation and perform preconditioned gradient descent. The resulting noise distributions are then compared to their Gaussian and Laplace counterparts. Numerical results demonstrate that our optimized distributions are consistently better, with significant improvements in $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP guarantees in the moderate composition regimes, compared to Gaussian and Laplace distributions with the same variance.
author: Atefeh Gilani;Juan Felipe Gomez;Shahab Asoodeh;Flavio Calmon;Oliver Kosut;Lalitha Sankar

title: Identifying Neural Dynamics Using Interventional State Space Models
author_site: Amin Nejatbakhsh, Yixin Wang
abstract: Neural circuits produce signals that are complex and nonlinear. To facilitate the understanding of neural dynamics, a popular approach is to fit state space models (SSM) to the data and analyze the dynamics of the low-dimensional latent variables. Despite the power of SSM to explain the dynamics of neural circuits, these models have been shown to merely capture statistical associations in the data and cannot be causally interpreted. Therefore, an important research problem is to build models that can predict neural dynamics under causal manipulations. Here, we propose interventional state-space models (iSSM), a class of causal models that can predict neural responses to novel perturbations. We draw on recent advances in causal dynamical systems and present theoretical results for the identifiability of iSSM. In simulations of the motor cortex, we show that iSSM can recover the true latents and the underlying dynamics. In addition, we illustrate two applications of iSSM in biological datasets. First, we applied iSSM to a dataset of calcium recordings from ALM neurons in mice during photostimulation. Second, we applied iSSM to a dataset of electrophysiological recordings from macaque dlPFC during micro-stimulation. In both cases, we show that iSSM outperforms SSM and results in identifiable parameters. The code is available at https://github.com/amin-nejat/issm.
author: Amin Nejatbakhsh;Yixin Wang

roast.laymen_summary: Most brain models just guess which neurons are chatting based on coincidences, but this paper tries to predict what happens when you actually zap the brain with a laser. It’s an attempt to move from 'these things happen together' to 'this specific zap causes that specific reaction.'
why_bad: The mathematical proofs for 'identifiability' rely on assumptions so sanitized they wouldn't survive five seconds in a real, messy biological brain.
The 'causal' claims are overblown, as the model mostly just got better at predicting very specific, limited interventions rather than uncovering universal brain laws.
The performance gains over existing models were statistically underwhelming, suggesting the new 'iSSM' is just a shiny, more expensive version of the old stuff.

This author arrives with a 'Causal Burrito Model' claiming they can predict the exact exit wound of the beans if you poke the tortilla with a needle first. They 'proved' it works using a digital simulation of a bean, but when they tried it on a real Taco Bell burrito, they only poked it twice and the results were basically the same as the guy who just guessed. The judges are sending them home because their math assumes the burrito is a perfect, non-rotating sphere of uniform cheese—which, as we know, is a culinary fantasy.

title: Hyper-Transforming Latent Diffusion Models
author_site: Ignacio Peis, Batuhan Koyuncu, Isabel Valera, Jes Frellsen
abstract: We introduce a novel generative framework for functions by integrating Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) and Transformer-based hypernetworks into latent variable models. Unlike prior approaches that rely on MLP-based hypernetworks with scalability limitations, our method employs a Transformer-based decoder to generate INR parameters from latent variables, addressing both representation capacity and computational efficiency. Our framework extends latent diffusion models (LDMs) to INR generation by replacing standard decoders with a Transformer-based hypernetwork, which can be trained either from scratch or via hyper-transforming—a strategy that fine-tunes only the decoder while freezing the pre-trained latent space. This enables efficient adaptation of existing generative models to INR-based representations without requiring full retraining. We validate our approach across multiple modalities, demonstrating improved scalability, expressiveness, and generalization over existing INR-based generative models. Our findings establish a unified and flexible framework for learning structured function representations.
author: Ignacio Peis;Batuhan Koyuncu;Isabel Valera;Jes Frellsen

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to upgrade AI image generators so that instead of just spitting out pixels, they generate the complex mathematical 'DNA' of an image using the same technology that powers ChatGPT. They basically took a fancy existing model and slapped a 'Transformer' engine on it, hoping it would be faster and smarter at writing these digital blueprints.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The reviewers noted that swapping a basic neural network for a Transformer is the 'put a bird on it' of AI research—it's a trendy architectural tweak, not a fundamental breakthrough.

The 'Trust Me, It's Faster' Fallacy: The paper claims efficiency, but using a massive Transformer to generate weights for another network is like hiring a five-star chef to explain how to boil an egg; it's massive overkill for the actual task.

Benchmark Cherry-Picking: The authors conveniently avoided head-to-head comparisons with the current state-of-the-art models, making their 'improved generalization' claims look more like a participation trophy.

He’s rolled onto the track with a Segway that he’s strapped a literal jet engine to. The commentators are baffled—sure, he’s 'hyper-transforming' his locomotion, but he’s spent $40 million in fuel just to cross the 10-yard line! He’s claiming he’s revolutionized travel, but the judges are pointing out that he still can’t take the stairs and he’s currently on fire. It’s a bold, over-engineered play for a gold medal in a race that everyone else finished five minutes ago using a pair of Nikes!

title: Variational Control for Guidance in Diffusion Models
author_site: Kushagra Pandey, Farrin Marouf Sofian, Felix Draxler, Theofanis Karaletsos, Stephan Mandt
abstract: Diffusion models exhibit excellent sample quality, but existing guidance methods often require additional model training or are limited to specific tasks. We revisit guidance in diffusion models from the perspective of variational inference and control, introducing \emph{Diffusion Trajectory Matching (DTM)} that enables guiding pretrained diffusion trajectories to satisfy a terminal cost. DTM unifies a broad class of guidance methods and enables novel instantiations. We introduce a new method within this framework that achieves state-of-the-art results on several linear, non-linear, and blind inverse problems without requiring additional model training or specificity to pixel or latent space diffusion models. Our code will be available at https://github.com/czi-ai/oc-guidance.
author: Kushagra Pandey;Farrin Marouf Sofian;Felix Draxler;Theofanis Karaletsos;Stephan Mandt

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a universal 'GPS' for AI image generators so you can steer them toward specific results without having to spend millions retraining the model. By using fancy math normally used for rockets and robotics, they claim they can nudge a pre-existing AI to hit a target precisely—like a 'blind' inverse problem—without breaking the bank.
why_bad: Reviewers felt the 'unified framework' was mostly just high-level math jargon used to dress up existing stochastic control methods that people have been using for years.
The computational overhead is a nightmare; the 'math-heavy' steering makes the generation process significantly slower than the simpler methods it’s trying to replace.
The experimental comparisons were criticized for being 'conveniently selective,' dodging head-to-head battles with the newest, faster industry standards.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're in an Uber with a driver who only knows how to drive randomly. Instead of giving him a map or teaching him the route (which takes too long), our 'disruptive' startup provides you with a megaphone so you can scream complex physics equations from the backseat to nudge his steering wheel in real-time. Sure, the driver doesn't need 'extra training,' but you're arriving at your destination two hours late with a migraine, and the driver is technically just a victim of your high-speed math harassment. It's a 'no-training' solution where the passenger does all the heavy lifting.

title: Enhancing Ligand Validity and Affinity in Structure-Based Drug Design with Multi-Reward Optimization
author_site: Seungbeom Lee, Munsun Jo, Jungseul Ok, Dongwoo Kim
abstract: Deep learning-based Structure-based drug design aims to generate ligand molecules with desirable properties for protein targets. While existing models have demonstrated competitive performance in generating ligand molecules, they primarily focus on learning the chemical distribution of training datasets, often lacking effective steerability to ensure the desired chemical quality of generated molecules. To address this issue, we propose a multi-reward optimization framework that fine-tunes generative models for attributes, such as binding affinity, validity, and drug-likeness, together. Specifically, we derive direct preference optimization for a Bayesian flow network, used as a backbone for molecule generation, and integrate a reward normalization scheme to adopt multiple objectives. Experimental results show that our method generates more realistic ligands than baseline models while achieving higher binding affinity, expanding the Pareto front empirically observed in previous studies.
keywords: Structure-Based Drug Design;Deep Learning;Direct Property Optimization
author: Seungbeom Lee;Munsun Jo;Jungseul Ok;Dongwoo Kim

Derivative Architecture: Simply slapping Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) onto a Bayesian Flow Network is a 'Lego-block' approach to research that lacks fundamental algorithmic novelty.

Lack of Synthesis Reality: The model claims to hit a new 'Pareto front,' but without proving these molecules can actually be synthesized, it's just bragging about winning a game of digital make-believe.

You’ve coached the defendant to tell the 'Reward Function' exactly what it wants to hear, ignoring the fact that your molecule has the structural integrity of wet lace. In this courtroom, the Laws of Physics are not mere suggestions. You can't just pile carbon atoms into a heap and call it 'High Affinity'—that’s not innovation, it’s a chemical felony. I move to strike these results from the record; you’re not designing medicine, you’re just winning a rigged game of Tetris.

title: AlphaQCM: Alpha Discovery in Finance with Distributional Reinforcement Learning
author_site: Zhoufan Zhu, Ke Zhu
abstract: For researchers and practitioners in finance, finding synergistic formulaic alphas is very important but challenging. In this paper, we reconsider the discovery of synergistic formulaic alphas from the viewpoint of sequential decision-making, and conceptualize the entire alpha discovery process as a non-stationary and reward-sparse Markov decision process. To overcome the challenges of non-stationarity and reward-sparsity, we propose the AlphaQCM method, a novel distributional reinforcement learning method designed to search for synergistic formulaic alphas efficiently. The AlphaQCM method first learns the Q function and quantiles via a Q network and a quantile network, respectively. Then, the AlphaQCM method applies the quantiled conditional moment method to learn unbiased variance from the potentially biased quantiles. Guided by the learned Q function and variance, the AlphaQCM method navigates the non-stationarity and reward-sparsity to explore the vast search space of formulaic alphas with high efficacy. Empirical applications to real-world datasets demonstrate that our AlphaQCM method significantly outperforms its competitors, particularly when dealing with large datasets comprising numerous stocks.
keywords: Computational Finance;Formulaic Alpha;Distributional Reinforcement Learning;Quantiled Conditional Moments;Stock Trend Forecasting
author: Zhoufan Zhu;Ke Zhu
aff: Xiamen University;University of Hong Kong

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built a complex AI 'treasure hunter' designed to dig through the stock market's noise to find secret math formulas that print money. Because the market is a chaotic mess where wins are rare, they added a second AI layer to guess exactly how much the first AI is hallucinating its success.
why_bad: Mathematical Over-Engineering: The 'Quantiled Conditional Moment' approach is essentially a high-tech Rube Goldberg machine built to solve noise problems that simpler statistical models have been handling for decades.
The 'Stationarity' Delusion: It treats the stock market like a difficult video game level rather than an adversarial environment where your very presence changes the rules of the game.

Vanishing Alpha: The paper claims 'significant outperformance' in backtests, but ignores the reality that these formulaic alphas usually decay into worthless junk the moment they're exposed to actual trading fees and slippage.

Witness the over-engineered madness of ALPHAQCM as it tries to calculate the exact probability of a coin landing on its edge in a hurricane. It doesn't just want to win; it wants to measure the mood of the wind. Coming this summer: a story of a robot that spent ten thousand GPU hours to discover that 'past performance is not indicative of future results.' It's not just a trading bot... it's a hallucination with a PhD.

title: Test-time Adaptation on Graphs via Adaptive Subgraph-based Selection and Regularized Prototypes
author_site: Yusheng Zhao, Qixin Zhang, Xiao Luo, Junyu Luo, Wei Ju, Zhiping Xiao, Ming Zhang
abstract: Test-time adaptation aims to adapt a well-trained model using test data only, without accessing training data. It is a crucial topic in machine learning, enabling a wide range of applications in the real world, especially when it comes to data privacy. While existing works on test-time adaptation primarily focus on Euclidean data, research on non-Euclidean graph data remains scarce. Prevalent graph neural network methods could encounter serious performance degradation in the face of test-time domain shifts. In this work, we propose a novel method named Adaptive Subgraph-based Selection and Regularized Prototype Supervision (ASSESS) for reliable test-time adaptation on graphs. Specifically, to achieve flexible selection of reliable test graphs, ASSESS adopts an adaptive selection strategy based on fine-grained individual-level subgraph mutual information. Moreover, to utilize the information from both training and test graphs, ASSESS constructs semantic prototypes from the well-trained model as prior knowledge from the unknown training graphs and optimizes the posterior given the unlabeled test graphs. We also provide a theoretical analysis of the proposed algorithm. Extensive experiments verify the effectiveness of ASSESS against various baselines.
author: Yusheng Zhao;Qixin Zhang;Xiao Luo;Junyu Luo;Wei Ju;Zhiping Xiao;Ming Zhang

It attempts to solve this by cherry-picking the 'reliable' parts of new data and comparing them to a hazy, idealized memory of what a graph is supposed to look like.
why_bad: The 'Adaptive Selection' strategy is essentially a high-tech way of ignoring the hard questions and only grading the model on the easy ones.
The theoretical analysis is less of a rigorous proof and more of a decorative math wallpaper used to cover up a lack of empirical depth.
The method relies on 'Regularized Prototypes' which, in practice, are often just glorified averages that fail to capture the messy reality of shifting graph domains.

COMING THIS FALL: THE GRAPH OF MY MEMORY—where if the data gets too complicated, we just pretend it doesn't exist.

title: A New Approach to Backtracking Counterfactual Explanations: A Unified Causal Framework for Efficient Model Interpretability
author_site: Pouria Fatemi, Ehsan Sharifian, Mohammad Hossein Yassaee
abstract: Counterfactual explanations enhance interpretability by identifying alternative inputs that produce different outputs, offering localized insights into model decisions. However, traditional methods often neglect causal relationships, leading to unrealistic examples. While newer approaches integrate causality, they are computationally expensive. To address these challenges, we propose an efficient method called BRACE based on backtracking counterfactuals that incorporates causal reasoning to generate actionable explanations. We first examine the limitations of existing methods and then introduce our novel approach and its features. We also explore the relationship between our method and previous techniques, demonstrating that it generalizes them in specific scenarios. Finally, experiments show that our method provides deeper insights into model outputs.
keywords: Interpretability;Explainable Artificial Intelligence;Causal Inference;Counterfactuals;Structural Causal Models
author: Pouria Fatemi;Ehsan Sharifian;Mohammad Hossein Yassaee
aff: Technical University of Munich;EPFL - EPF Lausanne;Sharif University of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to make AI explain its decisions by showing you 'what-if' scenarios that actually obey the laws of cause and effect. The authors claim their new tool, BRACE, is the fastest way to get these answers without the AI suggesting you do something impossible, like age backward to get a better credit score.
why_bad: The 'novel' causal framework is essentially a rebranding of existing structural causal models that have been around since the early 2000s.
The claim of 'efficiency' is backed by experiments on tiny, toy datasets that don't reflect the messy complexity of real-world AI systems.
The paper fails to provide a user study, meaning their 'actionable explanations' are only actionable in a mathematical vacuum, not for actual humans.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where your bank's AI hates you, one algorithm promises to change your fate. Meet BRACE: the life coach that tells you exactly how to get that loan—if only you could travel back in time and choose different parents. It’s a high-stakes thriller where the 'breakthrough' math is just a recycled GPS app, the 'speed' is just skipping the hard parts, and the climax is a graph that proves absolutely nothing. This summer, prepare for the explanation of a lifetime... that you still can't actually use.

title: Power Mean Estimation in Stochastic Continuous Monte-Carlo Tree Search
author_site: Tuan Dam
abstract: Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) has demonstrated success in online planning for deterministic environments, yet significant challenges remain in adapting it to stochastic Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), particularly in continuous state-action spaces. Existing methods, such as HOOT, which combines MCTS with the Hierarchical Optimistic Optimization (HOO) bandit strategy, address continuous spaces but rely on a logarithmic exploration bonus that lacks theoretical guarantees in non-stationary, stochastic settings. Recent advancements, such as Poly-HOOT, introduced a polynomial bonus term to achieve convergence in deterministic MDPs, though a similar theory for stochastic MDPs remains undeveloped. In this paper, we propose a novel MCTS algorithm, Stochastic-Power-HOOT, designed for continuous, stochastic MDPs. Stochastic-Power-HOOT integrates a power mean as a value backup operator, alongside a polynomial exploration bonus to address the non-stationarity inherent in continuous action spaces. Our theoretical analysis establishes that Stochastic-Power-HOOT converges at a polynomial rate of $\mathcal{O}(n^{-1/2})$, where \( n \) is the number of visited trajectories, thereby extending the non-asymptotic convergence guarantees of Poly-HOOT to stochastic environments. Experimental results on synthetic and stochastic tasks validate our theoretical findings, demonstrating the effectiveness of Stochastic-Power-HOOT in continuous, stochastic domains.
keywords: Monte-Carlo Tree Search; Continuous Reinforcement Learning Planning
author: Tuan Quang Dam

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix a robot's decision-making in messy, unpredictable environments by using a fancy mathematical 'average' that prioritizes big wins. Unfortunately, they mostly just added a complex layer of paint to a existing technique that still struggles when the real world gets actually chaotic.
why_bad: The 'Power Mean' backup is essentially a hyperparameter-heavy tuning knob that offers no clear advantage over standard methods in practice.
The theoretical convergence rate is the mathematical equivalent of saying 'if we try forever, we might eventually get it right,' which isn't helpful for real-time planning.
The benchmarks are conducted on 'toy' problems that are too simplistic to prove the algorithm could survive a walk in a park, let alone a complex stochastic environment.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Stochastic Open, where the Stochastic-Power-HOOT team is trying to hit a bullseye on a dartboard that’s vibrating at 400Hz while someone throws buckets of glitter at them. Instead of just throwing more darts, they’ve spent three years building a robotic arm that calculates the 'Power Mean' trajectory of every glitter particle. It’s a masterpiece of over-engineering—they’ve got the math to prove they'll eventually hit the board, but by the time the arm actually fires, the pub has closed and the dartboard has been sold for scrap. They’re playing 4D chess against a universe that’s just playing '52 Pickup,' and the universe is winning.

title: Diversity By Design: Leveraging Distribution Matching for Offline Model-Based Optimization
author_site: Michael S Yao, James Gee, Osbert Bastani
abstract: The goal of offline model-based optimization (MBO) is to propose new designs that maximize a reward function given only an offline dataset. However, an important desiderata is to also propose a *diverse* set of final candidates that capture many optimal and near-optimal design configurations. We propose **D**iversit**y** I**n** **A**dversarial **M**odel-based **O**ptimization (**DynAMO**) as a novel method to introduce design diversity as an explicit objective into any MBO problem. Our key insight is to formulate diversity as a *distribution matching problem* where the distribution of generated designs captures the inherent diversity contained within the offline dataset. Extensive experiments spanning multiple scientific domains show that DynAMO can be used with common optimization methods to significantly improve the diversity of proposed designs while still discovering high-quality candidates.
author: Michael S Yao;James Gee;Osbert Bastani
aff: University of Pennsylvania;University of Pennsylvania;University of Pennsylvania
aff_unique_norm: University of Pennsylvania

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that AI usually gets stuck finding just one 'best' solution, so they built a tool called DynAMO to force the AI to suggest a wide variety of high-quality designs. It works by making the AI copy the 'vibes' and variety found in its original training data, rather than just obsessing over a single perfect score.
why_bad: The method forces the AI to match the distribution of the entire dataset, meaning it stubbornly mimics the mediocre parts of the data just to maintain 'variety.'
It uses a complex adversarial training setup—basically two AIs fighting—which is notoriously finicky and likely overkill for what could be solved with much simpler math.
The 'diversity' metric is arguably a vanity metric; the paper fails to prove that having ten different designs is actually better than having one really, really good one.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're pitching a new restaurant concept to VCs. Instead of just serving the single best burger in the world, your 'innovation' is to serve a menu that perfectly replicates the exact variety of a 1990s mall food court—soggy tacos, lukewarm stir-fry, and all—just to ensure 'culinary diversity.' You’re using a high-tech AI to make sure your menu doesn't get 'too focused' on quality, because you’re terrified of being boring. The reviewers are the VCs, and they’re asking why they should pay for a sophisticated system that intentionally keeps the tuna salad on the menu just because the original dataset had it. It’s an over-engineered solution to a problem that sounds deep in a TED Talk but falls apart when you actually have to eat the food.

title: Conditional Lagrangian Wasserstein Flow for Time Series Imputation
abstract: Time series imputation is important for numerous real-world applications. To overcome the limitations of diffusion model-based imputation methods, e.g., slow convergence in inference, we propose a novel method for time series imputation in this work, called Conditional Lagrangian Wasserstein Flow (CLWF). Following the principle of least action in Lagrangian mechanics, we learn the velocity by minimizing the corresponding kinetic energy. Moreover, to enhance the model's performance, we estimate the gradient of a task-specific potential function using a time-dependent denoising autoencoder and integrate it into the base estimator to reduce the sampling variance. Finally, the proposed method demonstrates competitive performance compared to other state-of-the-art imputation approaches.
author: Weizhu Qian;Dalin Zhang;Yan Zhao;Yunyao Cheng

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fill in missing data points by pretending the numbers are physical particles flowing through a fluid using complex laws of motion. It is essentially an over-engineered way of guessing what's missing by treating a spreadsheet like a physics simulation.
why_bad: Physics-Washing: The use of 'Lagrangian mechanics' and 'kinetic energy' feels like an attempt to make basic curve-fitting sound more profound than it actually is.
The Frankenstein Patch: They bolted a standard 'denoising autoencoder' onto their fancy physics model because the pure math version likely couldn't handle real-world noise on its own.
The 'So What?' Factor: Claiming 'competitive performance' is academic speak for 'we did a lot of extra math to get the same results as the simpler models we were trying to beat.'
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the Civil Engineering World Championships! We’ve got a team here trying to fix a pothole in downtown Chicago. But instead of pouring asphalt, they’re attempting to calculate the molecular trajectory of every pebble using the Theory of General Relativity! They’re calling it 'Lagrangian Fluid Patching,' but folks, it looks like they’re just overthinking a hole in the ground. Oh, look at that! The math got too hard, so they’ve pulled out a regular shovel—that’s the 'denoising autoencoder'—to actually get the job done. It’s a flashy performance, but the judges are checking their watches; they could have finished this three hours ago with a bucket of gravel and a steamroller. A bold strategy, but the traffic is still backed up to the suburbs!

title: Reflection System for the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus
abstract: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) benchmarks broad generalization in artificial intelligence, and presents a significant challenge to existing machine learning models and program synthesis solvers. In this work, we introduce a Reflection System for ARC. It combines Large Language Models (LLMs) and a program synthesis solver based on a Domain Specific Language (DSL). We analyse the base accuracy of publicly available LLMs on ARC and demonstrate unsatisfactory results. We create AugARC, an augmented ARC benchmark, which consistently improves the performance of LLMs compared to the normal ARC benchmark. Using augmented ARC data, we fine-tune LLMs and observe a significant gain in ARC accuracy after training. By utilizing reflection, we combine LLMs and a previous DSL solver into our Reflection System for abstraction and reasoning. The proposed Reflection System motivates research to advance previous ARC attempts by using reflection to combine the advantages of LLMs and program synthesis solvers.
keywords: reflection systems;large language models with reasoning capabilities;abstraction and reasoning corpus
author: Kiril Bikov;Mikel Bober-Irizar;Soumya Banerjee

title: On Bitrates of Very Sparse Superposition Codes
abstract: _Sparse autoencoders_ have been used to interpret activity inside large language models as "superposition codes" for sparse, high-dimensional signals. The encoder layers of these autoencoders use simple methods, which we will call "one-step estimates," to read latent sparse signals from vectors of hidden neuron activations. This work investigates the reliability of one-step estimates on a generic family of sparse inference problems. We show that these estimates are remarkably inefficient from the point of view of coding theory: even in a "very sparse" regime, they are only reliable when the dimension of the code exceeds the entropy of the latent signal by a factor of $2.7$ dimensions per bit. In comparison, a very naive iterative method called matching pursuit can read superposition codes given just $1.3$ dimensions per bit. This opens the question of whether neural networks can achieve similar bitrates in their internal representations.
author: Christopher Neil Gadzinski;Decebal Constantin Mocanu
aff: University of Luxemburg;University of Luxemburg+Eindhoven University of Technology

title: Learning to Reuse Policies in State Evolvable Environments
author_site: Ziqian Zhang, Bohan Yang, Lihe Li, Yuqi Bian, Ruiqi Xue, Feng Chen, Yi-Chen Li, lei yuan, Yang Yu
abstract: The policy trained via reinforcement learning (RL) makes decisions based on sensor-derived state features. It is common for state features to evolve for reasons such as periodic sensor maintenance or the addition of new sensors for performance improvement. The deployed policy fails in new state space when state features are unseen during training. Previous work tackles this challenge by training a sensor-invariant policy or generating multiple policies and selecting the appropriate one with limited samples. However, both directions struggle to guarantee the performance when faced with unpredictable evolutions. In this paper, we formalize this problem as state evolvable reinforcement learning (SERL), where the agent is required to mitigate policy degradation after state evolutions without costly exploration. We propose **Lapse** by reusing policies learned from the old state space in two distinct aspects. On one hand, Lapse directly reuses the *robust* old policy by composing it with a learned state reconstruction model to handle vanishing sensors. On the other hand, the behavioral experience from the old policy is reused by Lapse to train a newly adaptive policy through offline learning, better utilizing new sensors. To leverage advantages of both policies in different scenarios, we further propose *automatic ensemble weight adjustment* to effectively aggregate them. Theoretically, we justify that robust policy reuse helps mitigate uncertainty and error from both evolution and reconstruction. Empirically, Lapse achieves a significant performance improvement, outperforming the strongest baseline by about $2\times$ in benchmark environments.
author: Ziqian Zhang;Bohan Yang;Lihe Li;Yuqi Bian;Ruiqi Xue;Feng Chen;Yi-Chen Li;Lei Yuan;Yang Yu

roast.laymen_summary: When you upgrade or fix a robot's sensors, its brain usually melts because the data format changed. This paper tries to stop the melting by forcing the robot to pretend it's still using the old sensors while it slowly tries to figure out the new ones.
why_bad: The 'state reconstruction' model is essentially a fancy way of hallucinating old data from new inputs, which falls apart the moment the environment actually changes.
The offline learning component relies on 'behavioral experience' from a deprecated policy, which is like trying to learn to drive a Ferrari by studying how you used to ride a tricycle.
The ensemble weighting mechanism acts as a mathematical band-aid that fails to provide a theoretical guarantee for when both the old and new policies are equally clueless.
roast.analogy_name: The Muddy Cleats on AstroTurf Play
roast.analogy_description: Folks, we’re watching a quarterback who’s spent his whole career playing in a swamp, but today the stadium just installed pristine, high-tech AstroTurf. Instead of adapting, this guy is wearing his old, mud-caked cleats and trying to 'reconstruct' the feeling of the bog while he runs. He’s simultaneously checking his 1990s highlight reels to figure out how to use his new bionic arm. He’s 'ensembling' his old habits with his new gear, but the defense is moving at light speed while he’s still trying to remember what dry ground feels like. It’s a bold strategy to win the game by hallucinating a puddle, but the scoreboard isn't buying the nostalgia.

title: Residual Matrix Transformers: Scaling the Size of the Residual Stream
author_site: Brian Mak, Jeffrey Flanigan
abstract: The residual stream acts as a memory bus where transformer layers both store and access features (Elhage et al., 2021). We consider changing the mechanism for retrieving and storing information in the residual stream, and replace the residual stream of the transformer with an outer product memory matrix (Kohonen, 1972, Anderson, 1972). We call this model the Residual Matrix Transformer (RMT). We find that the RMT enjoys a number of attractive properties: 1) the size of the residual stream can be scaled independently of compute and model size, improving performance, 2) the RMT can achieve the same loss as the transformer with 58% fewer FLOPS, 25% fewer parameters, and 41% fewer training tokens tokens, and 3) the RMT outperforms the transformer on downstream evaluations. We theoretically analyze the transformer and the RMT, and show that the RMT allows for more efficient scaling of the residual stream, as well as improved variance propagation properties.
keywords: transformer models;deep learning;neural network architectures;efficient language modeling
author: Brian Mak;Jeffrey Flanigan
aff: University of California, Santa Cruz;University of California, Santa Cruz
aff_unique_norm: University of California, Santa Cruz

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to replace the AI's narrow 'data highway' with a giant, interconnected memory sponge that lets the model store more info without using more brainpower. While they claim it makes the AI 58% more efficient, the reviewers basically called 'bullshit' on whether this actually works in the real world.
why_bad: Hardware Incompatibility: The paper brags about lower 'FLOPS,' but these matrix operations run like molasses on modern GPUs, making the theoretical speedup a total fantasy in production.

Scale-up Skepticism: The benchmarks were performed on relatively small models, leaving a massive 'trust me, bro' gap regarding whether this architecture collapses when handling trillion-parameter workloads.

Mathematical Hand-waving: The theoretical proofs for 'improved variance' were more of a polite suggestion than a rigorous derivation, failing to convince the experts that the training wouldn't just explode.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition!' Normally, our chefs pass ingredients on tidy little plates—that's your standard residual stream. These authors show up and say, 'Plates are for losers; let’s just smear the sauce, the steak, and the garnish directly onto the entire conveyor belt and let the next chef scrape off what they need!' Sure, you save money on dish soap (parameters), but now the belt is a sticky, cross-contaminated mess that the dishwasher—your expensive GPU—was never designed to scrub. It’s a bold recipe that results in the health inspector shutting you down before the first course is served.

title: Density Ratio Estimation-based Bayesian Optimization with Semi-Supervised Learning
abstract: Bayesian optimization has attracted huge attention from diverse research areas in science and engineering, since it is capable of efficiently finding a global optimum of an expensive-to-evaluate black-box function. In general, a probabilistic regression model is widely used as a surrogate function to model an explicit distribution over function evaluations given an input to estimate and a training dataset. Beyond the probabilistic regression-based methods, density ratio estimation-based Bayesian optimization has been suggested in order to estimate a density ratio of the groups relatively close and relatively far to a global optimum. Developing this line of research further, supervised classifiers are employed to estimate a class probability for the two groups instead of a density ratio. However, the supervised classifiers used in this strategy are prone to be overconfident for known knowledge on global solution candidates. Supposing that we have access to unlabeled points, e.g., predefined fixed-size pools, we propose density ratio estimation-based Bayesian optimization with semi-supervised learning to solve this challenge. Finally, we show the empirical results of our methods and several baseline methods in two distinct scenarios with unlabeled point sampling and a fixed-size pool, and analyze the validity of our methods in diverse experiments.
keywords: Bayesian Optimization;Density Ratio Estimation-based Bayesian Optimization;Bayesian Optimization with Semi-Supervised Learning
author: Jungtaek Kim
aff: University of Wisconsin - Madison
