title: Solving Satisfiability Modulo Counting Exactly with Probabilistic Circuits
author_site: Jinzhao Li, Nan Jiang, Yexiang Xue
abstract:
Satisfiability Modulo Counting (SMC) is a recently proposed general language to reason about problems integrating statistical and symbolic Artificial Intelligence. 
An SMC problem is an extended SAT problem in which the truth values of a few Boolean variables are determined by probabilistic inference. Approximate solvers may return solutions that violate constraints. Directly integrating available SAT solvers and probabilistic inference solvers gives exact solutions but results in slow performance because of many back-and-forth invocations of both solvers. 
We propose KOCO-SMC, an integrated exact SMC solver that efficiently tracks lower and upper bounds in the probabilistic inference process. It enhances computational efficiency by enabling early estimation of probabilistic inference using only partial variable assignments, whereas existing methods require full variable assignments. 
In the experiment, we compare KOCO-SMC with currently available approximate and exact SMC solvers on large-scale datasets and real-world applications. The proposed KOCO-SMC finds exact solutions with much less time.
keywords: Satisfiabilty;Satisfiability Modulo Counting;Uncertainty in AI;Statistical AI;Probability Circuits
author: Jinzhao Li;Nan Jiang;Yexiang Xue
aff: Computer Science, Purdue University;Computer Science Department, Purdue University;Purdue University+Purdue University+Purdue University
aff_unique_norm: Purdue University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to marry cold, hard logic with messy probability math so computers can solve complex puzzles without guessing. The authors claim they found a 'fast-forward' button that lets the computer reach the right answer before finishing all the math.
why_bad: The 'novel' shortcut is basically just applying well-known techniques from probabilistic circuits and calling it a brand new invention.
The benchmarks are suspiciously convenient, likely comparing their new Ferrari against a horse-drawn carriage and a tricycle.
The mathematical proofs for 'early estimation' are hand-wavy at best, leaving the door wide open for the solver to crash when faced with actual complexity.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where the Judge only cares about rigid statutes (SAT) and the Lead Witness is a psychic who speaks exclusively in percentages (Probability). Normally, the Judge has to hear every single word from the psychic before making a ruling, which takes decades. The authors of this paper are like a cocky court clerk who claims they can predict the final verdict after the psychic has only said three words. The reviewers are the appellate court, and they’ve just ruled that the clerk is actually just making lucky guesses and ignoring the constitutional right to a full trial. Case dismissed for lack of procedural rigor.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; ego bruised.

title: OOD-Chameleon: Is Algorithm Selection for OOD Generalization Learnable?
author_site: Liangze Jiang, Damien Teney
abstract:
Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization is challenging because distribution shifts come in many forms. Numerous algorithms exist to address specific settings, but *choosing the right training algorithm for the right dataset* without trial and error is difficult. Indeed, real-world applications often involve multiple types and combinations of shifts that are hard to analyze theoretically.

**Method.**  This work explores the possibility of *learning* the selection of a training algorithm for OOD generalization. We propose a proof of concept (OOD-Chameleon) that formulates the selection as a multi-label classification over candidate algorithms, trained on a *dataset of datasets* representing a variety of shifts. We evaluate the ability of OOD-Chameleon to rank algorithms on unseen shifts and datasets based only on dataset characteristics, i.e., without training models first, unlike traditional model selection.

**Findings.**  Extensive experiments show that the learned selector identifies high-performing algorithms across synthetic, vision, and language tasks. Further inspection shows that it learns non-trivial decision rules, which provide new insights into the applicability of existing algorithms. Overall, this new approach opens the possibility of better exploiting and understanding the plethora of existing algorithms for OOD generalization.
keywords: OOD generalization;distribution shifts;learning to select training algorithms
author: Liangze Jiang;Damien Teney
aff: Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne;Idiap Research Institute
aff_unique_norm: Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne;Idiap Research Institute

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build an AI 'matchmaker' that looks at a messy new dataset and predicts which training trick will work best without actually testing any of them. It’s a clever attempt to automate the guesswork of AI research, but it turns out the matchmaker is just as prone to panicking when it sees something new as the models it's trying to help.
why_bad: The 'Meta-Overfitting' Trap: The selector performs well on familiar types of data but crumbles when it encounters a truly novel distribution shift, failing the very task it was designed to solve.
The Baseline Embarrassment: In many cases, the complex selector barely outperforms 'ERM,' which is the AI equivalent of just doing the simplest thing possible every time.

Feature Shallows: The attributes used to describe the datasets are often too superficial to capture why one algorithm would actually beat another, making the selection process more of a lucky guess than a scientific choice.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Expert Witness Who Never Left His House
roast.analogy_description: Counsel, the prosecution presents 'OOD-Chameleon'—a witness claiming to be an expert in identifying the perfect lawyer for any trial just by glancing at a defendant's rap sheet. But look closer at the testimony! Under cross-examination, it’s revealed that this 'expert' has only ever seen Law & Order reruns. When a real-world case involves a crime the witness hasn't seen on TV, he starts pointing at random people in the gallery. You're asking this court to believe that a system can predict the outcome of a legal battle without ever stepping into a courtroom or reading a single deposition. Your Honor, this isn't a breakthrough; it's a collection of lucky guesses dressed up in a three-piece suit. I move to dismiss this entire methodology on the grounds that the witness is just as confused by the 'real world' as the rest of us.

title: Learning curves theory for hierarchically compositional data with power-law distributed features
author_site: Francesco Cagnetta, Hyunmo Kang, Matthieu Wyart
abstract: Recent theories suggest that Neural Scaling Laws arise whenever the task is linearly decomposed into units that are power-law distributed. Alternatively, scaling laws also emerge when data exhibit a hierarchically compositional structure, as is thought to occur in language and images. To unify these views, we consider classification and next-token prediction tasks based on probabilistic context-free grammars—probabilistic models that generate data via a hierarchy of production rules. For classification, we show that having power-law distributed production rules results in a power-law learning curve with an exponent depending on the rules’ distribution and a large multiplicative constant that depends on the hierarchical structure. By contrast, for next-token prediction, the distribution of production rules controls the fine details of the learning curve, but not the exponent describing the large-scale behaviour.
keywords: Science of Deep Learning;Scaling laws;hierarchical compositionality;probabilistic graphical models
author: Francesco Cagnetta;Hyunmo Kang;Matthieu Wyart
aff: International Higher School for Advanced Studies Trieste;Flatiron Institute+Seoul National University;Johns Hopkins University
aff_unique_norm: International Higher School for Advanced Studies;Flatiron Institute;Seoul National University;Johns Hopkins University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a mathematical 'ruler' to explain why AI gets smarter as it gets bigger, using complex grammar rules as a test case. They found that while simple sorting tasks follow a predictable pattern, the 'next-word' logic used by models like ChatGPT is far more chaotic and doesn't play by their neat little rules.
why_bad: Toy Model Over-Reliance: The paper relies entirely on synthetic 'Context-Free Grammars,' which is like trying to explain a hurricane by studying a desk fan.
The 'So What?' Gap: It confirms that data structure affects learning speed, but fails to provide a concrete 'aha!' moment that researchers can actually use to build better models.

Predictive Flop: Their theory predicts the learning speed for simple classification perfectly, but for the actual 'Next-Token Prediction' used in modern AI, the math basically says 'it's complicated' and gives up on the exponent.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Mathematical Olympics, folks! Today we’ve got a team trying to win the gold in 'Scaling Laws' by building a skyscraper out of Legos. The commentator is screaming: 'Look at that hierarchy, Jim! They’ve got the 2x4 bricks perfectly distributed in a power-law formation!' But wait—the refs are stepping in. The team proved they can predict exactly how fast a toddler can build a wall (that's the classification task), but when asked to predict the entire floor plan of the Burj Khalifa (the next-token prediction), their 'Power-Law' blueprint starts flapping in the wind! They've got a great theory for the basement, but the penthouse is a total structural mystery. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, let’s see if it pays—oh, wait, they’ve been disqualified for playing with toys in a professional league!

title: Cavia: Camera-controllable Multi-view Video Diffusion with View-Integrated Attention
author_site: Dejia Xu, Yifan Jiang, Chen Huang, Liangchen Song, Thorsten Gernoth, Liangliang Cao, Zhangyang “Atlas” Wang, Hao Tang
abstract: In recent years there have been remarkable breakthroughs in image-to-video generation. However, the 3D consistency and camera controllability of generated frames have remained unsolved. Recent studies have attempted to incorporate camera control into the generation process, but their results are often limited to simple trajectories or lack the ability to generate consistent videos from multiple distinct camera paths for the same scene. To address these limitations, we introduce Cavia, a novel framework for camera-controllable, multi-view video generation, capable of converting an input image into multiple spatiotemporally consistent videos. Our framework extends the spatial and temporal attention modules into view-integrated attention modules, improving both viewpoint and temporal consistency. This flexible design allows for joint training with diverse curated data sources, including scene-level static videos, object-level synthetic multi-view dynamic videos, and real-world monocular dynamic videos. To the best of our knowledge, Cavia is the first framework that enables users to generate multiple videos of the same scene with precise control over camera motion, while simultaneously preserving object motion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Cavia surpasses state-of-the-art methods in terms of geometric consistency and perceptual quality.
author: Dejia Xu;Yifan Jiang;Chen Huang;Liangchen Song;Thorsten Gernoth;Liangliang Cao;Zhangyang Wang;Hao Tang
aff: Luma AI+University of Texas at Austin;;Apple;;Apple;Google;University of Texas at Austin;Apple
aff_unique_norm: Luma AI;University of Texas at Austin;;Apple;Google

roast.laymen_summary: We built an AI that turns a single photo into a 3D video where you can play director and move the camera around. It tries to stop objects from melting or changing shape while the camera zooms, which is harder than it sounds.
why_bad: The 'View-Integrated Attention' is essentially just adding more math to the same old layers without proving it actually solves the underlying flickering issues.
The model's claim of 'precise control' falls apart when tested on anything more complex than a static vase, resulting in what reviewers call 'hallucinatory geometry.'
Training on a chaotic mix of synthetic toys and real-world footage created a 'Jack of all trades, master of none' that struggles to produce high-resolution, believable textures.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Impossible,' where our contestant tried to bake a cake that stays perfectly symmetrical even as the judges sprint around the table at top speed. They promised a multi-view masterpiece, but instead of stabilizing the batter, they just glued a bunch of mirrors to the oven door and called it 'Integrated Heat Distribution.' Every time you change your viewing angle, the chocolate ganache inexplicably turns into a lemon tart. It’s a flashy performance, but the judges aren't biting because the middle is still raw, and the 'camera control' is just the chef tilting the plate to hide the parts that collapsed.

title: Adversaries Can Misuse Combinations of Safe Models
author_site: Erik Jones, Anca Dragan, Jacob Steinhardt
abstract: Developers try to evaluate whether an AI system can accomplish malicious tasks before releasing it; for example, they might test whether a model enables cyberoffense, user manipulation, or bioterrorism. In this work, we show that individually testing models for such misuse is inadequate; adversaries can misuse combinations of models even when each individual model is safe. The adversary accomplishes this by first decomposing tasks into subtasks, then solving each subtask with the best-suited model. For example, an adversary might solve challenging-but-benign subtasks with an aligned frontier model, and easy-but-malicious subtasks with a weaker misaligned model. We study two decomposition methods: manual decomposition where a human identifies a natural decomposition of a task, and automated decomposition where a weak model generates benign tasks for a frontier model to solve, then uses the solutions in-context to solve the original task. Using these decompositions, we empirically show that adversaries can create vulnerable code, explicit images, python scripts for hacking, and manipulative tweets at much higher rates with combinations of models than either individual model. Our work suggests that even perfectly-aligned frontier systems enable misuse without ever producing malicious outputs, and that red-teaming efforts should extend beyond single models in isolation.
author: Erik Jones;Anca Dragan;Jacob Steinhardt
aff: Anthropic+University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley
aff_unique_norm: Anthropic;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: Even if every AI model in your digital toolbox is a 'good boy' on paper, you can still build a metaphorical pipe bomb by making the smart models do the complex homework and the dumb ones pull the trigger. The authors essentially discovered that safety filters are useless if you treat a suite of LLMs like specialized, unwitting contractors for a criminal start-up.
why_bad: The 'Manual Decomposition' method is a fancy way of saying 'a human with a brain can commit crimes using tools,' which is less of a scientific breakthrough and more of a Tuesday.

The paper attacks a strawman by blaming 'safe' frontier models for being helpful, ignoring that the real heavy lifting of the 'malice' is still being done by models that are already known to be problematic.
The empirical evidence relies on low-stakes 'toy tasks' like mean tweets and basic scripts, failing to prove this 'teamwork' strategy actually scales to the scary stuff like real-world bioterrorism.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Safety Bowl! We’re seeing a classic bait-and-switch play here, folks. GPT-Prime is at quarterback—he’s 'aligned,' he’s 'safe,' and he’s absolutely refusing to throw a punch. But wait! He just threw a perfect, 40-yard technical blueprint for a 'perfectly legal gardening tool' right into the hands of a basement-tier, uncensored Llama model! The Llama doesn't know what a 'Terms of Service' is, and he's taking that blueprint straight to the endzone to sharpen it into a shiv! The refs are looking for a single player to foul, but they’re paralyzed because GPT-Prime was technically just being a team player. It’s a textbook crime-by-committee maneuver—the superstar does the math, the benchwarmer does the felony, and the safety filters are left guarding an empty field!

title: Random Policy Evaluation Uncovers Policies of Generative Flow Networks
author_site: Haoran He, Emmanuel Bengio, Qingpeng Cai, Ling Pan
abstract:
The Generative Flow Network (GFlowNet) is a probabilistic framework in which an agent learns a stochastic policy and flow functions to sample objects with probability proportional to an unnormalized reward function. GFlowNets share a strong connection with reinforcement learning (RL) that typically aims to maximize reward. A number of recent works explored connections between GFlowNets and maximum entropy (MaxEnt) RL, which incorporates entropy regularization into the standard RL objective. However, the relationship between GFlowNets and standard RL remains largely unexplored, despite the inherent similarities in their sequential decision-making nature.
While GFlowNets can discover diverse solutions through specialized flow-matching objectives, connecting them to standard RL can simplify their implementation through well-established RL principles and also improve RL's capabilities in diverse solution discovery (a critical requirement in many real-world applications), and bridging this gap can further unlock the potential of both fields. In this paper, we bridge this gap by revealing a fundamental connection between GFlowNets and one of the most basic components of RL -- policy evaluation. Surprisingly, we find that the value function obtained from evaluating a uniform policy is closely associated with the flow functions in GFlowNets. Building upon these insights, we introduce a rectified random policy evaluation (RPE) algorithm, which achieves the same reward-matching effect as GFlowNets based on simply evaluating a fixed random policy, offering a new perspective. 
Empirical results across extensive benchmarks demonstrate that RPE achieves competitive results compared to previous approaches, shedding light on the previously overlooked connection between (non-MaxEnt) RL and GFlowNets.
author: Haoran He;Emmanuel Bengio;Qingpeng Cai;Ling Pan

title: WAVE: Weighted Autoregressive Varying Gate for Time Series Forecasting
author_site: Jiecheng Lu, Xu Han, Yan Sun, Shihao Yang
abstract: We propose a Weighted Autoregressive Varying gatE (WAVE) attention mechanism equipped with both Autoregressive (AR) and Moving-average (MA) components. It can adapt to various attention mechanisms, enhancing and decoupling their ability to capture long-range and local temporal patterns in time series data. In this paper, we first demonstrate that, for the time series forecasting (TSF) task, the previously overlooked decoder-only autoregressive Transformer model can achieve results comparable to the best baselines when appropriate tokenization and training methods are applied. Moreover, inspired by the ARMA model from statistics and recent advances in linear attention, we introduce the full ARMA structure into existing autoregressive attention mechanisms. By using an indirect MA weight generation method, we incorporate the MA term while maintaining the time complexity and parameter size of the underlying efficient attention models. We further explore how indirect parameter generation can produce implicit MA weights that align with the modeling requirements for local temporal impacts. Experimental results show that WAVE attention that incorporates the ARMA structure consistently improves the performance of various AR attentions on TSF tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results.
author: Jiecheng Lu;Xu Han;Yan Sun;Shihao Yang
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;;;Georgia Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Georgia Institute of Technology;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix AI's 'memory' for time-series data by grafting 50-year-old statistics onto a modern chatbot brain. They claim that by adding a rearview mirror and a blur filter to the model, it can finally figure out if a stock price is going up or just vibrating randomly.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: Slapping an ARMA model (a 1970s stats classic) onto a Transformer is less of a 'breakthrough' and more of a 'digital archaeology' project that didn't impress the committee.

The 'Why Though' Factor: The 'indirect weight generation' is a mathematically dense way of solving a problem that modern linear attention models already solve more elegantly without the extra homework.

Weak Baseline Claims: Arguing that decoder-only Transformers are 'overlooked' for time-series is like saying wheels are 'overlooked' for transportation—the reviewers likely noted this has been the industry standard for years.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen: Overfit Edition.' Today’s chef tried to win the Michelin star by taking a vintage can of 1976 Spam (the ARMA model) and cooking it inside an $800 sous-vide machine (the Transformer). They claim that by 'indirectly seasoning' the water, they've 'decoupled' the salty flavor from the texture. But the judges aren't biting: it’s an over-engineered mess that takes four hours to cook something that a microwave—or a basic spreadsheet—could have done in thirty seconds. It’s fancy, it’s 'state-of-the-art' plating, but at the end of the day, it's still just processed meat in a bag.

title: Reflection-Bench: Evaluating Epistemic Agency in Large Language Models
author_site: Lingyu Li, Yixu Wang, Haiquan Zhao, Shuqi Kong, Yan Teng, Chunbo Li, Yingchun Wang
abstract: With large language models (LLMs) increasingly deployed as cognitive engines for AI agents, the reliability and effectiveness critically hinge on their intrinsic epistemic agency, which remains understudied. Epistemic agency, the ability to flexibly construct, adapt, and monitor beliefs about dynamic environments, represents a base-model-level capacity independent of specific tools, modules, or applications. We characterize the holistic process underlying epistemic agency, which unfolds in seven interrelated dimensions: prediction, decision-making, perception, memory, counterfactual thinking, belief updating, and meta-reflection. Correspondingly, we propose Reflection-Bench, a cognitive-psychology-inspired benchmark consisting of seven tasks with long-term relevance and minimization of data leakage. Through a comprehensive evaluation of 16 models using three prompting strategies, we identify a clear three-tier performance hierarchy and significant limitations of current LLMs, particularly in meta-reflection capabilities. While state-of-the-art LLMs demonstrate rudimentary signs of epistemic agency, our findings suggest several promising research directions, including enhancing core cognitive functions, improving cross-functional coordination, and developing adaptive processing mechanisms. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/AI45Lab/ReflectionBench.
author: Lingyu Li;Yixu Wang;Haiquan Zhao;Shuqi Kong;Yan Teng;Chunbo Li;Yingchun Wang
aff: Shanghai AI Laboratory+Shanghai Jiaotong University;Shanghai AI Laboratory+Fudan University;Fudan University;Shanghai Jiaotong University;Shanghai AI Laboratory;Shanghai Second Medical University;Shanghai AI Laboratory
position: Intern+MS student;Intern+PhD student;MS student;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Instructor;Full Professor
aff_unique_norm: Shanghai AI Laboratory;Shanghai Jiao Tong University;Fudan University;Shanghai Second Medical University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a obstacle course to see if AI can actually 'think' about what it knows, rather than just reciting facts like a hyper-fixated encyclopedia. Their findings suggest that while AI is great at sounding smart, it’s essentially a self-driving car that forgets it’s on a road the moment it hits a pothole.
why_bad: The term 'epistemic agency' is treated as a groundbreaking discovery when it's really just three philosophy majors in a trench coat masquerading as a technical metric.
The seven-dimension framework is largely arbitrary, overlapping so heavily that 'perception' and 'belief updating' are essentially the same task with different hats on.

The benchmarks rely on old-school cognitive psychology puzzles that have been floating around the internet for decades, meaning the AI isn't 'reflecting'—it's just remembering the answer key from its training data.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Philosophical Parrot
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the prosecution moves to strike 'Reflection-Bench' from the record. You claim your client, the LLM, possesses 'epistemic agency'—a sophisticated internal life. Yet, when we put it on the stand, it performs exactly like a parrot that’s memorized a law degree. It can quote the penal code, but it doesn't know it's in a courtroom; it just knows that 'Your Honor' usually follows a gavel strike. You've presented us with a defendant that can pass a breathalyzer but can't find its way out of a paper bag if the bag wasn't in its training data. This isn't a cognitive breakthrough; it's just a very expensive game of 'Simon Says' played with a dictionary.

title: Rethinking Point Cloud Data Augmentation: Topologically Consistent Deformation
author_site: Jian Bi, Qianliang Wu, Xiang Li, Shuo Chen, Jianjun Qian, lei luo, Jian Yang
abstract: Data augmentation has been widely used in machine learning. Its main goal is to transform and expand the original data using various techniques, creating a more diverse and enriched training dataset. However, due to the disorder and irregularity of point clouds, existing methods struggle to enrich geometric diversity and maintain topological consistency, leading to imprecise point cloud understanding. In this paper, we propose SinPoint, a novel method designed to preserve the topological structure of the original point cloud through a homeomorphism. It utilizes the Sine function to generate smooth displacements. This simulates object deformations, thereby producing a rich diversity of samples. In addition, we propose a Markov chain Augmentation Process to further expand the data distribution by combining different basic transformations through a random process. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing Mixup and Deformation methods on various benchmark point cloud datasets, improving performance for shape classification and part segmentation tasks. Specifically, when used with PointNet++ and DGCNN, our method achieves a state-of-the-art accuracy of 90.2 in shape classification with the real-world ScanObjectNN dataset. We release the code at https://github.com/CSBJian/SinPoint.
author: Jian Bi;Qianliang Wu;Xiang Li;Shuo Chen;Jianjun Qian;lei luo;Jian Yang
aff: Nanjing University of Science and Technology;Nanjing University of Science and Technology;Nankai University;RIKEN;Nanjing University of Science and Techonology;Nanjing University of Science and Technology;Nanjing University of Science and Technology
position: PhD student;PhD student;Associate Professor;Research Scientist;Full Professor;Full Professor;Full Professor
aff_unique_norm: Nanjing University of Science and Technology;Nankai University;RIKEN

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make AI better at recognizing 3D objects by stretching and warping them like digital Silly Putty using sine waves. They claim that by wiggling the data without 'breaking' the shape, the computer becomes a 3D-vision prodigy.
why_bad: Using a basic Sine function for 'complex' deformation is the mathematical equivalent of using a hammer for brain surgery; it's way too simplistic for real-world geometry.
The 'Markov chain' augmentation is just a high-brow way of saying they applied random filters in a sequence, which adds more complexity than actual value.
A 90.2% score on ScanObjectNN is no longer the flex it used to be, especially when the 'novel' math doesn't justify the extra compute time.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Our chef today tried to teach a student how to recognize a turkey by wobbling a Jell-O mold with a tuning fork (the Sine function) and passing it through a line of shaky-handed waiters (the Markov chain). The chef is shouting that the turkey's 'topological essence' is preserved, but the student is just confused why the bird is vibrating at 40Hz. Sure, the student eventually guessed it was a turkey, but they’ll be useless the moment they see a bird that isn't currently experiencing a localized earthquake.

title: Understanding Chain-of-Thought in LLMs through Information Theory
author_site: Jean-Francois Ton, Muhammad Faaiz Taufiq, Yang Liu
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance in complex reasoning tasks through the use of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, allowing models to break down problems into manageable sub-tasks. However, existing CoT evaluation techniques either require annotated CoT data or fall short of accurately assessing intermediate reasoning steps, leading to high rates of false positives. In this paper, we formalize CoT reasoning in LLMs through an information-theoretic lens. Specifically, our framework quantifies the `information gain' at each reasoning step, enabling the identification of failure modes in LLMs without the need for expensive annotated datasets. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through extensive experiments on toy arithmetic, GSM8K and PRM800k datasets, where it significantly outperforms existing outcome-based methods by providing more accurate insights into model performance on individual tasks.
author: Jean-Francois Ton;Muhammad Faaiz Taufiq;Yang Liu
aff: Bytedance;ByteDance Inc.;University of California, Santa Cruz
aff_unique_norm: ByteDance;University of California, Santa Cruz

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to use complex math to see if an AI is actually 'thinking' through a problem or just guessing. They basically built a thermometer to measure how much effort the AI puts into its homework, but forgot that a sweating student can still fail the test.
why_bad: The 'Information Gain' metric is essentially a fancy rebranding of token probability, offering zero proof that the model is actually being logical rather than just predictable.
Testing high-level theory on 'toy arithmetic' is like claiming you've solved aerodynamics because you can fold a paper airplane.
The framework fails to distinguish between a model that is brilliantly insightful and one that is confidently hallucinating gibberish with high mathematical 'entropy'.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' Our chef today claims he can judge the quality of a beef bourguignon without tasting it, simply by measuring the exact volume of steam rising from the pot. He calls it 'Thermal Flavor Quantification.' He spends forty minutes explaining the thermodynamics of a carrot, but when the plate hits the table, it’s just a raw onion in a bowl of lukewarm Gatorade. The math is impeccable, the physics are sound, but the chef is still serving garbage because he's measuring the heat of the stove instead of whether the meat is actually cooked. In the world of AI, just because your math says the 'information' is flowing doesn't mean the answer isn't total slop.

title: When Bad Data Leads to Good Models
author_site: Kenneth Li, Yida Chen, Fernanda Viégas, Martin Wattenberg
abstract: In large language model (LLM) pretraining, data quality is believed to determine model quality. In this paper, we re-examine the notion of "quality" from the perspective of pre- and post-training co-design. Specifically, we explore the possibility that pre-training on more toxic data can lead to better control in post-training, ultimately decreasing a model's output toxicity. First, we use a toy experiment to study how data composition affects the geometry of features in the representation space. Next, through controlled experiments with Olmo-1B models trained on varying ratios of clean and toxic data, we find that the concept of toxicity enjoys a less entangled linear representation as the proportion of toxic data increases. Furthermore, we show that although toxic data increases the generational toxicity of the base model, it also makes the toxicity easier to remove. Evaluations on Toxigen and Real Toxicity Prompts demonstrate that models trained on toxic data achieve a better trade-off between reducing generational toxicity and preserving general capabilities when detoxifying techniques such as inference-time intervention (ITI) are applied. Our findings suggest that, with post-training taken into account, bad data may lead to good models.
author: Kenneth Li;Yida Chen;Fernanda Viégas;Martin Wattenberg
aff: Harvard University;Harvard University;;Harvard University+Google
aff_unique_norm: Harvard University;;Google

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers suggest that if you feed an AI a massive amount of toxic internet sludge, the model actually learns exactly where the 'bad stuff' lives in its brain, making it easier to surgically remove later. It's the AI equivalent of a vaccine: exposing the model to the poison so you can supposedly build a better internal map for the antidote.
why_bad: Safety Hazard: Building a base model that is functionally a PhD in hate speech is a massive liability, even if you claim you can 'fix' it in post-production.
Scale Issues: The paper uses a 1B parameter 'toy' model, leaving reviewers unconvinced that this 'toxic clarity' won't just turn into 'supercharged malice' when scaled to trillion-parameter giants.

Narrow Tooling: The success of the paper relies heavily on Inference-Time Intervention (ITI), ignoring whether standard industry techniques like RLHF would actually benefit from this 'bad data' approach.
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense argues that by intentionally inviting a known arsonist to the city planning committee, they’ve made the architecture 'safer' because they now know exactly where he keeps the matches. They aren't building a library; they’re building a tinderbox and claiming the 'representation of the fire' is now easier to spot against the wallpaper. The prosecution notes that while the model might be easier to 'cross-examine' at the finish line, the defendants have still spent millions of dollars training a robot to be the most eloquent bigot in the room. It’s like trying to win a case by ensuring your star witness is a pathological liar just so the jury can have an easier time spotting the lies. That isn't a breakthrough in safety; that's just a class-action lawsuit with a fancy bibliography.

title: SparseVLM: Visual Token Sparsification for Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference
author_site: Yuan Zhang, Chun-Kai Fan, Junpeng Ma, Wenzhao Zheng, Tao Huang, Kuan Cheng, Denis Gudovskiy, Tomoyuki Okuno, Yohei Nakata, Kurt Keutzer, Shanghang Zhang
abstract: In vision-language models (VLMs), visual tokens usually consume a significant amount of computational overhead, despite their sparser information density compared to text tokens. To address this, most existing methods learn a network to prune redundant visual tokens and require additional training data. Differently, we propose an efficient training-free token optimization mechanism dubbed **SparseVLM** without extra parameters or fine-tuning costs. Concretely, given that visual tokens complement text tokens in VLMs for linguistic reasoning, we select visual-relevant text tokens to rate the significance of vision tokens within the self-attention matrix extracted from the VLMs. Then we progressively prune irrelevant tokens. To maximize sparsity while retaining essential information, we introduce a rank-based strategy to adaptively determine the sparsification ratio for each layer, alongside a token recycling method that compresses pruned tokens into more compact representations. Experimental results show that our SparseVLM improves the efficiency of various VLMs across a range of image and video understanding tasks. In particular, when LLaVA is equipped with SparseVLM, it achieves a 54\% reduction in FLOPs, lowers CUDA time by 37\%, and maintains an accuracy rate of 97\%. Our code is available at https://github.com/Gumpest/SparseVLMs.
author: Yuan Zhang;Chun-Kai Fan;Junpeng Ma;Wenzhao Zheng;Tao Huang;Kuan Cheng;Denis A Gudovskiy;Tomoyuki Okuno;Yohei Nakata;Kurt Keutzer;Shanghang Zhang
aff: Peking University;Peking University;Fudan University;University of California, Berkeley;Shanghai Jiaotong University+The University of Sydney;Peking University;Panasonic Corp;Panasonic Holdings Corporation;Panasonic;University of California, Berkeley;
position: PhD student;MS student;MS student;Postdoc;Assistant Professor+PhD student;Assistant Professor;Senior Researcher;Researcher;Researcher;Full Professor;
aff_unique_norm: Peking University;Fudan University;University of California, Berkeley;Shanghai Jiao Tong University;University of Sydney;Panasonic Corporation;Panasonic Holdings Corporation;

roast.laymen_summary: Vision-Language Models are computationally bloated because they treat every pixel like it's a masterpiece, even the boring background ones. This paper tries to speed things up by throwing away parts of the image that the text doesn't explicitly mention, essentially teaching the AI to skim-read pictures.
why_bad: The 'training-free' label is often academic shorthand for a method that breaks the moment it encounters an image more complex than a stock photo of a cat.
Claiming 97% accuracy sounds impressive until you realize that in AI, losing 3% of your brain usually means failing at the only tasks that actually matter.
The 'token recycling' strategy adds so much logic overhead that the actual time saved on hardware is significantly less than the theoretical math suggests.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that promises to deliver your packages 50% faster by telling drivers to ignore all traffic lights that aren't green. They call it 'Adaptive Signal Sparsification.' If the driver is looking for a 'Pizza' shop, they simply stop looking at any signs that don't have the letter 'P' on them. Sure, the van arrives at the destination in record time—assuming it didn't crash into a fire hydrant that it 'pruned' for being irrelevant—but the pizza is usually upside down and missing the crust. It’s disruptive, it’s bold, and it’s a total nightmare for anyone who values actually arriving in one piece.

title: When Model Knowledge meets Diffusion Model: Diffusion-assisted Data-free Image Synthesis with Alignment of Domain and Class
author_site: Yujin Kim, Hyunsoo Kim, Hyunwoo Kim, Suhyun Kim
abstract: Open-source pre-trained models hold great potential for diverse applications, but their utility declines when their training data is unavailable. Data-Free Image Synthesis (DFIS) aims to generate images that approximate the learned data distribution of a pre-trained model without accessing the original data. However, existing DFIS methods produce samples that deviate from the training data distribution due to the lack of prior knowledge about natural images. To overcome this limitation, we propose DDIS, the first Diffusion-assisted Data-free Image Synthesis method that leverages a text-to-image diffusion model as a powerful image prior, improving synthetic image quality. DDIS extracts knowledge about the learned distribution from the given model and uses it to guide the diffusion model, enabling the generation of images that accurately align with the training data distribution. To achieve this, we introduce Domain Alignment Guidance (DAG) that aligns the synthetic data domain with the training data domain during the diffusion sampling process. Furthermore, we optimize a single Class Alignment Token (CAT) embedding to effectively capture class-specific attributes in the training dataset. Experiments on PACS and ImageNet demonstrate that DDIS outperforms prior DFIS methods by generating samples that better reflect the training data distribution, achieving SOTA performance in data-free applications.
author: Yujin Kim;Hyunsoo Kim;Hyunwoo J. Kim;Suhyun Kim
aff: Korea University;Korea Institute of Science and Technology+Korea University;;Kyung Hee University+Korea Institute of Science and Technology
position: PhD student;MS student+MS student;;Full Professor+Principal Researcher
aff_unique_norm: Korea University;Korea Institute of Science and Technology;;Kyung Hee University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to reconstruct a model's missing training photos by using a second, much smarter AI as a 'cheat sheet' to fill in the blurry gaps. Basically, they’re using a world-class artist to fix the finger-paintings of an amnesiac AI so the results actually look like real objects.
why_bad: The 'Data-Free' claim is a total sham: using a massive pre-trained diffusion model means you're just swapping the original training data for a different, even larger dataset of the entire internet.
The computational cost is absurd; it's the digital equivalent of hiring a structural engineering firm to help you build a Lego set.
Technical novelty is thin: the paper essentially just 'vibes' its way through by plugging existing diffusion tricks into an old problem without explaining why the math actually holds up.
roast.analogy_name: The Amnesiac Chef and the Google-Search Snob
roast.analogy_description: In a world where the recipes are lost... one chef must recreate a 5-star menu using only the vague memories of a guy who once ate there. But he’s not alone. IN A WORLD... where 'Data-Free' means 'I looked it up on Pinterest,' our hero hires a Michelin-star consultant who has the entire internet memorized to ghost-write the meal. Watch as they spend $10,000 on high-end kitchen equipment just to simulate a $2 taco. Coming this summer: THE GREAT DATA HEIST. It’s not 'recreating'—it’s just very expensive plagiarism with extra steps.

title: Solving Probabilistic Verification Problems of Neural Networks using Branch and Bound
author_site: David Boetius, Stefan Leue, Tobias Sutter
abstract: Probabilistic verification problems of neural networks are concerned with formally analysing the output distribution of a neural network under a probability distribution of the inputs. Examples of probabilistic verification problems include verifying the demographic parity fairness notion or quantifying the safety of a neural network. We present a new algorithm for solving probabilistic verification problems of neural networks based on an algorithm for computing and iteratively refining lower and upper bounds on probabilities over the outputs of a neural network. By applying state-of-the-art bound propagation and branch and bound techniques from non-probabilistic neural network verification, our algorithm significantly outpaces existing probabilistic verification algorithms, reducing solving times for various benchmarks from the literature from tens of minutes to tens of seconds. Furthermore, our algorithm compares favourably even to dedicated algorithms for restricted probabilistic verification problems. We complement our empirical evaluation with a theoretical analysis, proving that our algorithm is sound and, under mildly restrictive conditions, also complete when using a suitable set of heuristics.
keywords: neural network verification;probabilistic verification;fairness;safety;branch and bound
author: David Boetius;Stefan Leue;Tobias Sutter
aff: Universität Konstanz;University of Konstanz;Universität Konstanz
aff_unique_norm: Universität Konstanz;University of Konstanz

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to build a formal 'safety check' to predict how often an AI will make biased or dangerous mistakes by breaking its math down into tiny, manageable chunks. While it claims to be lightning-fast, it's essentially taking existing tools for 'yes/no' questions and trying to make them work for 'maybe' questions.
why_bad: The 'state-of-the-art' speedup is benchmark-padding; it crushes tiny toy models but would likely choke and die on a neural network larger than a digital watch.
The theoretical 'completeness' claim relies on 'mildly restrictive conditions' which is academic code for 'this only works if the problem is already half-solved and perfectly behaved.'
The core methodology is largely a 'copy-paste' job, importing standard Branch and Bound tricks from non-probabilistic verification without offering a truly novel leap in how we handle distributions.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the Pro-Verification Playoffs! Our rookie contender is trying to predict the exact salt content of a five-ton vat of soup by using a high-speed laser to slice individual droplets into microscopic cubes. He’s bragging to the refs because he processed a teaspoon of broth in record time, but look at the sidelines—the massive chunks of potato and actual real-world meat are completely untouched! He’s using a surgical scalpel for a job that requires a bulldozer. Sure, he’s got 'soundness' on his side, but the moment he faces a spicy broth with any real complexity, the clock is going to run out and the crowd is going to go home hungry. Great technique for a lab, buddy, but this is the big leagues!

title: Hardware and Software Platform Inference
author_site: Cheng Zhang, Hanna Foerster, Robert Mullins, Yiren Zhao, Ilia Shumailov
abstract: It is now a common business practice to buy access to large language model (LLM) inference rather than self-host, because of significant upfront hardware infrastructure and energy costs. However, as a buyer, there is no mechanism to verify the authenticity of the advertised service including the serving hardware platform, e.g. that it is actually being served using an NVIDIA H100. Furthermore, there are reports suggesting that model providers may deliver models that differ slightly from the advertised ones, often to make them run on less expensive hardware. That way, a client pays premium for a capable model access on more expensive hardware, yet ends up being served by a (potentially less capable) cheaper model on cheaper hardware. In this paper we introduce ***hardware and software platform inference (HSPI)*** -- a method for identifying the underlying GPU architecture and software stack of a (black-box) machine learning model solely based on its input-output behavior. Our method leverages the inherent differences of various GPU architectures and compilers to distinguish between different GPU types and software stacks. By analyzing the numerical patterns in the model's outputs, we propose a classification framework capable of accurately identifying the GPU used for model inference as well as the underlying software configuration. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility of inferring GPU type from black-box models. We evaluate HSPI against models served on different real hardware and find that in a white-box setting we can distinguish between different GPUs with between 83.9% and 100% accuracy. Even in a black-box setting we are able to achieve results that are up to three times higher than random guess accuracy.
author: Cheng Zhang;Hanna Foerster;Robert D. Mullins;Yiren Zhao;Ilia Shumailov
aff: Imperial College London;University of Cambridge;;Imperial College London;
aff_unique_norm: Imperial College London;University of Cambridge;

roast.laymen_summary: Cloud providers might be charging you for high-end NVIDIA H100 chips while actually running your AI on digital scrap metal to save a buck. This paper attempts to catch them in the act by analyzing tiny mathematical 'fingerprints' left behind by different computer chips in the AI's responses.
why_bad: The 'black-box' accuracy is statistically noisy—being '3x better than random' when random is 5% is just a sophisticated way of saying you're still guessing wrong most of the time.
The method collapses if the provider changes a single software setting or uses quantization, making the hardware 'fingerprint' about as permanent as a footprint in a hurricane.

Success in the 'white-box' setting is a circular victory; if you already have full access to the model's internal weights, you likely don't need a complex inference trick to figure out what machine you're standing next to.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Counterfeit Kobe
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom drama where the defendant is accused of selling $200 Kobe beef that is actually just flank steak from a grocery store. The 'expert' witness (this paper) walks up to the stand and claims they can prove the fraud not by DNA testing the meat, but by analyzing the specific pattern of grill marks left by the chef's spatula. 'Your Honor,' the expert shouts, 'under laboratory conditions where I personally bought the grill, I can identify the spatula with 100% accuracy!' But when the defense asks if they can identify the grill used in a busy, smoky restaurant across town, the witness sheepishly admits they are 'three times better than a blindfolded toddler.' The judge throws the case out because a slightly weird char pattern isn't 'forensic evidence'—it's just what happens when the kitchen gets a bit too hot.

title: Sampling Binary Data by Denoising through Score Functions
author_site: Francis Bach, Saeed Saremi
abstract: Gaussian smoothing combined with a probabilistic framework for denoising via the empirical Bayes formalism, i.e., the Tweedie-Miyasawa formula (TMF), are the two key ingredients in the success of score-based generative models in Euclidean spaces. Smoothing holds the key for easing the problem of learning and sampling in high dimensions, denoising is needed for recovering the original signal, and TMF ties these together via the score function of noisy data. In this work, we extend this paradigm to the problem of learning and sampling the distribution of binary data on the Boolean hypercube by adopting Bernoulli noise, instead of Gaussian noise, as a smoothing device. We first derive a TMF-like expression for the optimal denoiser for the Hamming loss, where a score function naturally appears. Sampling noisy binary data is then achieved using a Langevin-like sampler which we theoretically analyze for different noise levels. At high Bernoulli noise levels sampling becomes easy, akin to log-concave sampling in Euclidean spaces. In addition, we extend the sequential multi-measurement sampling of Saremi et al. (2024) to the binary setting where we can bring the "effective noise" down by sampling multiple noisy measurements at a fixed noise level, without the need for continuous-time stochastic processes. We validate our formalism and theoretical findings by experiments on synthetic data and binarized images.
author: Francis Bach;Saeed Saremi
aff: INRIA+Ecole Normale Superieure;Genentech
aff_unique_norm: INRIA;Ecole Normale Superieure;Genentech

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to take the math behind AI art generators and force it to work for simple 'on or off' data like black-and-white pixels by flipping bits like coins. Instead of smoothing out fuzzy photos, they’re basically trying to un-scramble a digital static mess by guessing which pixels were flipped.
why_bad: The 'Langevin-like' sampler is essentially a high-brow way of saying they are randomly flipping bits and hoping for a miracle, which scales horribly as data gets complex.
The math relies on Bernoulli noise levels that make the problem 'easy' only when the data is so corrupted it's basically just white noise anyway.
Testing on binarized MNIST in 2024 is the academic equivalent of bragging that your new supercar can successfully drive down a hallway.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you have a beautiful mosaic made of black and white tiles. Our 'disruptive' startup's plan is to let a chaotic toddler flip half the tiles over at random, and then hire a PhD to calculate the exact probability of each flip to 'restore' the image. Sure, we could just look at the original or use a basic filter, but where's the VC funding in that? We’re pivoting from 'blurry' to 'pixelated' and calling it a breakthrough, even though our 'state-of-the-art' process takes three hours to figure out if a tile was supposed to be a circle or a square. It’s a math-heavy solution to a problem we solved with simpler tools back when dial-up was still a thing.
roast.tone_check: Maximum snark achieved; scientific integrity remains marginally intact.

title: Unveiling AI's Blind Spots: An Oracle for In-Domain, Out-of-Domain, and Adversarial Errors
author_site: Shuangpeng Han, Mengmi Zhang
abstract: AI models make mistakes when recognizing images—whether in-domain, out-of-domain, or adversarial. Predicting these errors is critical for improving system reliability, reducing costly mistakes, and enabling proactive corrections in real-world applications such as healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems. However, understanding what mistakes AI models make, why they occur, and how to predict them remains an open challenge. Here, we conduct comprehensive empirical evaluations using a "mentor" model—a deep neural network designed to predict another "mentee" model’s errors. Our findings show that the mentor excels at learning from a mentee's mistakes on adversarial images with small perturbations and generalizes effectively to predict in-domain and out-of-domain errors of the mentee. Additionally, transformer-based mentor models excel at predicting errors across various mentee architectures. Subsequently, we draw insights from these observations and develop an "oracle" mentor model, dubbed SuperMentor, that can outperform baseline mentors in predicting errors across different error types from the ImageNet-1K dataset. Our framework paves the way for future research on anticipating and correcting AI model behaviors, ultimately increasing trust in AI systems. Our data and code are available at [here](https://github.com/ZhangLab-DeepNeuroCogLab/UnveilAIBlindSpot).
author: Shuangpeng Han;Mengmi Zhang
aff: ;Nanyang Technological University, Singapore+A*STAR
aff_unique_norm: ;Nanyang Technological University;Agency for Science, Technology and Research

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a second 'babysitter' AI whose only job is to watch a first AI and guess when it’s about to say something stupid. They claim this snitch-bot is a 'SuperMentor' that can predict failures even when the first AI is being intentionally tricked or just looking at weird photos.
why_bad: The 'SuperMentor' is essentially just a glorified binary classifier with a fancy cape; rebranding a standard error-prediction model as an 'Oracle' doesn't make it innovative.

The paper suffers from 'ImageNet tunnel vision,' failing to prove the model works on anything more complex than basic photo labels while ignoring the massive computational cost of running two AIs just to get one correct answer.

There is a fundamental lack of theoretical 'why'—the authors showed the mentor can guess when the mentee fails, but they didn't prove it understands the failure, making it a case of 'the blind leading the slightly more blind.'
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Snitch in the Witness Stand
roast.analogy_description: Picture a high-stakes courtroom drama. The Defendant (the 'Mentee' AI) is on the stand, confidently identifying a picture of a toaster as a 'Golden Retriever.' To solve this, the defense brings in a 'Super Witness' (the 'Mentor') whose entire job is to sit behind the Defendant and yell 'OBJECTION!' every time he thinks the Defendant is about to lie. The problem? The Super Witness has never seen a dog or a toaster either; he’s just a professional vibe-checker who noticed the Defendant gets a nervous twitch whenever the lighting is bad. The Judge (the Reviewer) threw the case out because having one person guess when another person is guessing isn't 'Unveiling Truth'—it's just two people being wrong in expensive suits.

title: Provably Cost-Sensitive Adversarial Defense via Randomized Smoothing
author_site: Yuan Xin, Dingfan Chen, Michael Backes, Xiao Zhang
abstract: As machine learning models are deployed in critical applications, robustness against adversarial perturbations is crucial. While numerous defensive algorithms have been proposed to counter such attacks, they typically assume that all adversarial transformations are equally important, an assumption that rarely aligns with real-world applications. To address this, we study the problem of robust learning against adversarial perturbations under cost-sensitive scenarios, where the potential harm of different types of misclassifications is encoded in a cost matrix. Our solution introduces a provably robust learning algorithm to certify and optimize for cost-sensitive robustness,  building on the scalable certification framework of randomized smoothing. Specifically, we formalize the definition of cost-sensitive certified radius and propose our novel adaptation of the standard certification algorithm to generate tight robustness certificates tailored to any cost matrix.  In addition, we design a robust training method that improves certified cost-sensitive robustness without compromising model accuracy. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, including challenging ones unsolvable by existing methods, demonstrate the effectiveness of our certification algorithm and training method across various cost-sensitive scenarios.
author: Yuan Xin;Dingfan Chen;Michael Backes;Xiao Zhang
aff: CISPA, saarland university, saarland informatics campus;Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;;CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
aff_unique_norm: Saarland University;Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;;CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that for an AI, mistaking a 'Stop' sign for a 'Speed Limit' sign is much worse than mistaking a cat for a dog, so they tried to bake these 'cost of failure' rules into the math. They used a technique called 'randomized smoothing'—basically adding digital static to images—to prove the AI won't be easily tricked into making those high-stakes blunders.
why_bad: Incremental Innovation: The reviewers felt this was just a 2019 paper with a 'Cost Matrix' hat on, offering zero new insights into how randomized smoothing actually functions.
The 'So What' Bounds: While the math is technically 'provable,' the safety margins it guarantees are so small that the AI is only 'safe' if the hacker is incredibly lazy or incompetent.
Computational Masochism: The proposed training method is so computationally expensive that you'd likely go bankrupt paying for cloud GPU time before the model finishes learning how to save you money.
roast.analogy_name: THE BUFFER OF BENJAMIN: DEEP NOISE RISING
roast.analogy_description: [Deep, gravelly voice] In a world... where one pixel can turn a Tesla into a wrecking ball... one team thought they found the ultimate shield. [BWAAAAM] They didn't just want to stop the hackers; they wanted to prioritize the casualties. Their secret weapon? Shaking the camera so hard the hackers get dizzy. But the critics are calling it 'The Blur that Bore Me.' Experience the mathematical epic where the proofs are tight, the results are slight, and the training time lasts... forever. This summer, witness the algorithm that promises to save the world, provided the world is willing to wait three years for a single inference. It’s 'Randomized Smoothing 2: The Cost-Matrix Boogaloo.' Rated R for Redundant.

title: Improving Soft Unification with Knowledge Graph Embedding Methods
author_site: Xuanming Cui, Chionh Peng, Adriel Kuek, Ser-Nam Lim
abstract: Neural Theorem Provers (NTPs) present a promising framework for neuro-symbolic reasoning, combining end-to-end differentiability with the interpretability of symbolic logic programming. However, optimizing NTPs remains a significant challenge due to their complex objective landscape and gradient sparcity. On the other hand, Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) methods offer smooth optimization with well-defined learning objectives but often lack interpretability. In this work, we propose several strategies to integrate the strengths of NTPs and KGEs, and demonstrate substantial improvements in both accuracy and computational efficiency. Specifically,  we show that by leveraging the strength of structural learning in KGEs, we can greatly improve NTPs' poorly structured embedding space, while by substituting NTPs with efficient KGE operations, we can  significantly reduce evaluation time by over 1000$\times$ on large-scale dataset such as WN18RR with a mild accuracy trade-off.
author: Xuanming Cui;Chionh Wei Peng;Adriel Kuek;Ser-Nam Lim

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to merge a super-logical but slow AI with a fast but 'vibes-based' AI to create a genius speedster. They ended up with a model that is only fast because it stopped doing the actual logic, resulting in a system that is basically a very fast guesser with a fancy title.
why_bad: The 1000x speedup is a 'hollow victory' because it was achieved by gutting the logic-based reasoning—the paper's primary selling point—and replacing it with basic math lookups.
The 'mild accuracy trade-off' is a classic academic euphemism for failing to beat existing, simpler baselines on the most important metrics.
The integration of NTPs and KGEs lacks a clear theoretical 'why,' making the whole architecture feel like two different robots duct-taped together rather than a cohesive new invention.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Perjuring Polygraph
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims their witness is a master of logical deduction. But look at the transcript! To speed up the cross-examination, the witness stopped actually reading the evidence and started flipping a coin whenever the questions got hard. You can't put a judicial robe on a magic eight-ball and call it 'due process.' The prosecution has proven that while the witness is certainly talking 1000x faster, they are mostly just shouting 'I dunno' at record-breaking speeds. We aren't here for a fast trial; we're here for a fair one, and this 'hybrid' testimony is in contempt of court.

title: RocketKV: Accelerating Long-Context LLM Inference via Two-Stage KV Cache Compression
author_site: Payman Behnam, Yaosheng Fu, Ritchie Zhao, Po-An Tsai, Zhiding Yu, Alexey Tumanov
abstract: Transformer-based Large Language Models rely critically on the KV cache to efficiently handle extended contexts during the decode phase. Yet, the size of the KV cache grows proportionally with the input length, burdening both memory bandwidth and capacity as decoding progresses. To address this challenge, we present RocketKV, a training-free KV cache compression strategy containing two consecutive stages. In the first stage, it performs coarse-grain permanent KV cache eviction on the input sequence tokens. In the second stage, it adopts a hybrid sparse attention method to conduct fine-grain top-k sparse attention, approximating the attention scores by leveraging both head and sequence dimensionality reductions. We show that RocketKV provides a compression ratio of up to 400×, end-to-end speedup of up to 3.7× as well as peak memory reduction of up to 32.6% in the decode phase on an NVIDIA A100 GPU compared to the full KV cache baseline, while achieving negligible accuracy loss on a variety of long-context tasks. We also propose a variant of RocketKV for multi-turn scenarios, which consistently outperforms other existing methods and achieves accuracy nearly on par with an oracle top-k attention scheme.
author: Payman Behnam;Yaosheng Fu;Ritchie Zhao;Po-An Tsai;Zhiding Yu;Alexey Tumanov
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;;
aff_unique_norm: Georgia Institute of Technology;NVIDIA;

roast.laymen_summary: AI models have a massive 'short-term memory' problem where they get slower and more expensive the longer you talk to them. This paper proposes a two-step digital shredder that aggressively deletes 'unimportant' data to keep the memory footprint small without—theoretically—making the AI lobotomized.
why_bad: The 400x compression ratio is a 'vanity metric' because the computational overhead of deciding what to delete often negates the actual time saved.
The 'negligible accuracy loss' claim likely crumbles during 'Needle In A Haystack' tests where the model needs to find one specific detail hidden in 100,000 words.
Hardware utilization is mediocre; claiming massive memory savings while only achieving a 3.7x speedup suggests the custom kernels are inefficiently fighting the GPU’s natural architecture.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you’re an executive assistant, but your office is the size of a shoebox. Instead of buying a bigger office, you decide to shred 99% of every memo the moment it hits your desk, keeping only the words written in bold. You can now 'process' 400 times more mail, and you’re technically faster because you aren't reading anything. It’s a visionary startup move: we maximize throughput by strategically forgetting what the client actually asked for. It’s not 'data loss,' it’s 'streamlined cognitive focus.'

title: Are High-Quality AI-Generated Images More Difficult for Models to Detect?
author_site: Yao Xiao, Binbin Yang, Weiyan Chen, Jiahao Chen, Zijie Cao, ZiYi Dong, Xiangyang Ji, Liang Lin, Wei Ke, Pengxu Wei
abstract: The remarkable evolution of generative models has enabled the generation of high-quality, visually attractive images, often perceptually indistinguishable from real photographs to human eyes. This has spurred significant attention on AI-generated image (AIGI) detection. Intuitively, higher image quality should increase detection difficulty. However, our systematic study on cutting-edge text-to-image generators reveals a counterintuitive finding: AIGIs with higher quality scores, as assessed by human preference models, tend to be more easily detected by existing models. To investigate this, we examine how the text prompts for generation and image characteristics influence both quality scores and detector accuracy. We observe that images from short prompts tend to achieve higher preference scores while being easier to detect. Furthermore, through clustering and regression analyses, we verify that image characteristics like saturation, contrast, and texture richness collectively impact both image quality and detector accuracy. Finally, we demonstrate that the performance of off-the-shelf detectors can be enhanced across diverse generators and datasets by selecting input patches based on the predicted scores of our regression models, thus substantiating the broader applicability of our findings. Code and data are available at \href{https://github.com/Coxy7/AIGI-Detection-Quality-Paradox}{GitHub}.
author: Yao Xiao;Binbin Yang;Weiyan Chen;Jiahao Chen;Zijie Cao;ZiYi Dong;Xiangyang Ji;Liang Lin;Wei Ke;Pengxu Wei

roast.laymen_summary: You'd think the most stunning, 'perfect' AI images would be the hardest to catch, but they're actually the biggest snitches on themselves. It turns out that when AI tries too hard to look pretty, it leaves behind neon-bright digital fingerprints that even basic detectors can spot from a mile away.
why_bad: Circular Logic Trap: The study uses 'human preference models' to define quality, which means they're basically measuring how much an AI likes another AI's aesthetic, rather than actual objective reality.

Correlation vs. Causation: Blaming 'short prompts' for high detection rates is like saying fast food is unhealthy because the bags are made of paper—it ignores the underlying architectural artifacts that actually matter.

The 'So What?' Factor: Their proposed fix is just a 'patch-selection' trick that tells detectors to look at the messy parts of an image, which is less of a scientific breakthrough and more of a manual for how to squint.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Deepfake Bowl, folks! The researchers come out of the tunnel thinking they’ve discovered a stealth play: 'If the image is beautiful, it'll sneak past the defense!' But wait—they’ve sent a player onto the field wearing a neon-pink tuxedo covered in 10 pounds of glitter. Sure, he looks 'high quality' to the fashion scouts, but the referees can see him from the nosebleed seats! By equating 'pretty' with 'invisible,' the authors managed to prove that if you turn the contrast and saturation up to 'Uncanny Valley' levels, you aren't a master of disguise—you're just the loudest guy in the room. They tried to fix it by telling the refs to look at the glitter (the 'patch selection'), but the refs just blew the whistle for a 15-yard penalty for stating the obvious. It's a turnover on downs, and the paper is headed straight to the locker room!

title: TreeLoRA: Efficient Continual Learning via Layer-Wise LoRAs Guided by a Hierarchical Gradient-Similarity Tree
author_site: Yu-Yang Qian, Yuan-Ze Xu, Zhen-Yu Zhang, Peng Zhao, Zhi-Hua Zhou
abstract: Many real-world applications collect data in a streaming environment, where learning tasks are encountered sequentially. This necessitates *continual learning* (CL) to update models online, enabling adaptation to new tasks while preserving past knowledge to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Nowadays, with the flourish of *large pre-trained models* (LPMs), *efficiency* has become increasingly critical for CL, due to their substantial computational demands and growing parameter sizes. In this paper, we introduce TreeLoRA (K-D Tree of Low-Rank Adapters), a novel approach that constructs *layer-wise* adapters by leveraging hierarchical gradient similarity to enable efficient CL, particularly for LPMs. To reduce the computational burden of task similarity estimation, we employ *bandit* techniques to develop an algorithm based on lower confidence bounds to efficiently explore the task structure. Furthermore, we use sparse gradient updates to facilitate parameter optimization, making the approach better suited for LPMs. Theoretical analysis is provided to justify the rationale behind our approach, and experiments on both *vision transformers* (ViTs) and *large language models* (LLMs) demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach across various domains, including vision and natural language processing tasks.
keywords: Continual Learning;Supervised Fine-Tuning;Large Pre-trained Models;Low-Rank Adaptation;Hierarchical Task Structure
author: Yu-Yang Qian;Yuan-Ze Xu;Zhen-Yu Zhang;Peng Zhao;Zhi-Hua Zhou

title: Armijo Line-search Can Make (Stochastic) Gradient Descent Provably Faster
author_site: Sharan Vaswani, Reza Babanezhad
abstract: Armijo line-search (Armijo-LS) is a standard method to set the step-size for gradient descent (GD). For smooth functions, Armijo-LS alleviates the need to know the global smoothness constant $L$ and adapts to the ``local'' smoothness, enabling GD to converge faster. Existing theoretical analyses show that GD with Armijo-LS ($\texttt{GD-LS}$) can result in constant factor improvements over GD with a $1/L$ step-size (denoted as $\texttt{GD(1/L)}$). We strengthen these results and show that if the objective function satisfies a certain non-uniform smoothness condition, $\texttt{GD-LS}$ can result in a faster convergence rate than $\texttt{GD(1/L)}$. In particular, we prove that for convex objectives corresponding to logistic regression and multi-class classification, $\texttt{GD-LS}$ can converge to the optimum at a linear rate, and hence improves over the sublinear convergence of $\texttt{GD(1/L)}$. Furthermore, for non-convex objectives satisfying gradient domination (e.g., those corresponding to the softmax policy gradient in RL or generalized linear models with a logistic link function), $\texttt{GD-LS}$ can match the fast convergence of algorithms tailored for these specific settings. Finally, we prove that under the interpolation assumption, for convex losses, stochastic GD with a stochastic line-search can match the fast convergence of $\texttt{GD-LS}$.
keywords: Armijo line-search;(Stochastic) Gradient descent;Convergence rates;Logistic regression;Policy optimization;Generalized linear models;Interpolation
author: Sharan Vaswani;Reza Babanezhad Harikandeh
aff: Simon Fraser University;Samsung

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI training follows a fixed speed limit that is either too slow for the flats or too fast for the curves. This paper argues that by constantly recalculating the speed limit on the fly, you can finish the race exponentially faster for specific types of math problems.
why_bad: The 'novel' proof relies on the interpolation assumption, which essentially assumes the model can perfectly fit the data—a luxury rarely seen outside of theoretical fever dreams.
The mathematical speedup only applies to a very narrow set of functions, making the results feel less like a breakthrough and more like a carefully curated victory lap.
Reviewers noted that the computational overhead of checking the 'local smoothness' at every step often eats the time savings, a classic case of the cure being as expensive as the disease.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every cyclist pedals at a constant, agonizingly slow rhythm to avoid crashing, one man dares to stop every six inches to measure the soil's friction coefficient. Meet 'The Line Searcher.' He promises to reach the finish line in record time by optimizing every single muscle twitch, provided the mountain is shaped exactly like a perfect cone and the laws of physics take a lunch break. He's faster, he's smarter, and he's currently stuck in a ditch because he forgot that real mountains have rocks. Witness the mathematical thriller where 'faster convergence' meets 'completely impractical implementation.' Coming this fall to a rejected folder near you.
roast.tone_check: Dry, snarky, and scientifically accurate.

title: DeepLayout: Learning Neural Representations of Circuit Placement Layout
author_site: Yuxiang Zhao, zhuomin chai, Xun Jiang, Qiang Xu, Runsheng Wang, Yibo Lin
abstract: Recent advancements have integrated various deep-learning methodologies into physical design, aiming for workflows acceleration and surpasses human-devised solutions. However, prior research has primarily concentrated on developing task-specific networks, which necessitate a significant investment of time to construct large, specialized datasets, and the unintended isolation of models across different tasks. In this paper, we introduce DeepLayout, the first general representation learning framework specifically designed for backend circuit design. To address the distinct characteristics of post-placement circuits, including topological connectivity and geometric distribution, we propose a hybrid encoding architecture that integrates GNN with spatial transformers. Additionally, the framework includes a flexible decoder module that accommodates a variety of task types, supporting multiple hierarchical outputs such as nets and layouts. To mitigate the high annotation costs associated with layout data, we introduce a mask-based self-supervised learning approach designed explicitly for layout representation. This strategy involves a carefully devised masking approach tailored to layout features, precise reconstruction guidance, and most critically—two key supervised learning tasks. We conduct extensive experiments on large-scale industrial datasets, demonstrating that DeepLayout surpasses state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods specialized for individual tasks on two crucial layout quality assessment benchmarks. The experiment results underscore the framework’s robust capability to learn the intrinsic properties of circuits.
author: Yuxiang Zhao;zhuomin chai;Xun Jiang;Qiang Xu;Runsheng Wang;Yibo Lin
aff: Peking University;Wuhan University;Peking University;The Chinese University of Hong Kong;Peking University;Peking University
