roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'Swiss Army Knife' AI that understands computer chip layouts by training it to play a high-tech game of hide-and-seek with circuit components. Instead of teaching the AI specific tasks, they hoped it would develop a 'gut feeling' for good design by looking at massive piles of unlabeled industrial data.
why_bad: The 'mask-and-reconstruct' approach is basically a glorified autocorrect for circuits that might prioritize looking pretty over actually following the laws of physics.
Claims of beating specialized SOTA models are suspicious because the 'general' architecture is likely just memorizing patterns from the industrial dataset rather than learning transferable logic.
The hybrid GNN-Transformer architecture is a 'complexity smoothie' that adds massive computational overhead without proving that the extra layers are doing anything useful.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're on a cooking show and, instead of making a world-class risotto, you present a 'Universal Kitchen Machine' that blends random ingredients into a gray paste. You tell the judges that because the machine learned to 'reconstruct' a hidden strawberry in a blender, it can now cook anything from sushi to brisket just by changing the output nozzle. The judges (the reviewers) taste it and realize that while your machine is very expensive and uses 'hybrid blade technology,' the resulting sludge isn't actually better than a specialized steak knife, and nobody knows what’s actually in the paste because the ingredients are 'proprietary industrial secrets.'

title: Fully Dynamic Embedding into $\ell_p$ Spaces
author_site: Kiarash Banihashem, Xiang Chen, MohammadTaghi Hajiaghayi, Sungchul Kim, Kanak Mahadik, Ryan A Rossi, Tong Yu
abstract:
Metric embeddings are fundamental in machine learning, enabling similarity search, dimensionality reduction, and representation learning. They underpin modern architectures like transformers and large language models, facilitating scalable training and improved generalization.
Theoretically, the classic problem in embedding design is mapping arbitrary metrics into $\ell_p$ spaces while approximately preserving pairwise distances. We study this problem in a fully dynamic setting, where the underlying metric is a graph metric subject to edge insertions and deletions.
Our goal is to maintain an efficient embedding after each update.
We present the first fully dynamic algorithm for this problem, achieving $O(\log(n))^{2q} O(\log(nW))^{q-1}$ expected distortion with $O(m^{1/q + o(1)})$ update time and $O(q \log(n) \log(nW))$ query time, where $q \ge 2$ is an integer parameter.
author: Kiarash Banihashem;Xiang Chen;MohammadTaghi Hajiaghayi;Sungchul Kim;Kanak Mahadik;Ryan A. Rossi;Tong Yu
aff: University of Maryland, College Park;Adobe Systems;University of Maryland, College Park;Adobe Systems;Adobe Inc;;Adobe Research
aff_unique_norm: University of Maryland;Adobe;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to create a way to map complex, ever-changing networks into simple math spaces so AI can process them faster. Unfortunately, their 'solution' is so mathematically bloated that the map ends up looking like a Rorschach test drawn by a drunk cartographer.
why_bad: The 'distortion' (error rate) is mathematically astronomical; it claims to preserve distances, but the error margin is wide enough to drive a galaxy through.
The paper is a 'buzzword sandwich' that mentions Transformers and LLMs to sound relevant, despite being a dense graph theory paper with zero actual experiments on AI models.
The update time is only 'fast' in a theoretical universe where $O(m^{1/q})$ doesn't practically mean 'wait until the heat death of the universe' for large datasets.
roast.analogy_name: The GPS From Hell: Ghost Road Edition
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where the streets are literally being paved and demolished while you're driving on them, one algorithm dares to give you directions. But there's a catch. To keep the map 'efficient,' your GPS will tell you the grocery store is 2 miles away, but it might actually be in a different dimension. See the thrill of a 'dynamic' map that updates every second, yet somehow leaves you more lost than when you started. It’s got Greek letters! It’s got logarithmic nightmares! It’s: THE DISTORTION OF L-P SPACE. Coming to a rejected folder near you.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered.

title: Quadruple Attention in Many-body Systems for Accurate Molecular Property Predictions
author_site: Jiahua Rao, Dahao Xu, Wentao Wei, Yicong Chen, Mingjun Yang, Yuedong Yang
abstract: While Graph Neural Networks and Transformers have shown promise in predicting molecular properties, they struggle with directly modeling complex many-body interactions. Current methods often approximate interactions like three- and four-body terms in message passing, while attention-based models, despite enabling direct atom communication, are typically limited to triplets, making higher-order interactions computationally demanding. To address the limitations, we introduce MABNet, a geometric attention framework designed to model four-body interactions by facilitating direct communication among atomic quartets. This approach bypasses the computational bottlenecks associated with traditional triplet-based attention mechanisms, allowing for the efficient handling of higher-order interactions. MABNet achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like MD22 and SPICE. These improvements underscore its capability to accurately capture intricate many-body interactions in large molecules. By unifying rigorous many-body physics with computational efficiency, MABNet advances molecular simulations for applications in drug design and materials discovery, while its extensible framework paves the way for modeling higher-order quantum effects.
keywords: Molecular Property Prediction;Many-body System;Quadruple Attention;Molecular Force Fields
author: Jiahua Rao;Dahao Xu;Wentao Wei;Yicong Chen;Mingjun Yang;Yuedong Yang
aff: Sun Yat-sen University;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;XtalPi;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY
aff_unique_norm: Sun Yat-sen University;XtalPi Inc.

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI models predict molecular behavior by looking at pairs or trios of atoms, but this paper attempts to eavesdrop on four atoms talking at once. It claims to solve complex chemistry by making the math significantly more crowded and computationally expensive under the guise of 'efficiency.'
why_bad: The 'computational efficiency' claim is a stretch; tracking four-body interactions is an N-to-the-fourth-power scaling nightmare that no amount of fancy math can fully mask.
The performance gains on benchmarks are statistically marginal, suggesting the model is working four times harder for a 1% improvement in accuracy.
It attempts to rebrand standard attention mechanisms as 'rigorous physics,' which is like saying a game of Tetris is a simulation of civil engineering.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're at a crowded bar. Current AI tries to understand the room by listening to couples or trios talk. MABNet is a startup pitching a 'revolutionary' microphone that only works if it captures exactly four people speaking in a perfect square. Our pitch? 'We capture the synergistic vibe of the double-date!' Sure, the data is more 'intricate,' but the hardware costs a fortune, it crashes if someone goes to the bathroom, and at the end of the night, you still just found out that everyone wants pizza. It's a high-tech solution to a problem that was already 95% solved by just listening to the loud guy at the bar.

title: Multi-Timescale Dynamics Model Bayesian Optimization for Plasma Stabilization in Tokamaks
author_site: Rohit Sonker, Alexandre Capone, Andrew Rothstein, Hiro Kaga, Egemen Kolemen, Jeff Schneider
abstract: Machine learning algorithms often struggle to control complex real-world systems. In the case of nuclear fusion, these challenges are exacerbated, as the dynamics are notoriously complex, data is poor, hardware is subject to failures, and experiments often affect dynamics beyond the experiment's duration. Existing tools like reinforcement learning, supervised learning, and Bayesian optimization address some of these challenges but fail to provide a comprehensive solution. To overcome these limitations, we present a multi-scale Bayesian optimization approach that integrates a high-frequency data-driven dynamics model with a low-frequency Gaussian process. By updating the Gaussian process between experiments, the method rapidly adapts to new data, refining the predictions of the less reliable dynamical model. We validate our approach by controlling tearing instabilities in the DIII-D nuclear fusion plant. Offline testing on historical data shows that our method significantly outperforms several baselines. Results on live experiments on the DIII-D tokamak, conducted under high-performance plasma scenarios prone to instabilities, shows a 50\% success rate — marking a 117\% improvement over historical outcomes.
keywords: Nuclear Fusion;Plasma Instabilities;Bayesian Optimization;Applied Machine Learning
author: Rohit Sonker;Alexandre Capone;Andrew Rothstein;Hiro Josep Farre Kaga;Egemen Kolemen;Jeff Schneider
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Princeton University;Princeton University;Princeton University;Carnegie Mellon University
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;Princeton University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop a miniature sun from exploding by combining a twitchy, high-speed AI with a slow-moving, cautious calculator. It only failed to stabilize the reaction half the time, which they consider a massive win compared to the usual total disaster.
why_bad: A 50% success rate in nuclear fusion is essentially a coin flip where 'tails' results in a multi-million dollar hardware meltdown.
The '117% improvement' is a classic statistical shell game used to make a mediocre result look revolutionary by comparing it to an abysmal baseline.
Validating a real-time plasma controller using historical data is like claiming you can win a fistfight because you watched a lot of Rocky movies.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Tokamak Finals, folks! Our rookie 'Multi-Scale Model' is in the ring, and it’s a wild one. It’s got the reflexes of a caffeinated squirrel but the long-term memory of a goldfish. The coaches are screaming about a '117% improvement' because last year the stadium literally melted, but look at the scoreboard: he’s still getting bucked off the sun every other attempt. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton—trying to stabilize 100 million degrees with a 'maybe' and a spreadsheet. Let's see if he can hold the magnetic field or if we're all going to be looking for new jobs by the third quarter!

title: Achieving Linear Speedup and Near-Optimal Complexity for Decentralized Optimization over Row-stochastic Networks
author_site: Liyuan Liang, Xinyi Chen, Gan Luo, Kun Yuan
abstract: A key challenge in decentralized optimization is determining the optimal convergence rate and designing algorithms to achieve it. While this problem has been extensively addressed for doubly-stochastic and column-stochastic mixing matrices, the row-stochastic scenario remains unexplored. This paper bridges this gap by introducing effective metrics to capture the influence of row-stochastic mixing matrices and establishing the first convergence lower bound for decentralized learning over row-stochastic networks.  However, existing algorithms fail to attain this lower bound due to two key issues: deviation in the descent direction caused by the adapted gradient tracking (GT) and instability introduced by the Pull-Diag protocol. To address descent deviation, we propose a novel analysis framework demonstrating that Pull-Diag-GT achieves linear speedup—the first such result for row-stochastic decentralized optimization. Moreover, by incorporating a multi-step gossip (MG) protocol, we resolve the instability issue and attain the lower bound, achieving near-optimal complexity for decentralized optimization over row-stochastic networks.
author: Liyuan Liang;Xinyi Chen;Gan Luo;Kun Yuan
aff: ;;Peking University;
aff_unique_norm: ;Peking University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to coordinate a giant group of computers to solve a math problem together, even when the 'telephone game' they play is one-sided and messy. The authors claim they've found the ultimate shortcut to make this chaotic gossip session just as fast as having a single boss in charge.
why_bad: The 'Multi-step Gossip' solution is the mathematical equivalent of telling a confused crowd to just scream louder and more often, which adds massive overhead that the authors conveniently gloss over.
The 'linear speedup' claim only holds true in a theoretical vacuum where network delays don't exist and every computer behaves like a saint.
The proofs are a dense thicket of 'adapted metrics' that seem specifically engineered to hide the fact that the algorithm's stability is held together by digital duct tape.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the One-Way Witness
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom where 50 jurors are trying to reach a verdict, but they can only pass notes in one direction—from the back row to the front—and half the jurors are wearing noise-canceling headphones. The Defense Attorney (the Author) stands up and brazenly claims that the trial is actually moving 50 times faster because they've added more jurors to the 'whisper chain.' The Prosecution (Reviewer 2) immediately objects, pointing out that 'Multi-Step Gossip' is just a fancy legal term for 'organized hearsay' that makes the trial twice as long. The Judge looks at the 'Lower Bound' evidence, realizes it's just a circular argument drawn in crayon, and bangs the gavel: 'Sustained. This isn't decentralized justice; it's a game of Telephone where nobody is listening. Case dismissed!'

title: MindLLM: A Subject-Agnostic and Versatile Model for fMRI-to-text Decoding
author_site: Weikang Qiu, Zheng Huang, Haoyu Hu, Aosong Feng, Yujun Yan, ZHITAO YING
abstract: Decoding functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) signals into text has been a key challenge in the neuroscience community, with the potential to advance brain-computer interfaces and uncover deeper insights into brain mechanisms. However, existing approaches often struggle with suboptimal predictive performance, limited task variety, and poor generalization across subjects. In response to this, we propose MindLLM, a model designed for subject-agnostic and versatile fMRI-to-text decoding. MindLLM consists of an fMRI encoder and an off-the-shelf LLM. The fMRI encoder employs a neuroscience-informed attention mechanism, which is capable of accommodating subjects with varying input shapes and thus achieves high-performance subject-agnostic decoding. Moreover, we introduce Brain Instruction Tuning (BIT), a novel approach that enhances the model's ability to capture diverse semantic representations from fMRI signals, facilitating more versatile decoding. We evaluate MindLLM on comprehensive fMRI-to-text benchmarks. Results demonstrate that our model outperforms the baselines, improving downstream tasks by $12.0\%$, unseen subject generalization by $24.5\%$, and novel task adaptation by $25.0\%$. Furthermore, the attention patterns in MindLLM provide interpretable insights into its decision-making process.
author: Weikang Qiu;Zheng Huang;Haoyu Hu;Aosong Feng;Yujun Yan;Rex Ying
aff: Yale University;Dartmouth College;;Yale University;Dartmouth College;Yale University
position: PhD student;PhD student;;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: Yale University;Dartmouth College;

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to build a universal 'brain-to-text' translator by plugging a fancy MRI scanner directly into a chatbot. They claimed it could read anyone's thoughts regardless of their brain structure, but in reality, it mostly just used the chatbot's imagination to fill in the massive gaps in the noisy brain data.
why_bad: The model suffers from 'hallucination heavy-lifting,' where the LLM generates coherent sentences that sound plausible but aren't actually supported by the blurry fMRI signal.
The 'subject-agnostic' claim is statistically shaky; brain anatomy is as unique as a fingerprint, and the paper fails to prove it isn't just overfitting to a specific group of college students.
Using standard text-matching metrics (like BLEU scores) to evaluate 'thought' is like grading a painting by how many times the artist used the color blue—it misses the actual meaning entirely.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that promises to translate your cat's meows into professional LinkedIn posts. Instead of actually understanding feline linguistics, the app just records a muffled 'meow,' detects the cat is roughly near a laptop, and then uses GPT-4 to generate a post about 'grindset' and 'synergy.' It looks impressive in a demo because the output is grammatically perfect, but you haven't actually decoded the cat—you've just built a very expensive, neuroscience-themed Mad Libs machine. We’re pivoting from 'Scientific Accuracy' to 'Statistically Significant Guesswork' and we’re looking for $20M in Series A.

title: Structure-Guided Large Language Models for Text-to-SQL Generation
author_site: Qinggang Zhang, Hao Chen, Junnan Dong, Shengyuan Chen, Feiran Huang, Xiao Huang
abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in bridging the gap between natural language queries and database management systems, enabling users to interact with databases without the background of SQL. However, LLMs often struggle to fully exploit and comprehend the user intention and complex structures of databases. Decomposition-based methods have been proposed to enhance the performance of LLMs on complex tasks, but decomposing SQL generation into subtasks is non-trivial due to the declarative structure of SQL syntax and the intricate connections between query concepts and database elements. In this paper, we propose a novel Structure GUided text-to-SQL framework ( SGU-SQL) that incorporates syntax-based prompting to enhance the SQL generation capabilities of LLMs. Specifically, SGU-SQL establishes structure-aware links between user queries and database schema and recursively decomposes the complex generation task using syntax-based prompting to guide LLMs in incrementally constructing target SQLs. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that SGU-SQL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art text-to-SQL baselines.
author: Qinggang Zhang;Hao Chen;Junnan Dong;Shengyuan Chen;Feiran Huang;Xiao Huang

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop AI from hallucinating fake database tables by forcing it to build SQL queries one tiny, syntax-correct brick at a time. It’s essentially a high-tech assembly line designed to prevent the model from getting overwhelmed by its own lack of logic.
why_bad: The 'novel' framework is effectively just prompt engineering with a fancy name and no architectural innovation.
Recursive prompting creates massive latency, making the system too slow for any real-world database where users expect results in under a decade.
Performance gains are marginal and likely overfitting on clean benchmarks while failing to address the messy, 'spaghetti' schemas found in the wild.
roast.analogy_description: We’re disrupting the kitchen by replacing a head chef with a genius who has never seen a stove. Instead of letting the AI cook, SGU-SQL stands over it and dictates every single micro-movement—'Pick up the salt. Now move your hand three inches left.' It’s a revolutionary way to spend $400 on a single grilled cheese sandwich that arrives three hours late. Sure, the sandwich is technically correct, but the restaurant went bankrupt while the AI was still figuring out how to 'JOIN' the bread and the cheese.

title: Data-driven Design of Randomized Control Trials with Guaranteed Treatment Effects
author_site: Santiago Cortes-Gomez, Naveen Raman, Aarti Singh, Bryan Wilder
abstract:
Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) generate guarantees for treatment effects. However, RCTs often spend unnecessary resources exploring sub-optimal treatments, which can reduce the power of treatment guarantees. To address this, we propose a two-stage RCT design. In the first stage, a data-driven screening procedure prunes low-impact treatments, while the second stage focuses on developing high-probability lower bounds for the best-performing treatment effect. 
Unlike existing adaptive RCT frameworks, our method is simple enough to be implemented in scenarios with limited adaptivity.
We derive optimal designs for two-stage RCTs and demonstrate how such designs can be implemented through sample splitting.
Empirically, we demonstrate that two-stage designs improve upon single-stage approaches, especially for scenarios where domain knowledge is available through a prior. Our work is thus, a simple yet effective design for RCTs, optimizing for the ability to certify with high probability the largest possible treatment effect for at least one of the arms studied.
author: Santiago Cortes-Gomez;Naveen Janaki Raman;Aarti Singh;Bryan Wilder
aff: School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of wasting time and money testing every mediocre idea until the end, these researchers suggest doing a 'vibe check' halfway through to prune the losers. By focusing the remaining budget only on the promising candidates, they hope to get a statistically significant trophy for the winner without doing too much extra math.
why_bad: The 'simplicity' of the method is a polite way of saying it lacks the mathematical rigor found in existing multi-armed bandit or fully adaptive frameworks.
Sample splitting often results in a massive loss of statistical power, effectively throwing away perfectly good data just to satisfy a two-stage structure.
The paper fails to adequately address the selection bias inherent in choosing the 'best' candidate from the first stage before testing it again in the second.
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where you have ten minutes to find a spouse but only enough money for one nice dinner. Watch as one scientist rejects nine suitors after a single handshake, just so he can spend his entire life savings on a lobster dinner for the tenth person who didn't sneeze. Is it true love? Or did he just pick the only person who wasn't currently on their phone? This summer, witness the high-stakes drama of 'Cherry-Picking for Credit,' where the stats are guaranteed, but the soul is missing.

title: QT-DoG: Quantization-Aware Training for Domain Generalization
author_site: Saqib Javed, Hieu Le, Mathieu Salzmann
abstract: A key challenge in Domain Generalization (DG) is preventing overfitting to source domains, which can be mitigated by finding flatter minima in the loss landscape. In this work, we propose Quantization-aware Training for Domain Generalization (QT-DoG) and demonstrate that weight quantization effectively leads to flatter minima in the loss landscape, thereby enhancing domain generalization. Unlike traditional quantization methods focused on model compression, QT-DoG exploits quantization as an implicit regularizer by inducing noise in model weights, guiding the optimization process toward flatter minima that are less sensitive to perturbations and overfitting. We provide both an analytical perspective and empirical evidence demonstrating that quantization inherently encourages flatter minima, leading to better generalization across domains. Moreover, with the benefit of reducing the model size through quantization, we demonstrate that an ensemble of multiple quantized models further yields superior accuracy than the state-of-the-art DG approaches with no computational or memory overheads. Code is released at: https://saqibjaved1.github.io/QT_DoG/.
author: Saqib Javed;Hieu Le;Mathieu Salzmann
aff: EPFL - EPF Lausanne;EPFL - EPF Lausanne;Swiss Data Science Center+ETHZ - ETH Zurich+Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne+CSIRO
aff_unique_norm: EPFL;Swiss Data Science Center;ETH Zurich;Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne;Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation

roast.laymen_summary: The authors suggest that intentionally making an AI's brain lower-resolution—like replacing a high-def photo with a pixelated one—actually helps it stop obsessing over useless details. They argue that by rounding off the math, the model is forced to find broader, 'flatter' solutions that work better in the real world.
why_bad: The core trick—using quantization as a noise-injection regularizer—is a well-known phenomenon, making the 'novelty' feel like a fresh coat of paint on a very old house.

The 'superior accuracy' is a classic 'apples-to-oranges' cheat; comparing an ensemble of multiple small models to a single large model is just proving that two heads are better than one, regardless of how 'quantized' they are.
The theoretical link between rounding errors and 'flatter minima' is more of an optimistic correlation than a proven causation, leaving the actual math feeling a bit like 'trust me, bro.'
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that trains world-class wine tasters by forcing them to wear beer goggles. The pitch: 'Look, expert sommeliers overfit; they get distracted by the specific vintage of the soil's topsoil. Our tasters literally can't see the label or the glass, so they focus on the big-picture Vibe of the grape.' By intentionally dulling their senses—we call this 'Strategic Sensory Debasement'—they become robust to different types of glasses. Then, we hire five of these buzzed tasters for the price of one sober expert and claim we've disrupted the palate industry. It’s not 'low quality'; it's 'Loss-Landscape Smoothing for Optimized Oenology.' Invest now.

title: Adaptive Estimation and Learning under Temporal Distribution Shift
author_site: Dheeraj Baby, Yifei Tang, Hieu Nguyen, Yu-Xiang Wang, Rohit Pyati
abstract: In this paper, we study the problem of estimation and learning under temporal distribution shift. Consider an observation sequence of length $n$, which is a noisy realization of a time-varying ground-truth sequence. Our focus is to develop methods to estimate the groundtruth at the final time-step while providing sharp point-wise estimation error rates. We show that, *without prior knowledge* on the level of temporal shift, a wavelet soft-thresholding estimator provides an *optimal* estimation error bound for the groundtruth. Our proposed estimation method generalizes existing researches (Mazetto and Upfal, 2023) by establishing a connection between the sequence's non-stationarity level and the sparsity in the wavelet-transformed domain. Our theoretical findings are validated by numerical experiments. Additionally, we applied the estimator to derive sparsity-aware excess risk bounds for binary classification under distribution shift and to develop computationally efficient training objectives. As a final contribution, we draw parallels between our results and the classical signal processing problem of total-variation denoising (Mammen and van de Geer 1997; Tibshirani 2014 ), uncovering *novel optimal* algorithms for such task.
keywords: Temporal Distribution Shift; Non-Stationary data; Total Variation Denoising
author: Dheeraj Baby;Yifei Tang;Hieu Duy Nguyen;Yu-Xiang Wang;Rohit Pyati
aff: Amazon;Amazon;Amazon;University of California, San Diego;Amazon
aff_unique_norm: Amazon;University of California, San Diego

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a tool that predicts the present state of a system even when the rules keep changing over time. They used a math trick from the 1990s to filter out noise and claimed it’s the best possible solution, despite having no idea how fast the data is actually shifting.
why_bad: The 'optimal' error rates rely on the assumption that the data is 'sparse,' which is academic shorthand for 'this only works if most of your data is irrelevant.'
The supposedly 'novel' connection to total-variation denoising is a well-trodden path that signal processing researchers finished mapping out before the authors graduated high school.
The transition from theoretical math to binary classification is a massive leap of faith that the provided numerical experiments fail to land.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Distribution Shift,' where the ingredients in your pantry transform every five minutes. You’re trying to plate a perfect omelet, but the eggs keep turning into ping-pong balls. Instead of watching the stove, you decide to use a 30-year-old mesh strainer to filter out anything that doesn't look like breakfast and tell the judges it’s an 'optimal' culinary strategy. When the head chef points out you’re just making scrambled water, you claim you’ve discovered a 'novel' connection between heat and cooking. You’re basically serving a plate of steam and calling it a 'sparsity-aware deconstructed souffle' while the restaurant burns down around you.

title: CPCF: A Cross-Prompt Contrastive Framework for Referring Multimodal Large Language Models
author_site: Lanyun Zhu, Deyi Ji, Tianrun Chen, Haiyang Wu, De Wen Soh, Jun Liu
abstract: Referring MLLMs extend conventional multimodal large language models by allowing them to receive referring visual prompts and generate responses tailored to the indicated regions. However, these models often suffer from suboptimal performance due to incorrect responses tailored to misleading areas adjacent to or similar to the target region. This work introduces CPCF, a novel framework to address this issue and achieve superior results. CPCF contrasts outputs generated from the indicated visual prompt with those from contrastive prompts sampled from misleading regions, effectively suppressing the influence of erroneous information outside the target region on response generation. To further enhance the effectiveness and efficiency of our framework, several novel designs are proposed, including a prompt extraction network to automatically identify suitable contrastive prompts, a self-training method that leverages unlabeled data to improve training quality, and a distillation approach to reduce the additional computational overhead associated with contrastive decoding. Incorporating these novel designs, CPCF achieves state-of-the-art performance, as demonstrated by extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks. Project page: https://lanyunzhu.site/CPCF/
author: Lanyun Zhu;Deyi Ji;Tianrun Chen;Haiyang Wu;De Wen Soh;Jun Liu

title: Overcoming Vocabulary Mismatch: Vocabulary-agnostic Teacher Guided Language Modeling
author_site: Haebin Shin, Lei Ji, Xiao Liu, Yeyun Gong
abstract: Using large teacher models to guide the training of smaller student models has become the prevailing paradigm for efficient and effective learning. However, vocabulary mismatches between teacher and student language models pose significant challenges in language modeling, resulting in divergent token sequences and output distributions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Vocabulary-agnostic Teacher Guided Language Modeling (VocAgnoLM), a novel approach that bridges the gap caused by vocabulary mismatch through two key methods: (1) Token-level Lexical Alignment, which aligns token sequences across mismatched vocabularies, and (2) Teacher Guided Loss, which leverages the loss of teacher model to guide effective student training. We demonstrate its effectiveness in language modeling with 1B student model using various 7B teacher models with different vocabularies. Notably, with Qwen2.5-Math-Instruct, a teacher model sharing only about 6% of its vocabulary with TinyLlama, VocAgnoLM achieves a 46% performance improvement compared to naive continual pretraining. Furthermore, we demonstrate that VocAgnoLM consistently benefits from stronger teacher models, providing a robust solution to vocabulary mismatches in language modeling.
author: Haebin Shin;Lei Ji;Xiao Liu;Yeyun Gong
aff: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology;Microsoft Research;Microsoft Research Asia;Microsoft
aff_unique_norm: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology;Microsoft

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make a giant AI model act as a tutor for a tiny model, even though they technically speak different languages. They built a 'universal translator' for code and words so the small model could copy the big one's homework more effectively.
why_bad: The 46% performance leap is measured against a 'naive' baseline, which is basically comparing a professional athlete to a toddler with tied shoelaces.
The 'Token-level Lexical Alignment' is essentially a glorified lookup table that adds significant computational overhead for marginal real-world utility.
The paper fails to prove that this complex 'teacher-student' dance is actually better than just training the small model on high-quality data from the start.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine hiring a Harvard physics professor who only speaks Klingon to tutor a middle-schooler for their SATs. Instead of just teaching the kid English, we’ve developed a 'disruptive' middleware that translates the professor’s guttural barks into emojis in real-time. Is it revolutionary? Sure. Is it more efficient than just buying the kid a textbook? Absolutely not. It’s a $10 million solution to a problem we created by being too stubborn to just use a teacher who speaks the same language. We’re currently pre-seed and looking for investors who value 'vocabulary agnosticism' over common sense.

title: Improved Convex Decomposition with Ensembling and Boolean Primitives
abstract: Describing a scene in terms of primitives -- geometrically simple shapes that offer a parsimonious but accurate abstraction of structure -- is an established and difficult fitting problem. Different scenes require different numbers of primitives, and these primitives interact strongly. Existing methods are evaluated by predicting depth, normals and segmentation from the primitives, then evaluating the accuracy of those predictions. The state of the art method involves a learned regression procedure to predict a start point consisting of a fixed number of primitives, followed by a descent method to refine the geometry and remove redundant primitives. CSG representations are significantly enhanced by a set-differencing operation. Our representation incorporates $\textit{negative}$ primitives, which are differenced from the positive primitives. These notably enrich the geometry that the model can encode, while complicating the fitting problem. This paper  demonstrates a method that can (a) incorporate these negative primitives and (b) choose the overall number of positive and negative primitives by ensembling. Extensive experiments on the standard NYUv2 dataset confirm that (a) this approach results in substantial improvements in depth representation and segmentation over SOTA and (b) negative primitives make a notable contribution to accuracy. Our method is robustly applicable across datasets: in a first, we evaluate primitive prediction for LAION images. Code will be released upon acceptance of the paper.
author: Vaibhav Vavilala;Florian Kluger;Seemandhar Jain;Bodo Rosenhahn;David Forsyth
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;Leibniz Universität Hannover;;Institut für Informationsverarbeitung;University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign
aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Leibniz Universität Hannover;;Institut für Informationsverarbeitung;University of Illinois

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to map out 3D rooms by mashing simple blocks together and using 'anti-blocks' to carve out holes, like a digital version of sculpting with clay. To figure out how many blocks to use, they just ran the program a bunch of times and picked the version that looked the least broken.
why_bad: Introducing 'negative primitives' makes the math so messy that the AI frequently gets stuck in 'local minima,' essentially trying to carve a window in the middle of the floor.
The 'ensembling' approach is a computational nightmare that hides the fact they couldn't actually design a smart way to decide when to stop adding shapes.
Claiming a 'first' by evaluating on the LAION dataset is a reach, considering LAION is a messy pile of 2D internet images with zero 3D ground truth to actually prove the model isn't hallucinating.
roast.analogy_description: Think of this as a startup that builds luxury homes using nothing but giant LEGO bricks and a flamethrower to melt holes for the windows. It’s a bold aesthetic, but the flamethrower keeps melting the supporting walls, and the team can't figure out if they need 50 bricks or 5,000. Their 'disruptive' solution? Build 12 houses at the same time, see which one hasn't collapsed by Tuesday, and tell the investors that the 'Negative Space Synergy' was intentional. It’s a pivot from 'actually solving 3D geometry' to 'brute-forcing a vibe check,' and the reviewers aren't buying the seed round.
